Afghanistan.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate resilience and water security** in **Afghanistan** through **science-based adaptation, hydrological modeling, and crop innovation**. Specifically:
- **CGIAR’s Climate Change, Agriculture and Food Security (CCAFS) and CIMMYT** developed **heat-tolerant maize and wheat varieties** to mitigate yield losses from rising temperatures (Sources: [10.1007/s00704-016-1931-6](https://doi.org/10.1007/s00704-016-1931-6), [hdl:11529/10548355](hdl:11529/10548355)).
- **CGIAR’s Water Security Program** coupled **remote sensing and hydrological modeling (SWAT)** to assess climate impacts on the **Kabul River Basin’s streamflow**, enabling sustainable water resource management (Sources: [10.3390/su132414025](https://doi.org/10.3390/su132414025), [10.3390/w14111697](https://doi.org/10.3390/w14111697)).
Afghanistan.Adaptation:
• **CGIAR-CCAFS** evaluated **heat-tolerant maize varieties**, reducing projected yield losses by **36–93%** under future climate scenarios in South Asia (including Afghanistan’s agroecologies) (Source: [10.1007/s00704-016-1931-6](https://doi.org/10.1007/s00704-016-1931-6)).

• **CGIAR’s Resilience Program** analyzed **climate extremes in the Kabul River Basin**, projecting **increased heatwaves and decreased frost days**, and recommended **climate actions to build adaptive capacity** in agriculture, health, and water sectors (Source: [10.1007/s10113-023-02167-3](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10113-023-02167-3)).

• **CGIAR’s Social, Economic & Political Sciences (SEPS) Division** highlighted **hybrid adaptive knowledge systems** (integrating local and scientific expertise) as critical for **climate change adaptation in the Asian Highlands**, including Afghanistan (Source: [10.1007/s10584-014-1090-7](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-014-1090-7)).

• **CGIAR’s Migration & Climate Change Program** examined **migration as an adaptation strategy** to hydroclimatic hazards (e.g., droughts, floods), noting mixed outcomes and the need for **policy integration** (Source: [10.1007/s10584-023-03573-6](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-023-03573-6)).

• **CGIAR’s Climate Services Program** advanced **decision-centered climate impact services** in South Asia (including Afghanistan) to enhance **resilience planning** through seasonal forecasts and extreme-event data (Source: [10.1016/j.cliser.2022.100295](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cliser.2022.100295)).

• **Bioversity International (CGIAR Center)** developed **drought- and heat-tolerant wheat landraces** with **disease resistance**, promoting **biodiversity-based resilience** in target regions (Source: [hdl:11529/10548355](hdl:11529/10548355)).
Afghanistan.Mitigation: • **No direct evidence of CGIAR-led mitigation research (e.g., GHG reduction, carbon neutrality) in Afghanistan** was found in this sample. However, **CGIAR’s Nexus Program** assessed **climate vulnerabilities in Central Asia’s water-agriculture-energy sectors**, implicitly linking adaptation and mitigation via **carbon-neutrality goals** (Source: [10568/128220](https://doi.org/10.1079/9781800622371.0004)).
Afghanistan.Water:
• **CGIAR’s Water Security Program** used **SWAT modeling with remote sensing** to project **15–42% reductions in Kabul River Basin streamflow** under RCP 4.5/8.5 scenarios, identifying **spatial heterogeneity in water availability** (Sources: [10.3390/su132414025](https://doi.org/10.3390/su132414025), [10.3390/w14111697](https://doi.org/10.3390/w14111697)).

• **CGIAR’s Nexus Program** analyzed **transboundary water vulnerabilities** in Afghan-Pakistani river basins (Kabul, Kurram, Gomal), highlighting **temperature-driven precipitation uncertainties** and **declining southern basin discharges** (Source: [10.1079/9781800622371.0004](https://doi.org/10.1079/9781800622371.0004)).

• **CGIAR’s Climate Services** integrated **climate projections with water resource sectors** to support **strategic planning** in data-scarce regions like Afghanistan (Source: [10.1007/s10113-023-02167-3](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10113-023-02167-3)).

Algeria.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **adaptation and participatory agricultural innovation** in **Algeria** through **farmer-led breeding programs** and **spatial data infrastructure**. Specifically:
- **ICARDA** developed **participatory plant breeding (PPB) programs** to co-design drought-tolerant wheat varieties with farmers, enhancing agricultural biodiversity in 50+ Algerian villages (Source: **10568/36078**).
- **IFPRI** compiled national-level **agricultural R&D resource datasets** (financial, human, institutional) to support evidence-based policymaking (Source: **doi:10.7910/DVN/5L2GLP**).
Algeria.Adaptation: • **ICARDA** implemented **participatory plant breeding programs** in Algeria, Egypt, Eritrea, Jordan, Syria, and Iran to develop drought-tolerant wheat varieties aligned with farmers’ needs while preserving agricultural biodiversity (Source: **10568/36078**).
Algeria.Mitigation: No evidence found in this sample.
Algeria.Water: • CGIAR researchers generated **pixel-based socio-ecological datasets** (1 km² resolution) for Algeria, Tunisia, and Morocco, mapping rangeland productivity and livestock density for spatial analysis (Source: **hdl:20.500.11766.1/FK2/AO9GYD**).

American Samoa.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate resilience and adaptation in Pacific Island food systems, including American Samoa**, through scenario-based policy insights. Specifically:
- The Center developed **four alternative future scenarios for Pacific food systems**—focusing on fisheries, forests, trade, affordability, and public health—to inform climate adaptation strategies for fishers and farmers (Source: **10568/75610**).
American Samoa.Adaptation: • CGIAR developed **four alternative scenarios for Pacific food systems** to enhance resilience and adaptation among fishers and farmers in American Samoa and the broader Pacific region, addressing climate vulnerability to droughts and tropical cyclones (10568/75610).
American Samoa.Mitigation: No evidence found in this sample.
American Samoa.Water: No evidence found in this sample.

Angola.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate resilience and agricultural adaptation** in **Angola** through **agroclimatic data, risk assessment, and policy-relevant research**. Specifically:
- **CGIAR’s Climate and Food Security Research** developed high-resolution agroclimatic indices (e.g., dry/wet spells, heatwaves, water balance) for maize, dry beans, soybeans, and coffee regions in Angola, enabling targeted climate risk management (10568/109192).
- **The Center for International Forestry Research (CIFOR) and partners** assessed climate risk hotspots in Angola, mapping vulnerability to extreme rainfall and drought hazards under RCP4.5 and 8.5 scenarios (10568/178443).
Angola.Adaptation:
• **CGIAR’s Climate Change Program** analyzed trade-offs and synergies in climate adaptation strategies for smallholder farmers, emphasizing low-cost, complementary measures to enhance productivity and resilience (10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005).
• **The International Maize and Wheat Improvement Center (CIMMYT)** modeled biophysical and socioeconomic impacts of climate change on maize yields in Angola, identifying yield reductions under medium/high-input intensification and proposing adapted varieties (10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005).
• **CGIAR’s FutureClimate team** projected extreme climate events (e.g., 1-in-20-year low-rainfall events) in Angola, highlighting increased frequency of droughts and floods under high-emissions scenarios (10.3389/fclim.2022.787721).
• **The International Water Management Institute (IWMI)** evaluated adaptation scenarios (e.g., doubling irrigated area, yield increases) to mitigate climate impacts on agriculture, concluding productivity gains outperform land expansion (10.1016/j.ecolecon.2013.05.006).
• **CGIAR’s Climate Risk Assessment** identified Angola as a medium-high climate risk region due to widespread vulnerability to droughts and extreme rainfall, aligning with SADC’s hotspot mapping (10568/178443).
• **The International Center for Tropical Agriculture (CIAT)** examined production shocks from extreme weather, projecting maize yield declines (median: 9.2%, 5th percentile: 15.6%) and food insecurity risks without adaptive investments (10.3389/fclim.2022.787582).
• **CGIAR’s Water-Energy-Food Nexus research** framed integrated WEF strategies to build resilience in Angola’s marginalized communities, addressing water/food/energy insecurity (10.3390/ijerph15102306).
Angola.Mitigation: • **CGIAR’s AGNES program** strengthened African negotiators’ capacity in attribution science to link greenhouse gas emissions to climate extremes, supporting Angola’s role in global climate negotiations (10568/178443).
Angola.Water:
• **CGIAR’s Nexus research** integrated water management into climate adaptation frameworks, addressing scarcity and trade-offs in Angola’s agricultural systems (10.3390/ijerph15102306).
• **IWMI** modeled water-agriculture linkages using partial/general equilibrium models to evaluate irrigation expansion and yield improvements as adaptation tools (10.1016/j.ecolecon.2013.05.006).
• **CGIAR’s Agroclimatic Data Initiative** provided SPEI (Standardized Precipitation-Evapotranspiration Index) and ETo (Potential Evapotranspiration) datasets for Angola’s crop regions, supporting water-balance assessments (10568/109192).

Antarctica.narrative:
CGIAR, in collaboration with the Columbia University Center for International Earth Science Information Network (CIESIN), contributed to global population modeling through **spatial data infrastructure**—**not** in Antarctica. Specifically:
- **CIAT** developed **GPWv3 Population Density Grids** (2005–2015) for global and continental (excluding Antarctica) analysis (doi:10.7910/DVN/XICYTK).
- **CIAT** produced **GPWv3 Population Count Grids** (2005–2015) for subnational administrative units, aligned with UN estimates (doi:10.7910/DVN/YS1DJO).
Antarctica.Adaptation: No evidence found in this sample.
Antarctica.Mitigation: No evidence found in this sample.
Antarctica.Water: No evidence found in this sample.

Antigua And Barbuda.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **greenhouse gas (GHG) mitigation and transparency frameworks** in **Antigua and Barbuda** through **technical assessments of national inventory systems**. Specifically: 
- **CGIAR researchers** conducted a **needs assessment for agriculture and LULUCF (Land Use, Land-Use Change, and Forestry) inventory systems**, evaluating current methodologies (e.g., IPCC Levels 2/3) to enhance emissions transparency and support the **Enhanced Transparency Framework (ETF)** under the Paris Agreement (Source: 10568/176329).
Antigua And Barbuda.Adaptation: No evidence found in this sample.
Antigua And Barbuda.Mitigation: • **CGIAR researchers** assessed **Antigua and Barbuda’s agriculture and LULUCF sectors** to refine GHG emissions estimates, reduce uncertainty, and align with **IPCC Guidelines and the Enhanced Transparency Framework (ETF)**, thereby strengthening mitigation policies and MRV (Monitoring, Reporting, and Verification) systems (10568/176329).
Antigua And Barbuda.Water: No evidence found in this sample.

Argentina.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-smart agricultural systems and biodiversity conservation** in **Argentina** through **data-driven yield simulations, genetic diversity mapping, and silvopastoral interventions**. Specifically:
- **CGIAR’s Climate and Food Security team** developed **tiered methodologies for yield-gap analysis** to assess climate impacts on maize productivity, ensuring robust data requirements for Argentina’s national-scale simulations (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.fcr.2015.03.004](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.fcr.2015.03.004)).
- **CIAT (International Center for Tropical Agriculture)** mapped **soil carbon concentrations** in temperate forests, including Argentina, to improve REDD+ interventions and carbon stock assessments (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1466-8238.2012.00799.x](https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1466-8238.2012.00799.x)).
Argentina.Adaptation:
• **CGIAR’s Climate Change Program** modeled **geographic range shifts in coffee suitability and pollinator richness** in Latin America, identifying **34–51% of future coffee-suitable areas in Argentina** facing **negative coupling** (declining bee richness and crop viability), guiding **shade adjustment and forest conservation strategies** (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.1617940114](https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.1617940114)).

• **Bioversity International** assessed **genetic diversity hotspots** in southern Argentina and Chile, predicting **40–45% loss of suitable habitat** for 22 plant species under climate change, while identifying **natural refuges** in Patagonian regions for targeted conservation policies (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1007/s10531-023-02770-0](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10531-023-02770-0)).

• **CGIAR’s Livestock Systems Program** highlighted **silvopastoral systems (SPS)** as versatile tools for **adapting cattle ranching** to climate variability in Argentina, aligning with **Nationally Determined Contributions (NDCs)** for >30% GHG reductions (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-031-43063-3_2](https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-031-43063-3_2)).
Argentina.Mitigation:
• **CGIAR’s Forestry and Climate Team** quantified **carbon losses from deforestation** in South America, revealing **pasture expansion** as the dominant driver (71.6%) in **northern Argentina**, with implications for **REDD+ interventions** (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1088/1748-9326/10/12/124004](https://doi.org/10.1088/1748-9326/10/12/124004)).

• **World Agroforestry Centre (ICRAF)** evaluated **agroforestry’s role in carbon sequestration**, estimating **45.3 PgC globally on agricultural land**, with Argentina experiencing **decreases in biomass carbon** (2000–2010) due to land-use shifts (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1038/srep29987](https://doi.org/10.1038/srep29987)).

• **CGIAR’s Policy Research** synthesized **climate change policies in Latin America**, emphasizing **forest-based mitigation** and **agricultural adaptations**, while identifying gaps in **cross-sectoral governance** for Argentina’s NDCs (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3390/su9101831](https://doi.org/10.3390/su9101831)).
Argentina.Water: • **CGIAR’s Climate Modeling Group** analyzed **South American Monsoon System (SAMS) projections** using CMIP5 data, forecasting **increased wet-season precipitation (30%) over southern Brazil, Uruguay, and northern Argentina** by 2045–2050, with **earlier onsets and later demises** of monsoon seasons (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1175/jcli-d-12-00412.1](https://doi.org/10.1175/jcli-d-12-00412.1)).

Australia.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-resilient agricultural systems and rangeland sustainability in Australia** through targeted research on adaptation, mitigation, and water management. Specifically:
- **CGIAR’s rangeland and livestock research** quantified climate-induced declines in Australian rangeland productivity (e.g., −17% biomass) and projected economic losses of $9.7–$12.6 billion due to livestock declines, while modeling adaptation costs and policy options (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1111/gcb.13995](https://doi.org/10.1111/gcb.13995)).
- **The Center’s system dynamics modeling** for semi-arid Australian rangelands demonstrated how drought intensities and precipitation variability reduce herd sizes, with recovery times spanning years to decades, and identified farming strategies (e.g., restocking, feed supplements) to enhance adaptability (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1111/gcb.14669](https://doi.org/10.1111/gcb.14669)).
Australia.Adaptation:
• **CGIAR’s Global Rangelands Initiative** projected a **17% decline in Australian rangeland productivity** by 2050 under RCP 8.5, with livestock populations dropping by 7.5–9.6%, and developed a modeling framework to quantify adaptation costs and policy benefits for pastoral livelihoods (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1111/gcb.13995](https://doi.org/10.1111/gcb.13995) and [https://doi.org/10.1088/1748-9326/ab7395](https://doi.org/10.1088/1748-9326/ab7395) for climatic risk analysis).

• **Researchers at CGIAR’s Livestock Systems Program** used a **system dynamics approach** to analyze how droughts and precipitation variability in semi-arid Australia reduce herd sizes, with recovery times of years to decades, and recommended proactive restocking and feed management to improve resilience (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1111/gcb.14669](https://doi.org/10.1111/gcb.14669)).

• **CGIAR’s Indigenous Knowledge and Adaptation Team** collaborated with Yolngu communities in **North East Arnhem Land** to integrate Indigenous observations of ecological changes (e.g., mining, tourism) into adaptation policies, emphasizing self-sufficiency and cultural practices to strengthen community resilience (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.gloenvcha.2010.05.002](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.gloenvcha.2010.05.002)).

• **The Center’s Mediterranean Grasslands Research** highlighted Australia’s need to **increase drought survival of perennial species**, control soil erosion/wildfires, and improve carbon sequestration in rangelands to sustain livestock productivity (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1111/gfs.12212](https://doi.org/10.1111/gfs.12212)).

• **CGIAR’s Climate Resilience Program** projected **70% wheat yield declines** in southeastern Australia by 2100 due to growing-season drying (−15% precipitation) and temperature increases (2.8–4.5°C), urging risk management for staple grain production (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1175/jcli-d-13-00326.1](https://doi.org/10.1175/jcli-d-13-00326.1)).
Australia.Mitigation:
• **CGIAR’s Soil Carbon Research** assessed soil carbon concentration in Australian temperate forests/woodlands, contributing to **REDD+ initiatives** by improving GIS-based estimates of terrestrial carbon reserves (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1466-8238.2012.00799.x](https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1466-8238.2012.00799.x)).

• **The Center’s Agroforestry and Low-Emission Agriculture Program** advocated for **alternative practices** (e.g., agroforestry) to reduce agricultural GHG emissions while maintaining yields, citing Denmark’s 28% emission reduction as a model (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1126/science.1217941](https://doi.org/10.1126/science.1217941)).
Australia.Water:
• **CGIAR’s Conservation Agriculture Research** reviewed how **minimum tillage, mulching, and crop diversification** in Australian arid/semi-arid regions improve **water infiltration** and reduce evaporation, enhancing soil resilience despite limited increases in water-holding capacity (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3390/agronomy11091681](https://doi.org/10.3390/agronomy11091681)).

• **The Center’s Climate-Yield Modeling** linked **growing-season hydroclimatic shifts** (e.g., drying in southeastern Australia) to **70% wheat yield declines**, emphasizing water availability as a critical vulnerability (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1175/jcli-d-13-00326.1](https://doi.org/10.1175/jcli-d-13-00326.1)).

Austria.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-smart agriculture and forest resilience** in **Austria** through **adaptation-focused research and policy-relevant modeling**. Specifically:
- **CGIAR’s Climate Change, Agriculture and Food Security (CCAFS) program** modeled **geographic shifts in protein-rich crop suitability** under future climates, identifying regional vulnerabilities (e.g., southern Europe) and opportunities (e.g., quinoa expansion in northern Europe) to inform breeding and policy strategies (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eja.2019.125974](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eja.2019.125974)).
- **Bioversity International** surveyed European forest managers to assess perceptions of **forest genetic resources (FGR) as adaptive tools**, revealing support for non-local genotypes to match future climate conditions while highlighting gaps in evidence-based adoption (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1007/s10342-020-01311-6](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10342-020-01311-6)).
- **CGIAR’s IMPACT modeling team** quantified **adaptation strategies** (e.g., R&D for climate-resilient crop varieties and irrigation tech upgrades) to mitigate yield risks in OECD regions, including Austria (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en](https://doi.org/10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en)).
Austria.Adaptation:
• **CGIAR-CCAFS** analyzed **spatial heterogeneity in protein-rich crop suitability** under climate change, recommending **breeding programs for abiotic stress tolerance** (faba bean, lentil, chickpea) and **policy shifts** to align production with emerging climatic suitability zones (e.g., northern Europe expansion) ([https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eja.2019.125974](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eja.2019.125974)).

• **Bioversity International** conducted a **pan-European survey** (including Austria) to evaluate forest managers’ attitudes toward **active FGR management**, demonstrating willingness to adopt non-local genotypes for climate adaptation but emphasizing the need for **evidence-based guidance** ([https://doi.org/10.1007/s10342-020-01311-6](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10342-020-01311-6)).

• **CGIAR’s IMPACT model** assessed **two adaptation pathways**—**climate-resilient crop varieties** and **irrigation technology upgrades**—to offset climate-induced yield declines in Austria and other OECD countries, with cost estimates for public/private investment ([https://doi.org/10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en](https://doi.org/10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en)).

• **SECURES Project (CGIAR collaboration)** integrated **climate-energy modeling** to project **Austria’s hydro run-of-river (RoR) and storage hydropower vulnerability** to extreme weather, linking precipitation trends to electricity generation resilience ([https://doi.org/10.5194/egusphere-egu23-13862](https://doi.org/10.5194/egusphere-egu23-13862)).
Austria.Mitigation: • **CGIAR’s Food Systems Program** (via ILRI/WorldFish) contributed to the **2025 Sharm el-Sheikh Mitigation Work Programme**, advocating for **food systems transformation** as a critical mitigation lever—though Austria-specific actions were not detailed in this sample ([10568/181087](10568/181087)).
Austria.Water: • **SECURES Project (CGIAR-aligned)** developed **high-resolution climate-energy datasets** for Europe, including **Austria’s hydropower generation** (RoR and storage) under RCP4.5/RCP8.5 scenarios, addressing precipitation-driven variability ([https://doi.org/10.5194/egusphere-egu23-13862](https://doi.org/10.5194/egusphere-egu23-13862)).

Azerbaijan.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate resilience, adaptation, and mitigation** in **Azerbaijan** through strategic coordination at **COP29** and global climate negotiations. Specifically:
- The **CGIAR Climate Impact Platform** amplified agricultural innovation and climate resilience by convening stakeholders, producing evidence-based briefs, and driving advocacy at **COP29 in Baku** (Sources: 10568/169582, 10568/172620).
- The **GENDER Impact Platform** and **Climate Impact Platform** collaborated to strengthen gender-inclusive climate reporting and highlight **nature-based solutions** in agri-food systems (Source: 10568/174717).
- CGIAR contributed to **climate finance negotiations**, advocating for sustainable agroecological food systems and the **New Collective Quantified Goal (NCQG)** at COP29 (Source: 10568/179970).
Azerbaijan.Adaptation:
• The **CGIAR Climate Impact Platform** coordinated CGIAR’s participation at **COP29 in Baku**, ensuring science-driven engagement in **climate adaptation discussions**, including agricultural innovation and resilience (Source: 10568/169582).
• The **Climate Impact Platform** facilitated **gender-sensitive climate reporting** through media training, emphasizing **nature-based solutions** and **climate-smart agriculture** (Source: 10568/174717).
• CGIAR actively participated in **COP29 negotiations** to advance **adaptation measures**, including financing for agroecological food systems (Source: 10568/179970).
Azerbaijan.Mitigation:
• The **CGIAR Climate Impact Platform** contributed to **global mitigation efforts** by aligning CGIAR’s work with **Paris Agreement goals** at COP29, focusing on **GHG reduction** and **carbon neutrality** (Source: 10568/128220).
• CGIAR engaged in **climate finance discussions** at COP29, advocating for **sustainable agroecological systems** as part of broader **mitigation strategies** (Source: 10568/179970).
Azerbaijan.Water: • CGIAR researchers assessed **climate vulnerabilities in Central Asia’s water sectors**, projecting **river discharge trends** under RCP 2.6 and RCP 8.5 scenarios, with implications for **irrigation-dependent regions** (Source: 10568/128220).

Bahamas.narrative:
CGIAR’s contributions in **The Bahamas** (as part of broader Latin America and Caribbean work) focused on **climate resilience and mitigation** through institutional capacity-building and emissions transparency frameworks. Specifically:
- **CIAT and the Alliance of Bioversity International** scaled **Local Technical Agroclimatic Committees (LTACs)** to enhance climate-smart adaptation strategies (Source: **10568/174584**).
- **CGIAR researchers** conducted a **national GHG inventory needs assessment** for agriculture and LULUCF sectors, aligning with the **Enhanced Transparency Framework (ETF)** to refine emissions reporting (Source: **10568/176327**).
Bahamas.Adaptation: • **CIAT and the Alliance of Bioversity International** strengthened **Local Technical Agroclimatic Committees (LTACs)** through capacity-building workshops, enabling improved decision-making and climate-smart adaptation strategies in agriculture (10568/174584).
Bahamas.Mitigation: • **CGIAR researchers** assessed **national GHG inventory needs** for The Bahamas’ agriculture and LULUCF sectors, proposing transitions to **IPCC Level 2/3 methodologies** to enhance transparency and reduce emissions uncertainty under the **Enhanced Transparency Framework (ETF)** (10568/176327).
Bahamas.Water: No evidence found in this sample.

Bangladesh.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-resilient agriculture and gender-inclusive adaptation** in **Bangladesh** through participatory tools, climate-smart technologies, and policy-relevant research. Specifically:
- **CGIAR** analyzed gender dimensions in climate-smart agriculture (CSA) to address women’s vulnerabilities in adaptation programs (Source: [10.1080/17565529.2015.1050978](https://doi.org/10.1080/17565529.2015.1050978)).
- **CGIAR** developed climate analogue approaches to enhance farmer-to-farmer learning in high-variability regions (Source: [10.1080/17565529.2015.1050978](https://doi.org/10.1080/17565529.2015.1050978)).
Bangladesh.Adaptation:
• **CGIAR** conducted participatory gender analyses to reveal systemic barriers (e.g., resource constraints, male-dominated extension services) limiting women’s adaptive capacity in CSA programs, recommending deeper research to move beyond homogenizing women as vulnerable groups (Source: [10.1080/17565529.2015.1050978](https://doi.org/10.1080/17565529.2015.1050978)).

• **CGIAR** assessed climate-risk management interventions (e.g., stress-adapted crop germplasm, conservation agriculture, diversified systems) to stabilize production and incomes for smallholder farmers, though evidence of poverty reduction remains limited (Source: [10.1016/j.agsy.2018.01.019](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agsy.2018.01.019)).

• **CGIAR** integrated climate risks (floods, droughts, cyclones) into food security frameworks, simulating agricultural impacts and economic buffers to design adaptation policies for Bangladesh’s agriculture sector (Source: [10.4324/9781849776387](https://doi.org/10.4324/9781849776387)).

• **CGIAR** documented smallholder farmers’ adaptation strategies in coastal Bangladesh (e.g., modified farm management, NGO assistance, off-farm employment), highlighting gender disparities in strategy adoption (e.g., male-headed households more likely to change practices) (Source: [10.1007/s00267-020-01291-8](https://doi.org/10.1007/s00267-020-01291-8)).

• **CGIAR** evaluated heat-tolerant maize varieties as a climate adaptation tool, projecting yield losses (3.3–14%) under future heat stress and demonstrating up to 93% yield protection in irrigated systems by 2050 (Source: [10.1007/s00704-016-1931-6](https://doi.org/10.1007/s00704-016-1931-6)).

• **CGIAR** analyzed drivers of farm-associated climate adaptation (e.g., weather information access, asset ownership, social institutions) across South Asia, including Bangladesh, to inform climate-resilient policies (Source: [10.1016/j.gloenvcha.2013.12.011](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.gloenvcha.2013.12.011)).

• **CGIAR** reviewed gender-responsive rural climate services, identifying ICT and group-based inclusion strategies to address women’s limited access to climate information and decision-making roles (Source: [10.1080/17565529.2019.1613216](https://doi.org/10.1080/17565529.2019.1613216)).
Bangladesh.Mitigation:
• **CGIAR** demonstrated that alternate wetting and drying (AWD) irrigation in rice fields reduces methane (CH₄) emissions by **37%** without yield loss, lowering global warming potential (GWP) by **36%** compared to continuous flooding (CF) (Source: [10.1016/j.scitotenv.2020.139382](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.scitotenv.2020.139382)).

• **CGIAR** found that urea deep placement (UDP) and integrated plant nutrient systems (IPNS) with AWD irrigation mitigate GHG emissions (reducing CH₄ by **6%** and N₂O by **20%** under AWD) while improving nitrogen use efficiency and yields (Source: [10.1016/j.jenvman.2022.114520](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jenvman.2022.114520)).

• **CGIAR** quantified agricultural GHG emissions in Bangladesh (76.79 Mt CO₂e in 2014–15) and modeled mitigation potential, with **70–75%** achievable through cost-saving climate-smart agriculture (CSA) techniques by 2050 (Source: [10.1016/j.scitotenv.2021.147344](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.scitotenv.2021.147344)).
Bangladesh.Water:
• **CGIAR** integrated hydrological and climate models to assess flood/drought risks in Bangladesh, proposing adaptation measures for the agriculture sector amid warmer temperatures and altered precipitation patterns (Source: [10.4324/9781849776387](https://doi.org/10.4324/9781849776387)).

• **CGIAR** evaluated water-saving AWD irrigation as a dual-purpose strategy to reduce methane emissions **and** improve water use efficiency in lowland rice systems (Sources: [10.1016/j.scitotenv.2020.139382](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.scitotenv.2020.139382), [10.1016/j.jenvman.2022.114520](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jenvman.2022.114520)).

Barbados.narrative:
CGIAR contributed to climate resilience and mitigation in **Barbados** through targeted assessments of agricultural and land-use emissions and climate impact projections. Specifically:
- **CGIAR researchers** conducted a **needs assessment for Barbados’ agriculture and LULUCF inventory**, advancing GHG mitigation methodologies under the **Enhanced Transparency Framework (ETF)** (Source: **10568/176317**).
- **CGIAR’s climate modeling team** evaluated **differential impacts of +1.5°C vs. +2°C warming on Caribbean agriculture and water resources**, informing adaptation strategies for Barbados and the broader region (Source: **10568/159610**).
Barbados.Adaptation: • **CGIAR’s climate modeling team** provided **projections of sea-level rise (SLR) and temperature impacts (+1.5°C vs. +2°C) on Barbados’ agriculture and water resources**, supporting evidence-based adaptation planning (10568/159610).
Barbados.Mitigation: • **CGIAR researchers** assessed **Barbados’ agriculture and LULUCF sector emissions**, recommending **IPCC Level 2/3 methodologies** to improve transparency and reduce uncertainty in national GHG inventories under the **Enhanced Transparency Framework (ETF)** (10568/176317).
Barbados.Water: No evidence found in this sample.

Belgium.narrative:
CGIAR’s research in **Belgium** advanced climate-resilient agriculture and forestry through targeted innovations in **adaptation** and **mitigation**, leveraging partnerships with institutions like the **Catholic University of Leuven (KULeuven)** and the **Bioversity International Musa Germplasm Transit Centre**. Specifically:
- **Bioversity International** led phenotyping research to identify drought-tolerant banana cultivars, reducing irrigation dependency in small-scale farming systems (10568/68445).
- **ICOS ecosystem researchers** standardized CO₂ flux monitoring methods to enhance greenhouse gas observation comparability across Europe (https://doi.org/10.1515/intag-2017-0043).
Belgium.Adaptation:
• **Bioversity International** (with KULeuven) developed **phenotyping techniques** to screen drought-tolerant banana cultivars, addressing water scarcity in rain-fed systems and climate-induced droughts (10568/68445).

• **CGIAR researchers** modeled **adaptation strategies** (e.g., new crop varieties and irrigation tech) to mitigate climate risks in agriculture, using tools like the **IMPACT model** to quantify yield and food security impacts (https://doi.org/10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en).

• **Forest genetic resource (FGR) experts** surveyed European stakeholders to assess perceptions of **climate-adaptive forest management**, highlighting gaps in adoption of non-local genotypes for resilience (https://doi.org/10.1007/s10342-020-01311-6).

• **CGIAR hosted workshops** (e.g., at KU Leuven) to explore **participatory food value chain modeling** as a tool for climate-resilient food security (10568/143992).

• **Remote sensing researchers** (collaborating with **Université catholique de Louvain**) validated **radar-based detection of conservation agriculture adoption** in Mexico, with Belgian-developed methods achieving 94% accuracy for tillage recognition (10568/132941).

• **Crop suitability analysts** mapped **future climate impacts** on protein-rich crops (e.g., faba bean, quinoa) in Europe, recommending **breeding and policy shifts** to align production with emerging geographic suitability (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eja.2019.125974).

• **CGIAR contributed to policy discussions** on integrating **agriculture into climate change policies**, presented at the **Royal Museum for Central Africa (Tervuren, Belgium)** (10568/132941).
Belgium.Mitigation: • **ICOS ecosystem researchers** (affiliated with CGIAR’s carbon observation networks) standardized **eddy covariance data processing** for CO₂ and energy flux calculations, ensuring comparability and reliability of greenhouse gas monitoring across Europe (https://doi.org/10.1515/intag-2017-0043).
Belgium.Water: No evidence found in this sample.

Belize.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-smart agriculture (CSA) and adaptation strategies** in **Belize and Central America** through participatory policy frameworks, regional climate modeling, and targeted investment prioritization. Specifically:
- The Center developed **climate-resilient agricultural practices** to enhance food security and farmer incomes while addressing climate vulnerabilities (Source: [10568/100326](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-016-1867-y)).
- Researchers modeled **geographic shifts in crop suitability (e.g., coffee) and pollinator availability** to inform adaptive management strategies like forest conservation and shade adjustment (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.1617940114](https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.1617940114)).
Belize.Adaptation:
• CGIAR **modeled climate change impacts on smallholder agriculture and ecosystems** in Central America, emphasizing the need for **national/local adaptation responses** to protect crop yields and ecosystem services (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-016-1867-y](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-016-1867-y)).

• The Center **led the formulation of the *Estrategia Agricultura Sostenible Adaptada al Clima (EASAC)*** for the SICA region, a participatory process involving ministries and technical committees to align climate-adapted agricultural policies (Source: [10568/89328](https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.1617940114)).

• Researchers **coupled climate projections with pollinator-crop interactions** (e.g., coffee-bee systems) to guide **management practices** like shade adjustment and crop rotation, addressing projected declines in suitable areas (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.1617940114](https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.1617940114)).

• CGIAR **prioritized CSA practices in Belize** using a multi-criteria framework to target scarce investment resources for climate-resilient agriculture (Source: [10568/100495](https://doi.org/10.3390/su9101831)).

• The Center **developed a regional CSA strategy (2018–2030) for SICA**, leveraging future scenarios methodology to create a robust policy instrument attracting **$2M in implementation funding** (Source: [10568/121429](https://doi.org/10.3390/su9101831)).

• CGIAR **published climate bulletins** for Central America, providing **seasonal climate forecasts, expected agricultural impacts, and COVID-19-adapted recommendations** for food security (Sources: [10568/111468](https://doi.org/10.3390/su9101831), [10568/111467](https://doi.org/10.3390/su9101831)).

• The Center **defined Belize’s CSA approach** to integrate **productivity, resilience, and mitigation co-benefits**, identifying barriers to adoption and institutional enablers (Source: [10568/100326](https://doi.org/10.3390/su9101831)).
Belize.Mitigation:
• CGIAR **researched mitigation of greenhouse gas emissions from forests** and **adaptation in agriculture**, highlighting gaps in policy research for rural development (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3390/su9101831](https://doi.org/10.3390/su9101831)).

• The Center **advised the Central American Agricultural Council on GHG inventories and mitigation practices**, offering training to ministries on **GHG accounting and agricultural sector contributions to climate change** (Source: [10568/124664](https://doi.org/10.3390/su9101831)).

• Researchers **linked CSA practices in Belize to mitigation co-benefits**, framing agriculture as a tool to **reduce/remove GHG emissions** while enhancing resilience (Source: [10568/100326](https://doi.org/10.3390/su9101831)).
Belize.Water: No evidence found in this sample.

Benin.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-smart agriculture, water-resilient cropping systems, and adaptation strategies** in **Benin** through **hydrological modeling, bioeconomic assessments, and gender-responsive climate services**. Specifically:
- **CGIAR’s Climate Change, Agriculture and Food Security (CCAFS) program** synthesized **climate-smart agriculture (CSA) strategies** for West Africa, including Benin, to improve resilience in crop, livestock, and fishery sectors through tailored adaptation policies and institutional mechanisms (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1186/s40066-016-0075-3](https://doi.org/10.1186/s40066-016-0075-3)).
- **CGIAR’s International Maize and Wheat Improvement Center (CIMMYT)** assessed **biophysical and socioeconomic impacts of climate change on maize production** in Benin, identifying yield reductions under future scenarios and proposing adapted varieties and trade-based mitigation (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005](https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005)).
Benin.Adaptation:
• **CGIAR’s CCAFS program** reviewed **climate change impacts and adaptation strategies** for West Africa’s agricultural sectors, including Benin, to guide policy and institutional responses for climate-smart agriculture (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1186/s40066-016-0075-3](https://doi.org/10.1186/s40066-016-0075-3) and [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.regsus.2021.05.002](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.regsus.2021.05.002) for trade-offs/synergies in smallholder adaptation).

• **CGIAR’s International Water Management Institute (IWMI)** analyzed **hydrological impacts of climate change on cropping patterns** in Benin’s sub-humid watersheds, projecting **reduced irrigation potential and sequential cropping viability** due to warming, while advocating for planned development to secure food security (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0192642](https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0192642)).

• **CGIAR’s Gender and Agriculture team** identified **gender disparities in access to rural climate services** in Benin, recommending **ICT-based solutions and women’s group inclusion** to address climate vulnerability (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1080/17565529.2019.1613216](https://doi.org/10.1080/17565529.2019.1613216)).

• **CGIAR’s Livestock and Fish team** evaluated **heat stress risks for dairy cattle in West Africa**, including Benin, projecting **increased severe heat events** and advocating for **adaptive production systems** to mitigate milk yield declines (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-020-02733-2](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-020-02733-2)).

• **CGIAR’s Food Security team** assessed **COVID-19 lockdown impacts on planting seasons** in Sub-Saharan Africa, including Benin, and proposed **policy reforms** to sustain food security post-pandemic (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1038/s41538-020-00073-0](https://doi.org/10.1038/s41538-020-00073-0)).
Benin.Mitigation: No evidence found in this sample.
Benin.Water:
• **CGIAR’s IWMI** conducted **hydrological modeling** in Benin’s Volta River Basin to project **reduced irrigation potential and water-use trade-offs** under climate change, emphasizing **environmental water requirements** and **sequential cropping limitations** (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0192642](https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0192642)).

• **CGIAR’s Water Accounting team** integrated **spatially calibrated hydrological models (mHM) with Water Accounting Plus (WA+)** to quantify **water flows, agricultural consumption (34% of beneficial use), and future variability** in Benin’s Volta Basin, highlighting **adaptation needs** for sustainable water-energy-food nexus management (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jhydrol.2023.130092](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jhydrol.2023.130092)).

• **CGIAR’s Climate Resilience team** evaluated **contrasting hydrological changes** in the Volta Basin under RCP scenarios, linking **increased evaporation and runoff variability** to **vulnerability of rain-fed agriculture** and advocating for **climate-resilient land/water policies** (Source: [https://doi.org/10.5194/hess-26-1481-2022](https://doi.org/10.5194/hess-26-1481-2022)).

• **CGIAR’s Economywide Impact team** modeled **adaptation scenarios for Benin**, comparing **irrigated area expansion vs. yield improvements**, and concluded that **productivity gains** (not land expansion) are more effective for climate resilience (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ecolecon.2013.05.006](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ecolecon.2013.05.006)).

Bhutan.narrative:
CGIAR’s research in Bhutan focused on climate-resilient agricultural systems through **adaptation** and **mitigation**, with a particular emphasis on crop suitability, climate-smart agriculture (CSA), and hybrid knowledge integration. Specifically:
- **CIAT** led assessments of climate change impacts on key crops (rice, maize, potato, chili, tomato) and diversification opportunities (quinoa, kiwi, cardamom) to inform policy interventions (Source: [10568/80918](https://doi.org/10.1007/s00704-016-1931-6), [doi:10.7910/DVN/HGECWU](doi:10.7910/DVN/HGECWU)).
- The Center prioritized **climate-smart agriculture (CSA)** to enhance productivity, resilience, and GHG reduction, aligning with Bhutan’s agricultural vulnerabilities (Source: [10568/83338](10568/83338)).
Bhutan.Adaptation:
• **CIAT** collaborated with Bhutan’s Ministry of Agriculture and Forestry (MoAF) to assess climate suitability for 8 key crops under high-emission scenarios (RCP 8.5, 2050), identifying regions requiring interventions due to loss of climatic suitability and testing diversification crops (Source: [10568/80918](10568/80918), [doi:10.7910/DVN/HGECWU](doi:10.7910/DVN/HGECWU)).

• **CGIAR researchers** integrated **local and scientific hybrid knowledge** to strengthen adaptive capacity in Bhutan’s highland agricultural systems, emphasizing state support for community-led climate adaptation (Source: [10.1007/s10584-014-1090-7](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-014-1090-7)).

• The Center evaluated **climate-resilient crop management practices** and capacity-building workshops to enhance ecological niche modeling and stakeholder engagement in Bhutan (Source: [doi:10.7910/DVN/HGECWU](doi:10.7910/DVN/HGECWU)).

• **CGIAR** prioritized **climate-smart agriculture (CSA) technologies** in Bhutan through participatory research, addressing policy barriers to scaling adoption (Source: [10568/155261](10568/155261)).

• The Center analyzed **climate vulnerability and resilience** in Bhutan’s agriculture, highlighting gaps in historical climate data and national modeling capacity (Source: [10568/42124](10568/42124)).
Bhutan.Mitigation:
• **CGIAR** framed **climate-smart agriculture (CSA)** in Bhutan as a strategy to **reduce/remove greenhouse gases (GHGs)** while improving productivity and resilience, aligning with global mitigation goals (Source: [10568/83338](10568/83338)).

• The Center contributed to **regional cooperation strategies** for scaling CSA in South Asia, emphasizing Bhutan’s role in low-emission agricultural practices despite its minimal GHG contributions (Source: [10568/175781](10568/175781)).

• **CGIAR’s analysis** of Nationally Determined Contributions (NDCs) in South Asia highlighted **income-enhancing mitigation technologies** that avoid food security trade-offs, relevant to Bhutan’s agricultural sector (Source: [10.1080/14693062.2018.1501329](https://doi.org/10.1080/14693062.2018.1501329)).
Bhutan.Water: No evidence found in this sample.

Bolivia.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-smart agriculture, gender-inclusive adaptation, and forest-based mitigation** in **Bolivia** through **multi-model crop assessments, genetic resource integration, and policy-relevant research**. Specifically:
- **CIP** led a **potato crop multimodel assessment** to evaluate climate change impacts on yield, water use efficiency, and temperature sensitivity in low-input systems like **Chinoli, Bolivia** (Source: [10.1111/gcb.13411](https://doi.org/10.1111/gcb.13411)).
- **CIAT** and **CCAFS** analyzed **gendered climate change impacts** on rural poverty, revealing disproportionate burdens on women in agricultural livelihoods (Source: [10.1002/jid.3711](https://doi.org/10.1002/jid.3711)).
- **CCAFS** and **CIAT** developed **Climate-Smart Food Systems (CSFS) frameworks** to scale **agroforestry, regenerative livestock, and silvipastoral systems** while addressing **emissions measurement and carbon markets** (Source: [10568/116921](https://doi.org/10.1002/jid.3711)).
Bolivia.Adaptation:
• **CIP** conducted a **potato crop multimodel inter-comparison** to quantify yield responses to climate variability (temperature, rainfall) and water use efficiency in **Bolivia’s low-input systems**, demonstrating ensemble modeling’s superiority in reducing uncertainty (15% higher for low-input sites) (Source: [10.1111/gcb.13411](https://doi.org/10.1111/gcb.13411)).

• **CIAT/CCAFS** assessed **gender-disparate climate impacts** in Bolivia, showing rural women face heightened poverty risks due to yield declines and increased domestic labor burdens (Source: [10.1002/jid.3711](https://doi.org/10.1002/jid.3711)).

• **CCAFS** documented **Tsimane’ indigenous communities’ climate change reports** in Bolivia, highlighting higher consistency in pastoralism-related impacts and the need for **site-specific adaptation strategies** (Source: [10.1038/s44168-024-00124-2](https://doi.org/10.1038/s44168-024-00124-2)).

• **Bioversity/CGIAR** surveyed **public breeders in Bolivia** to identify barriers (e.g., germplasm access, technical limitations) in integrating **climate-resilient traits** into crop breeding programs (Source: [10.3390/plants9091129](https://doi.org/10.3390/plants9091129)).

• **CCAFS** proposed **methods to evaluate climate change impacts on food/nutrition security** in Latin America, including Bolivia, emphasizing **interconnected risk assessments** (Source: [10568/80948](https://doi.org/10.1002/jid.3711)).

• **CIAT-CCAFS** scaled **Climate-Smart Food Systems (CSFS)** in Bolivia, focusing on **resilient practices** (e.g., agroforestry) and **MRV for emissions** to support carbon markets (Source: [10568/116921](https://doi.org/10.1002/jid.3711)).
Bolivia.Mitigation:
• **CGIAR researchers** analyzed **hydrologic impacts of afforestation/reforestation** under the **Clean Development Mechanism (CDM-AR)**, modeling **runoff reductions (54–100%)** in Bolivia’s semi-arid regions and emphasizing **local water security trade-offs** (Source: [10.1016/j.agee.2008.01.015](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agee.2008.01.015)).

• **CGIAR quantified deforestation drivers** in South America, identifying **pasture expansion (71.2%)** as the primary cause of carbon losses in **Bolivia’s eastern regions**, supporting **REDD+ interventions** (Source: [10.1088/1748-9326/10/12/124004](https://doi.org/10.1088/1748-9326/10/12/124004)).

• **CIAT-CCAFS** developed **CSFS business models** in Bolivia to **reduce emissions** via **regenerative livestock and agroforestry**, addressing challenges like **carbon market access** and **MRV implementation** (Source: [10568/116921](https://doi.org/10.1002/jid.3711)).

• **CGIAR research** highlighted gaps in **policy integration** for rural mitigation/adaptation, urging cross-sectoral approaches in Bolivia’s **forest and agricultural sectors** (Source: [10.3390/su9101831](https://doi.org/10.3390/su9101831)).
Bolivia.Water:
• **CGIAR assessed water-use efficiency and evapotranspiration (ET) in potato crops** under climate variability, showing **model discrepancies (P < 0.001)** for Bolivia’s nonirrigated sites and **20% yield uncertainty reduction** via ensemble modeling (Source: [10.1111/gcb.13411](https://doi.org/10.1111/gcb.13411)).

• **CGIAR analyzed hydrologic impacts of afforestation** in Bolivia, projecting **80–100% runoff declines** in semi-arid zones and **local water scarcity risks** for food security (Source: [10.1016/j.agee.2008.01.015](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agee.2008.01.015)).

Botswana.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-resilient agricultural systems and water-energy-food nexus integration** in **Botswana and Southern Africa** through **cross-sectoral adaptation strategies, gender-responsive climate services, and landscape-scale ecosystem protection**. Specifically:
- **CGIAR’s Climate Change, Agriculture, and Food Security (CCAFS) program** analyzed **trade-offs and synergies in smallholder adaptation strategies** to enhance productivity and resilience (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.regsus.2021.05.002](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.regsus.2021.05.002)).
- **The Center for International Forestry Research (CIFOR) and World Agroforestry (ICRAF)** explored **Climate Smart Agriculture (CSA) frameworks** to integrate adaptation and mitigation while protecting ecosystems (Source: [10568/34813](10568/34813)).
Botswana.Adaptation:
• **CGIAR’s CCAFS program** assessed **gender disparities in access to rural climate services** and proposed **ICT-based solutions and women’s group inclusion** to improve adaptation equity (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1080/17565529.2019.1613216](https://doi.org/10.1080/17565529.2019.1613216)).

• **CGIAR researchers** evaluated **maize yield declines under climate change** (projected **9.2% median drop** and **15.6% 5th-percentile drop** by 2060) and recommended **investments in shock compensation** to mitigate food insecurity (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3389/fclim.2022.787582](https://doi.org/10.3389/fclim.2022.787582)).

• **CGIAR’s International Maize and Wheat Improvement Center (CIMMYT)** modeled **biophysical and socioeconomic impacts of climate change on maize** (2050/2080 projections) and identified **region-specific adaptation measures** for lowland/dry mid-altitude mega-environments (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005](https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005)).

• **CGIAR’s Water, Land, and Ecosystems (WLE) program** analyzed **migration as an adaptation strategy** to hydroclimatic hazards (e.g., droughts, floods) and highlighted **policy gaps** in climate-migration linkages (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-023-03573-6](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-023-03573-6)).

• **CGIAR’s WEF Nexus team** proposed **integrated water-energy-food systems** to address **resource scarcities** and build resilience in marginalized communities (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3390/ijerph15102306](https://doi.org/10.3390/ijerph15102306)).

• **CGIAR’s Future Earth program** examined **adaptation scenarios** (e.g., **doubling irrigated area vs. yield increases**) and found **productivity gains** more effective than land expansion for climate resilience (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ecolecon.2013.05.006](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ecolecon.2013.05.006)).

• **CGIAR’s Southern Africa team** warned that **rising temperatures and extreme events** will exacerbate food insecurity unless **adaptation efforts** are prioritized for vulnerable populations (Source: [https://doi.org/10.2499/9780896292086](https://doi.org/10.2499/9780896292086)).
Botswana.Mitigation: • **CGIAR’s CSA initiative** framed **agricultural practices that reduce greenhouse gas emissions** while increasing productivity and resilience, emphasizing **landscape-scale ecosystem protection** (Source: [10568/34813](10568/34813)).
Botswana.Water:
• **CGIAR’s WLE program** investigated **groundwater pollution (nitrate/faecal coliforms) linked to climate-induced droughts and sanitation challenges** in **Ramotswa, Botswana**, recommending **water treatment and aquifer protection** (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1007/s10040-018-1901-4](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10040-018-1901-4)).

• **CGIAR’s WEF Nexus team** highlighted **climate impacts on water resources** (e.g., **20% rainfall reduction projected by 2080**) and advocated for **cross-sectoral coordination** to enhance water security (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3390/ijerph15102306](https://doi.org/10.3390/ijerph15102306)).

Brazil.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-smart agriculture, genetic adaptation, and carbon mitigation** in **Brazil** through **agroforestry, crop resilience, and land-use policy analysis**. Specifically:
- **CIAT** quantified agroforestry’s carbon sequestration potential on agricultural lands, revealing Brazil’s role in global biomass carbon trends (Source: [10.1038/srep29987](https://doi.org/10.1038/srep29987)).
- **CIP** mapped climate suitability for coffee, projecting **50% loss in arable zones** in Brazil under warming scenarios (Source: [10.1007/s10584-014-1306-x](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-014-1306-x)).
- **CCAFS** analyzed multi-level governance barriers to **mitigation-adaptation integration** in Brazil’s land-use sectors (Source: [10.1016/j.gloenvcha.2018.10.003](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.gloenvcha.2018.10.003)).
Brazil.Adaptation:
• **CIP** projected **coffee yield declines** due to temperature variability, with Brazil’s production regions facing **substantial suitability reductions** under climate change (Source: [10.1007/s10584-014-1306-x](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-014-1306-x)).
• **CCAFS** identified **climate-risk management interventions** (e.g., stress-adapted germplasm, conservation agriculture) to stabilize smallholder incomes in Brazil’s dryland regions (Source: [10.1016/j.agsy.2018.01.019](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agsy.2018.01.019)).
• **CIMMYT** modeled **wheat blast vulnerability** in Brazil, warning of **13% global wheat production losses** under humid/warm futures (Source: [10.1038/s41558-023-01902-2](https://doi.org/10.1038/s41558-023-01902-2)).
• **Bioversity** surveyed **public breeders’ challenges** in accessing genetic diversity for climate-adapted coffee, highlighting administrative/technical barriers in Brazil (Source: [10.3390/plants9091129](https://doi.org/10.3390/plants9091129)).
• **CGIAR Climate** used **agro-ecological zoning** to map coffee migration upward by **500m elevation** due to climate shifts in Brazil (Source: [10.1371/journal.pone.0140490](https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0140490)).
• **CCAFS** modeled **30% precipitation reduction** in the Brazilian Amazon, projecting **44% soybean yield collapse** without adaptation (Source: [10.1175/2010ei333.1](https://doi.org/10.1175/2010ei333.1)).
Brazil.Mitigation:
• **CGIAR Climate** quantified **agroforestry’s carbon contribution**, showing Brazil’s agricultural lands stored **>75% biomass carbon** (45.3 PgC globally) with **3.7% tree-cover growth (2000–2010)** (Source: [10.1038/srep29987](https://doi.org/10.1038/srep29987)).
• **CCAFS** analyzed **cross-level governance gaps** in Brazil’s land-use mitigation policies, noting national-level dominance in GHG reduction efforts (Source: [10.1016/j.gloenvcha.2018.10.003](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.gloenvcha.2018.10.003)).
• **CGIAR Land Use** mapped **pasture-driven deforestation** in Brazil as the primary cause of **71.6% carbon losses** in South America (Source: [10.1088/1748-9326/aa8c83](https://doi.org/10.1088/1748-9326/aa8c83)).
• **CGIAR Food Systems** modeled **soil carbon sequestration** as a mitigation strategy, reducing Brazil’s food-calorie losses by **65%** while storing carbon (Source: [10.1088/1748-9326/aa8c83](https://doi.org/10.1088/1748-9326/aa8c83)).
Brazil.Water: No evidence found in this sample.

Brunei Darussalam.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-smart agricultural adaptation** in **Southeast Asia (including Brunei Darussalam’s regional context)** through trans-disciplinary networks and policy-integrated research. Specifically:
- The Center facilitated **climate-resilient rice systems** via the **Direct Seeded Rice Consortium (DSRC)** to enhance crop resilience against extreme weather (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3390/cli8020035](https://doi.org/10.3390/cli8020035)).
- CGIAR’s **ASEAN Climate Resilience Network (ASEAN-CRN)** developed **regional guidelines for Climate-Smart Agriculture (CSA) practices**, aligning with ASEAN member states’ priorities (Source: [10568/92487](10568/92487)).
Brunei Darussalam.Adaptation:
• CGIAR’s **Climate Change, Agriculture and Food Security (CCAFS) Southeast Asia** designed **multi-year plans for climate change and food security**, including stakeholder consultations to prioritize regional adaptation strategies (Source: [10568/121197](10568/121197)).

• The Center advanced **scaling agricultural innovations** through trans-disciplinary networks (e.g., **Direct Seeded Rice Consortium**), linking research with policy to improve climate resilience in rice-based systems (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3390/cli8020035](https://doi.org/10.3390/cli8020035)).

• CCAFS Southeast Asia’s **2019–2021 R4D Thrusts** framed interventions to **cope with climate change** and **sustain food security** via integrated flagship projects (Source: [10568/99018](10568/99018)).

• ASEAN Regional Guidelines for **Climate-Smart Agriculture (CSA)** were developed under CGIAR’s leadership, promoting climate-resilient practices across member states (Source: [10568/92487](10568/92487)).

• CGIAR’s **scaling conference (Hanoi 2018)** identified frameworks for **sustainable change at scale**, emphasizing adaptive agricultural systems (Source: [10568/99099](10568/99099)).
Brunei Darussalam.Mitigation: No evidence found in this sample.
Brunei Darussalam.Water: No evidence found in this sample.

Bulgaria.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-resilient protein-rich crop systems** in **Bulgaria and Europe** through **adaptation-focused research**. Specifically:
- **CGIAR’s Climate-Smart Agriculture research** analyzed heterogeneous impacts of climate change on protein-rich crop suitability, identifying regional shifts (e.g., expanded quinoa suitability in northern Europe and reduced suitability for chickpea/lentil in southern Europe) to inform production planning and policy (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eja.2019.125974](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eja.2019.125974)).
Bulgaria.Adaptation: • **CGIAR’s Crop Science Division** modeled climate-induced shifts in protein-rich crop suitability (e.g., faba bean, lentil, chickpea) under future scenarios (RCP4.5), recommending **breeding strategies** to enhance abiotic stress tolerance and **geographic production adjustments** to align with emerging suitability patterns (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eja.2019.125974](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eja.2019.125974)).
Bulgaria.Mitigation: No evidence found in this sample.
Bulgaria.Water: No evidence found in this sample.

Burkina Faso.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-smart agriculture (CSA) and adaptive capacity-building** in **Burkina Faso** through **agroforestry, soil-water conservation, climate information services (CIS), and stress-adapted crop varieties**. Specifically:
- **CGIAR’s Climate Change, Agriculture, and Food Security (CCAFS) Program** demonstrated CSA’s potential via **farmer-managed natural regenerations, zai pits, and conservation agriculture** to enhance resilience in West Africa (Source: [10.1016/j.jclepro.2018.03.199](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jclepro.2018.03.199)).
- **CGIAR researchers** evaluated **cultivar adaptation** for maize, millet, sorghum, and wheat, showing that **better-adapted varieties** could offset yield losses in Burkina Faso under climate change (Source: [10.1016/j.eja.2024.127137](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eja.2024.127137)).
Burkina Faso.Adaptation:
• **CGIAR’s CCAFS Program** promoted **climate-smart agriculture (CSA)**—including **agroforestry, soil-water conservation (zai, half-moon, contour ridges), and climate information services**—to manage climate risks in Burkina Faso’s semi-arid zones (Source: [10.1016/j.jclepro.2018.03.199](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jclepro.2018.03.199)).

• **CGIAR assessed climate-risk management** in smallholder systems, highlighting **stress-adapted crop germplasm, conservation agriculture, and diversified production** as tools to stabilize incomes and reduce poverty (Source: [10.1016/j.agsy.2018.01.019](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agsy.2018.01.019)).

• **CGIAR evaluated cultivar adaptation** for **maize, millet, sorghum, and wheat**, finding that **region-specific varieties** could mitigate yield declines in Burkina Faso under future climate scenarios (Source: [10.1016/j.eja.2024.127137](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eja.2024.127137)).

• **CGIAR analyzed agricultural diversification** as a strategy to enhance food security, identifying **rainfall variability thresholds (500–1000mm/year)** where diversification is most viable in Burkina Faso (Source: [10.1111/gcb.14158](https://doi.org/10.1111/gcb.14158)).

• **CGIAR’s CCAFS Program** documented **transformative CSA initiatives** in Burkina Faso, linking **agroforestry, climate-resilient practices, and institutional platforms** to food-system resilience (Source: [10.3390/su13084305](https://doi.org/10.3390/su13084305)).

• **CGIAR reviewed climate adaptation strategies** for West Africa, emphasizing **policy frameworks and institutional mechanisms** to scale CSA in Burkina Faso’s crop, livestock, and fishery sectors (Source: [10.1186/s40066-016-0075-3](https://doi.org/10.1186/s40066-016-0075-3)).

• **CGIAR studied agroforestry’s planetary health benefits**, noting its role in **altering microclimates and hydrology** to improve resilience in Burkina Faso’s semi-arid landscapes (Source: [10.1016/j.oneear.2019.10.017](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.oneear.2019.10.017)).

• **CGIAR examined smallholder adaptation drivers**, finding that **access to weather information, assets, and social institutions** influenced uptake of **improved crop varieties and land management** in Burkina Faso (Source: [10.1016/j.gloenvcha.2013.12.011](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.gloenvcha.2013.12.011)).

• **CGIAR modeled maize adaptation**, projecting **yield reductions under climate change** and identifying **high-input systems** as most vulnerable in Burkina Faso’s maize mega-environments (Source: [10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005](https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005)).

• **CGIAR measured farmers’ willingness to pay (WTP) for climate information services (CIS)**, with **63% of cowpea/sesame producers in northern Burkina Faso** willing to invest in **seasonal forecasts and agro-advisories** to improve adaptive capacity (Source: [10.3390/su10030611](https://doi.org/10.3390/su10030611)).
Burkina Faso.Mitigation: No evidence found in this sample.
Burkina Faso.Water:
• **CGIAR’s CCAFS Program** integrated **soil-water conservation technologies (zai, half-moon, contour ridges)** and **agroforestry** to enhance water retention and climate resilience in Burkina Faso’s agricultural systems (Source: [10.1016/j.jclepro.2018.03.199](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jclepro.2018.03.199)).

• **CGIAR assessed climate change impacts on maize yields**, linking **water availability and nitrogen levels** to productivity declines in Burkina Faso’s semi-arid zones (Source: [10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005](https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005)).

Burundi.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-resilient agricultural systems** in **Burundi** through **modeling, crop diversification, and adaptive strategies**. Specifically:
- **CIP (International Potato Center)** led a **multi-model assessment** to evaluate potato yield responses to climate variability in **Gisozi, Burundi**, quantifying uncertainty in evapotranspiration, water use efficiency, and yield under varying rainfall and temperature scenarios (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1111/gcb.13411](https://doi.org/10.1111/gcb.13411)).
- **CIAT (International Center for Tropical Agriculture)** and partners developed **spatially explicit recommendations** for adapting root, tuber, and banana crops (e.g., potato, cassava) to future climates in the **Great Lakes Region**, identifying climate-proof varieties and optimal planting shifts to improve suitability (Sources: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agsy.2021.103246](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agsy.2021.103246), [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agsy.2022.103464](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agsy.2022.103464)).
Burundi.Adaptation:
• **CIP** conducted a **potato crop multimodel inter-comparison** in **Gisozi, Burundi**, demonstrating that ensemble modeling reduced yield prediction uncertainty (from 38% to 20%) and highlighted temperature sensitivity as a critical factor for adaptation, with yields declining **4.6% per °C** under nonirrigated conditions (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1111/gcb.13411](https://doi.org/10.1111/gcb.13411)).

• **CIAT and partners** analyzed **agricultural diversification strategies** across Africa, showing that households in **Burundi’s agroecological zones** (500–1000mm rainfall) with higher farming diversity achieve greater food security, though limits exist due to land constraints (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1111/gcb.14158](https://doi.org/10.1111/gcb.14158)).

• **CGIAR’s Climate Change Program** explored **scenario-guided adaptive action** in East Africa, emphasizing **strategic planning capacity** and collaborative pathways to embed resilience in food systems, with Burundi’s context informing regional adaptation frameworks (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.gloenvcha.2014.03.001](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.gloenvcha.2014.03.001)).

• **World Agroforestry Centre (ICRAF)** promoted **fodder trees** as drought-resilient feed sources for livestock, addressing production shortages in Burundi’s highlands where smallholders integrate them into mixed farming systems (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cosust.2013.11.008](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cosust.2013.11.008)).

• **CIAT** developed a **spatial prioritization framework** for the **Great Lakes Region**, mapping **socio-agroecological homologues (SAHs)** to target climate-smart agriculture (CSA) investments, including Burundi’s potato and banana systems (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agsy.2022.103464](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agsy.2022.103464)).

• **CGIAR’s Systemwide Program on Climate Change** synthesized **trade-offs and synergies** in smallholder adaptation strategies (e.g., water management, crop diversification), with Burundi’s context illustrating challenges like labor demands and resource competition (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.regsus.2021.05.002](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.regsus.2021.05.002)).
Burundi.Mitigation: • **CGIAR’s Climate Program** assessed **land cover/land use change (LCLUC)** and **greenhouse gas (GHG) influences** on regional climate dynamics in East Africa, highlighting heterogeneous yield impacts—including Burundi’s vulnerability—to inform mitigation-informed food security strategies (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-011-0116-7](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-011-0116-7)).
Burundi.Water:
• **CIP** evaluated **water use efficiency (WUE)** and **evapotranspiration (ET)** in potato systems under climate variability, showing **15% higher uncertainty** in low-input sites like **Gisozi, Burundi**, compared to high-input sites, with ET and nitrogen uptake as key adaptation levers (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1111/gcb.13411](https://doi.org/10.1111/gcb.13411)).

• **IFPRI (International Food Policy Research Institute)** modeled **economywide water-agriculture linkages** in Sub-Saharan Africa, demonstrating that **productivity gains (e.g., yield increases)** outperform irrigated area expansion as adaptation measures, with Burundi’s limited baseline irrigation informing regional water management strategies (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ecolecon.2013.05.006](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ecolecon.2013.05.006)).

Cabo Verde.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **Climate-Smart Agriculture (CSA)** in **Cabo Verde** through institutional capacity building and policy-aligned research. Specifically:
- **CGIAR’s Climate Change, Agriculture and Food Security (CCAFS) program** developed a **country-specific CSA baseline** to guide investments in productivity, resilience, and GHG reduction (10568/106069).
- **CGIAR’s AICCRA Project** (in collaboration with WASCAL and RUFORUM) integrated **CSA and Climate Information Services (CIS)** into university curricula across West Africa, including thematic modules on **soil carbon sequestration, disaster risk management, and pest/disease adaptation** (10568/179514).
Cabo Verde.Adaptation:
• **CGIAR’s CCAFS program** synthesized **climate-smart adaptation strategies** for Cabo Verde’s farming systems, emphasizing **resilience in crop, livestock, and fishery sectors** through policy and institutional mechanisms (10568/106069).
• **CGIAR researchers** analyzed **trade-offs and synergies in smallholder adaptation strategies** (e.g., water/soil management, risk mitigation) to enhance **yield stability and sustainability** in sub-Saharan Africa, including Cabo Verde’s context (10.1016/j.regsus.2021.05.002).
• **CGIAR’s bioeconomic modeling** evaluated **adaptation measures for maize systems**, projecting **yield reductions under climate change** and recommending **input-level adjustments** (e.g., nitrogen management) to improve resilience (10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005).
• **CGIAR’s IMPACT/GTAP-W models** assessed **two adaptation scenarios** (doubling irrigated area or increasing yields by 25%) to **cope with climate change impacts** in Sub-Saharan Africa, highlighting productivity gains as superior to irrigation expansion (10.1016/j.ecolecon.2013.05.006).
Cabo Verde.Mitigation:
• **CGIAR’s CSA framework** in Cabo Verde explicitly targets **GHG reduction/removal** alongside productivity and adaptation, with a focus on **soil carbon sequestration** and **low-emission agricultural practices** (10568/106069).
• **CGIAR’s AICCRA Project** trained **324 stakeholders (26% women)** in **soil carbon sequestration techniques** and **disaster risk management**, aligning with mitigation goals through curriculum integration (10568/179514).
Cabo Verde.Water: No evidence found in this sample.

Cambodia.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-resilient agricultural systems and inclusive adaptation strategies** in **Cambodia** through **risk management, gender-responsive climate services, and trans-disciplinary networks**. Specifically:
- CGIAR’s research assessed **climate-risk management interventions** (e.g., stress-adapted crop germplasm, conservation agriculture, and diversified production systems) to stabilize smallholder incomes and reduce poverty in risk-prone systems (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agsy.2018.01.019](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agsy.2018.01.019)).
- The Center developed **gender-responsive rural climate services**, addressing inequalities in access to ICTs and climate information for women, while proposing inclusion strategies for women’s groups and cross-sectoral collaboration (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1080/17565529.2019.1613216](https://doi.org/10.1080/17565529.2019.1613216)).
- CGIAR facilitated **participatory scenario planning** for Southeast Asia, including Cambodia, to explore food-climate futures and inform policy under uncertain climatic and socioeconomic changes (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.envsoft.2016.05.008](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.envsoft.2016.05.008)).
Cambodia.Adaptation:
• CGIAR’s research demonstrated that **stress-adapted crop germplasm, conservation agriculture, and diversified production systems** stabilize agricultural production and incomes, mitigating climate-related risks for smallholder farmers in Cambodia (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agsy.2018.01.019](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agsy.2018.01.019) ).

• The Center analyzed **gender-based disparities in climate service access**, highlighting barriers like socio-cultural norms and ICT limitations, and proposed **women’s group inclusion and tailored ICT solutions** to improve climate information uptake (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1080/17565529.2019.1613216](https://doi.org/10.1080/17565529.2019.1613216) ).

• CGIAR developed **multi-factor scenario tools** to project climate impacts on rice yields in the Mekong Basin (including Cambodia), evaluating adaptation options like **supplementary irrigation and adjusted planting dates** to offset yield declines in rainfed systems (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1007/s12571-011-0154-z](https://doi.org/10.1007/s12571-011-0154-z) ).

• The Center promoted **Climate-Smart Villages (CSV)** through a step-by-step guide, enabling localized implementation of climate-smart agriculture (CSA) and food security strategies (Source: [10568/107725](10568/107725) ).

• CGIAR’s **Direct Seeded Rice Consortium (DSRC)** in Asia, including Cambodia, fostered **trans-disciplinary networks** to enhance resilience to extreme weather events through climate-smart agricultural practices (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3390/cli8020035](https://doi.org/10.3390/cli8020035) ).

• The Center provided **pest-smart interventions and Early Warning Systems** for rice farmers, addressing climate-induced pest/disease challenges (Source: [10568/97537](10568/97537) ).

• CGIAR reviewed **migration as an adaptation strategy** to hydroclimatic changes, identifying droughts/floods as triggers for mobility while emphasizing the need for integrated climate-migration policies (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-023-03573-6](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-023-03573-6) ).

• The Center assessed **water resource management strategies** in the Greater Mekong Subregion, including Cambodia, to improve resilience and safeguard food production amid climate variability (Source: [https://doi.org/10.5337/2010.212](https://doi.org/10.5337/2010.212) ).
Cambodia.Mitigation: No evidence found in this sample.
Cambodia.Water:
• CGIAR evaluated **climate-induced variability in water cycles** and its impact on rice yields in Cambodia’s Mekong Basin, proposing **supplementary irrigation and adjusted planting dates** as adaptation measures (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1007/s12571-011-0154-z](https://doi.org/10.1007/s12571-011-0154-z) ).

• The Center analyzed **water availability, quality, and groundwater resilience** in the Greater Mekong Subregion, including Cambodia, to inform climate-adaptive water management policies (Source: [https://doi.org/10.5337/2010.212](https://doi.org/10.5337/2010.212) ).

Cameroon.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-smart agriculture and resilience-building** in **Cameroon** through targeted research on cocoa, maize, and indigenous food systems. Specifically:
- **CGIAR’s Climate Change, Agriculture and Food Security (CCAFS)** and **Bioversity International** assessed cocoa productivity responses to climate change, projecting yield increases in Cameroon under elevated CO₂ and wetter scenarios (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agrformet.2025.110393](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agrformet.2025.110393)).
- **CGIAR’s Cocoa Research Program** analyzed climate vulnerability of cocoa, identifying Cameroon’s southern regions as least vulnerable and recommending shade-tree adaptation strategies (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.scitotenv.2016.03.024](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.scitotenv.2016.03.024)).
Cameroon.Adaptation:
• **CGIAR’s CCAFS** evaluated cocoa’s vulnerability to climate change in Cameroon, highlighting the need for shade-tree adaptation to mitigate temperature stress and deforestation risks (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.scitotenv.2016.03.024](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.scitotenv.2016.03.024) ).

• **CGIAR’s Maize Program** modeled maize yield reductions in Cameroon under climate change, emphasizing the need for improved varieties and input management to sustain food security (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005](https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005) ).

• **CGIAR’s Climate-Smart Agriculture Team** reviewed regional adaptation strategies for smallholder farmers, including Cameroon, focusing on trade-offs and synergies in water, soil, and crop management (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.regsus.2021.05.002](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.regsus.2021.05.002) ).

• **CGIAR’s Regional Adaptation Planning** divided West Africa into climate adaptation zones, positioning Cameroon’s cocoa regions as suitable for intensification or diversification (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1007/s11027-016-9707-y](https://doi.org/10.1007/s11027-016-9707-y) ).

• **CGIAR’s Indigenous Knowledge Program** documented the Baka people’s food systems in Cameroon, highlighting territorial management techniques as resilient practices against climate change (Source: [https://doi.org/10.4060/cb5131en](https://doi.org/10.4060/cb5131en) ).

• **Bioversity International** mapped vulnerability of 100 priority tree species in Central Africa, including Cameroon, to guide restoration efforts under climate and anthropogenic threats (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.biocon.2022.109554](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.biocon.2022.109554) ).
Cameroon.Mitigation: • **CGIAR’s Climate Change Research** analyzed cocoa productivity under elevated CO₂, suggesting potential mitigation through carbon fertilization effects in Cameroon’s cocoa-growing regions (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agrformet.2025.110393](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agrformet.2025.110393) ).
Cameroon.Water: No evidence found in this sample.

Canada.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate resilience and mitigation** in **Canada** through targeted research on agricultural adaptation, greenhouse gas reduction, and policy-aligned innovations. Specifically:
- CGIAR’s research centers developed **climate-smart agricultural strategies** to enhance productivity and livelihoods in rangelands and smallholder systems (Source: [Adaptation]).
