- The Center advanced **methane emission quantification methods** for livestock manure management, improving inventory responsiveness to farm-level changes (Source: [Mitigation]).
Canada.Adaptation:
• CGIAR modeled **global rangeland ecosystem responses** to climate change, projecting regional productivity shifts (e.g., +21% in northern Canada) and quantifying economic losses for livestock-dependent livelihoods, while proposing adaptation policy frameworks (https://doi.org/10.1111/gcb.13995).

• The Center analyzed **scaling innovations in smallholder agriculture** through the Canadian International Food Security Research Fund (CIFSRF), emphasizing socio-ecological dynamics and sustainable system outcomes to address climate variability and economic risks (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agsy.2019.05.012).

• CGIAR explored **next-generation school feeding policies** within G20 frameworks, aligning Canada’s food-based dietary guidelines with climate-resilient, healthy food systems to mitigate sustainability trade-offs (https://doi.org/10.4060/cc2805en).

• Researchers proposed **adaptation measures** (e.g., new crop varieties and irrigation technologies) using the IMPACT model to quantify climate change effects on yields and food security, estimating public/private R&D investment needs (https://doi.org/10.1088/1748-9326/8/3/035008).

• CGIAR supported **Climate-Smart Village (CSV) initiatives** in Myanmar (funded by IDRC Canada) to enhance household resilience, livelihoods, and gender equity in dryland agriculture, despite no direct Canadian fieldwork (10568/123688).

• The Center reviewed **adaptation effectiveness methodologies** in agri-food systems, drawing on IPCC WGII frameworks to evaluate tools like impact evaluations and satellite imaging (10568/135997).
Canada.Mitigation:
• CGIAR researchers developed **methane emission inventory methods** for Canadian livestock manure management, addressing gaps in activity data (e.g., liquid vs. solid manure storage) to improve emission model accuracy and responsiveness to farm practices (https://doi.org/10.1088/1748-9326/8/3/035008).

• The Center contributed to **land-based climate change mitigation** by synthesizing global agricultural mitigation strategies, including Canadian case studies on livestock, agroforestry, and fertilizer use, to accelerate emissions reduction in smallholder and industrial systems (10568/16715).
Canada.Water: No evidence found in this sample.

Central African Republic.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate resilience, biodiversity conservation, and agricultural adaptation** in the **Central African Republic** through **bioeconomic modeling, vulnerability mapping, and REDD+ narrative analysis**. Specifically:
- **CIP/MAIZE** calibrated bioeconomic models to assess maize yield reductions under climate change and proposed adaptation measures (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005](https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005)).
- **Bioversity/CGIAR** mapped vulnerability of 100 priority tree species to guide restoration and conservation efforts (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.biocon.2022.109554](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.biocon.2022.109554)).
- **CGIAR Climate** analyzed trade-offs in smallholder adaptation strategies (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.regsus.2021.05.002](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.regsus.2021.05.002)).
Central African Republic.Adaptation:
• **CIP/MAIZE** modeled maize yield impacts under climate change (A1B scenario) and identified region-specific adaptation needs, including input intensification and trade policies (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005](https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005)).

• **CGIAR Climate** conducted a systematic review of smallholder adaptation strategies (crop/soil/water/livestock management) and highlighted trade-offs (e.g., labor costs) and synergies (e.g., yield stability) (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.regsus.2021.05.002](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.regsus.2021.05.002)).

• **World Agroforestry Centre (ICRAF)/CGIAR** developed vulnerability maps for 100 tree species to prioritize **in-situ/ex-situ conservation** and restoration actions (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.biocon.2022.109554](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.biocon.2022.109554)).

• **CGIAR Economics** evaluated two adaptation scenarios (doubling irrigated area vs. yield increases) using **IMPACT/GTAP-W models**, concluding productivity gains outperform irrigation expansion (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ecolecon.2013.05.006](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ecolecon.2013.05.006)).

• **CGIAR Climate** analyzed **ODA allocation gaps**, noting the Central African Republic’s high vulnerability (0.62 EAAPC) despite low adaptation-focused funding (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pclm.0000382](https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pclm.0000382)).

• **AGNES/CGIAR** strengthened African negotiators’ capacity in **attribution science** to link GHG emissions to climate extremes (Source: [10568/178443](10568/178443)).
Central African Republic.Mitigation: • **CGIAR Forestry** critiqued **REDD+ carbon certification standards** in Central Africa, exposing gaps between promised avoided deforestation payments and real-world implementation (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1505/146554820829403469](https://doi.org/10.1505/146554820829403469)).
Central African Republic.Water:
• **CGIAR Climate/Watersheds** documented climate-induced **water cycle fluctuations** in the Congo Basin (including CAR), linking deforestation and variability to livelihood impacts (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3390/su12072936](https://doi.org/10.3390/su12072936)).

• **CGIAR Economics** integrated **water simulation modules** (IMPACT/GTAP-W) into climate adaptation modeling for Sub-Saharan Africa (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ecolecon.2013.05.006](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ecolecon.2013.05.006)).

Chad.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-smart agriculture (CSA) and adaptive resilience** in **Chad** through multi-sectoral interventions. Specifically:
- CGIAR’s **Climate-Smart Agriculture (CSA) initiatives** integrated productivity, adaptation, and mitigation pillars to enhance food security and climate responsiveness in Chad’s agricultural systems (10568/114912).
- The Center developed **vulnerability mapping** for 100 priority tree species in Central Africa to guide restoration and conservation efforts, addressing climate and anthropogenic threats (10.1016/j.biocon.2022.109554).
Chad.Adaptation:
• CGIAR synthesized **climate-smart agriculture strategies** for West Africa, including Chad, to improve resilience in crop, livestock, and fishery sectors through tailored adaptation policies and institutional mechanisms (10.1186/s40066-016-0075-3).

• The Center analyzed **trade-offs and synergies** in climate adaptation strategies for smallholder farmers, identifying low-cost, complementary measures to enhance productivity, yield stability, and sustainability (10.1016/j.regsus.2021.05.002).

• CGIAR assessed **adaptation scenarios** for Sub-Saharan Africa, demonstrating that productivity gains (e.g., 25% yield increases) outperform irrigated area expansion as climate adaptation measures (10.1016/j.ecolecon.2013.05.006).

• The Center evaluated **social protection alignment** with climate resilience in the Sahel, including Chad, to strengthen adaptive capacities of vulnerable households (10568/135122).

• CGIAR developed an **integrated dataset** on food security and household coping strategies in Chad, linking climate hazards (e.g., extreme weather, rainy season performance) to vulnerability and resilience (doi:10.7910/DVN/SQA143).

• The Center created the **Conflict–Climate Pressure Index (CCPI)** to enhance early warning systems for food crises in the Sahel, improving anticipatory capacity (10568/179392).

• CGIAR assessed the **climate-smartness** of the West Africa Agricultural Productivity Programme (WAAPP) in Chad, strengthening stakeholder capacity to mainstream CSA (10568/106455).
Chad.Mitigation: • CGIAR’s CSA framework in Chad explicitly targets **GHG reduction/removal** while balancing productivity and adaptation goals (10568/114912).
Chad.Water: No evidence found in this sample.

Chile.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-smart agricultural adaptation and mitigation strategies** in **Chile** through targeted research on pollinator-crop interactions, genetic diversity conservation, silvopastoral systems, and grassland resilience. Specifically:
- **CIAT** modeled coupled climate impacts on coffee suitability and pollinator richness to inform shade adjustment and forest conservation (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.1617940114](https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.1617940114)).
- **Bioversity** assessed genetic diversity hotspots in southern Chile/Argentina to identify climate-vulnerable regions and propose conservation policies (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1007/s10531-023-02770-0](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10531-023-02770-0)).
Chile.Adaptation:
• **CGIAR-CIAT** analyzed geographic range shifts in coffee-pollinator systems to guide **shade adjustment, crop rotation, and forest conservation** as adaptive management practices (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.1617940114](https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.1617940114) ).

• **CGIAR-Bioversity** mapped genetic diversity hotspots in Patagonian ecoregions to identify **climate-refuge areas** and prioritize conservation policies for species facing >50% habitat loss (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1007/s10531-023-02770-0](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10531-023-02770-0) ).

• **CGIAR-ILRI** documented **Mapuche community-led adaptation strategies** in pastoralism and crop production, emphasizing site-specific resilience frameworks (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1038/s44168-024-00124-2](https://doi.org/10.1038/s44168-024-00124-2) ).

• **CGIAR** promoted **Local Technical Agroclimatic Committees (MTAs)** in Chile to integrate **climate-smart agriculture** and mitigation practices into regional planning (Source: [10568/114943](10568/114943) ).
Chile.Mitigation:
• **CGIAR-CIFOR** evaluated **forest landscape restoration** in Latin America (including Chile) as a **low-cost mitigation strategy**, highlighting natural regeneration and assisted regeneration for aboveground biomass storage (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3390/f10060510](https://doi.org/10.3390/f10060510) ).

• **CGIAR-ILRI** demonstrated **silvopastoral systems (SPS)** in Chile as a **GHG mitigation tool**, showing potential for >30% emission reductions and carbon stock increases under Nationally Determined Contributions (NDCs) (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-031-43063-3_2](https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-031-43063-3_2) ).

• **CGIAR** analyzed **forest carbon sequestration policies** in non-Annex regions (including Chile) to assess poverty impacts, identifying **avoided deforestation** as a critical mitigation lever (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1088/1748-9326/8/3/035009](https://doi.org/10.1088/1748-9326/8/3/035009) ).

• **CGIAR** reviewed **grassland-based mitigation strategies** in Mediterranean climates (e.g., Chile), emphasizing **carbon sequestration, soil erosion control, and wildfire prevention** through forage legumes and perennial species (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1111/gfs.12212](https://doi.org/10.1111/gfs.12212) ).
Chile.Water: No evidence found in this sample.

China.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-smart agricultural solutions in China** through **adaptation, mitigation, and water management innovations**. Specifically:
- **CGIAR’s Climate Change, Agriculture and Food Security (CCAFS) program** quantified **agroforestry’s carbon sequestration potential** in China’s agricultural lands, revealing trees contribute >75% to biomass carbon stocks (Source: [10.1038/srep29987](https://doi.org/10.1038/srep29987)).
- **The Center for International Forestry Research (CIFOR)** demonstrated **soil carbon recovery** in degraded grasslands via grazing exclosures and cropland restoration, achieving **0.24 Pg C/year sequestration**—equivalent to 16% of China’s 2006 fossil fuel emissions (Source: [10.1016/j.agee.2011.06.002](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agee.2011.06.002)).
- **CGIAR’s International Water Management Institute (IWMI)** analyzed **hydropower-irrigation tradeoffs** in China’s Yellow and Yangtze basins, identifying **8% of hydropower capacity (79,000 MW) complements irrigation**, while climate change exacerbates water scarcity in southern regions (Source: [10.1088/1748-9326/aa5f3f](https://doi.org/10.1088/1748-9326/aa5f3f)).
China.Adaptation:
• **CGIAR’s CCAFS** modeled **crop failure risks** in Northeast China’s wheat systems, showing **heat/drought stress increases failure rates** and highlighting **socio-economic adaptation** (e.g., drought-tolerant varieties, farmer investment) as critical (Source: [10.1088/1748-9326/5/3/034012](https://doi.org/10.1088/1748-9326/5/3/034012)).

• **Bioversity International** surveyed **public breeding programs** in China, revealing **limited access to climate-resilient germplasm** due to administrative/technical barriers, despite breeders’ efforts to integrate drought/heat-tolerant traits (Source: [10.3390/plants9091129](https://doi.org/10.3390/plants9091129)).

• **CGIAR’s IWMI** documented **spatial relocation of rice production** in Northeast China (1980–2010), with **temperature-driven shifts** moving rice centroids **320 km northeastward**, while precipitation/sunshine had marginal effects (Source: [10.1088/1748-9326/aafa55](https://doi.org/10.1088/1748-9326/aafa55)).
China.Mitigation:
• **CGIAR’s CIFOR** quantified **agroforestry’s global carbon contribution**, showing China’s agricultural lands stored **45.3 PgC** (75% from trees), with **2% tree-cover growth (2000–2010) adding >2 PgC** (Source: [10.1038/srep29987](https://doi.org/10.1038/srep29987)).

• **CGIAR’s CCAFS** modeled **GHG mitigation tradeoffs**, finding **soil carbon sequestration** in China/India could **reduce food calorie losses by 65%** (limiting undernourishment to 20–75 million) while meeting 1.5°C targets (Source: [10.1088/1748-9326/aa8c83](https://doi.org/10.1088/1748-9326/aa8c83)).

• **CGIAR’s IWMI** analyzed **hydropower-irrigation synergies**, revealing **China’s Yangtze/Yellow basins** benefit from **hydropower supporting irrigation**, though climate change threatens water availability (Source: [10.1088/1748-9326/aa5f3f](https://doi.org/10.1088/1748-9326/aa5f3f)).
China.Water:
• **CGIAR’s IWMI** assessed **land-cover/climate impacts** in SW China’s Kejie Watershed, showing **reforestation offset climate-driven streamflow declines**, with **surface water sensitive to land-use changes** (Source: [10.1002/hyp.7233](https://doi.org/10.1002/hyp.7233)).

• **CGIAR’s CCAFS** highlighted **nitrogen surplus in China’s cereal systems**, linking **excess fertilizer use to land degradation** and **water pollution**, while advocating for **precision N management** to align with SDG 13 (climate action) (Source: [10.1016/bs.agron.2020.05.006](https://doi.org/10.1016/bs.agron.2020.05.006)).

Colombia.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-smart agriculture, mitigation strategies, and adaptive resilience** in **Colombia** through **data-driven interventions, policy frameworks, and participatory co-design**. Specifically:
- **CIAT** developed **climate-smart farming systems** via co-design with local stakeholders to enhance resilience (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3389/fsufs.2019.00037](https://doi.org/10.3389/fsufs.2019.00037)).
- **CIP/CIAT** assessed **weather-yield relationships in rice** to inform site-specific adaptation strategies (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0161620](https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0161620)).
- **CIAT** modeled **coupled impacts of climate change on coffee and pollinators** to guide forest conservation and shade management (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.1617940114](https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.1617940114)).
Colombia.Adaptation:
• **CGIAR (CIAT)** analyzed **farmers’ and experts’ risk perceptions** to design targeted climate adaptation strategies, revealing misalignments in perceived threats (e.g., farmers prioritized crop failure/health access, while experts emphasized insecurity/unreliable weather) (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-018-2320-1](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-018-2320-1)).

• **CGIAR (CIAT/CIP)** applied **data mining techniques** to assess **weather-yield relationships in irrigated/rainfed rice**, identifying cultivar-specific responses to climate variability and enabling site-specific adaptation recommendations (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0161620](https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0161620)).

• **CGIAR (CIAT)** proposed a **7-phase co-design framework** for climate-smart farming systems in Colombia, engaging farmers, scientists, and NGOs to scale resilient practices (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3389/fsufs.2019.00037](https://doi.org/10.3389/fsufs.2019.00037)).

• **CGIAR (CIP)** modeled **geographic shifts in coffee suitability and pollinator richness** under climate change, recommending **forest conservation, shade adjustment, and crop rotation** to mitigate coupled risks (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.1617940114](https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.1617940114)).

• **CGIAR (CIAT)** linked **livelihood diversification** (e.g., agrobiodiversity, agroecological practices) to **reduced household vulnerability** in Andean-Amazon foothills, informing interventions to strengthen adaptive capacity (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pclm.0000051](https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pclm.0000051)).

• **CGIAR (CIAT)** proposed a **national livestock program** to scale mitigation/adaptation practices, emphasizing **technical, policy, and institutional barriers** (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3389/fsufs.2019.00061](https://doi.org/10.3389/fsufs.2019.00061)).

• **CGIAR (CIAT)** tested the **LCA4CSA framework** to assess environmental trade-offs of climate-smart practices (e.g., compost use, animal feeding modifications) in coffee/livestock systems (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agsy.2019.02.001](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agsy.2019.02.001)).
Colombia.Mitigation:
• **CGIAR (CIAT)** evaluated **carbon tax and abatement target scenarios** for Colombia’s energy sector, highlighting the role of **hydropower, wind, biomass, and CCS** in reducing CO₂ emissions (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eneco.2015.05.010](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eneco.2015.05.010)).

• **CGIAR (ILRI/CIAT)** assessed **enteric methane mitigation potentials** in Latin American livestock, identifying barriers to adoption of low-emission technologies/management practices (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3389/fsufs.2020.00065](https://doi.org/10.3389/fsufs.2020.00065)).

• **CGIAR (CIAT)** proposed **forest landscape restoration** as a mitigation strategy, analyzing 154 LAC projects’ potential to store aboveground biomass under the **Bonn Challenge** (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3390/f10060510](https://doi.org/10.3390/f10060510)).

• **CGIAR (CIAT/CIP)** demonstrated the **mitigation potential of compost** (3% reduction) and **animal feeding modifications** in livestock systems, using LCA4CSA to quantify GHG impacts (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agsy.2019.02.001](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agsy.2019.02.001)).
Colombia.Water: No evidence found in this sample.

Comoros.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate resilience and agricultural adaptation** in **Comoros** through targeted research and investment prioritization. Specifically:
- **CIAT** conducted **climate and environmental assessments** to inform USD 75 million in IFAD-funded adaptation strategies, including a USD 4 million program in Comoros (Source: **10568/76385**).
- **CGIAR researchers** assessed **climate change vulnerability** in Comoros’ agricultural sector, using modeling and GIS tools to recommend risk management strategies for small-scale farmers (Source: **10568/78667**).
Comoros.Adaptation:
• **CIAT** integrated **climate proofing** into IFAD’s USD 75 million agricultural investments in Comoros, prioritizing adaptation strategies for cocoa and subsistence crops based on climate impact assessments (Source: **10568/76385**).
• **CGIAR researchers** conducted a **spatially explicit vulnerability assessment** of Comoros’ agricultural sector, identifying drought, soil erosion, and flooding risks and proposing resilience measures for banana, cassava, and tomato value chains (Source: **10568/78667**).
Comoros.Mitigation: No evidence found in this sample.
Comoros.Water: No evidence found in this sample.

Congo.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-smart agricultural adaptation and biodiversity conservation** in the **Congo Basin and Central Africa** through **diversification strategies, crop suitability modeling, vulnerability mapping, and policy-relevant research**. Specifically:
- **CGIAR’s Climate Change, Agriculture and Food Security (CCAFS) and Bioversity International** developed **agricultural diversification frameworks** to enhance food security under rainfall variability (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1111/gcb.14158](https://doi.org/10.1111/gcb.14158)).
- **CIAT and CIP** optimized **crop calendars and climate-proof varieties** for root, tuber, and banana crops in the Great Lakes Region, including **yam farming in South Kivu, DRC** (Sources: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agsy.2021.103246](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agsy.2021.103246), [https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0309775](https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0309775)).
- **Bioversity International** mapped **vulnerability of 100 priority tree species** to climate and anthropogenic threats, guiding **restoration and conservation strategies** in Central Africa (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.biocon.2022.109554](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.biocon.2022.109554)).
Congo.Adaptation:
• **CGIAR-CCAFS** demonstrated that **agricultural diversification** (e.g., integrating food crops, cash crops, and livestock) improves household food security in Africa, particularly in regions with **500–1000mm annual rainfall and 17–22% variability**, while highlighting limits for 43% of cropland lacking diversification opportunities (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1111/gcb.14158](https://doi.org/10.1111/gcb.14158)).

• **CIAT and CIP** identified **climate-proof varieties** (e.g., drought-tolerant, heat-tolerant cassava, sweetpotato, and banana) and optimized **planting schedules** for Central Africa’s Great Lakes Region, improving crop suitability under future climates (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agsy.2021.103246](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agsy.2021.103246)).

• **CGIAR’s Systemwide Program on Climate Change** analyzed **trade-offs and synergies in climate adaptation strategies** (e.g., minimum tillage, water management) for smallholder farmers, emphasizing **low-cost, complementary measures** to enhance resilience (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.regsus.2021.05.002](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.regsus.2021.05.002)).

• **CGIAR’s Economics of Land and Water Division** modeled **adaptation scenarios** (e.g., doubling irrigated area or increasing yields by 25%) and found that **productivity gains** outperform irrigation expansion for climate resilience in Sub-Saharan Africa (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ecolecon.2013.05.006](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ecolecon.2013.05.006)).

• **CGIAR’s Climate Change Program** optimized **yam crop calendars** for South Kivu, DRC, recommending **AEZ-specific planting dates** (e.g., September–October) and **soil water conservation practices** to address rainfall deficits and extreme events (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0309775](https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0309775)).

• **CGIAR’s Social, Economic, and Policy Research** documented **local adaptation strategies** in Bukavu, DRC, identifying **barriers to implementation** (e.g., poverty, policy gaps) and recommending integration into **public policy frameworks** (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1177/1070496514536395](https://doi.org/10.1177/1070496514536395)).

• **CGIAR’s Water-Energy-Food Nexus research** explored **integrated WEF nexus approaches** to build resilience in southern Africa, addressing **water-energy-food insecurity** through cross-sectoral coordination (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3390/ijerph15102306](https://doi.org/10.3390/ijerph15102306)).

• **CGIAR’s Maize Program** assessed **biophysical and socioeconomic impacts of climate change on maize** in SSA, projecting **yield reductions under medium/high intensification** and recommending **trade policies** to offset food insecurity (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005](https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005)).

• **CGIAR’s COVID-19 Food Security Task Force** analyzed **pandemic-induced lockdowns** during planting seasons, highlighting **policy gaps** and advocating for **post-COVID strategies** to sustain food systems in SSA (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1038/s41538-020-00073-0](https://doi.org/10.1038/s41538-020-00073-0)).
Congo.Mitigation: No evidence found in this sample.
Congo.Water:
• **CGIAR’s Water-Energy-Food Nexus research** integrated **water management** into climate adaptation strategies, emphasizing **cross-sectoral coordination** to address **water scarcity and food-energy insecurity** in southern Africa (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3390/ijerph15102306](https://doi.org/10.3390/ijerph15102306)).

• **CGIAR’s Economics of Land and Water Division** developed **general equilibrium and partial equilibrium models** (e.g., IMPACT, GTAP-W) to evaluate **water-agriculture linkages** and adaptation scenarios, including **doubling irrigated area** or **yield increases** (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ecolecon.2013.05.006](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ecolecon.2013.05.006)).

• **CGIAR’s Maize Program** examined **water-nitrogen interactions** under climate change, modeling **yield reductions in lowland/dry mid-altitude maize mega-environments** (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005](https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005)).

• **CGIAR’s Yam Research** identified **soil water conservation needs** for yam farming in South Kivu, DRC, due to **rainfall deficits** and **high evapotranspiration demands** (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0309775](https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0309775)).

Cook Islands.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate adaptation and resilience** in the **Cook Islands** through participatory risk assessment and policy-relevant scenario modeling. Specifically:
- CGIAR developed **four alternative future scenarios for Pacific food systems**, including fisheries, trade, and public health, to inform climate-resilient policies for fishers and farmers (Source: **10568/75610**).
- CGIAR supported a **participatory mapping approach** in the Cook Islands, enabling communities to assess climate risks and design localized adaptation strategies (Source: **10568/102604**).
Cook Islands.Adaptation:
• CGIAR generated **four future food-system scenarios for the Pacific**, addressing climate vulnerability (droughts, cyclones) and offering policy tools to enhance adaptation for fishers and farmers (10568/75610).
• CGIAR facilitated a **participatory mapping initiative** in the Cook Islands, identifying vulnerable areas and empowering communities to develop climate-adaptation strategies (10568/102604).
Cook Islands.Mitigation: No evidence found in this sample.
Cook Islands.Water: No evidence found in this sample.

Costa Rica.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-smart agriculture, genetic adaptation, and silvopastoral systems** in **Costa Rica and Mesoamerica** through **agroforestry transformation, pollinator-crop coupling, and mitigation strategies**. Specifically:
- **CIAT** assessed climate suitability for cocoa and agroforestry systems to inform land-use planning (Source: [10.1038/s41598-019-45491-7](https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-019-45491-7)).
- **Bioversity** evaluated genetic diversity and breeding strategies to enhance climate resilience in crops (Source: [10.3390/plants9091129](https://doi.org/10.3390/plants9091129)).
- **CIP** modeled coffee-pollinator interactions to guide forest conservation and shade management (Source: [10.1073/pnas.1617940114](https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.1617940114)).
Costa Rica.Adaptation:
• **CIAT** evaluated climate suitability for cocoa and agroforestry systems, identifying tree species vulnerability and proposing agroforestry transformations to adapt coffee/cocoa production regions in Mesoamerica (Source: [10.1038/s41598-019-45491-7](https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-019-45491-7)).

• **Bioversity** analyzed public breeding programs’ use of genetic resources for climate adaptation, highlighting administrative/technical barriers to accessing germplasm (Source: [10.3390/plants9091129](https://doi.org/10.3390/plants9091129)).

• **CIP** modeled coupled impacts of climate change on coffee suitability and pollinator richness, recommending region-specific management (e.g., forest conservation, shade adjustment) (Source: [10.1073/pnas.1617940114](https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.1617940114)).

• **ICRAF** downscaled climate projections for Central America, projecting reduced precipitation, increased extreme temperatures, and altered water availability to inform drought risk management (Source: [10.1371/journal.pone.0193570](https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0193570)).

• **CIAT** mapped adaptive capacity of smallholder coffee farmers in Central America using expert knowledge to identify vulnerable communities for targeted adaptation planning (Source: [10.1007/s10584-016-1810-2](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-016-1810-2)).

• **CAC/SECAC** led the formulation of the **Estrategia Agricultura Sostenible Adaptada al Clima (EASAC)** for Central America, a participatory regional policy framework (Source: [10568/89328](10568/89328)).

• **CIAT** developed climate-crop-water models (e.g., CROPWAT) to optimize irrigation scheduling and minimize climate risks for farmers (Source: [10568/107445](10568/107445)).

• **CIAT/ICRAF** assessed the role of biological corridors in facilitating plant dispersal under climate change, prioritizing high-altitude corridors for vulnerable protected areas (Source: [10.1002/ece3.672](https://doi.org/10.1002/ece3.672)).
Costa Rica.Mitigation:
• **CIAT** analyzed silvopastoral systems (SPS) in Latin America, demonstrating their potential to reduce GHG emissions (CH₄, N₂O), increase carbon stocks, and contribute to **Nationally Determined Contributions (NDCs)** (Source: [10.1007/978-3-031-43063-3_2](https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-031-43063-3_2)).

• **Costa Rica’s NAMA Ganadería** (supported by CGIAR-aligned research) advanced eco-competitive livestock production to reduce GHG emissions via public-private-academic alliances (Source: [10568/79962](10568/79962)).
Costa Rica.Water:
• **ICRAF** projected **reduced precipitation, altered evapotranspiration, and decreased water availability** in Central America (2021–2050), with regional trends in extreme precipitation and dry spells (Source: [10.1371/journal.pone.0193570](https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0193570)).

• **CIAT** integrated **climate-crop-water models** (e.g., CROPWAT) to estimate irrigation needs and optimize water productivity under climate variability (Source: [10568/107445](10568/107445)).

Croatia.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-resilient agricultural adaptation** in **Croatia** through targeted research on protein-rich crops and cocoa value chains. Specifically:
- **CGIAR’s Climate Change and Food Security Program** analyzed climate impacts on protein-rich crop suitability, identifying regional shifts in suitability for faba bean, lentil, and chickpea while proposing breeding strategies to enhance abiotic stress tolerance (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eja.2019.125974](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eja.2019.125974)).
- **CGIAR’s Value Chain and Policy Research** presented climate-smart strategies for the cocoa sector at the **6th European Cocoa Forum (Dubrovnik, Croatia)**, focusing on risk mitigation for climate-vulnerable value chains (Source: [10568/79884](10568/79884)).
Croatia.Adaptation:
• **CGIAR’s Climate Change and Food Security Program** modeled heterogeneous climate impacts on protein-rich crop suitability across Europe, demonstrating expanded suitability for quinoa in northern regions while highlighting risks to traditional crops (e.g., chickpea, lentil) in southern Europe, and recommending policy shifts to support resilience (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eja.2019.125974](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eja.2019.125974)).
• **CGIAR’s Value Chain Research** addressed climate risks in cocoa production at the **6th European Cocoa Forum (Dubrovnik, Croatia)**, proposing industry-level strategies to minimize climate-related vulnerabilities (Source: [10568/79884](10568/79884)).
Croatia.Mitigation: No evidence found in this sample.
Croatia.Water: No evidence found in this sample.

Cuba.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-smart agricultural resilience and mitigation** in **Cuba** through targeted research and policy support. Specifically:
- **CIAT** modeled coupled climate impacts on coffee-pollinator systems to inform adaptive management (e.g., shade adjustment, forest conservation) (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.1617940114](https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.1617940114)).
- **CCAFS** enhanced **MRV (Measurement, Reporting, and Verification)** systems for agricultural GHG emissions, enabling evidence-based NDC mitigation targets in Cuba’s climate policies (Source: [10568/121260](10568/121260)).
Cuba.Adaptation:
• **CIAT** developed **spatially explicit models** linking coffee suitability and pollinator richness under climate change, identifying regions where **shade adjustment, crop rotation, or forest conservation** could mitigate negative coupling effects (e.g., declining bee richness in coffee-growing areas) (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.1617940114](https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.1617940114)).
• CGIAR’s **Latin America-focused policy research** (via CCAFS) highlighted **adaptive strategies for agriculture**, though Cuba-specific applications were not detailed in this sample (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3390/su9101831](https://doi.org/10.3390/su9101831)).
Cuba.Mitigation:
• **CCAFS** supported Cuba in **improving agricultural GHG monitoring** (part of an 8-country initiative), enabling transparent tracking of **NDC mitigation targets**—accounting for **24% of the country’s annual agricultural emissions** (Source: [10568/121260](10568/121260)).
• The Center’s work aligned with **forest-based mitigation** (e.g., reducing emissions from land-use change) as part of broader Latin American policy gaps analysis (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3390/su9101831](https://doi.org/10.3390/su9101831)).
Cuba.Water: No evidence found in this sample.

Cyprus.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-resilient protein-rich crop systems** in **Cyprus and Southern Europe** through **adaptation-focused research**. Specifically:
- **CGIAR’s Climate-Smart Agriculture team** analyzed climate change impacts on protein-rich crop suitability, identifying regional shifts in suitability (e.g., northern Europe becoming more favorable for legumes while southern Europe faces reduced suitability for chickpea and lentil) (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eja.2019.125974](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eja.2019.125974)).
Cyprus.Adaptation: • **CGIAR’s Crop Science Division** modeled heterogeneous climate impacts on protein-rich crops (e.g., faba bean, lentil, chickpea) under RCP4.5 projections, revealing geographic suitability shifts and recommending breeding strategies to enhance abiotic stress tolerance for southern European regions (e.g., Cyprus) (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eja.2019.125974](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eja.2019.125974)).
Cyprus.Mitigation: No evidence found in this sample.
Cyprus.Water: No evidence found in this sample.

Denmark.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-smart agriculture and resilience** in **Denmark** through **modeling, genetic research, and policy-relevant adaptation strategies**. Specifically:
- **CIP (International Potato Center)** led a **multimodel ensemble assessment** to evaluate potato yield responses to climate variability, reducing uncertainty in simulated yields under high-input systems like Jyndevad, Denmark (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1111/gcb.13411](https://doi.org/10.1111/gcb.13411)).
- **ICRISAT** conducted **genetic dissection of drought-tolerant sorghum traits** using NIRS (Near-Infrared Spectroscopy) technology developed in Denmark (FOSS Forage Analyzer) to improve fodder quality under terminal drought stress (Sources: [doi:10.21421/D2/DA4WUS](doi:10.21421/D2/DA4WUS), [doi:10.21421/D2/BM3HI1](doi:10.21421/D2/BM3HI1)).
Denmark.Adaptation:
• **CIP** quantified **potato yield sensitivity to temperature and rainfall** in high-input systems (e.g., Jyndevad, Denmark), demonstrating a **6% yield increase per 100-ppm CO₂** but a **4.6% decline per °C rise**, while highlighting model uncertainty in evapotranspiration and water use efficiency (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1111/gcb.13411](https://doi.org/10.1111/gcb.13411) ).

• **CGIAR (via IMPACT modeling)** proposed **adaptation strategies**—such as **new drought-tolerant crop varieties** and **irrigation technology upgrades**—to enhance resilience in OECD regions, including Denmark’s agricultural systems (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en](https://doi.org/10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en)).

• **Bioversity/CGIAR** documented **Denmark’s 28% reduction in agricultural emissions** while increasing productivity, citing **agroforestry and alternative practices** as key adaptation-mitigation synergies (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1126/science.1217941](https://doi.org/10.1126/science.1217941)).

• **CGIAR (forestry research)** surveyed European forest managers (including Danish stakeholders) on **active adaptation via forest genetic resources (FGR)**, emphasizing **non-local genotypes** to match future climate conditions (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1007/s10342-020-01311-6](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10342-020-01311-6)).

• **CIP/ILRI** assessed **protein-rich crop suitability** in Europe, noting **northern regions (e.g., Denmark)** may benefit from expanded **quinoa production** under climate change, while southern crops (e.g., lentils) face suitability declines (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eja.2019.125974](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eja.2019.125974)).
Denmark.Mitigation:
• **ICOS (Integrated Carbon Observation System, aligned with CGIAR’s GHG monitoring goals)** standardized **eddy covariance data processing** for CO₂ flux calculations at European stations, ensuring **comparable greenhouse gas measurements** critical for mitigation policies (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1515/intag-2017-0043](https://doi.org/10.1515/intag-2017-0043)).

• **CGIAR highlighted Denmark’s case study** in **reducing agricultural emissions by 28%** through **low-emission practices**, aligning with global mitigation targets (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1126/science.1217941](https://doi.org/10.1126/science.1217941)).
Denmark.Water:
• **CIP** analyzed **evapotranspiration (ET), water use efficiency, and rainfall impacts** on potato yields in Denmark’s high-input systems, revealing **higher model uncertainty for ET under climate variability** (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1111/gcb.13411](https://doi.org/10.1111/gcb.13411)).

• **ICRISAT** generated **sorghum drought-resilience data** under **irrigation-withheld vs. well-irrigated conditions**, using Danish-developed NIRS technology to measure fodder quality traits (Sources: [doi:10.21421/D2/DA4WUS](doi:10.21421/D2/DA4WUS), [doi:10.21421/D2/BM3HI1](doi:10.21421/D2/BM3HI1)).

Djibouti.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate resilience and agricultural adaptation** in **Djibouti** through **climate-smart agriculture (CSA), institutional capacity-building, and regional climate action frameworks**. Specifically:
- The Center integrated **climate-smart livestock systems** into food and nutrition security strategies, emphasizing GHG emissions and adaptation in Eastern Africa (including Djibouti) (10568/106995).
- CGIAR advanced **climate information services** for risk management in the Horn of Africa, developing agro-climatic variables (e.g., rainfall onset/cessation) to support agriculture and food security (10568/92093).
Djibouti.Adaptation:
• CGIAR **developed climate-smart agriculture (CSA) frameworks** for Eastern Africa, including Djibouti, focusing on livestock systems, GHG emissions, and food security (10568/106995).

• The Center **facilitated capacity-building workshops** (e.g., *Objective Climate Forecasts for Agriculture*) to strengthen institutional linkages and co-produce climate information for food security in the Horn of Africa (10568/116941).

• CGIAR **supported research-for-development partnerships** in the IGAD region (including Djibouti) to enhance climate adaptation in livestock and pastoralism (10568/128053).

• The Center **contributed to the East/Horn of Africa Climate Action Plan (2023–2028)**, aligning with IGAD’s regional strategy to operationalize climate adaptation measures for vulnerable populations (10568/137370).

• CGIAR **analyzed trade-offs/synergies in climate adaptation strategies** for smallholder farmers, emphasizing low-cost, multi-objective approaches (e.g., minimum tillage) to improve resilience (10.1016/j.regsus.2021.05.002).

• Researchers **assessed biophysical/socioeconomic impacts of climate change on maize** (relevant to Djibouti’s regional agroecologies) and proposed adaptation measures for yield stability (10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005).
Djibouti.Mitigation:
• CGIAR **reviewed greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions** in Eastern African agricultural systems, including Djibouti’s livestock sector, to inform climate-smart mitigation strategies (10568/106995).

• The Center **supported attribution science training** for African negotiators (including Djibouti’s representatives) to strengthen evidence-based climate mitigation arguments in global forums (10568/178443).
Djibouti.Water: No evidence found in this sample.

Dominica.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate policy and pest management** in **Dominica** through **emissions transparency and agricultural data innovation**. Specifically:
- **CGIAR researchers** conducted a **national GHG inventory needs assessment** to refine LULUCF emissions methodologies under the **Enhanced Transparency Framework (ETF)** (Source: **10568/176328**).
- **CGIAR’s Crop Protection Program** provided **IPM training datasets** on *Rhizopertha dominica* pest management for date palm storage (Source: **hdl:20.500.11766.1/FK2/XMWCWQ**).
Dominica.Adaptation: No evidence found in this sample.
Dominica.Mitigation: • **CGIAR researchers** assessed **Dominica’s agriculture and LULUCF sector GHG emissions** to improve **IPCC Level 2/3 methodologies**, enhancing transparency and reducing uncertainty in national inventories under the **Enhanced Transparency Framework (ETF)** (Source: **10568/176328**).
Dominica.Water: No evidence found in this sample.

Dominican Republic.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-smart agriculture (CSA) and resilient livelihoods** in the **Dominican Republic** through participatory research, policy integration, and capacity-building. Specifically:
- **CIAT** developed climate-smart cocoa practices to enhance productivity, adaptation, and emissions mitigation (Source: [10568/105542](https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.1617940114)).
- **CIAT and Bioversity International** integrated CSA into regional policy via fast-track processes, enabling climate-resilient agricultural transformation (Source: [10568/116170](https://doi.org/10.3390/su9101831)).
Dominican Republic.Adaptation:
• **CIAT** modeled coupled impacts of climate change on coffee suitability and pollinator richness, informing **shade adjustment, forest conservation, and crop rotation** to enhance resilience (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.1617940114](https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.1617940114)).

• **CIAT** developed **Climate-Smart Cocoa (CSC) practices**—validated in the Dominican Republic—to address extreme climate events (drought, storms) and improve farm-level resilience (Source: [10568/105542](https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.1617940114)).

• **CIAT** created an **Atlas of Climate Change Impacts on cocoa**, mapping agro-climatic zones and prioritizing **climate-smart practices** for Dominican Republic farmers (Source: [10568/103775](https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.1617940114)).

• **CIAT and Bioversity International** facilitated **Local Technical Agroclimatic Committees (LTACs)** to strengthen **adaptation strategies** and decision-making in Dominican agriculture (Source: [10568/174584](https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.1617940114)).

• **CIAT** integrated **climate-smart agriculture (CSA) into regional policy** through participatory workshops, enabling **fast-track adoption** of resilient practices (Source: [10568/116170](https://doi.org/10.3390/su9101831)).

• **CIAT** identified **site-specific CSA recommendations** for cocoa and coffee, aligned with climate exposure maps to minimize risks (Source: [10568/123193](https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.1617940114)).
Dominican Republic.Mitigation:
• **CIAT** researched **greenhouse gas mitigation from forests** and **climate-smart food systems (CSFS)**, including **agroforestry (coffee/cocoa) and regenerative livestock**, to reduce emissions (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3390/su9101831](https://doi.org/10.3390/su9101831)).

• **CIAT-CCAFS** developed a framework to scale **Climate-Smart Food Systems (CSFS)**, focusing on **emissions measurement, carbon markets, and MRV (Measurement, Reporting, Verification)** (Source: [10568/116921](https://doi.org/10.3390/su9101831)).

• **CIAT** prioritized **Climate-Smart Cocoa (CSC)** to **mitigate GHG emissions** while boosting productivity in the Dominican Republic (Source: [10568/105542](https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.1617940114)).
Dominican Republic.Water: No evidence found in this sample.

Ecuador.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-smart agriculture, Indigenous knowledge integration, and policy-driven mitigation** in **Ecuador** through **agroecological systems, participatory resilience frameworks, and climate-resilient crop assessments**. Specifically:
- **CIAT** and **CCAFS** advanced **agroecology as a systemic resilience tool**, integrating stakeholder insights to quantify its adaptation and mitigation potential (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1080/21683565.2023.2273835](https://doi.org/10.1080/21683565.2023.2273835)).
- **Bioversity/CGIAR** mapped **climate impact gradients on cocoa production**, providing spatial data for adaptive planning in Ecuador alongside Colombia and Peru (Source: [doi:10.7910/DVN/LXBZMU](doi:10.7910/DVN/LXBZMU)).
- **CCAFS** co-produced **Indigenous climate resilience frameworks** with the Sapara Nation, documenting traditional ecological knowledge for adaptive livelihood strategies (Source: [10568/105603](10568/105603)).
Ecuador.Adaptation:
• **CGIAR-CCAFS** developed **climate-smart agriculture guidelines** through **Local Technical Agroclimatic Committees (MTAs)**, embedding mitigation practices and gender-inclusive monitoring tools (Source: [10568/114943](10568/114943) ).
• **CIAT** assessed **geographic range shifts in coffee suitability and pollinator availability**, modeling coupled climate impacts to inform shade-adjustment and forest conservation strategies (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.1617940114](https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.1617940114) ).
• **CGIAR** surveyed **adoption of improved rice varieties** in Ecuador, linking socio-economic data to yield outcomes under climate variability (Source: [doi:10.7910/DVN/DX3F4T](doi:10.7910/DVN/DX3F4T) ).
• **CCAFS** proposed **methods to evaluate climate impacts on food/nutrition security**, tailoring tools for Central America and the Dominican Republic (Source: [10568/80948](10568/80948) ).
• **CGIAR** documented **Indigenous climate resilience** in Ecuadorian Amazonia, using participatory workshops to integrate traditional knowledge into adaptive planning (Source: [10568/105603](10568/105603) ).
Ecuador.Mitigation:
• **CGIAR** analyzed **hydrologic impacts of afforestation/reforestation under CDM-AR**, quantifying runoff reductions (54–100%) in Ecuador’s semi-arid tropics and grassland conversions (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agee.2008.01.015](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agee.2008.01.015) ).
• **CGIAR researchers** evaluated **REDD+ policy dilemmas**, exposing trade-offs between environmental goals and bureaucratic priorities in Ecuador’s incentive-based forest conservation programs (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3390/f9110725](https://doi.org/10.3390/f9110725) ).
• **CGIAR** synthesized **evidence on agroecology’s mitigation potential**, combining quantitative GHG assessments with stakeholder perceptions in Latin America (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1080/21683565.2023.2273835](https://doi.org/10.1080/21683565.2023.2273835) ).
• **CGIAR** identified **research gaps in forest-based mitigation**, highlighting limited policy focus on cross-sectoral approaches in rural development (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3390/su9101831](https://doi.org/10.3390/su9101831) ).
Ecuador.Water: No evidence found in this sample.

Egypt.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-resilient agriculture, gender-inclusive adaptation, and policy-informed mitigation** in **Egypt** through **multi-disciplinary research and technological innovation**. Specifically:
- **CGIAR’s Climate Change, Agriculture, and Food Security (CCAFS) program** designed **AI-driven policy frameworks** to balance economic, social, and environmental goals in Egypt’s agriculture sector (Source: [Mitigation]).
- **CGIAR’s Gender and Resilience teams** analyzed **gender disparities in climate service access** and proposed **ICT-based solutions** to enhance women’s inclusion in rural climate adaptation (Source: [Adaptation]).
- **CGIAR’s Crop Science and Genomics teams** advanced **CRISPR-based genome editing** to develop **climate-resilient crops** with enhanced stress tolerance (Source: [Adaptation]).
Egypt.Adaptation:
• **CGIAR’s CCAFS program** demonstrated that **urgent adaptation investments in Egypt’s agriculture sector** are critical to counteract **biophysical yield declines (10%+ by 2050)** due to temperature and water stress, particularly for maize, sugar crops, and fruits/vegetables (Source: [https://doi.org/10.2499/p15738coll2.134318](https://doi.org/10.2499/p15738coll2.134318) and [https://doi.org/10.2499/p15738coll2.134321](https://doi.org/10.2499/p15738coll2.134321) ).

• **CGIAR’s Gender, Agriculture, and Nutrition teams** identified **gender-based barriers** (e.g., ICT access, socio-cultural norms) limiting women’s participation in climate services and recommended **inclusive communication channels** and **women’s groups** to improve climate information uptake (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1080/17565529.2019.1613216](https://doi.org/10.1080/17565529.2019.1613216)).

• **CGIAR’s Crop Science Division** explored **CRISPR/Cas genome editing** to enhance **stress tolerance mechanisms** in crops, aiming to improve yield and quality under climate change (Source: [https://doi.org/10.4324/9781003382102-5](https://doi.org/10.4324/9781003382102-5)).

• **CGIAR’s Resilience Program** evaluated **climate-resilient crop traits, water management, and soil fertility improvements** as critical adaptation strategies for **fruits/vegetables, potatoes, rice, and wheat**, while acknowledging that **high-water-consuming cereals (e.g., maize)** may require global cooperation to offset productivity losses (Source: [https://doi.org/10.2499/p15738coll2.134321](https://doi.org/10.2499/p15738coll2.134321)).

• **CGIAR’s Water Productivity team** conducted **water profitability analyses** in the **Nile Delta**, identifying **policy levers** (e.g., subsidized inputs, land consolidation) to improve **water and land productivity** for climate-resilient food security (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3390/cli10020017](https://doi.org/10.3390/cli10020017)).

• **CGIAR’s Aquatic Sciences teams** developed an **ingredient database for sustainable local fish feeds** (e.g., black soldier fly larvae, cassava peel) to support **resilient aquaculture systems** in Egypt (Source: [doi:10.7910/DVN/Y1HVFF](doi:10.7910/DVN/Y1HVFF)).
Egypt.Mitigation:
• **CGIAR’s Policy Research team** introduced a **multi-objective, AI-driven policy framework** to identify **efficient portfolios** balancing **poverty reduction, economic growth, and climate change mitigation** in Egypt, addressing trade-offs in national economic policies (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1038/s41467-022-32415-9](https://doi.org/10.1038/s41467-022-32415-9)).

• **CGIAR’s Global Change Program** emphasized the need for **global cooperation on greenhouse gas mitigation** to reduce Egypt’s vulnerability to **climate-induced food price shocks** and **import disruptions** (Source: [https://doi.org/10.2499/p15738coll2.134318](https://doi.org/10.2499/p15738coll2.134318)).
Egypt.Water:
• **CGIAR’s Water Management teams** assessed **water productivity and profitability** in the **Nile Delta**, highlighting the need for **integrated water-land policies** to enhance resilience under climate change (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3390/cli10020017](https://doi.org/10.3390/cli10020017)).

• **CGIAR’s CCAFS program** recommended **shifting toward imported, low-water cereals** (e.g., maize) as a **water-saving adaptation strategy**, while investing in **water-efficient technologies** for high-value crops (Source: [https://doi.org/10.2499/p15738coll2.134321](https://doi.org/10.2499/p15738coll2.134321)).

El Salvador.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-smart adaptation** in **El Salvador** through **vulnerability assessments, agroecological modeling, and policy frameworks** to safeguard smallholder livelihoods and ecosystems. Specifically:
- **CIAT/CGIAR** developed **regional climate impact models** to project crop yield losses and ecosystem service degradation for smallholders (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-016-1867-y](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-016-1867-y)).
- **CIP/World Agroforestry Centre** assessed **coffee and cocoa agroforestry vulnerability**, proposing **tree species diversification** to mitigate abiotic stress under climate change (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-019-45491-7](https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-019-45491-7)).
El Salvador.Adaptation:
• **CGIAR (CIAT/CIP)** modeled **climate suitability shifts for coffee, cocoa, and agroforestry trees**, identifying **El Salvador as the most vulnerable** to Arabica coffee loss and recommending **species transformation** in agroforestry systems (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-019-45491-7](https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-019-45491-7)).

• **CGIAR (CIAT)** quantified **smallholder vulnerability** using **IPCC frameworks**, mapping **adaptive capacity gradients** (higher near urban areas, lower in drought-prone regions) and prioritizing **innovation support** for subsistence farmers (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-016-1792-0](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-016-1792-0)).

• **CGIAR (CIP/World Agroforestry Centre)** coupled **pollinator and coffee suitability models**, revealing **73–88% coffee habitat loss by 2050** and proposing **shade adjustment/crop rotation** to align pollinator availability with climate-resilient zones (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.1617940114](https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.1617940114)).

• **CGIAR (CIAT)** simulated **drybean yield declines (10–38%)** in El Salvador’s Lake Nicaragua corridor, classifying **high-impact spots** and recommending **crop diversification** with external support (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1007/s11027-015-9696-2](https://doi.org/10.1007/s11027-015-9696-2)).

• **CGIAR (CAC/SECAC)** led the **formulation of the *Estrategia Agricultura Sostenible Adaptada al Clima (EASAC)***, a **regional policy framework** for climate-adapted agriculture, integrating **multi-stakeholder participation** (Source: [10568/89328](10568/89328)).

• **CGIAR (CIAT)** developed an **integrated vulnerability framework** for coffee farmers, identifying **flexible adaptation strategies** and **government/institutional support gaps** in El Salvador (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0088463](https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0088463)).

• **CGIAR (Bioversity/CIAT)** evaluated **biological corridors** for plant dispersal in fragmented landscapes, highlighting **altitudinal gradients** as critical for **protected area connectivity** under climate change (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1002/ece3.672](https://doi.org/10.1002/ece3.672)).
El Salvador.Mitigation: No evidence found in this sample.
El Salvador.Water: No evidence found in this sample.

Equatorial Guinea.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-smart agriculture and adaptation strategies** in **sub-Saharan Africa (including Equatorial Guinea’s regional context)** through **resilience-focused research and policy-relevant interventions**. Specifically:
- **CGIAR’s Climate Change, Agriculture and Food Security (CCAFS) program** synthesized **climate-smart agriculture (CSA) strategies** for crop, livestock, and fishery sectors, tailoring adaptation policies to regional farming systems (Source: [10.1186/s40066-016-0075-3](https://doi.org/10.1186/s40066-016-0075-3)).
- **The Center for International Forestry Research (CIFOR) and Bioversity** mapped **vulnerability of priority tree species** to climate and anthropogenic threats, guiding **conservation and restoration** in Central Africa (Source: [10.1016/j.biocon.2022.109554](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.biocon.2022.109554)).
Equatorial Guinea.Adaptation:
• **CGIAR’s CCAFS program** analyzed **climate change impacts on farming systems** (crop, livestock, fishery) and designed **adaptation strategies** to improve resilience, including policy frameworks for climate-smart agriculture in West/Central Africa (Source: [10.1186/s40066-016-0075-3](https://doi.org/10.1186/s40066-016-0075-3)).

• **CGIAR researchers** evaluated **trade-offs and synergies in smallholder adaptation strategies** (e.g., crop/livestock/water management), identifying low-cost interventions to enhance **productivity, yield stability, and sustainability** (Source: [10.1016/j.regsus.2021.05.002](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.regsus.2021.05.002)).

• **CGIAR’s International Maize and Wheat Improvement Center (CIMMYT)** assessed **maize yield reductions under climate change** (2050/2080) and proposed **adapted varieties and management measures** for sub-Saharan Africa’s maize mega-environments (Source: [10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005](https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005)).

• **CGIAR’s modeling teams** compared **adaptation scenarios** (e.g., doubling irrigated area vs. yield increases) using **water-agriculture-general equilibrium models**, concluding that **productivity gains** outperform irrigation expansion for climate resilience (Source: [10.1016/j.ecolecon.2013.05.006](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ecolecon.2013.05.006)).
Equatorial Guinea.Mitigation: No evidence found in this sample.
Equatorial Guinea.Water:
• **CGIAR’s water-agriculture research** integrated **water simulation modules** into economic models to evaluate **irrigation expansion and yield improvements** as climate adaptation tools, though no direct Equatorial Guinea-specific studies were identified (Source: [10.1016/j.ecolecon.2013.05.006](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ecolecon.2013.05.006)).

• **CGIAR’s CCAFS program** addressed **water management strategies** as part of broader climate adaptation frameworks for smallholder farmers (Source: [10.1016/j.regsus.2021.05.002](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.regsus.2021.05.002)).

Eritrea.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate resilience and agricultural adaptation** in **Eritrea** through **bioeconomic modeling, climate-smart agriculture (CSA), and regional policy integration**. Specifically:
- **CIP (Maize Program)** calibrated bioeconomic models to assess climate change impacts on maize yields under varying nitrogen levels and adaptation scenarios (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005](https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005)).
- **ILRI (Livestock & Pastoralism)** advanced climate-smart livestock systems and regional adaptation partnerships in the IGAD region (Source: [10568/128053](10568/128053)).
- **ASARECA/CGIAR** reviewed National Adaptation Plans (NAPAs) and integrated climate adaptation into national development strategies for Eritrea (Source: [10568/33403](10568/33403)).
Eritrea.Adaptation:
• **CIP (Maize Program)** modeled biophysical and socioeconomic impacts of climate change on maize production, identifying yield reductions under medium/high-input intensification and regional variability (e.g., Western vs. Eastern Africa) to inform adaptation strategies (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005](https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005)).

• **ILRI** facilitated research-for-development partnerships to support climate adaptation in livestock and pastoral systems across the IGAD region, including Eritrea (Source: [10568/128053](10568/128053)).

• **CGIAR/ASARECA** assessed climate adaptation strategies in National Adaptation Plans (NAPAs) for Eritrea, focusing on vulnerable populations and integration into Poverty Reduction Strategy Programs (PRSPs) (Source: [10568/33403](10568/33403)).

• **IFPRI** analyzed climate vulnerabilities in East Africa, including Eritrea, and proposed resilience-building measures through scenario modeling and policy recommendations (Source: [https://doi.org/10.2499/9780896292055](https://doi.org/10.2499/9780896292055)).

• **CGIAR** reviewed climate adaptation trade-offs/synergies for smallholder farmers, categorizing strategies (e.g., water/soil management) and recommending low-cost, complementary approaches (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.regsus.2021.05.002](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.regsus.2021.05.002)).

• **CGIAR** linked climate change to migration risks in East Africa, highlighting drought-induced vulnerabilities and youth outmigration trends (Source: [10568/110278](10568/110278)).

• **UNHCR/CGIAR** contributed to the **Regional Climate Action Plan (2023–2028)** for the East/Horn of Africa, aligning adaptation measures with displaced populations’ needs (Source: [10568/137370](10568/137370)).
Eritrea.Mitigation: • **CGIAR** reviewed greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions in East African agricultural systems, including Eritrea’s livestock production, and promoted climate-smart agriculture (CSA) as a mitigation strategy (Source: [10568/106995](10568/106995)).
Eritrea.Water: No evidence found in this sample.

Estonia.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-smart agricultural adaptation** in **Estonia** through **crop resilience and digital innovation**. Specifically:
- **CGIAR’s Climate Change, Agriculture and Food Security (CCAFS) program** modeled climate change impacts on protein-rich crop suitability, identifying regional shifts in suitability (e.g., northern Europe benefiting from expanded quinoa production) and recommending targeted breeding for abiotic stress tolerance in faba beans, lentils, and chickpeas (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eja.2019.125974](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eja.2019.125974)).
- **CGIAR’s research** leveraged the **IMPACT model** to evaluate adaptation strategies (e.g., R&D for climate-resilient crop varieties and irrigation technology upgrades) while quantifying investment needs for OECD-aligned resilience (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en](https://doi.org/10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en)).
Estonia.Adaptation:
• **CGIAR’s CCAFS program** analyzed climate projections for 13 protein-rich crops across Europe, demonstrating heterogeneous suitability shifts (e.g., northern Europe becoming more favorable for quinoa) and recommending **breeding programs** to enhance abiotic stress tolerance in faba beans, lentils, and chickpeas to align with future climate conditions (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eja.2019.125974](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eja.2019.125974)).

• **CGIAR researchers** used the **IMPACT model** to assess adaptation strategies, including **R&D for climate-resilient crop varieties** and **irrigation technology improvements**, while estimating public/private investment requirements for OECD countries (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en](https://doi.org/10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en)).

• **CGIAR’s digital agriculture initiatives** (e.g., **MyFarmTrees** and **AI-supported advisory services**) were highlighted as scalable solutions for **climate resilience and on-farm restoration**, though Estonia-specific applications were not detailed in this sample (Source: [10568/148916](10568/148916)).
Estonia.Mitigation: No evidence found in this sample.
Estonia.Water: No evidence found in this sample.

Eswatini.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-resilient agriculture and food security** in **Eswatini** through **bioeconomic modeling, adaptation strategies, and yield stabilization research**. Specifically:
- **CIMMYT (Maize Program)** calibrated bioeconomic models to project maize yield declines under climate change and evaluated adaptation measures for future climate scenarios (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005](https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005)).
- **CIAT & IFPRI** analyzed trade-offs and synergies in smallholder climate adaptation strategies, emphasizing low-cost, complementary approaches to enhance resilience (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.regsus.2021.05.002](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.regsus.2021.05.002)).
Eswatini.Adaptation:
• **CIMMYT** assessed biophysical and socioeconomic impacts of climate change on maize yields in Eswatini (Southern Africa) under varying nitrogen levels and emission scenarios, identifying region-specific vulnerabilities and adaptation pathways (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005](https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005)).

• **CIAT & IFPRI** conducted a systematic review of climate adaptation strategies for smallholder farmers, categorizing crop/soil/water/livestock management interventions and quantifying trade-offs (e.g., labor costs) and synergies (e.g., yield stability) (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.regsus.2021.05.002](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.regsus.2021.05.002)).

• **IFPRI** modeled two adaptation scenarios for Southern Africa (including Eswatini): (1) doubling irrigated area, and (2) increasing rainfed/irrigated yields by 25%, concluding productivity gains outperform irrigation expansion (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ecolecon.2013.05.006](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ecolecon.2013.05.006)).

• **FutureClimate & CIMMYT** projected median maize yield declines of **9.2%** and **15.6%** (5th percentile) in Southern Africa by the 2060s, with low-frequency drought events occurring every **3.5 years**, recommending investments to offset production shocks (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3389/fclim.2022.787582](https://doi.org/10.3389/fclim.2022.787582)).

• **IFPRI** published a comprehensive analysis of Eswatini’s agricultural climate risks, linking adaptation to food security and sustainability (Source: [10568/154090](10568/154090)).
Eswatini.Mitigation: No evidence found in this sample.
Eswatini.Water:
• **IFPRI** integrated a **water simulation module** into general equilibrium models (GTAP-W) to evaluate economy-wide impacts of climate change on agriculture, testing irrigation expansion scenarios for Southern Africa (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ecolecon.2013.05.006](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ecolecon.2013.05.006)).

• **CIMMYT** examined climate change impacts on maize production under **three nitrogen levels**, indirectly addressing water-use efficiency in high-input systems (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005](https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005)).

Ethiopia.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-resilient agricultural systems** in **Ethiopia** through evidence-based adaptation strategies, climate-smart innovations, and participatory variety management. Specifically:
- **CGIAR’s Climate Change, Agriculture and Food Security (CCAFS) program** analyzed farmer perceptions and adaptation decisions to climate variability, identifying key socio-economic and environmental determinants (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1017/s0021859610000687](https://doi.org/10.1017/s0021859610000687)).
- **The Center** evaluated climate-smart agriculture (CSA) practices like minimum tillage and cereal-legume intercropping, demonstrating their poverty-reduction potential in rainfall-stressed regions (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1111/ajae.12161](https://doi.org/10.1111/ajae.12161)).
Ethiopia.Adaptation:
• **CGIAR’s CCAFS and International Livestock Research Institute (ILRI)** examined how smallholder farmers in the **Nile Basin** perceive climate change and adapt through livestock ownership, extension services, and credit access, with adaptation influenced by education, gender, and agro-ecological factors (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1017/s0021859610000687](https://doi.org/10.1017/s0021859610000687)).

• **CGIAR’s East Africa regional team** identified adaptation strategies (e.g., soil/water conservation, crop-livestock integration, tree planting) among **Central Rift Valley farmers**, with adaptation decisions shaped by education, extension contact, and climate information access (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1186/s40066-017-0100-1](https://doi.org/10.1186/s40066-017-0100-1)).

• **CGIAR’s CCAFS and CIMMYT** demonstrated that **climate-smart agriculture (CSA) practices**—such as minimum tillage and cereal-legume intercropping—reduce poverty and mitigate rainfall stress in Ethiopia, while cautioning against overgeneralizing CSA benefits (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1111/ajae.12161](https://doi.org/10.1111/ajae.12161)).

• **CGIAR’s International Center for Tropical Agriculture (CIAT) and partners** assessed farmer attitudes toward adaptation in **Borana, Ethiopia**, revealing preferences for crop diversification and planting adjustments over soil/water management, with barriers including food insecurity and liquidity constraints (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.crm.2017.03.001](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.crm.2017.03.001)).

• **CGIAR’s CGIAR Climate** explored **citizen science-driven variety management** for climate adaptation, using farmer-led trials in Ethiopia to improve wheat and bean variety recommendations based on climatic responses (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.1813720116](https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.1813720116)).

• **CGIAR’s International Food Policy Research Institute (IFPRI)** projected **climate suitability shifts for Arabica coffee** in Ethiopia, highlighting potential gains in highland regions despite global declines in lower-altitude suitability (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0124155](https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0124155)).

• **CGIAR’s CCAFS** evaluated **adaptation and mitigation practices** in smallholder farms, noting limited adoption of reduced tillage and manure application despite co-benefits for soil conservation (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1080/14693062.2022.2028597](https://doi.org/10.1080/14693062.2022.2028597)).

• **CGIAR’s CIMMYT and partners** assessed **cultivar adaptation potential** for rainfed cereals (maize, wheat, sorghum) in Ethiopia, finding that better-adapted varieties could offset climate-induced yield declines by 2050–2090 (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eja.2024.127137](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eja.2024.127137)).
Ethiopia.Mitigation: • **CGIAR’s CCAFS** investigated **GHG emissions from mitigation practices** (e.g., reduced tillage, manure use) in Ethiopian cereal crops, identifying soil conservation co-benefits but noting data gaps for accurate emission estimates (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1080/14693062.2022.2028597](https://doi.org/10.1080/14693062.2022.2028597)).
Ethiopia.Water: No evidence found in this sample.

Fiji.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate change adaptation in Fiji’s fisheries and food systems** through multi-disciplinary research on resilience, livelihood strategies, and policy frameworks. Specifically:
- The Center assessed **economic impacts of climate change on fisheries** and modeled adaptation strategies like aquaculture expansion and marine protected areas (MPAs) to offset declining coastal catches (Source: [10.1016/j.marpol.2015.12.023](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.marpol.2015.12.023)).
- Researchers developed **supply-demand models** to project fish demand growth and production constraints under climate change, highlighting the need for scaled-up natural resource management (NRM) (Source: [10.1016/j.marpol.2015.12.011](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.marpol.2015.12.011)).
Fiji.Adaptation:
• CGIAR evaluated **climate change adaptation strategies** for Fiji’s fisheries sector, demonstrating that **aquaculture development** and **locally managed marine areas (LMMAs)** could mitigate production losses but require significant scaling to reverse declining trends (Source: [10.1016/j.marpol.2015.12.023](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.marpol.2015.12.022)).

• The Center analyzed **baseline scenarios** (without adaptation) for Pacific fisheries, projecting Fiji’s transition to a **net fish importer** by 2050 due to climate-induced production stagnation (Source: [10.1016/j.marpol.2015.12.011](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.marpol.2015.12.011)).

• Researchers documented **climate vulnerability in coastal communities**, emphasizing the need for **national policies** to preserve marine biodiversity and livelihoods amid rising sea levels and cyclones (Source: [10.1016/j.marpol.2015.12.022](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.marpol.2015.12.022)).

• CGIAR explored **resilience-building scenarios** for Pacific food systems, including fisheries and forests, to guide policy for **adaptation among fishers and farmers** (Source: [10568/75610](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.marpol.2015.12.023)).

• The Center conducted **cross-cultural climate impact studies** among Fiji’s *iTaukei* communities, revealing high consistency in local reports of climate change effects on **pastoralism and crop production**, critical for tailored adaptation planning (Source: [10.1038/s44168-024-00124-2](https://doi.org/10.1038/s44168-024-00124-2)).

• Researchers highlighted **limited adaptive capacity** in Fiji’s agriculture-dependent livelihoods, warning of severe food security risks from **extreme weather events** (Source: [10568/145066](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.marpol.2015.12.022)).
Fiji.Mitigation: No evidence found in this sample.
Fiji.Water: No evidence found in this sample.

Finland.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate resilience and food systems innovation** in **Finland** through indigenous knowledge integration and cross-sectoral adaptation strategies. Specifically:
- CGIAR documented **indigenous territorial management techniques** of the **Inari Sámi** to preserve biodiversity and climate-resilient food systems (Source: [https://doi.org/10.4060/cb5131en](https://doi.org/10.4060/cb5131en)).
- The Center analyzed **climate-proofing agricultural production** via modeling adaptation measures (e.g., new crop varieties, irrigation tech) to enhance food security (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en](https://doi.org/10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en)).
Finland.Adaptation:
• CGIAR highlighted **indigenous food systems (Inari Sámi)** as models for climate-resilient territorial management, emphasizing unique practices at risk of disappearing (Source: [https://doi.org/10.4060/cb5131en](https://doi.org/10.4060/cb5131en) ).

• The Center modeled **adaptation strategies** (e.g., R&D for climate-adapted crop varieties, irrigation tech upgrades) to mitigate climate risks in agriculture, estimating investment needs for OECD-aligned policies (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en](https://doi.org/10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en) ).

• CGIAR surveyed **European forest owners/managers** to assess perceptions of **forest genetic resources (FGR) management** as a tool for climate adaptation, identifying barriers to non-local planting material adoption (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1007/s10342-020-01311-6](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10342-020-01311-6) ).

• Researchers projected **geographic shifts in protein-rich crop suitability** under climate change (e.g., quinoa expansion in northern Europe, chickpea/lentil declines in southern Europe), recommending policy shifts to support resilience (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eja.2019.125974](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eja.2019.125974) ).
Finland.Mitigation:
• CGIAR standardized **eddy covariance data processing** for CO₂/energy flux monitoring at **ICOS ecosystem stations** (including Finland) to improve comparability and reliability of greenhouse gas observations (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1515/intag-2017-0043](https://doi.org/10.1515/intag-2017-0043) ).

• The Center contributed to the **BIODEV Programme** (aligned with Finland’s development priorities), aiming to bridge **global knowledge gaps** on linking mitigation and adaptation through biocarbon approaches in West Africa—*though no direct Finnish mitigation outputs were identified in this sample* (Sources: [doi:10.34725/DVN/ZDW61D](doi:10.34725/DVN/ZDW61D), [doi:10.34725/DVN/K44TXF](doi:10.34725/DVN/K44TXF), [doi:10.34725/DVN/BWGWTR](doi:10.34725/DVN/BWGWTR), [doi:10.34725/DVN/DZQ3AQ](doi:10.34725/DVN/DZQ3AQ), [doi:10.34725/DVN/LNYOZP](doi:10.34725/DVN/LNYOZP) ).
Finland.Water: No evidence found in this sample.

France.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-smart agriculture, resilience, and GHG monitoring** in **France** through targeted research and policy-relevant innovations. Specifically:
- **CGIAR’s Climate Change, Agriculture, and Food Security (CCAFS) program** developed **climate-resilient crop varieties and irrigation technologies** to offset heat stress and yield stagnation in maize production (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1111/gcb.12069](https://doi.org/10.1111/gcb.12069)).
- **Bioversity International** and **CIMMYT** assessed **adaptive strategies for protein-rich crops** (e.g., faba beans, lentils) and forest genetic resources (FGR) to enhance resilience in southern Europe (Sources: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eja.2019.125974](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eja.2019.125974), [https://doi.org/10.1007/s10342-020-01311-6](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10342-020-01311-6)).
- **ICOS (Integrated Carbon Observation System) and CGIAR’s climate modeling teams** standardized **GHG flux monitoring** to improve comparability of CO₂ and energy flux data across European ecosystems (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1515/intag-2017-0043](https://doi.org/10.1515/intag-2017-0043)).
France.Adaptation:
• **CGIAR-CCAFS** quantified **heat stress impacts on French maize yields** and projected that **12% yield technology improvements** are needed by 2035 to offset climate-induced stagnation, using climate model simulations calibrated with historical data (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1111/gcb.12069](https://doi.org/10.1111/gcb.12069)).

• **Bioversity International** analyzed **climate suitability shifts for protein-rich crops** in Europe, revealing **declining suitability for chickpeas/lentils in southern France** and expanded potential for quinoa, recommending **breeding programs to enhance abiotic stress tolerance** (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eja.2019.125974](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eja.2019.125974)).
