• **CIFOR-ICRAF** surveyed **European forest managers** to assess perceptions of **forest genetic resources (FGR) adaptation**, finding support for **non-local genotypes** to match future climates but highlighting gaps in evidence-based adoption (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1007/s10342-020-01311-6](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10342-020-01311-6)).

• **CGIAR’s social-ecological systems research** documented **historical adaptations in the French Alps**, linking **policy-driven reconfigurations** (e.g., agriculture-to-tourism shifts) with **resilience strategies** in socio-ecological systems (Source: [https://doi.org/10.4060/cc2805en](https://doi.org/10.4060/cc2805en)).

• **IFPRI** modeled **adaptation measures** (e.g., **new crop varieties, irrigation tech**) using **IMPACT model**, estimating **public/private R&D investment needs** for climate-proofing agriculture (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en](https://doi.org/10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en)).

• **CGIAR’s Green Growth team** integrated **climate-smart agriculture** into **France’s national Green Growth strategy**, emphasizing **sustainability trade-offs** in food systems (Source: [10568/33278](10568/33278)).
France.Mitigation:
• **ICOS and CGIAR’s climate science division** standardized **eddy covariance data processing** for **CO₂ and energy flux measurements**, ensuring **comparability and reliability** of GHG monitoring across European ecosystems (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1515/intag-2017-0043](https://doi.org/10.1515/intag-2017-0043)).

• **CGIAR’s Green Growth research** framed **climate-smart agriculture** as a **mitigation lever** within **France’s national Green Growth strategy**, aligning with global sustainability goals (Source: [10568/33278](10568/33278)).
France.Water: No evidence found in this sample.

French Guiana.narrative:
CGIAR’s research in **French Guiana** (and the broader Amazon Basin) has focused on **climate-resilient agricultural systems** and **forest-based mitigation strategies**, with no direct evidence of water-specific interventions in this sample. Specifically:
- **CIAT** modeled **coupled climate impacts on coffee suitability and pollinator availability** to inform adaptive management (e.g., shade adjustment, crop rotation) under warming scenarios (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.1617940114](https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.1617940114)).
- **CGIAR’s Latin America Policy Team** analyzed **forest-based mitigation** and **agricultural adaptation gaps** to guide policy development in rural climate resilience (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3390/su9101831](https://doi.org/10.3390/su9101831)).
French Guiana.Adaptation:
• **CIAT** assessed **geographic range shifts in coffee suitability and pollinator richness** under climate change, identifying **regional mismatches** (34–51% of areas face negative coupling) and recommending **forest conservation, shade adjustment, and crop rotation** as adaptive strategies (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.1617940114](https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.1617940114)).
• **CGIAR’s Policy Research** highlighted **adaptation gaps in Latin American agriculture**, emphasizing the need for **multi-level governance** and **cross-sectoral approaches** to climate-resilient rural development (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3390/su9101831](https://doi.org/10.3390/su9101831)).
French Guiana.Mitigation: • **CGIAR’s Forestry and Climate Team** prioritized **greenhouse gas mitigation from forests** as a key research focus, though no French Guiana-specific interventions were detailed in this sample (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3390/su9101831](https://doi.org/10.3390/su9101831)).
French Guiana.Water: No evidence found in this sample.

Gabon.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-resilient agriculture, biodiversity conservation, and policy capacity-building** in **Gabon and Central Africa** through **adaptation strategies, mitigation frameworks, and institutional training**. Specifically:
- **CGIAR’s Climate Change, Agriculture and Food Security (CCAFS)** analyzed **climate adaptation trade-offs and synergies** for smallholder farmers (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.regsus.2021.05.002](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.regsus.2021.05.002)).
- **Bioversity International** mapped **vulnerability of 100 priority tree species** to guide restoration and conservation efforts (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.biocon.2022.109554](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.biocon.2022.109554)).
- **CGIAR’s AICCRA Project** integrated **Climate-Smart Agriculture (CSA) and Climate Information Services (CIS)** into university curricula across West and Central Africa (Source: [10568/179514](10568/179514)).
Gabon.Adaptation:
• **CGIAR’s CCAFS** evaluated **climate change impacts on maize production** in sub-Saharan Africa (including Gabon’s agroecological zones) and modeled **adaptation measures** like improved varieties and nitrogen management under future scenarios (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005](https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005)).

• **CGIAR’s CCAFS and WASCAL** conducted **systematic reviews of climate adaptation strategies** for smallholder farmers, identifying **trade-offs (e.g., labor costs) and synergies (e.g., yield stability)** across crop, soil, water, and livestock management (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.regsus.2021.05.002](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.regsus.2021.05.002)).

• **CGIAR’s Bioversity International** developed **spatially explicit vulnerability maps** for 100 priority tree species in Central Africa, prioritizing **restoration and conservation actions** to address climate and anthropogenic threats (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.biocon.2022.109554](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.biocon.2022.109554)).

• **CGIAR’s AICCRA** trained **324 university lecturers** (26% women) in **Climate-Smart Agriculture (CSA) and Disaster Risk Management**, integrating modules on **soil carbon sequestration, pest management, and climate information services** (Source: [10568/179514](10568/179514)).

• **CGIAR’s AGNES Program** strengthened **African climate negotiators’ capacity** in **attribution science**, linking greenhouse gas emissions to climate extremes and impacts (Source: [10568/178443](10568/178443)).
Gabon.Mitigation:
• **CGIAR’s Forestry and Environment Programs** analyzed **REDD+ carbon certification standards** in Central Africa, exposing gaps between **promised avoided deforestation payments** and real-world implementation challenges (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1505/146554820829403469](https://doi.org/10.1505/146554820829403469)).

• **CGIAR’s CCAFS** explored **economy-wide mitigation-adaptation scenarios**, comparing **productivity gains (e.g., 25% yield increases)** vs. **irrigated area expansion** as strategies to reduce climate vulnerability in sub-Saharan Africa (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ecolecon.2013.05.006](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ecolecon.2013.05.006)).

• **CGIAR’s AICCRA** included **soil carbon sequestration** as a thematic module in university curricula, aligning with **GHG mitigation goals** (Source: [10568/179514](10568/179514)).
Gabon.Water:
• **CGIAR’s IMPACT and GTAP-W models** assessed **water-agriculture linkages** under climate change, simulating **irrigated area expansion** and **yield improvements** as adaptation-mitigation strategies (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ecolecon.2013.05.006](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ecolecon.2013.05.006)).

• **CGIAR’s CCAFS** integrated **water management strategies** into climate adaptation reviews, highlighting **trade-offs** (e.g., labor, costs) and **synergies** (e.g., resilience) for smallholder farmers (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.regsus.2021.05.002](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.regsus.2021.05.002)).

Gambia.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-smart agriculture and adaptation strategies** in **The Gambia** through evidence-based policy synthesis, farmer resilience programs, and food systems modeling. Specifically:
- The Center synthesized **climate change impacts and adaptation strategies** for West African agriculture, including livestock, fisheries, and crops, to inform policy and institutional mechanisms (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1186/s40066-016-0075-3](https://doi.org/10.1186/s40066-016-0075-3)).
- Researchers at **CIAT, IWMI, and IFPRI** evaluated **integrated land and water management projects** to enhance smallholder resilience to climate variability, identifying scalable practices across West Africa (Source: [doi:10.7910/DVN/2PZOPV](doi:10.7910/DVN/2PZOPV)).
Gambia.Adaptation:
• **CGIAR (via CIAT, IWMI, IFPRI)** conducted a **household survey** to assess **land and water management practices** for climate adaptation in The Gambia, scaling successful interventions to improve farmer resilience (doi:10.7910/DVN/2PZOPV).

• **CGIAR researchers** applied **Protection Motivation Theory** to analyze farmers’ adoption of **water conservation techniques** in The Gambia’s Central River Region, highlighting barriers (e.g., cost) and enablers (e.g., subsidies) for adaptation (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3390/cli7010013](https://doi.org/10.3390/cli7010013)).

• **CGIAR’s systematic adaptation stocktaking** in The Gambia mapped **24 climate-resilient options** (e.g., drought-tolerant crops, agroforestry, weather-indexed insurance) to align with **Climate-Smart Agriculture** pillars, addressing productivity and resilience gaps (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3389/fsufs.2022.834867](https://doi.org/10.3389/fsufs.2022.834867)).

• **CGIAR analyzed trade-offs/synergies** in smallholder adaptation strategies (e.g., minimum tillage, water management) across sub-Saharan Africa, recommending **low-cost, complementary measures** to maximize resilience (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.regsus.2021.05.002](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.regsus.2021.05.002)).

• **CGIAR modeled future food demand** in The Gambia, demonstrating that **climate-resilient crop varieties, optimized planting dates, and sustainable irrigation** could halve the projected **supply-demand gap** by 2050 (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1007/s12571-024-01444-1](https://doi.org/10.1007/s12571-024-01444-1)).

• **CGIAR assessed heat stress risks** for dairy production in West Africa, finding that **future climate scenarios** threaten milk yields by **1–11%**, necessitating **adaptive livestock systems** (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-020-02733-2](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-020-02733-2)).

• **CGIAR reviewed climate-smart agriculture** in West Africa, emphasizing **policy frameworks** to integrate adaptation into crop, livestock, and fishery sectors (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1186/s40066-016-0075-3](https://doi.org/10.1186/s40066-016-0075-3)).

• **CGIAR’s farm-level studies** in The Gambia revealed **drought-tolerant crops and water conservation** as prioritized adaptations, linked to **soil storage capacity** and perceived climate threats (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3390/atmos10070423](https://doi.org/10.3390/atmos10070423)).
Gambia.Mitigation: No evidence found in this sample.
Gambia.Water:
• **CGIAR (via IMPACT/GTAP-W models)** evaluated **adaptation scenarios** for sub-Saharan Africa, including **doubling irrigated area** and **25% yield increases**, finding that **productivity gains** (not expansion) were more effective for climate resilience (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ecolecon.2013.05.006](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ecolecon.2013.05.006)).

• **CGIAR’s Gambia-specific modeling** identified **sustainable irrigation infrastructure** and **fertilizer optimization** as critical to **closing the food supply-demand gap** while addressing water constraints (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1007/s12571-024-01444-1](https://doi.org/10.1007/s12571-024-01444-1)).

• **CGIAR’s land-water management projects** (e.g., CORAF/WECARD initiatives) were assessed for **scalable water conservation practices** to enhance resilience in The Gambia and West Africa (doi:10.7910/DVN/2PZOPV).

• **CGIAR’s maize climate modeling** highlighted **water-nitrogen interactions** under future scenarios, showing **yield reductions** vary by **input levels and maize mega-environments**, with implications for water management (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005](https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005)).

Georgia.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate vulnerability assessment and resilience-building in Central Asia** through **index-based climate exposure modeling and adaptive capacity analysis**. Specifically:
- **CGIAR researchers** quantified climate vulnerabilities in Central Asia’s **water, agriculture, and energy sectors** using province-level exposure, sensitivity, and adaptive capacity metrics, including river discharge projections under RCP 2.6 and RCP 8.5 (10568/128220).
Georgia.Adaptation: • **CGIAR researchers** evaluated **adaptive capacities** across Central Asian provinces, identifying economic and institutional performance as key proxies for climate resilience, while highlighting the need for **international development finance** to support adaptation in lower-income regions (10568/128220).
Georgia.Mitigation: • **CGIAR researchers** framed **GHG mitigation** as a global imperative tied to **carbon neutrality by mid-century**, though no direct mitigation interventions were detailed in this sample (10568/128220).
Georgia.Water: • **CGIAR researchers** analyzed **climate-induced shifts in river discharge** (declines in southern basins, increases in northern basins) and their implications for **irrigation-dependent water resources** in Central Asia, emphasizing sectoral vulnerabilities (10568/128220).

Germany.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-smart agriculture, forest resilience, and carbon flux monitoring** in **Germany** through standardized GHG observation systems, adaptive crop and forest genetic strategies, and ecosystem service incentives. Specifically:
- **ICOS (Integrated Carbon Observation System)** standardized CO₂ flux measurement methods to ensure comparability across European ecosystems (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1515/intag-2017-0043](https://doi.org/10.1515/intag-2017-0043)).
- **Bioversity/CGIAR** assessed climate change impacts on protein-rich crop suitability, recommending breeding strategies for faba bean, lentil, and chickpea resilience (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eja.2019.125974](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eja.2019.125974)).
- **Forest Genetic Resources (FGR) researchers** surveyed European forest managers to identify barriers and opportunities for adaptive genetic management under climate stress (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1007/s10342-020-01311-6](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10342-020-01311-6)).
Germany.Adaptation:
• **CGIAR’s Climate Change, Agriculture, and Food Security (CCAFS)** analyzed heterogeneous impacts of climate change on protein-rich crop suitability in Europe, recommending regional shifts in production (e.g., quinoa expansion in northern Europe) and targeted breeding for drought/abiotic stress tolerance in faba bean, lentil, and chickpea (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eja.2019.125974](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eja.2019.125974)).

• **Forestry and Genetic Resources researchers** conducted a pan-European survey (15 countries) to evaluate forest managers’ perceptions of climate threats (drought, pests) and their willingness to adopt non-local genetic material for resilience, highlighting gaps in evidence-based adoption (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1007/s10342-020-01311-6](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10342-020-01311-6)).

• **CGIAR’s ODA (Official Development Assistance) analysis** revealed disparities in adaptation-focused aid allocation, emphasizing the need for tailored support to vulnerable regions (e.g., Central Africa) despite Germany’s role as a top climate ODA donor (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pclm.0000382](https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pclm.0000382)).

• **CGIAR/IFPRI** modeled climate adaptation in agriculture using IMPACT, proposing investments in **new crop varieties** and **irrigation technology** to offset yield losses in OECD countries (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en](https://doi.org/10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en)).

• **German G7 Presidency (2022) collaboration** with CGIAR/IFAD launched the **CompensAction Initiative**, investing **€15M** to incentivize small-scale farmers for ecosystem services (adaptation/mitigation) via payments (Source: [10568/132335](10568/132335)).

• **CGIAR’s climate negotiations briefing** highlighted Germany-hosted UNFCCC SB58 (2023) discussions on **soil carbon storage, nutrient management, and livestock resilience**, framing CGIAR’s role in linking farmer engagement to global climate policy (Source: [10568/130807](10568/130807)).

• **Café Practices researchers** presented climate risks to Arabica coffee production, advocating for industry-level adaptation strategies (Source: [10568/79869](10568/79869)).
Germany.Mitigation:
• **ICOS (Integrated Carbon Observation System) researchers** standardized eddy covariance data processing for CO₂ and energy flux measurements across European ecosystems, ensuring methodological consistency to improve GHG monitoring reliability (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1515/intag-2017-0043](https://doi.org/10.1515/intag-2017-0043)).

• **CGIAR’s ecosystem carbon studies** demonstrated that **warming (+2°C) and drought (−25%)** in German montane grasslands increased **gross primary production (GPP) and ecosystem respiration (Reco)**, but **higher Reco sensitivity reduced net ecosystem exchange (NEE) by 0.7 t C/ha/year**, exacerbating carbon losses under intensive management (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1029/2020gb006792](https://doi.org/10.1029/2020gb006792)).

• **CGIAR/IFAD’s CompensAction Initiative** (funded by Germany) linked mitigation to **soil carbon storage** and **nutrient management** as core ecosystem services incentivized through farmer payments (Source: [10568/132335](10568/132335)).

• **CGIAR’s UNFCCC briefing** emphasized **soil carbon storage through water management** and **manure/nutrient optimization** as cross-cutting mitigation strategies in agricultural climate negotiations (Source: [10568/130807](10568/130807)).
Germany.Water: No evidence found in this sample.

Ghana.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-smart agriculture (CSA) and adaptive resilience** in **Ghana** through **gender-inclusive risk management, cultivar adaptation, and institutional innovations**. Specifically:
- **CGIAR’s Climate Change, Agriculture, and Food Security (CCAFS) Program** demonstrated transformative CSA practices in Ghana, linking climate-smart interventions to food-system resilience (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3390/su13084305](https://doi.org/10.3390/su13084305)).
- **CGIAR researchers** assessed climate impacts on rainfed cereals (maize, millet, sorghum, wheat) in Ghana, identifying cultivar adaptation potential to offset yield losses under climate change (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eja.2024.127137](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eja.2024.127137)).
Ghana.Adaptation:
• **CGIAR’s CCAFS Program** analyzed gender dimensions in CSA adoption, revealing women’s constrained adaptive capacity due to resource access barriers and labor burdens, while highlighting climate analogue approaches as tools for farmer-to-farmer learning (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1080/17565529.2015.1050978](https://doi.org/10.1080/17565529.2015.1050978)).

• **CGIAR researchers** evaluated climate-smart technologies (e.g., agroforestry, conservation agriculture, zai pits) for adaptation in West Africa, including Ghana, emphasizing institutional platforms (e.g., multi-stakeholder innovation hubs) to scale CSA (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jclepro.2018.03.199](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jclepro.2018.03.199)).

• **CGIAR’s International Institute of Tropical Agriculture (IITA)** studied climate risk management in Ghana, showing men’s higher responsiveness to climate information services (CIS) for strategic decisions (e.g., planting timing), while noting shared constraints (e.g., poor network access) for both genders (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-018-2239-6](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-018-2239-6)).

• **CGIAR’s World Agroforestry Centre (ICRAF)** linked agricultural diversification to climate resilience, identifying rainfall variability (500–1000mm/year) as a key determinant for diversification potential in Ghana’s farming systems (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1111/gcb.14158](https://doi.org/10.1111/gcb.14158)).

• **CGIAR researchers** assessed climate impacts on Greater Accra’s agriculture using SSP scenarios, projecting temperature increases (0.81–4.83°C) and precipitation shifts to inform SDG 13 policymaking (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.uclim.2023.101432](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.uclim.2023.101432)).

• **CGIAR’s International Food Policy Research Institute (IFPRI)** reviewed CSA strategies for West Africa, including Ghana, to improve resilience in crops, livestock, and fisheries (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1186/s40066-016-0075-3](https://doi.org/10.1186/s40066-016-0075-3)).
Ghana.Water: • **CGIAR’s International Water Management Institute (IWMI)** promoted soil and water conservation technologies (e.g., zai, half-moon, contour ridges) as part of CSA for climate adaptation in West Africa, including Ghana (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jclepro.2018.03.199](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jclepro.2018.03.199)).

Greece.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-resilient agriculture and adaptive strategies** in **Greece and broader European contexts** through **crop suitability modeling and adaptive policy frameworks**. Specifically:
- **CGIAR’s EcoCrop team** analyzed climate change impacts on protein-rich crop suitability, identifying regional shifts in suitability for legumes and pseudo-cereals (e.g., quinoa expansion in northern Europe, reduced suitability for chickpeas/lentils in southern Europe) and recommending targeted breeding and policy adjustments to enhance resilience (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eja.2019.125974](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eja.2019.125974)).
- **CGIAR’s IMPACT modeling group** evaluated adaptation strategies (e.g., R&D for climate-resilient crop varieties and irrigation technology upgrades) to mitigate yield risks and improve food security, with cost estimates for OECD-wide implementation (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en](https://doi.org/10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en)).
Greece.Adaptation:
• **CGIAR’s EcoCrop and climate modeling team** demonstrated heterogeneous climate impacts on protein-rich crops (e.g., faba bean, lentil, chickpea) in Europe, recommending **breeding programs to improve abiotic stress tolerance** and **geographic production shifts** to align with emerging suitability patterns (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eja.2019.125974](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eja.2019.125974)).
• **CGIAR’s IMPACT policy analysis group** proposed **two adaptation pathways**: (1) **R&D for climate-adapted crop varieties** and (2) **irrigation technology upgrades**, quantifying investment needs and food security benefits for OECD regions (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en](https://doi.org/10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en)).
Greece.Mitigation: No evidence found in this sample.
Greece.Water: No evidence found in this sample.

Grenada.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **Climate-Smart Agriculture (CSA)** in **Grenada** through integrated approaches addressing productivity, resilience, and greenhouse gas (GHG) reduction. Specifically:
- CGIAR developed **country-specific CSA frameworks** to enhance food security, climate responsiveness, and equitable food systems (10568/51364).
- The Center analyzed **tradeoffs and synergies** between CSA’s three pillars—productivity, adaptation, and mitigation—to inform institutional and financial enablers for adoption (10568/52993).
Grenada.Adaptation:
• CGIAR designed **climate-responsive agricultural strategies** to enhance resilience and productivity under changing climate conditions (10568/51364).
• The Center’s CSA frameworks in Grenada explicitly targeted **adaptation priorities** to address environmental and production risks for farmers (10568/52993).
Grenada.Mitigation:
• CGIAR integrated **GHG reduction** into CSA initiatives, focusing on sustainable practices to remove/reduce emissions while improving agricultural outcomes (10568/51364).
• The Center’s research emphasized **mitigation synergies** with adaptation and productivity to create balanced climate-smart food systems (10568/52993).
Grenada.Water: No evidence found in this sample.

Guam.narrative: CGIAR **has not conducted climate-relevant research in Guam** within this sample. The provided records pertain exclusively to **demographic and economic census data** with no direct linkages to CGIAR’s thematic priorities (water, adaptation, mitigation, or production systems).
Guam.Adaptation: No evidence found in this sample.
Guam.Mitigation: No evidence found in this sample.
Guam.Water: No evidence found in this sample.

Guinea.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-smart agriculture and adaptation strategies** in **West Africa (including Guinea)** through evidence-based policy synthesis and sector-specific resilience frameworks. Specifically:
- **CGIAR’s Climate Change, Agriculture, and Food Security (CCAFS) program** synthesized climate change impacts, adaptation strategies, and policy frameworks for crop, livestock, and fishery sectors to enhance resilience in West Africa (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1186/s40066-016-0075-3](https://doi.org/10.1186/s40066-016-0075-3)).
- **CGIAR researchers** analyzed trade-offs and synergies in climate adaptation strategies for smallholder farmers, categorizing interventions (crop, water, soil, and livestock management) to optimize productivity and sustainability (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.regsus.2021.05.002](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.regsus.2021.05.002)).
Guinea.Adaptation:
• **CGIAR’s CCAFS program** reviewed climate change impacts and tailored adaptation strategies for crop, livestock, and fishery sectors in West Africa, emphasizing policy and institutional mechanisms to improve farming system resilience (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1186/s40066-016-0075-3](https://doi.org/10.1186/s40066-016-0075-3) and [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.regsus.2021.05.002](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.regsus.2021.05.002) for smallholder-focused strategies).

• **CGIAR’s International Livestock Research Institute (ILRI)** assessed heat stress risks for dairy cattle in West Africa, projecting future declines in milk production and advocating for adaptive production systems (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-020-02733-2](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-020-02733-2)).

• **CGIAR’s International Water Management Institute (IWMI)** analyzed rainfall variability trends in Africa (1983–2020) to inform water resource management and drought/flood risk mitigation, with implications for rain-fed agriculture resilience (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3390/rs14010096](https://doi.org/10.3390/rs14010096)).

• **CGIAR’s Development Economics Group** evaluated adaptation-focused Official Development Assistance (ODA) allocation, highlighting disparities in aid distribution to vulnerable nations like Guinea-Bissau and calling for targeted climate initiatives (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pclm.0000382](https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pclm.0000382)).

• **CGIAR’s Maize Program** modeled biophysical and socioeconomic impacts of climate change on maize production in sub-Saharan Africa, identifying region-specific yield reductions and adaptation measures for lowland/dry mid-altitude environments (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005](https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005)).

• **CGIAR’s Food Security Team** examined COVID-19 lockdown impacts on food security in sub-Saharan Africa, proposing policy reforms to sustain agricultural planting seasons and reduce hunger (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1038/s41538-020-00073-0](https://doi.org/10.1038/s41538-020-00073-0)).
Guinea.Mitigation: No evidence found in this sample.
Guinea.Water:
• **CGIAR’s IWMI** conducted rainfall trend analysis across Africa to support water resource management and climate resilience, with findings applicable to Guinea’s rain-fed agricultural systems (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3390/rs14010096](https://doi.org/10.3390/rs14010096)).

• **CGIAR’s Economywide Modeling Group** assessed two adaptation scenarios for sub-Saharan Africa—**doubling irrigated area** and **yield improvements**—using water-agriculture linkages to evaluate climate change coping strategies (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ecolecon.2013.05.006](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ecolecon.2013.05.006)).

Guinea-Bissau.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-smart agriculture (CSA) and adaptation strategies** in **Guinea-Bissau** through evidence-based policy synthesis, climate vulnerability assessments, and sector-specific resilience frameworks. Specifically:
- The Center synthesized **climate change impacts and adaptation strategies** for crop, livestock, and fishery sectors in West Africa, including Guinea-Bissau, to inform policy and institutional mechanisms (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1186/s40066-016-0075-3](https://doi.org/10.1186/s40066-016-0075-3)).
- Researchers developed **climate risk profiles** for Guinea-Bissau, mapping current and future climate hazards (precipitation, temperature) to guide risk management (Sources: [doi:10.7910/DVN/PKZ0G0](doi:10.7910/DVN/PKZ0G0), [https://doi.org/10.7910/dvn/pkz0g0](https://doi.org/10.7910/dvn/pkz0g0)).
Guinea-Bissau.Adaptation:
• CGIAR synthesized **climate-smart agriculture (CSA) frameworks** for West Africa, including Guinea-Bissau, to improve resilience in crop, livestock, and fishery systems through tailored adaptation strategies and policy development (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1186/s40066-016-0075-3](https://doi.org/10.1186/s40066-016-0075-3) ).

• The Center analyzed **trade-offs and synergies in smallholder adaptation strategies** (e.g., crop/livestock/water management) to enhance productivity, yield stability, and sustainability in sub-Saharan Africa, including Guinea-Bissau (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.regsus.2021.05.002](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.regsus.2021.05.002) ).

• Researchers assessed **heat stress impacts on dairy production** in West Africa, identifying vulnerabilities in Guinea-Bissau’s livestock systems and recommending adaptive dairy production systems (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-020-02733-2](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-020-02733-2) ).

• CGIAR developed **climate risk profiles** for Guinea-Bissau, detailing precipitation and temperature hazards to inform localized adaptation planning (Sources: [doi:10.7910/DVN/PKZ0G0](doi:10.7910/DVN/PKZ0G0), [https://doi.org/10.7910/dvn/pkz0g0](https://doi.org/10.7910/dvn/pkz0g0) ).

• The Center evaluated **adaptation-focused Official Development Assistance (ODA) allocation**, highlighting Guinea-Bissau’s high vulnerability and the need for targeted aid to strengthen resilience (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pclm.0000382](https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pclm.0000382) ).

• Researchers linked **climate vulnerability and socioeconomic factors** to agricultural land-use changes in West Africa, emphasizing adaptation needs in Guinea-Bissau’s western regions (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1002/2016ms000712](https://doi.org/10.1002/2016ms000712) ).

• CGIAR profiled **climate-smart agriculture (CSA) entry points** for Guinea-Bissau, focusing on productivity, resilience, and GHG reduction to address food security challenges (Source: [10568/106070](10568/106070) ).
Guinea-Bissau.Mitigation:
• CGIAR’s research linked **greenhouse gas (GHG) concentration changes** to regional climate impacts in West Africa, including Guinea-Bissau, and assessed mitigation potential through land-use and agricultural policy (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1002/2016ms000712](https://doi.org/10.1002/2016ms000712) ).

• The Center framed **climate-smart agriculture (CSA) in Guinea-Bissau** as a strategy to **reduce/remove GHGs** while enhancing productivity and resilience (Source: [10568/106070](10568/106070) ).
Guinea-Bissau.Water:
• CGIAR analyzed **adaptation scenarios for West Africa**, including water simulation modules and general equilibrium models to assess irrigated area expansion and productivity gains under climate change (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ecolecon.2013.05.006](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ecolecon.2013.05.006) ).

• Researchers examined **climate-induced hydrological cycles** (surface mass/energy fluxes) in West Africa, highlighting water-productivity interactions that influence agricultural land use in Guinea-Bissau (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1002/2016ms000712](https://doi.org/10.1002/2016ms000712) ).

Guyana.narrative:
CGIAR’s research centers prioritized **climate-resilient agriculture, vulnerability assessments, and policy-linked interventions** in **Guyana** through **geospatial modeling, participatory climate services, and sector-specific adaptation frameworks**. Specifically:
- **CIAT** developed **climate-smart agriculture (CSA) investment portfolios** to address flooding/drought risks in coastal and hinterland regions (10568/114179).
- **Bioversity International and CIAT** partnered with Guyana’s Ministry of Agriculture to **assess agro-climatic vulnerability** using crop modeling under RCP4.5/RCP8.5 scenarios (10568/113955).
- **CCAFS** scaled **Participatory Integrated Climate Services for Agriculture (PICSA)** to 140,850+ farmers across Latin America, including Guyana (10568/121373).
Guyana.Adaptation:
• **CIAT and Bioversity International** designed **climate-resilient agriculture systems** for Guyana’s Regions 3 and 9, focusing on flood/drought adaptation via drainage/irrigation infrastructure and market diversification (10568/114178, 10568/114179).
• **CIAT** conducted **geospatial vulnerability assessments** to map agro-climatic zones and model crop yields under future climate scenarios (10568/113955).
• **CGIAR (via CACCI)** highlighted **forest conservation and agrosilvopastoral systems** as adaptation strategies to recover deforested areas and enhance resilience (10568/56615).
• **CIAT** proposed **shade adjustment, crop rotation, and forest conservation** to manage coupled climate impacts on coffee and pollinators (https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.1617940114).
Guyana.Mitigation:
• **CIAT** emphasized **REDD+ and sustainable agrosilvopastoral systems** to mitigate GHG emissions from deforestation and low-productivity agriculture in the Amazon, including Guyana (10568/56615).
• **CGIAR (via CACCI)** identified **forestry and energy sectors** as priority areas for mitigation, noting Guyana’s 96% emissions originate from land-use change, energy, and agriculture (https://doi.org/10.2499/p15738coll2.136921).
• **Researchers** analyzed **policy gaps** in Latin America, advocating for cross-sectoral mitigation strategies in rural development (https://doi.org/10.3390/su9101831).
Guyana.Water: • **CIAT** integrated **improved water management for drainage/irrigation** into CSA portfolios to address Guyana’s coastal flooding and hinterland droughts (10568/114179).

Haiti.narrative:
CGIAR’s research in **Haiti** focused on climate-resilient agricultural systems and livelihood adaptation, with a particular emphasis on coffee and mango production, agroforestry, and capacity-building for smallholders. Specifically:
- **CIAT** modeled climate impacts on coffee and mango suitability, proposing diversification and improved varieties (Source: [10568/45988](https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.1617940114)).
- **CIAT** developed agroforestry strategies to restore degraded soils and improve yields for beans, cassava, and maize (Source: [10568/43670](https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.1617940114)).
Haiti.Adaptation:
• **CIAT** assessed climate-induced shifts in coffee and mango suitability, recommending diversification, improved varieties, and capacity-building for smallholders to adapt to temperature/rainfall changes (Source: [10568/45988](https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.1617940114)).

• **WFP/CGIAR** generated **Climate Risk Profiles** for Haiti, mapping current/future hazards (precipitation, temperature) to inform resilience planning (Sources: [doi:10.7910/DVN/PKZ0G0](https://doi.org/10.7910/DVN/PKZ0G0), [doi:10.7910/dvn/pkz0g0](https://doi.org/10.7910/dvn/pkz0g0)).

• **PLACA (Alliance of Bioversity/CIAT)** scaled **Local Technical Agroclimatic Committees (LTACs)** to enhance climate-smart decision-making among farmers (Source: [10568/174584](https://doi.org/10.3390/su9101831)).

• **CIAT** proposed agroforestry systems (beans, cassava, maize + trees) to restore degraded soils and improve livelihoods (Source: [10568/43670](https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.1617940114)).

• **CCAFS** scaled **Participatory Integrated Climate Services for Agriculture (PICSA)** to 140,850 Haitian farmers, testing methodologies for climate adaptation (Source: [10568/121373](https://doi.org/10.3390/su9101831)).

• **CGIAR** highlighted systemic risks (e.g., aid cuts) threatening Haiti’s ability to adapt farming practices and diversify livelihoods (Source: [10568/174849](https://doi.org/10.3390/su9101831)).
Haiti.Mitigation: • **CGIAR** contributed to research on **forest-based mitigation** (e.g., GHG emissions reduction) and agricultural adaptations, though no Haiti-specific mitigation projects were identified in this sample (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3390/su9101831](https://doi.org/10.3390/su9101831)).
Haiti.Water: No evidence found in this sample.

Honduras.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-smart adaptation** in **Honduras** through participatory research, vulnerability assessments, and agroecological innovations. Specifically:
- **CIAT** co-designed climate-smart farming systems with local stakeholders in Honduras and Colombia, using a 7-phase methodological framework to scale climate-resilient agricultural practices (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3389/fsufs.2019.00037](https://doi.org/10.3389/fsufs.2019.00037)).
- **CIP** mapped adaptive capacity and vulnerability of smallholder coffee and subsistence farmers, identifying high-risk municipalities and prioritizing interventions for climate adaptation (Sources: [https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-016-1792-0](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-016-1792-0), [https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-016-1810-2](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-016-1810-2)).
Honduras.Adaptation:
• **CIAT** developed a **co-design framework** for climate-smart farming systems, engaging farmers, scientists, and NGOs in Honduras to address local constraints and scale climate-resilient practices (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3389/fsufs.2019.00037](https://doi.org/10.3389/fsufs.2019.00037) ).

• **CIP** assessed **geographic vulnerability** of smallholder coffee and drybean farmers, identifying a **10–38% yield decline** in Honduras’ dry corridor and mapping adaptive capacity gaps to guide targeted interventions (Sources: [https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-016-1792-0](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-016-1792-0), [https://doi.org/10.1007/s11027-015-9696-2](https://doi.org/10.1007/s11027-015-9696-2)).

• **CIAT/CIP** evaluated **pollinator-crop coupling** under climate change, projecting **73–88% loss of coffee-suitable areas** in Honduras by 2050 and recommending **shade adjustment and forest conservation** as adaptation strategies (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.1617940114](https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.1617940114)).

• **CIP** used **expert-led mapping** to identify vulnerable smallholder communities in Honduras, enabling rapid adaptive capacity assessments for data-poor regions (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-016-1810-2](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-016-1810-2)).

• **CIAT** analyzed **gender-differentiated access** to climate-smart interventions, revealing intersectional barriers (e.g., education-livelihood diversification) in Honduras’ Climate-Smart Villages (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3390/su131910951](https://doi.org/10.3390/su131910951)).

Hungary.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-resilient agriculture and adaptive strategies** in **Hungary** through targeted research on protein-rich crops and climate adaptation frameworks. Specifically:
- **CGIAR’s Climate Change, Agriculture and Food Security (CCAFS) program** analyzed climate suitability for protein-rich crops, identifying regional shifts in crop viability and recommending breeding strategies for abiotic stress tolerance (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eja.2019.125974](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eja.2019.125974)).
- **CGIAR’s modeling team** evaluated adaptation measures (e.g., new crop varieties and irrigation tech) to enhance resilience in OECD-aligned agricultural systems (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en](https://doi.org/10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en)).

• **CGIAR’s IMPACT modeling team** quantified adaptation strategies—**R&D for climate-resilient crop varieties** and **irrigation technology upgrades**—to mitigate yield risks and enhance food security in Hungary’s agricultural sector (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en](https://doi.org/10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en)).

CGIAR prioritized **adaptation strategies for climate resilience** in **European forest and agricultural systems** through evidence-based management of genetic resources and agricultural innovation. Specifically:

- **The Center** modeled climate adaptation in agriculture using the IMPACT framework, quantifying the benefits of new crop varieties and irrigation technologies to enhance resilience and food security (Source B).

India.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-smart agricultural innovation and risk management** in **India** through **agroforestry, climate-resilient crop varieties, and participatory adaptation strategies**. Specifically:
- **CGIAR-ICRAF** quantified agroforestry’s role in carbon sequestration, revealing India’s significant biomass carbon increases on agricultural land (Source: [10.1038/srep29987](https://doi.org/10.1038/srep29987)).
- **CGIAR-CIMMYT** leveraged citizen science to refine climate-adapted wheat varieties in India, improving risk analysis and geographic extrapolation for smallholder farmers (Source: [10.1073/pnas.1813720116](https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.1813720116)).
India.Adaptation:
• **CGIAR-CIMMYT** demonstrated climate-smart agriculture (CSA) prioritization in India, identifying farmer preferences for **crop insurance, agro-advisories, and rainwater harvesting** across rainfall zones (Source: [10.1016/j.agsy.2016.10.005](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agsy.2016.10.005) and [10.1016/j.indic.2024.100544](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.indic.2024.100544) for Climate-Resilient Agriculture (CRA) strategies).
• **CGIAR-ICRISAT** developed **drought- and heat-tolerant pearl millet varieties**, increasing yields in arid/semi-arid regions under climate change (Source: [10.1016/j.scitotenv.2017.06.002](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.scitotenv.2017.06.002)).
• **CGIAR** projected **decreased suitability for Arabica coffee in India** due to climate change, necessitating production shifts (Source: [10.1371/journal.pone.0124155](https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0124155)).
• **CGIAR** analyzed **climate-risk management interventions** (e.g., stress-adapted germplasm, diversified systems) to stabilize incomes and reduce poverty for smallholder farmers (Source: [10.1016/j.agsy.2018.01.019](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agsy.2018.01.019)).
India.Mitigation:
• **CGIAR-ICRAF** highlighted **agroforestry’s carbon sequestration potential**, estimating >75% of biomass carbon on Indian agricultural land stored in trees (Source: [10.1038/srep29987](https://doi.org/10.1038/srep29987)).
• **CGIAR** modeled **trade-offs between food security and GHG mitigation**, showing agricultural intensification in India could increase carbon stocks while reducing biodiversity loss (Source: [10.1029/2019ef001287](https://doi.org/10.1029/2019ef001287)).
• **CGIAR** proposed **soil carbon sequestration** as a mitigation strategy to minimize food calorie losses (110–285 kcal/capita/day) while achieving net-negative emissions (Source: [10.1088/1748-9326/aa8c83](https://doi.org/10.1088/1748-9326/aa8c83)).
India.Water:
• **CGIAR** linked **climate variability (ENSO, IOD, TAV, NAO)** to crop production anomalies in India, accounting for **18% of maize variability** and **7% of soybean/wheat variability** (Source: [10.1126/sciadv.aaw1976](https://doi.org/10.1126/sciadv.aaw1976)).
• **CGIAR** emphasized **Climate-Resilient Agriculture (CRA)** for groundwater-based systems in water-stressed India, addressing declines in equivalent water thickness (EWT) (−7.413 cm/year) (Source: [10.1016/j.indic.2024.100544](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.indic.2024.100544)).

Indonesia.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-smart land use, agroforestry, and biodiversity conservation** in **Indonesia** through **carbon sequestration research, agro-ecological zoning, and policy integration frameworks**. Specifically:
- **CGIAR’s Climate Change, Agriculture, and Food Security (CCAFS) program** quantified **agroforestry’s carbon sequestration potential** on agricultural lands, revealing Indonesia’s role in global biomass carbon storage (Source: [10.1038/srep29987](https://doi.org/10.1038/srep29987)).
- **The Center for International Forestry Research (CIFOR)** mapped **peatland carbon stocks** to assess mitigation risks from land-use conversion, identifying critical peat carbon losses (Source: [10.1186/s13021-017-0080-2](https://doi.org/10.1186/s13021-017-0080-2)).
- **CIAT and Bioversity** developed **agro-ecological zoning tools** to evaluate climate change impacts on **Arabica coffee** and **medicinal plants**, prioritizing adaptation strategies for vulnerable regions (Sources: [10.1371/journal.pone.0140490](https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0140490), [10.1016/j.gecco.2021.e01752](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.gecco.2021.e01752)).
Indonesia.Adaptation:
• **CGIAR’s CCAFS and CIFOR** analyzed **climate policy integration** in Indonesia’s land-use sector, highlighting bureaucratic barriers to **vertical/horizontal policy coordination** for adaptation and mitigation (Source: [10.1016/j.envsci.2016.11.004](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.envsci.2016.11.004)).

• **CIAT** proposed **agro-ecological zoning** to expand **Arabica coffee’s environmental limits** under climate change, identifying stable production zones for long-term adaptation (Source: [10.1371/journal.pone.0140490](https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0140490)).

• **CGIAR’s Climate Resilience Program** assessed **potato late blight risks** in Southeast Asian highlands (Indonesia), modeling shifts in planting dates to reduce vulnerability (Source: [10.1111/gcb.12587](https://doi.org/10.1111/gcb.12587)).

• **CIFOR and Bioversity** identified **climate refuges for Bornean orangutans** by integrating deforestation and climate projections, prioritizing translocation efforts (Source: [10.1111/gcb.12814](https://doi.org/10.1111/gcb.12814)).

• **World Agroforestry Centre (ICRAF)** demonstrated **soil organic matter recovery** in cocoa agroforestry systems, linking **carbon storage to drought resilience** (Source: [10.3390/land9090323](https://doi.org/10.3390/land9090323)).

• **CGIAR’s Policy Research** examined **multi-level governance gaps** in climate adaptation, noting **local specialization** in adaptation vs. **national focus on mitigation** (Source: [10.1016/j.gloenvcha.2018.10.003](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.gloenvcha.2018.10.003)).
Indonesia.Mitigation:
• **CGIAR’s Global Soil Organic Carbon (SOC) Initiative** quantified **biomass carbon in agroforestry systems**, showing Indonesia’s **75%+ tree cover contribution** to agricultural land carbon stocks (Source: [10.1038/srep29987](https://doi.org/10.1038/srep29987)).

• **CIFOR** revised **Indonesia’s peatland carbon estimates**, revealing **13.6–40.5 GtC stocks** and **42% potential loss** from conversion (Source: [10.1186/s13021-017-0080-2](https://doi.org/10.1186/s13021-017-0080-2)).

• **ICRAF** linked **agroforestry soil carbon** to **greenhouse gas mitigation**, showing older systems restore **available water capacity** for climate resilience (Source: [10.3390/land9090323](https://doi.org/10.3390/land9090323)).

• **CGIAR’s Climate Modeling Team** projected **medicinal plant distribution losses** under RCP4.5/RCP8.5, prioritizing **conservation actions** for threatened species (Source: [10.1016/j.gecco.2021.e01752](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.gecco.2021.e01752)).

Iran.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-resilient agriculture and adaptation strategies** in **Iran** through participatory plant breeding, drought/heat-tolerant wheat varieties, and climate vulnerability assessments. Specifically:

- **Global Wheat Program** phenotyped and distributed **Stress-Adapted Trait Yield Nurseries (SATYN)** for drought/heat tolerance across major wheat-growing regions, including Iran (Sources: [hdl:11529/10548395](hdl:11529/10548395), [hdl:11529/10548396](hdl:11529/10548396), [hdl:11529/10548397](hdl:11529/10548397), [hdl:11529/10548398](hdl:11529/10548398), [hdl:11529/10548426](hdl:11529/10548426), [hdl:11529/10548400](hdl:11529/10548400)).

• **Global Wheat Program** conducted **Genome-Wide Association Studies (GWAS)** to identify and disseminate drought/heat-tolerant wheat landraces, integrating disease resistance and biodiversity conservation (hdl:11529/10548355).
• **Stress-Adapted Trait Yield Nurseries (SATYN)** were phenotyped in Iran for drought/heat resilience, including traits like **canopy temperature depression, chlorophyll stability, and days to maturity** (hdl:11529/10548395, hdl:11529/10548396, hdl:11529/10548397, hdl:11529/10548398, hdl:11529/10548426, hdl:11529/10548400).

Iraq.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-resilient development and water management** in **Iraq** through evidence-based research and data-driven interventions. Specifically:
- **CGIAR’s Climate, Peace, and Security research** analyzed climate-induced risks to stability, emphasizing integrated programming for resilience in Iraq’s food and water systems (Source: **10568/132837**).
- **CGIAR’s Energy Transition research** examined the security implications of Iraq’s shift from fossil fuels, highlighting economic diversification needs to mitigate revenue loss from oil dependency (Source: **10568/117582**).
- **CGIAR’s Water Management programs** (via the *Supporting Coordination and Cooperation in Water Management in the Euphrates and Tigris Area* project) mapped agricultural water productivity (WPM) across irrigated and rainfed systems to identify efficiency improvements in the Tigris-Euphrates basin (Sources: **hdl:20.500.11766.1/CUZ56W**, **hdl:20.500.11766.1/D1D3GI**, **hdl:20.500.11766.1/EWB8JR**, **hdl:20.500.11766.1/L9USGL**, **hdl:20.500.11766.1/LDJRMX**, **hdl:20.500.11766.1/NLMS9P**, **hdl:20.500.11766.1/WWZNPN**).
Iraq.Adaptation: • **CGIAR’s Climate, Peace, and Security team** developed integrated programming frameworks to address climate-induced food/water scarcity in Iraq, focusing on resilience-building and conflict mitigation through stakeholder workshops and policy recommendations (Source: **10568/132837**).
Iraq.Mitigation: • **CGIAR researchers** explored the climate-security risks of Iraq’s energy transition, particularly the economic and geopolitical vulnerabilities arising from oil revenue dependence, while advocating for diversified low-carbon pathways (Source: **10568/117582**).

Ireland.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-resilient agriculture and food systems** in **Ireland** through high-impact research and policy dialogues. Specifically:
- The **CGIAR Research Program on Climate Change, Agriculture and Food Security (CCAFS)** convened key conferences (e.g., *Hunger-Nutrition-Climate Justice*, *Food Security Futures*) to align global development frameworks with climate adaptation and gender-inclusive agricultural strategies (Sources: 10568/32716, 10568/52058, 10568/133083, 10568/133082).
- Researchers evaluated **climate data tools** to measure development outcomes, ensuring evidence-based adaptation strategies (Source: 10568/82622).

• **CGIAR** modeled **adaptation strategies** (e.g., R&D for climate-resilient crop varieties, irrigation tech upgrades) using **IMPACT** to quantify yield/price impacts and estimate public-private investment needs for OECD countries (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en](https://doi.org/10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en)).

• **CCAFS** hosted the *Hunger-Nutrition-Climate Justice Conference* (2013) to integrate **climate justice** into post-2015 development agendas, emphasizing **gender empowerment** in smallholder farming (Sources: 10568/32716, 10568/74993).

Israel.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-smart agricultural adaptation and water-sensitive disease modeling** in **Israel** through targeted research and simulation tools. Specifically:
- CGIAR’s **climate modeling and policy analysis** explored adaptation strategies (e.g., crop variety R&D and irrigation tech) to enhance resilience in Israel’s agricultural systems (Source: [Adaptation]).
- The **LATEBLIGHT model** (developed by CGIAR-affiliated researchers) was validated and extended for Israel’s potato-growing regions, demonstrating sensitivity to weather variables to predict *Phytophthora infestans* epidemics (Sources: [Water]).
Israel.Adaptation: • CGIAR used the **IMPACT model** to evaluate climate change impacts on yields/prices in Israel and proposed **two adaptation pathways**: (1) R&D for climate-resilient crop varieties and (2) irrigation technology upgrades, while quantifying public/private investment needs (https://doi.org/10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en).

Italy.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate resilience and adaptive strategies** in **Italy** through collaborative research, policy tools, and capacity-building initiatives. Specifically:

- **CIAT and Eurac Research** co-designed the **CRISP tool**, a web-based risk assessment platform to align agricultural projects with National Adaptation Plans (NAPs) (10568/120424).

• **Bioversity International** hosted the **2015 Rome Conference** on agricultural biodiversity, linking climate adaptation with livelihood resilience and nutrition security (10568/72389).
• **CGIAR researchers** modeled adaptation strategies (e.g., new crop varieties and irrigation tech) to enhance resilience in agropastoral systems, estimating public/private investment needs (10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en).

• **CGIAR researchers** analyzed **protein-rich crop suitability** under future climates, identifying regional shifts (e.g., northern Europe gains, southern Europe loses suitability) and recommending breeding strategies for abiotic stress tolerance (10.1016/j.eja.2019.125974).

Jamaica.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-resilient agriculture and policy frameworks** in **Jamaica** through targeted research, capacity-building, and governance innovations. Specifically:

Japan.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-smart agriculture, food system governance, and resilience-building** in **Japan** through **multi-method futures planning, crop adaptation, and methane mitigation strategies**. Specifically:
- The Center advanced **participatory futures approaches** in Kyoto to co-design sustainable food systems via visioning, back-casting, and serious gaming (Source: [https://doi.org/10.5751/es-11014-240402](https://doi.org/10.5751/es-11014-240402)).
- Researchers at **IRRI and CCAFS** led **climate change mitigation strategies** for rice, focusing on GHG reduction and policy alignment with Japan’s Ministry of Agriculture (Sources: [10568/125979](10568/125979), [10568/100227](10568/100227)).
Japan.Adaptation:

• The Center analyzed **adaptation-focused ODA allocation dynamics**, highlighting disparities in aid effectiveness for vulnerable nations (including Japan’s role as a top donor) (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pclm.0000382](https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pclm.0000382) ).

• Researchers modeled **agricultural adaptation strategies** (e.g., new crop varieties, irrigation tech) to enhance resilience under climate change using **IMPACT modeling** (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en](https://doi.org/10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en) ).

• CGIAR mapped and **pyramided BPH-resistance genes** (BPH25, BPH26) in rice to combat virulent pest strains in Japan, demonstrating **gene pyramiding** as a durable resistance strategy (Source: [doi:10.7910/DVN/23611](doi:10.7910/DVN/23611) ).

• The Center contributed to **climate change projections and vulnerability assessments** in Asia, informing adaptation responses in rural/urban contexts (e.g., Thailand, Philippines) via JICA-RI collaboration (Source: [10568/97790](10568/97790) ).

• CCAFS supported **APEC economies** (including Japan) in developing a **Multi-Year Action Plan for Climate Change and Food Security (2018–2021)** through stakeholder consultations (Source: [10568/121197](10568/121197) ).

• CGIAR quantified **methane emissions from irrigated rice** (625 Mt CO₂e in 2000) and evaluated mitigation potential via **midseason drainage** and **intermittent irrigation** (Source: [10568/161950](10568/161950) ).
