Jordan.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate resilience and adaptive solutions** in **Jordan** through **genetic innovation, social protection integration, and climate-smart policy frameworks**. Specifically:
- **Bioversity International** analyzed barriers to accessing genetic resources for climate-adaptive crop breeding, identifying administrative and technical challenges (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3390/plants9091129](https://doi.org/10.3390/plants9091129)).
- **CIAT (Alliance of Bioversity International and CIAT)** explored adaptive social protection mechanisms in Jordan, linking climate-smart practices with social inclusion (Source: [10568/135126](10568/135126)).
Jordan.Adaptation:
• **Bioversity International** examined public breeders’ capacity to integrate genetic diversity for climate-adaptive traits, highlighting administrative and technical barriers to germplasm access (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3390/plants9091129](https://doi.org/10.3390/plants9091129) ).

• **CGIAR FOCUS Climate Security (CIAT/HER+)** investigated adaptive social protection in Jordan, identifying gaps in coverage and siloed approaches while recommending climate vulnerability assessments and community-led resilience strategies (Source: [10568/135118](10568/135118)).

• **CGIAR** assessed climate-induced food/water scarcity risks in Jordan, emphasizing integrated programming to address inequalities and security risks (Source: [10568/132837](10568/132837)).

• **CGIAR** analyzed Jordan’s policy discourse on climate-mobility-conflict nexus, finding limited integration of refugee populations into adaptation frameworks (Source: [10568/144971](10568/144971)).
Jordan.Mitigation: No evidence found in this sample.
Jordan.Water:
• **CGIAR (Al Murunah Project)** developed high-resolution climate projections for Jordan’s Wadi Al-Seer basin, informing resilient nature-based water solutions (RNBWS) for drought and extreme weather adaptation (Source: [https://doi.org/10.5337/2025.204](https://doi.org/10.5337/2025.204)).

• **CGIAR (Al Murunah Project)** integrated climate downscaling data into water management strategies, emphasizing localized RNBWS for drought resilience (Source: [https://doi.org/10.5337/2025.208](https://doi.org/10.5337/2025.208)).

• **CGIAR** mapped climate security risks in Jordan’s water resources, focusing on groundwater depletion, evapotranspiration, and flash floods (Source: [10568/132897](10568/132897)).

• **CGIAR** modeled climate impacts on Jordan’s Zarqa River Basin using SWAT, assessing surface water availability and soil conservation interventions (Source: [10568/99742](10568/99742)).

Kazakhstan.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-smart agricultural resilience and low-emissions development in Kazakhstan** through integrated modeling, soil carbon innovation, and policy-informed programs. Specifically:
- CGIAR’s **Climate Change, Agriculture and Food Security (CCAFS)** advanced **low-emissions livestock and soil carbon monitoring** to underpin Kazakhstan’s USD 500 million Sustainable Livestock Development Program (Source: 10568/121203, 10568/124646).
- The Center’s **bio-economic modeling** (e.g., BEFM) assessed **sub-national climate adaptation strategies** for Kazakhstan’s heterogeneous farming systems, highlighting regional disparities in income volatility and water-dependent risks (Source: 10.1016/j.agee.2014.02.033).
Kazakhstan.Adaptation:
• CGIAR’s **bio-economic farm model (BEFM)** evaluated **adaptation options for Kazakhstan’s agricultural producers** under climate change, revealing divergent impacts—positive income gains in northern commercial farms but heightened risks in arid smallholder systems due to water scarcity (10.1016/j.agee.2014.02.033).
• Researchers advocated for **smallholder adaptation strategies** in Central Asia, emphasizing the need for interdisciplinary collaboration to address water scarcity-driven migration and climate vulnerability (10.1080/17565529.2024.2436090).
• CGIAR analyzed **climate vulnerabilities in Kazakhstan’s water-agriculture-energy nexus**, projecting **declining river discharge in southern basins** and quantifying adaptive capacities via province-level exposure and sensitivity indices (10568/128220).
Kazakhstan.Mitigation:
• CGIAR’s **CCAFS** provided technical guidance to the **World Bank and Asian Development Bank** on **soil organic carbon (SOC) monitoring**, enabling Kazakhstan’s USD 500 million **Sustainable Livestock Development Program** and low-emissions livestock investments (10568/121203, 10568/124646).
• The Center highlighted **cost-effective SOC sequestration** as a mitigation strategy, recommending standardized **MRV (Measurement, Reporting, Verification) protocols** to scale carbon assets in agricultural systems (10568/114846).
• Research underscored **GHG mitigation via agroforestry and best agronomic practices**, though institutional barriers limit current MRV inclusion in national climate strategies (10568/98831).
Kazakhstan.Water:
• CGIAR’s **bio-economic modeling** warned that **irrigation water scarcity** in Kazakhstan—exacerbated by upstream demand and climate change—could worsen agricultural losses, particularly in arid zones (10.1016/j.agee.2014.02.033).
• Researchers identified **water-agriculture-energy nexus vulnerabilities**, with projections of **reduced river discharge in southern Kazakhstan** under high-emission scenarios (10568/128220).
• The Center emphasized **interdisciplinary water research** to address mobility pressures from scarcity, advocating for improved data and institutional capacity (10.1080/17565529.2024.2436090).

Kenya.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-resilient agricultural systems** in **Kenya** through evidence-based adaptation strategies, climate-smart agriculture (CSA), and hydrological modeling. Specifically:
- **CGIAR’s Climate Change, Agriculture, and Food Security (CCAFS) Program** analyzed climate-smart agricultural practices (e.g., improved seed, conservation agriculture, and good agricultural practices) in Kenya’s sweet potato and soybean value chains, demonstrating their economic viability (e.g., NPV of US$28,044 for good agricultural practices in sweet potatoes) (Source: **https://doi.org/10.1016/j.heliyon.2022.e09228**).
- **CGIAR researchers** assessed the potential of **cultivar adaptation** to offset climate-induced yield declines (6% projected reduction in East and Southern Africa by 2050) for maize, millet, sorghum, and wheat, highlighting remaining regional adaptation gaps (Source: **https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eja.2024.127137**).
Kenya.Adaptation:
• **CGIAR’s CCAFS Program** demonstrated that smallholder farmers in Kenya prioritized **climate-smart agricultural practices** (e.g., improved seed, conservation agriculture) for their productivity and resilience benefits, with cost-benefit analyses validating their economic feasibility (Source: **https://doi.org/10.1016/j.heliyon.2022.e09228**).

• **CGIAR researchers** evaluated **climate-risk management** strategies (e.g., stress-adapted crop germplasm, diversified production systems) to stabilize incomes and reduce poverty risks for smallholder farmers, emphasizing institutional interventions like index-based insurance (Source: **https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agsy.2018.01.019**).

• **CGIAR’s International Center for Tropical Agriculture (CIAT)** projected **shifts in climatic suitability for Arabica coffee** in Kenya, identifying potential gains in higher-elevation regions due to climate change (Source: **https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0124155**).

• **CGIAR’s International Maize and Wheat Improvement Center (CIMMYT)** assessed **cultivar adaptation potential** for rainfed cereals in Kenya, showing that better-adapted varieties could partially compensate for climate-induced yield losses (Source: **https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eja.2024.127137**).

• **CGIAR’s CCAFS Program** analyzed **agricultural diversification** as a strategy to enhance food security in Kenya, linking farming diversity to resilience against rainfall variability (Source: **https://doi.org/10.1111/gcb.14158**).

• **CGIAR researchers** developed a **Climate-Smart Agriculture Prioritization Framework** to evaluate adaptation options (e.g., improved seed, conservation agriculture) in Kenya’s value chains, using cost-benefit tools to identify viable practices (Source: **https://doi.org/10.1016/j.heliyon.2022.e09228**).

• **CGIAR’s CCAFS Program** reviewed **transformative agriculture** approaches in Kenya, emphasizing climate-smart practices to build resilience under increasing climate risks (Source: **https://doi.org/10.3390/su13084305**).
Kenya.Mitigation: No evidence found in this sample.
Kenya.Water:
• **CGIAR researchers** evaluated **ERA5 reanalysis improvements** for precipitation data in East Africa, including Kenya, to enhance agricultural and water-resource modeling accuracy (Source: **https://doi.org/10.3390/atmos11090996**).

• **CGIAR’s CCAFS Program** analyzed **hydrological extremes** (droughts/floods) in Kenya, projecting increased precipitation (up to 35%) and streamflow changes, necessitating site-specific adaptation measures (Source: **https://doi.org/10.1029/2022ef003011**).

• **CGIAR researchers** established **data requirements for crop yield simulations** in Kenya, addressing climate and soil data gaps critical for yield-gap analysis and climate-resilient planning (Source: **https://doi.org/10.1016/j.fcr.2015.03.004**).

Kiribati.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate resilience and adaptation in Pacific Island food systems, including Kiribati**, through scenario-based policy insights. Specifically:
- The Center developed **four alternative future scenarios for Pacific food systems**—focusing on fisheries, trade, affordability, and public health—to inform climate adaptation strategies for fishers and farmers (Source: **10568/75610**).
Kiribati.Adaptation: • CGIAR developed **four alternative scenarios for Pacific food systems** to enhance resilience against climate change impacts (e.g., tropical cyclones, droughts) among fishers and farmers, providing policy-makers with tools to test adaptation strategies (10568/75610).
Kiribati.Mitigation: No evidence found in this sample.
Kiribati.Water: No evidence found in this sample.

Korea.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-smart agriculture, resilience, and mitigation strategies** in **Korea** through policy analysis, technology dissemination, and ecosystem-based solutions. Specifically:
- **CGIAR’s Climate Change, Agriculture and Food Security (CCAFS)** supported APEC economies—including Korea—in developing a **multi-year climate change and food security plan** (10568/121197).
- **CGIAR researchers** evaluated **climate adaptation strategies** (e.g., new crop varieties, irrigation tech) using the **IMPACT model** to assess food security and economic resilience (10568/146482).
- **CGIAR’s Green Growth strategies** were analyzed for Korea’s integration of **climate-smart agriculture** into national sustainability frameworks (10568/33278).
Korea.Adaptation:
• **CGIAR’s CCAFS** facilitated stakeholder consultations to develop a **Multi-Year Action Plan for Climate Change and Food Security (2018–2021)** for APEC economies, including Korea (10568/121197).
• **CGIAR researchers** assessed Korea’s **climate change adaptation strategies** (e.g., agricultural extension, tech dissemination) to strengthen food system resilience and farmer incomes (https://doi.org/10.2499/p15738coll2.133382).
• **CGIAR analyzed adaptation measures** (e.g., new crop varieties, irrigation tech) via the **IMPACT model**, projecting food security trends and investment needs in Korea (https://doi.org/10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en).
• **CGIAR’s integrated assessment** projected Korea’s **food production and import dependence** under climate shocks, emphasizing adoption of climate-smart practices (10568/146482).
Korea.Mitigation:
• **CGIAR researchers** evaluated **mangrove and semi-mangrove species** (e.g., *Hibiscus hamabo*) for **carbon absorption potential** in Korea’s coastal areas to support net-zero goals (https://doi.org/10.5194/egusphere-egu23-11394).
• **CGIAR’s Green Growth analysis** highlighted Korea’s **climate-smart agriculture policies** as critical for sustainability, aligning with global mitigation objectives (10568/33278).
Korea.Water: No evidence found in this sample.

Kyrgyzstan.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-resilient water management, adaptation strategies, and mitigation co-benefits** in **Kyrgyzstan** through interdisciplinary research. Specifically:
- **CGIAR’s Water and Adaptation teams** analyzed climate vulnerabilities in Central Asia’s water-agriculture-energy nexus, quantifying river discharge projections under RCP scenarios and assessing adaptive capacities at provincial levels (10568/128220).
- **Bioversity International** conducted genetic studies on *Juglans regia* (walnut) populations to support biodiversity conservation in wild and cultivated ecosystems (doi:10.7910/DVN/VUP7OH).
- **CGIAR’s Climate Change and Adaptation researchers** examined transboundary water cooperation in the **Khojabakirgansai Basin**, documenting community-led adaptation to droughts and flash floods (10568/34723).
Kyrgyzstan.Adaptation:
• **CGIAR’s Climate Resilience team** explored smallholder adaptation strategies and climate-mobility nexus in rural Kyrgyzstan, emphasizing interdisciplinary collaboration to address water scarcity and extreme events (https://doi.org/10.1080/17565529.2024.2436090).
• **CGIAR’s Water and Agriculture Nexus researchers** assessed climate adaptation and mitigation trade-offs, highlighting provincial disparities in adaptive capacity (10568/128220).
• **ILRI (International Livestock Research Institute)** documented pastoralist women’s rangeland restoration efforts in Kyrgyzstan as a climate-adaptive livelihood strategy (10568/128162).
Kyrgyzstan.Mitigation: • **CGIAR’s Mitigation and Development team** analyzed the co-benefits of smallholder adaptation initiatives for reducing greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions in agriculture, aligning with IPCC’s AFOLU mitigation frameworks (10568/71042).
Kyrgyzstan.Water:
• **CGIAR’s Water Security researchers** evaluated climate vulnerabilities in Central Asia’s water sectors, projecting river discharge declines in southern basins and increased demand in irrigated agriculture (10568/128220).
• **CGIAR’s Soil and Water Management team** simulated crop productivity in irrigated areas (1951–2050) to inform sustainable soil-crop-climate modeling (hdl:20.500.11766.1/FK2/HMKJR8).
• **CGIAR’s Transboundary Water Cooperation unit** studied community-based water management in the **Khojabakirgansai Basin**, addressing inefficiencies in canal infrastructure and flash flood risks (10568/34723).

Laos.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-smart agriculture, resilience, and policy integration** in **Laos** through transdisciplinary research and participatory approaches. Specifically:
- CGIAR’s **Climate Change, Agriculture and Food Security (CCAFS)** developed **Climate-Smart Villages (CSV)** as participatory testing grounds for context-specific climate-smart agriculture (CSA) technologies, scaling innovations via farmer engagement, policy alignment, and market-driven approaches (10568/115833).
- The Center assessed **gender dimensions in climate adaptation**, identifying gaps in resource access and coping mechanisms for men and women farmers through Participatory Vulnerability Analysis (10568/100189).
- Researchers evaluated **adaptation options for rice production** in the Mekong Basin, modeling impacts of climate variability on rainfed and irrigated systems while proposing interventions like adjusted planting dates and supplementary irrigation (10.1007/s12571-011-0154-z).
Laos.Adaptation:
• CGIAR’s **CCAFS** established **Climate-Smart Villages (CSV)** in Laos as part of a regional scaling initiative, combining knowledge transfer, policy advocacy, and economic approaches to mainstream CSA (10568/115833).
• The Center analyzed **multi-factor climate scenarios** for Southeast Asia, including Laos, to inform policy through participatory modeling (e.g., GLOBIOM, IMPACT) and stakeholder workshops (10.1016/j.envsoft.2016.05.008).
• Researchers developed **pest-smart early warning systems** for rice crops, addressing climate-induced pest/disease risks with farmer-friendly interventions (10568/97537).
• A study on **maize expansion in northern Laos** highlighted unintended consequences (soil degradation, debt, food insecurity) and advocated for holistic policies integrating livelihood and environmental sustainability (10.1016/j.landusepol.2018.10.006).
• CGIAR assessed **gender gaps in climate adaptation**, documenting disparities in access to resources and technologies while promoting inclusive Participatory Vulnerability Analysis (10568/100189).
• The Center evaluated **climate impacts on rice yields** in the Mekong Basin, proposing adaptation measures (e.g., supplementary irrigation, fertilizer adjustments) to offset projected declines in rainfed systems (10.1007/s12571-011-0154-z).
• Researchers synthesized **transdisciplinary lessons** from rice-based systems (e.g., Direct Seeded Rice Consortium) to strengthen resilience networks in Laos and Southeast Asia (10.3390/cli8020035).
Laos.Mitigation: • CGIAR’s **CCAFS** explored **climate-smart agriculture as a mitigation strategy**, aligning national climate action (e.g., Nationally Determined Contributions) with poverty and hunger reduction in Laos through multisectoral partnerships (10.1787/50075aa6-en).
Laos.Water:
• CGIAR analyzed **climate-driven water cycle variability** in the Mekong Basin, modeling impacts on rice production and proposing adaptation measures like supplementary irrigation to safeguard food security (10.1007/s12571-011-0154-z).
• The Center reviewed **water resource management** in the Greater Mekong Subregion, identifying priority actions to enhance resilience and food production under climate change (10.5337/2010.212).

Latvia.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-smart agricultural adaptation** in **Latvia** through **crop resilience and policy-informed production systems**. Specifically:
- **CGIAR’s Climate Change, Agriculture and Food Security (CCAFS) program** modeled climate change impacts on protein-rich crop suitability, identifying regional shifts in suitability (e.g., northern Europe becoming more favorable for quinoa) and recommending targeted breeding for abiotic stress tolerance in faba beans, lentils, and chickpeas (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eja.2019.125974](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eja.2019.125974)).
- **CGIAR’s IMPACT modeling team** analyzed adaptation strategies (e.g., R&D for climate-resilient crop varieties and irrigation technology upgrades) to mitigate yield risks and enhance food security in OECD-aligned contexts (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en](https://doi.org/10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en)).
Latvia.Adaptation:
• **CGIAR’s CCAFS program** assessed heterogeneous climate change impacts on protein-rich crop suitability across Europe, highlighting the need for **geographic production shifts** (e.g., northern Europe) and **breeding programs** to improve tolerance to abiotic stresses in faba beans, lentils, and chickpeas (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eja.2019.125974](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eja.2019.125974)).
• **CGIAR’s IMPACT modeling team** proposed **adaptation measures** (e.g., R&D for climate-resilient crop varieties and irrigation technology) to reduce climate-related yield risks and improve resilience in agricultural systems (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en](https://doi.org/10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en)).
Latvia.Mitigation: No evidence found in this sample.
Latvia.Water: No evidence found in this sample.

Lebanon.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-resilient agriculture and water security** in **Lebanon** through high-resolution climate modeling, nature-based water solutions, and field-based crop resilience experiments. Specifically:
- CGIAR’s **climate modeling** generated high-resolution projections for the **Ras Baalbeck Basin** to inform water and food security adaptation (Source: [https://doi.org/10.5337/2025.204](https://doi.org/10.5337/2025.204)).
- The **Al Murunah project** integrated **resilient nature-based water solutions (RNBWS)** to enhance climate resilience in Lebanon’s agricultural water management (Source: [https://doi.org/10.5337/2025.208](https://doi.org/10.5337/2025.208)).
Lebanon.Adaptation:
• CGIAR developed **high-resolution climate projections** for the **Ras Baalbeck Basin**, linking temperature/precipitation trends to water and food security adaptation strategies (Source: [https://doi.org/10.5337/2025.204](https://doi.org/10.5337/2025.204)).

• The **Al Murunah project** (CGIAR-led) advanced **climate-resilient water management** through localized, high-resolution climate data to guide **nature-based solutions** in Lebanon’s agricultural systems (Source: [https://doi.org/10.5337/2025.208](https://doi.org/10.5337/2025.208)).

• CGIAR’s **DIVERSify project** conducted **field experiments** in **Kfardan (2018–2019) and Tal Amara (2020)** to assess **crop resilience** in wheat and faba beans under climate stress (Sources: [hdl:20.500.11766.1/FK2/MHOHHL](hdl:20.500.11766.1/FK2/MHOHHL), [hdl:20.500.11766.1/FK2/O5YFWX](hdl:20.500.11766.1/FK2/O5YFWX), [hdl:20.500.11766.1/FK2/HTBOKU](hdl:20.500.11766.1/FK2/HTBOKU)).

• CGIAR documented **gender-equality priorities** in climate-adaptive agriculture for the **MENA region**, including Lebanon (Source: [10568/131786](10568/131786)).
Lebanon.Mitigation: No evidence found in this sample.
Lebanon.Water: • CGIAR contributed **Feed the Future water indicators** for Lebanon (2014–2017), tracking water security metrics across the region (Sources: [hdl:20.500.11766.1/FK2/FOHYFF](hdl:20.500.11766.1/FK2/FOHYFF), [hdl:20.500.11766.1/FK2/CJTCIY](hdl:20.500.11766.1/FK2/CJTCIY), [hdl:20.500.11766.1/FK2/GEPLSF](hdl:20.500.11766.1/FK2/GEPLSF)).

Lesotho.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-smart agriculture (CSA) and adaptive resilience** in **Lesotho** through integrated systems approaches and policy-relevant research. Specifically:
- **CGIAR’s Climate-Smart Agriculture program** advanced CSA pillars (productivity, adaptation, mitigation) to enhance food security and reduce GHG emissions (Source: **10568/97084**).
- **CGIAR researchers** analyzed climate change impacts on maize yields and food security, identifying adaptation strategies for smallholder farmers (Sources: **https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005**, **https://doi.org/10.3389/fclim.2022.787582**).
Lesotho.Adaptation:
• **CGIAR’s Climate-Smart Agriculture program** designed CSA frameworks to improve resilience, productivity, and GHG reduction in Lesotho’s agricultural systems (10568/97084).
• **CGIAR researchers** evaluated trade-offs/synergies in climate adaptation strategies (e.g., crop/livestock/water management) for smallholder farmers, emphasizing low-cost, complementary approaches (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.regsus.2021.05.002).
• **CGIAR’s Southern Africa team** assessed climate change risks (e.g., extreme events, yield declines) and proposed investments to offset production shocks for maize and other staples (https://doi.org/10.3389/fclim.2022.787582).
• **CGIAR’s WEF Nexus research** integrated water-energy-food systems to build resilient communities and align policies for sustainable development (https://doi.org/10.3390/ijerph15102306).
• **CGIAR’s Participatory Integrated Climate Services for Agriculture (PICSA)** scaled climate innovation methods to Lesotho, reaching farmers with tailored adaptation tools (doi:10.7910/DVN/VBDO8K).
Lesotho.Mitigation: • **CGIAR’s Climate-Smart Agriculture program** framed mitigation as a core pillar of CSA in Lesotho, linking productivity gains with GHG reduction strategies (10568/97084).
Lesotho.Water:
• **CGIAR researchers** modeled water-agriculture linkages using partial/general equilibrium models to evaluate adaptation scenarios (e.g., doubling irrigated area vs. yield improvements) (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ecolecon.2013.05.006).
• **CGIAR’s WEF Nexus approach** addressed water scarcity and food-energy insecurity by coordinating cross-sectoral interventions (https://doi.org/10.3390/ijerph15102306).

Liberia.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-smart agriculture and adaptation strategies** in **Liberia** through targeted research on cocoa and coffee value chains, drought resilience, and heat stress mitigation. Specifically:
- **CGIAR** analyzed climate vulnerability and proposed adaptation strategies (e.g., shade trees) for cocoa and coffee systems in Liberia’s lowland humid climate to ensure future viability (10568/68881).
- **CGIAR** assessed heat stress risks for dairy cattle production in West Africa, including Liberia’s eastern regions, and recommended improved production systems with higher adaptive capacity (https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-020-02733-2).
Liberia.Adaptation:
• **CGIAR** developed climate-smart agricultural strategies for West Africa, including crop, livestock, and fishery sector adaptations, to improve resilience of farming systems in Liberia’s context (https://doi.org/10.1186/s40066-016-0075-3).

• **CGIAR** evaluated trade-offs and synergies in climate adaptation strategies (e.g., water management, soil/land management) for smallholder farmers, highlighting low-cost options to maximize resilience and productivity in Liberia’s agricultural systems (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.regsus.2021.05.002).

• **CGIAR** analyzed climate vulnerability of cocoa in West Africa, identifying Liberia’s southern regions as least vulnerable but emphasizing the need for shade trees to mitigate drought stress (https://doi.org/10.7910/dvn/5w7lgw).

• **CGIAR** proposed a comprehensive adaptation strategy for Liberia’s cocoa and coffee value chains, addressing climate risks and ensuring future viability post-Ebola crisis (10568/68881).

• **CGIAR** examined COVID-19 lockdown impacts on food security in Sub-Saharan Africa, including Liberia, and recommended policy reforms to sustain agricultural productivity (https://doi.org/10.1038/s41538-020-00073-0).

• **CGIAR** assessed heat stress risks for dairy production in West Africa, including Liberia, and called for improved systems to enhance adaptive capacity (https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-020-02733-2).

• **CGIAR** evaluated two adaptation scenarios for Sub-Saharan Africa—doubling irrigated area or increasing crop yields—finding that productivity gains are more effective for Liberia’s limited initial irrigated base (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ecolecon.2013.05.006).
Liberia.Mitigation: No evidence found in this sample.
Liberia.Water:
• **CGIAR** integrated water simulation modules into general equilibrium models to assess economy-wide impacts of climate change on agriculture in Sub-Saharan Africa, including Liberia’s limited irrigated areas (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ecolecon.2013.05.006).

• **CGIAR** analyzed climate change impacts on maize production, including water management strategies for lowland and dry mid-altitude environments relevant to Liberia’s agricultural zones (https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005).

Libya.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate vulnerability and risk management** in **Libya** through **evidence-based assessments of extreme weather impacts and resilience strategies**. Specifically:
- **CGIAR researchers** analyzed the compounding effects of **prolonged droughts, torrential rains, and changing rainfall patterns** on agrifood systems, highlighting their role in exacerbating food insecurity and infrastructure collapse (e.g., Libya’s 2023 dam failures) (Source: **10568/155076**).
Libya.Adaptation: • **CGIAR researchers** evaluated **climate vulnerability and adaptation measures** in Libya, focusing on **risk management for extreme weather events** (e.g., floods, droughts) and their cascading impacts on agricultural productivity, livelihoods, and food security (Source: **10568/155076**).
Libya.Mitigation: No evidence found in this sample.
Libya.Water: • **CGIAR researchers** documented the **interlinked risks of prolonged droughts, floods, and torrential rain** in Libya, emphasizing their destabilizing effects on water-dependent agrifood systems and infrastructure (Source: **10568/155076**).

Lithuania.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-smart agricultural adaptation** in **Lithuania** through **crop resilience and policy-informed production systems**. Specifically:
- **CGIAR’s Climate Change and Food Security Program** modeled future suitability of protein-rich crops (e.g., quinoa, faba bean) under climate change, identifying regional shifts in crop viability and recommending targeted breeding programs for abiotic stress tolerance (e.g., drought, heat) to enhance resilience in southern Europe (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eja.2019.125974](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eja.2019.125974)).
- **CGIAR’s Policy and Impact Analysis Team** quantified adaptation strategies (e.g., R&D for climate-resilient crop varieties and irrigation technology upgrades) using the IMPACT model, estimating investment needs to mitigate yield losses and improve food security in OECD-aligned regions (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en](https://doi.org/10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en)).
Lithuania.Adaptation:

Madagascar.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate resilience and agricultural adaptation** in **Madagascar** through targeted research on crop diversity, climate risk assessment, and policy integration. Specifically:
- **Bioversity International** developed a **seed resilience strategy** to enhance farmers’ access to climate-adapted crop varieties, piloted in Madagascar alongside other countries (10568/78539).
- **CGIAR researchers** conducted a **spatially explicit climate vulnerability assessment** for the Comoros and broader Southern Africa, identifying Madagascar as a **hotspot for drought and extreme rainfall risks** (10568/109192).
Madagascar.Adaptation:
• **Bioversity International** piloted a **crop diversity-based adaptation strategy** in Madagascar, combining climate modeling and participatory research to strengthen farmers’ resilience to shifting weather patterns (10568/78539).

• **CGIAR’s climate risk assessment** identified **northern Madagascar as a high-vulnerability hotspot** for drought and extreme rainfall, informing regional risk management for small-scale farmers (10568/109192).

• **CGIAR researchers** reviewed **National Adaptation Plans for Agriculture (NAPAs)** in Madagascar, assessing integration of climate adaptation into national development strategies (10568/33403).

• **CGIAR’s systematic review** highlighted **trade-offs and synergies in smallholder adaptation strategies** (e.g., soil/land/water management), with implications for Madagascar’s agricultural systems (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.regsus.2021.05.002).

• **CGIAR analyzed climate change impacts on maize production** in sub-Saharan Africa, including Madagascar’s **maize mega-environments**, projecting yield reductions under future scenarios (https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005).

• **CGIAR’s economy-wide modeling** evaluated **adaptation scenarios** (e.g., yield improvements, irrigation expansion) to mitigate climate risks in Sub-Saharan Africa, with relevance to Madagascar’s limited irrigated areas (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ecolecon.2013.05.006).

• **CGIAR documented climate adaptation strategies** in East Africa, including Madagascar, focusing on **food security vulnerabilities** and resilience-building through extreme-event preparedness (https://doi.org/10.2499/9780896292055).
Madagascar.Mitigation: No evidence found in this sample.
Madagascar.Water:
• **CGIAR’s WEF nexus research** (Water-Energy-Food) explored **integrated water management** as a cross-sectoral adaptation tool for southern Africa, addressing water scarcity and food-energy insecurity—relevant to Madagascar’s resource constraints (https://doi.org/10.3390/ijerph15102306).

• **CGIAR’s economy-wide modeling** included a **water simulation module** to assess irrigation expansion and productivity gains as climate adaptation measures for Sub-Saharan Africa (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ecolecon.2013.05.006).

Malawi.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-resilient agricultural systems and risk management** in **Malawi** through evidence-based interventions targeting smallholder farmers. Specifically:
- **CGIAR’s Climate Risk Management Program** assessed how stress-adapted crop germplasm, conservation agriculture, and diversified production systems stabilize incomes and reduce poverty risks under climate variability (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agsy.2018.01.019](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agsy.2018.01.019)).
- **CIAT and Bioversity** demonstrated that agricultural diversification—particularly in regions with 500–1000mm rainfall—enhances household food security, though constraints like market access and land availability persist (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1111/gcb.14158](https://doi.org/10.1111/gcb.14158)).
Malawi.Adaptation:
• **CGIAR’s Climate Risk Management Program** evaluated how **conservation agriculture (CA), improved cereal-legume rotations, and soil-water management** reduce vulnerability to droughts, with cost-benefit analyses confirming their economic viability for risk-averse smallholder farmers in Malawi (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ecolecon.2019.05.013](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ecolecon.2019.05.013)).

• **CGIAR’s Water-Energy-Food Nexus Team** analyzed climate impacts on water resources, recommending **integrated WEF nexus approaches** to build resilience in marginalized communities, though regional rainfall declines (projected 20% by 2080) threaten food-energy-water security (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3390/ijerph15102306](https://doi.org/10.3390/ijerph15102306)).

• **CGIAR’s Maize Program** modeled **climate-proof maize varieties** and adaptation measures for Malawi’s maize mega-environments, showing yield reductions under high-input systems but highlighting trade as a potential buffer for food insecurity (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005](https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005)).

• **CGIAR’s Gender and Climate Team** identified **gender gaps in access to climate services**, advocating for ICT-based solutions and women’s group inclusion to address differential climate information needs (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1080/17565529.2019.1613216](https://doi.org/10.1080/17565529.2019.1613216)).

• **CGIAR’s Climate-Smart Agriculture (CSA) Initiative** prioritized **improved seeds, good agricultural practices, and conservation agriculture** in Malawi’s value chains, with cost-benefit analyses validating their productivity and resilience benefits (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.heliyon.2022.e09228](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.heliyon.2022.e09228)).

• **World Agroforestry Centre (ICRAF)** promoted **fodder trees** as drought-resistant feed sources for livestock, addressing production shortages during extreme climatic events (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cosust.2013.11.008](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cosust.2013.11.008)).
Malawi.Mitigation: No evidence found in this sample.
Malawi.Water:
• **CGIAR’s CSA Program** integrated **soil-water conservation practices** (e.g., CA principles) with improved varieties to enhance drought resilience, with women’s bargaining power and access to information as key adoption drivers (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ecolecon.2019.05.013](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ecolecon.2019.05.013)).

• **CGIAR’s WEF Nexus Team** emphasized **cross-sectoral water management** to mitigate climate-induced resource scarcities, though implementation requires policy convergence (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3390/ijerph15102306](https://doi.org/10.3390/ijerph15102306)).

Malaysia.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-smart agricultural adaptation** in **Malaysia** through **resilient agrifood systems, crop diversity, and institutional scaling**. Specifically:
- **WorldFish** developed a **local fish feed database** using 13 sustainable ingredients (e.g., rubber seed meal, palm kernel cake) tested in Penang, Malaysia, to support smallholder resilience (doi:10.7910/DVN/Y1HVFF).
- **CCAFS SEA and partners** surveyed **Sarawak farmers** on climate change perceptions, crop diversity, and seed systems to document indigenous resilience strategies (doi:10.7910/DVN/XYKYDW).
Malaysia.Adaptation:
• **WorldFish** created an **open-access ingredient database** for sustainable fish feed formulation in Malaysia, integrating 13 locally sourced inputs (e.g., cassava peel, banana peel) to enhance smallholder aquaculture resilience (doi:10.7910/DVN/Y1HVFF).

• **CCAFS SEA and SIAS (Malaysia)** conducted **household surveys in Sarawak** to assess farmers’ perceptions of climate change, adaptive crop diversity use, and seed system effectiveness for resilience (doi:10.7910/DVN/XYKYDW).

• **CGIAR (via CCAFS SEA)** facilitated **Climate-Smart Village (CSV) workshops** in ASEAN, including Malaysia, to scale climate-resilient rural development through regional networks like the **ASEAN Climate Resilience Network** (10568/123058).

• **Direct Seeded Rice Consortium (DSRC)**—led by CGIAR partners—demonstrated **trans-disciplinary climate-smart agriculture networks** in Southeast Asia, including Malaysia, to improve resilience to extreme weather (https://doi.org/10.3390/cli8020035).

• **CCAFS SEA** outlined **R4D thrusts (2019–2021)** for Southeast Asia, emphasizing **food security and climate resilience** through integrated Climate-Smart Village interventions (10568/99018).

• **CGIAR++ centers** explored **institutional scaling frameworks** for agricultural innovations in Asia, including Malaysia, to embed sustainable change (10568/105560, 10568/99099).

Mali.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-smart agriculture (CSA) and adaptive resilience** in **Mali** through **scalable technologies, cultivar innovation, and gender-inclusive climate services**. Specifically:
- **CGIAR’s Climate Change, Agriculture, and Food Security (CCAFS) program** demonstrated **transformative CSA practices** (e.g., climate-smart groundnut varieties, intercropping, organic fertilizers) to enhance yields and food security in Mali, with bundled practices showing stronger impacts (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.gloenvcha.2023.102697](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.gloenvcha.2023.102697)).
- **CGIAR researchers** assessed **climate change impacts on rainfed cereals (maize, millet, sorghum, wheat)** in Mali, highlighting the potential of **adapted cultivars** to offset yield declines under future climate scenarios (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eja.2024.127137](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eja.2024.127137)).
- **CGIAR’s West Africa hub** reviewed **climate adaptation strategies** for crops, livestock, and fisheries, emphasizing **policy and institutional mechanisms** to improve resilience in Mali’s farming systems (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.gloenvcha.2013.12.011](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.gloenvcha.2013.12.011)).
Mali.Adaptation:
• **CGIAR’s CCAFS program** demonstrated that **bundling CSA practices** (e.g., climate-smart groundnut varieties, intercropping, organic fertilizers) in Mali led to **higher yields and improved food security**, with groundnut varieties showing the strongest association (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.gloenvcha.2023.102697](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.gloenvcha.2023.102697)).

• **CGIAR researchers** evaluated **climate change impacts on rainfed cereals** in Mali, finding that **adapted cultivars** could compensate for yield losses (6% regional decline projected by 2050), though **33% of potential production** remains vulnerable without further adaptation (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eja.2024.127137](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eja.2024.127137)).

• **CGIAR’s West Africa team** synthesized **climate-smart agriculture (CSA) adaptation strategies** for Mali, including **agroforestry (farmer-managed natural regenerations), soil/water conservation (zai, half-moon, contour ridges), and climate information services**, while identifying **policy gaps** in financing and institutional support (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jclepro.2018.03.199](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jclepro.2018.03.199)).

• **CGIAR’s global adaptation research** linked **farm-associated changes** (e.g., improved crop varieties, land management) in Mali to **access to weather information, assets, and social institutions**, highlighting drivers for climate-resilient strategies (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.gloenvcha.2013.12.011](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.gloenvcha.2013.12.011)).

• **CGIAR’s gender-responsive climate services research** identified **gender disparities in access to climate information** in Mali, recommending **women’s group inclusion and ICT-based solutions** to address socio-cultural barriers (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1080/17565529.2019.1613216](https://doi.org/10.1080/17565529.2019.1613216)).

• **CGIAR’s transformative agriculture review** analyzed **climate-smart groundnut varieties and bundling practices** in Mali as part of a **food-system transformation framework** (reroute, de-risk, reduce, realign) to enhance resilience (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3390/su13084305](https://doi.org/10.3390/su13084305)).
Mali.Mitigation: • **CGIAR’s CSA research in Mali** demonstrated that **bundling mitigation-oriented practices** (e.g., climate-smart groundnut varieties, organic fertilizers) **reduced greenhouse gas emissions** while improving yields and food security (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.gloenvcha.2023.102697](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.gloenvcha.2023.102697)).
Mali.Water: No evidence found in this sample.
