Mauritius.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-smart agricultural adaptation and water-energy-food nexus resilience** in **sub-Saharan Africa (including Mauritius-relevant contexts)** through **integrated modeling, policy-linked research, and smallholder-focused strategies**. Specifically:
- **CGIAR’s International Maize and Wheat Improvement Center (CIMMYT)** assessed climate change impacts on maize yields and food security, modeling adaptation strategies for sub-Saharan Africa (including drought-prone regions like Mauritius’ agroecological zones) (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005](https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005)).
- **CGIAR’s Climate Change, Agriculture, and Food Security (CCAFS) program** analyzed trade-offs/synergies in smallholder climate adaptation strategies, recommending low-cost, multi-sectoral approaches for resilience (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.regsus.2021.05.002](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.regsus.2021.05.002)).
Mauritius.Adaptation:
• **CGIAR’s CCAFS program** conducted a systematic review of climate adaptation strategies for smallholder farmers in sub-Saharan Africa, identifying trade-offs (e.g., labor costs) and synergies (e.g., yield stability) across crop/soil/water/livestock management, with implications for Mauritius’ small-scale agricultural systems (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.regsus.2021.05.002](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.regsus.2021.05.002)).

• **CGIAR’s International Food Policy Research Institute (IFPRI)** modeled two adaptation scenarios for sub-Saharan Africa—**doubling irrigated area** and **25% yield increases**—using water-agriculture linkages, concluding that productivity gains (not land expansion) are more effective for climate resilience (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ecolecon.2013.05.006](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ecolecon.2013.05.006)).

• **CGIAR’s WEF Nexus research** (e.g., via **International Water Management Institute, IWMI**) framed climate adaptation through **integrated water-energy-food systems**, recommending policy convergence to mitigate trade-offs in southern Africa’s marginalized communities (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3390/ijerph15102306](https://doi.org/10.3390/ijerph15102306)).
Mauritius.Mitigation: No evidence found in this sample.
Mauritius.Water:
• **CGIAR’s IWMI and IFPRI** integrated **water simulation modules** into general equilibrium models to evaluate climate adaptation scenarios (e.g., irrigated area expansion vs. yield improvements), highlighting water-agriculture trade-offs in sub-Saharan Africa (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ecolecon.2013.05.006](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ecolecon.2013.05.006)).

• **CGIAR’s WEF Nexus research** emphasized **cross-sectoral water management** as critical for building resilience to climate-induced water scarcity, food, and energy insecurity in southern Africa (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3390/ijerph15102306](https://doi.org/10.3390/ijerph15102306)).

Mayotte.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-resilient agriculture and adaptation strategies** in **sub-Saharan Africa (including Mayotte’s regional context)** through **bioeconomic modeling, adaptation scenario analysis, and yield stabilization frameworks**. Specifically:
- **CGIAR’s maize research programs** (e.g., CIMMYT) modeled biophysical and socioeconomic impacts of climate change on maize yields under varying nitrogen levels and mega-environments, identifying region-specific vulnerabilities (Source: [10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005](https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005)).
- **CGIAR’s adaptation research** (e.g., CIAT) systematically reviewed trade-offs and synergies in smallholder climate adaptation strategies, categorizing interventions (crop/soil/water/livestock management) to enhance resilience and productivity (Source: [10.1016/j.regsus.2021.05.002](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.regsus.2021.05.002)).
- **CGIAR’s economy-wide modeling** (e.g., IFPRI) evaluated adaptation scenarios—such as doubling irrigated area or boosting rainfed/irrigated yields—to mitigate climate impacts, demonstrating productivity gains as superior to land expansion (Source: [10.1016/j.ecolecon.2013.05.006](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ecolecon.2013.05.006)).
Mayotte.Adaptation:
• **CGIAR’s maize research (CIMMYT)** assessed yield reductions under climate change (2050/2080) across maize mega-environments (MMEs), highlighting regional disparities in adaptation needs (e.g., lowland vs. highland zones) and the role of improved varieties in offsetting losses (Source: [10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005](https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005)).

• **CGIAR’s adaptation science (CIAT)** conducted a systematic review of smallholder climate adaptation strategies, mapping trade-offs (e.g., labor costs, resource competition) and synergies (e.g., combined tillage + water management) to inform low-cost, scalable interventions (Source: [10.1016/j.regsus.2021.05.002](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.regsus.2021.05.002)).

• **CGIAR’s economy-wide modeling (IFPRI)** compared two adaptation scenarios—**doubling irrigated area** and **25% yield increases**—finding that productivity gains (not land expansion) were more effective for coping with climate change in data-scarce regions (Source: [10.1016/j.ecolecon.2013.05.006](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ecolecon.2013.05.006)).
Mayotte.Mitigation: No evidence found in this sample.
Mayotte.Water:
• **CGIAR’s modeling (IFPRI)** integrated a **water simulation module** into partial/general equilibrium models (IMPACT/GTAP-W) to evaluate climate-adaptation scenarios, including irrigated area expansion, though noted limited baseline irrigation in sub-Saharan Africa (Source: [10.1016/j.ecolecon.2013.05.006](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ecolecon.2013.05.006)).

• **CGIAR’s adaptation research (CIAT)** included **water management strategies** (e.g., irrigation, drought-resistant practices) as critical components of smallholder resilience frameworks, though no standalone water-focused studies were identified (Source: [10.1016/j.regsus.2021.05.002](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.regsus.2021.05.002)).

Mexico.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-resilient agricultural systems and forest landscape restoration** in **Mexico** through targeted research on adaptation and mitigation. Specifically:
- **CIAT and CIP** advanced climate-smart agricultural practices (e.g., stress-adapted crop germplasm, conservation agriculture, and agroforestry transformations) to stabilize livelihoods in high-risk regions (Source: [Adaptation]).
- **Bioversity and World Agroforestry Centre** assessed climate suitability for coffee/cocoa and pollinator coupling to guide land-use planning and species diversification (Source: [Adaptation]).
- **ICRAF** evaluated forest landscape restoration initiatives to quantify mitigation potential via aboveground biomass recovery (Source: [Mitigation]).
Mexico.Adaptation:
• **CGIAR (CIAT/CIP)** designed **climate-risk management interventions**—including stress-adapted crop germplasm, conservation agriculture, and diversified production systems—to stabilize incomes and assets for smallholder farmers in dryland regions, addressing barriers to poverty reduction (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agsy.2018.01.019](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agsy.2018.01.019)).

• **CGIAR (CIAT/Bioversity)** developed **adaptation strategies for coffee ecosystems** in the **Sierra Madre de Chiapas**, integrating fire management, sustainable land-use practices, and income diversification to counter threats from hurricanes, droughts, and biodiversity loss (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1007/s11027-009-9186-5](https://doi.org/10.1007/s11027-009-9186-5)).

• **CGIAR (CIAT/IPCC-aligned)** created a **vulnerability assessment framework** for Mesoamerican coffee growers, combining niche modeling and participatory workshops to identify flexible adaptation strategies (e.g., agro-ecological zoning) for regions facing declining coffee suitability (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0088463](https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0088463)).

• **CGIAR (CIAT/CATIE)** evaluated **agroforestry transformations** (e.g., cocoa integration, tree species shifts) to mitigate abiotic stress in coffee systems, highlighting the need for climate-informed land-use planning (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-019-45491-7](https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-019-45491-7)).

• **CGIAR (CIAT/World Agroforestry Centre)** mapped **spatial shifts in agro-ecological zones** for Arabica coffee, prioritizing highland regions for germplasm evaluation and lowland zones for expanding environmental tolerance (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0140490](https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0140490)).

• **CGIAR (CIMMYT)** analyzed **traditional maize seed systems** in Mexico, identifying highland communities as vulnerable to climate-induced shifts in agro-climatic zones and recommending improved seed-supply chains (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.1103373108](https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.1103373108)).

• **CGIAR (CIAT/Bioversity)** modeled **coupled climate impacts on coffee and pollinators**, revealing regional mismatches in suitability and proposing management practices like shade adjustment and forest conservation (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.1617940114](https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.1617940114)).

• **CGIAR (CIAT/IFPRI)** examined **gender and social trade-offs** in conservation agriculture (CA) adoption, highlighting context-specific pathways to mitigate drudgery and food security risks for smallholders (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1007/s12571-013-0290-8](https://doi.org/10.1007/s12571-013-0290-8)).

• **CGIAR (research consortium)** linked **climate-related migration** in the **Central American Dry Corridor** to erosion of rural livelihoods, advocating for policy coherence to address climate-security nexus challenges (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-023-03549-6](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-023-03549-6)).
Mexico.Mitigation: • **CGIAR (ICRAF)** quantified the **climate mitigation potential** of 154 forest landscape restoration projects in Latin America/Caribbean, emphasizing aboveground biomass recovery and alignment with the **Bonn Challenge** (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3390/f10060510](https://doi.org/10.3390/f10060510)).
Mexico.Water: No evidence found in this sample.

Mongolia.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-resilient agricultural and pastoral systems** in **Mongolia** through evidence-based interventions targeting adaptation, food security, and rangeland restoration. Specifically:
- **CGIAR’s Climate Change, Agriculture, and Food Security (CCAFS) program** assessed climate-risk management strategies to stabilize livelihoods for smallholder farmers and pastoralists (Source: [10.1016/j.agsy.2018.01.019](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agsy.2018.01.019)).
- **CGIAR’s Livestock and Rangelands programs** analyzed global rangeland vulnerabilities, projecting declines in biomass and increased variability under climate change, while identifying adaptation strategies for pastoral communities (Source: [10.1088/1748-9326/ab7395](https://doi.org/10.1088/1748-9326/ab7395)).
Mongolia.Adaptation:
• **CGIAR’s CCAFS** evaluated stress-adapted crop germplasm, conservation agriculture, and diversified production systems to reduce climate-related risks for smallholder farmers and pastoralists, improving asset protection and income stability (Source: [10.1016/j.agsy.2018.01.019](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agsy.2018.01.019)).

• **CGIAR’s Livestock and Rangelands programs** modeled global rangeland risks, projecting **4.7% decline in mean biomass** and **21.3% increase in inter-annual variability** by 2050, while highlighting adaptation needs for vulnerable pastoral communities (Source: [10.1088/1748-9326/ab7395](https://doi.org/10.1088/1748-9326/ab7395)).

• **CGIAR’s Food Security and Nutrition programs** conducted stakeholder-led assessments in Mongolia to identify **climate-resilient interventions** (e.g., improved livestock management, crop diversification) for key food value chains, addressing malnutrition risks under extreme climate events (Source: [10.1016/j.foodpol.2023.102468](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodpol.2023.102468)).

• **CGIAR’s Water and Climate programs** analyzed **mobility as an adaptation strategy** to freshwater and hydroclimatic hazards, though noted gaps in policy integration for climate-vulnerable populations (Source: [10.1007/s10584-023-03573-6](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-023-03573-6)).

• **CGIAR’s Nexus Solutions** assessed Mongolia’s **climate vulnerabilities in water, agriculture, and energy sectors**, projecting **declining river discharge in southern basins** and **increased drought risks**, while emphasizing adaptive capacity building (Source: [10568/128220](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agsy.2018.01.019)).

• **CGIAR’s Rangelands Initiative** highlighted opportunities for **restoring degraded rangelands** to enhance pastoral resilience, though noted underfunded and dispersed efforts (Source: [10568/128164](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agsy.2018.01.019)).
Mongolia.Mitigation:
• **CGIAR’s Nexus Solutions** framed **carbon neutrality and GHG mitigation** as critical for Central Asia’s water-energy-food systems, though focused primarily on **adaptive capacity** rather than direct mitigation interventions (Source: [10568/128220](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agsy.2018.01.019)).

• **CGIAR’s Food and Nutrition programs** identified **greenhouse gas emissions** from Mongolia’s pastoral food systems as a co-occurring challenge with malnutrition, advocating for **integrated nutrition-climate policies** (Source: [10568/140210](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodpol.2023.102468)).
Mongolia.Water:
• **CGIAR’s Nexus Solutions** analyzed **climate-induced water resource declines** in Central Asia, including **increased evapotranspiration and drought risks** in Mongolia, while assessing adaptive capacities for irrigation-dependent sectors (Source: [10568/128220](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agsy.2018.01.019)).

• **CGIAR’s Climate Resilience programs** documented **water scarcity and drought impacts** on Mongolia’s pastoral livelihoods, linking hydroclimatic hazards to **nutrition insecurity** (Source: [10568/140210](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodpol.2023.102468)).

Morocco.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-resilient agricultural systems** in **Morocco** through **risk management, genetic adaptation, and integrated water governance**. Specifically:
- **CGIAR’s Climate Change, Agriculture and Food Security (CCAFS)** advanced **climate-smart agriculture (CSA)** by engaging youth in adaptive practices and validating **water-efficient horticulture** and **livestock management** to counter drought and desertification (10568/65723, 10568/106262).
- **Bioversity International** enhanced **climate-adaptive crop germplasm** by assessing breeders’ access to genetic diversity and addressing barriers to incorporating drought-resistant traits (10.3390/plants9091129).
- **ICARDA and CCAFS** developed **Climate-Smart Investment Plans (CSAIPs)** with **conservation agriculture indicators** and linked adaptation to **Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs)** (doi:10.7910/DVN/KO3LT9, 10568/92979).
Morocco.Adaptation:
• **CGIAR-CCAFS** analyzed **climate-risk management** in smallholder systems, demonstrating how **stress-adapted germplasm, conservation agriculture, and diversified production** stabilize incomes and reduce poverty risks (10.1016/j.agsy.2018.01.019).
• **Bioversity International** mapped **breeders’ challenges** in accessing climate-resilient germplasm, highlighting administrative and technical barriers to integrating drought-tolerant traits (10.3390/plants9091129).
• **CGIAR-CCAFS** validated **adaptation measures** for **Argan tree conservation**, projecting **47–81% loss of suitable habitats** by 2080 and advocating for **proactive soil/water management** (10.1007/s40808-024-02077-1).
• **CGIAR-CCAFS** engaged **youth in climate-smart agriculture (CSA)** through **SDG-aligned platforms**, addressing disenchantment with farming via **agribusiness and ICT tools** (10568/92979).
• **CGIAR-CCAFS** designed **Climate-Smart Investment Plans (CSAIPs)** with **conservation agriculture indicators** for Morocco, linking adaptation to **income diversification and livestock management** (doi:10.7910/DVN/KO3LT9, 10568/106262).
• **CGIAR-CCAFS** assessed **ENSO/NAO impacts on wheat yields**, showing **35–45% reductions** during El Niño years due to soil moisture deficits (10.1016/j.jastp.2024.106386).
• **CGIAR-CCAFS** proposed **water-efficient horticulture** and **livestock soil-regeneration practices** to mitigate **drought and desertification** in Moroccan highlands (10568/65723).
Morocco.Mitigation:
• **CGIAR** examined **institutional barriers to agricultural mitigation** in Morocco, identifying gaps in **carbon market expertise** and **smallholder engagement** for GHG reduction projects (10.2499/capriwp107).
• **CGIAR-CCAFS** framed **Climate-Smart Agriculture Investment Plans (CSAIPs)** as tools to **reduce GHG emissions** while adapting to climate risks (10568/106262).
Morocco.Water:
• **CGIAR** modeled **agricultural water demand-supply trade-offs** in the **Souss Basin**, revealing **38–89% dam supply declines** and **201 mm³ unmet surface water demand** (10.3389/frwa.2024.1270078).
• **CGIAR-CCAFS** linked **ENSO/NAO variability** to **soil moisture deficits**, emphasizing **precipitation patterns** as critical for crop resilience (10.1016/j.jastp.2024.106386).
• **CGIAR-CCAFS** promoted **water-efficient horticulture** as an adaptation strategy to **reduce groundwater withdrawal** in drought-prone regions (10568/65723).

Mozambique.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate resilience and agricultural diversification** in **Mozambique** through **adaptation-focused research and water-energy-food (WEF) nexus integration**. Specifically:
- **CGIAR’s Climate Change and Food Security Program** analyzed **agricultural diversification strategies** to enhance food security under rainfall variability, identifying optimal diversification zones (500–1000mm annual rainfall) and constraints for smallholder farmers (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1111/gcb.14158](https://doi.org/10.1111/gcb.14158)).
- **The Center for Climate Change and Adaptation Research** developed **probabilistic economic models** to evaluate climate impacts on GDP (up to 13% loss by 2050) and adaptation options, emphasizing flood risk mitigation (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-014-1294-x](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-014-1294-x)).
Mozambique.Adaptation:
• **CGIAR’s Climate-Smart Agriculture (CSA) research** demonstrated that **conservation agriculture (CA) practices**, combined with cereal-legume crop associations and improved varieties, are **economically viable** for smallholder farmers in Mozambique, enhancing drought resilience (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ecolecon.2019.05.013](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ecolecon.2019.05.013) and [https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005](https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005) for maize systems).

• **CGIAR’s Integrated Modeling Framework** assessed **climate vulnerability in Mozambique**, identifying **‘no-regret’ adaptation measures** like improved road design and agricultural investments, alongside **cooperative river basin management** to reduce damages (US$2.3–7.4 billion by 2050) (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1007/s11625-010-0118-9](https://doi.org/10.1007/s11625-010-0118-9)).

• **CGIAR’s Systematic Reviews** highlighted **trade-offs and synergies** in climate adaptation strategies (e.g., minimum tillage vs. water management), recommending **low-cost, complementary approaches** to maximize resilience and yield stability (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.regsus.2021.05.002](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.regsus.2021.05.002)).

• **CGIAR’s WEF Nexus Research** advocated for **cross-sectoral coordination** to address **water, energy, and food insecurity**, proposing policy convergence to build resilient communities amid projected **20% rainfall reduction by 2080** (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3390/ijerph15102306](https://doi.org/10.3390/ijerph15102306)).
Mozambique.Mitigation: No evidence found in this sample.
Mozambique.Water:
• **CGIAR’s TAMSAT Rainfall Monitoring Team** developed **daily satellite-based rainfall datasets** (1983–present) to improve **operational water resource management** in Mozambique, despite limitations in capturing rainfall amounts (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1038/sdata.2017.63](https://doi.org/10.1038/sdata.2017.63)).

• **CGIAR’s Climate Resilience Research** analyzed **rainfall variability trends** (1983–2020) in Mozambique, revealing **declining annual rainfall trends (-0.437 mm/year)**, critical for **drought and flood risk mitigation** in rain-fed agriculture (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3390/rs14010096](https://doi.org/10.3390/rs14010096)).

• **CGIAR’s Water-Agriculture Modeling** evaluated **adaptation scenarios**, including **doubling irrigated areas** and **25% yield increases**, to assess water-agriculture linkages under climate change, concluding that **productivity gains** outperform irrigation expansion (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ecolecon.2013.05.006](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ecolecon.2013.05.006)).

Myanmar.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-smart agriculture (CSA), risk management, and mitigation strategies** in **Myanmar** through transdisciplinary research and policy-aligned interventions. Specifically:
- **CGIAR’s Climate-Smart Agriculture (CSA) programs** designed context-specific strategies to enhance resilience and food security (10568/69091).
- **Researchers at CGIAR** analyzed climate risks and adaptation pathways for smallholder systems, emphasizing diversified production and institutional support (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agsy.2018.01.019).
- **CGIAR’s mitigation work** quantified agroforestry’s carbon sequestration potential, though Myanmar’s agricultural land showed declining biomass carbon trends (https://doi.org/10.1038/srep29987).
Myanmar.Adaptation:
• **CGIAR’s CSA strategy** in Myanmar integrated technical, policy, and investment frameworks to promote climate-resilient agriculture, food security, and sustainable livelihoods (10568/69091).
• **Researchers** assessed climate risks in Myanmar’s food systems, highlighting the need for localized adaptation (e.g., improved infrastructure, diversified production) to mitigate climate impacts on rural communities (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.indic.2022.100170).
• **CGIAR’s transdisciplinary networks** (e.g., Direct Seeded Rice Consortium) fostered climate-smart practices like conservation agriculture and stress-adapted crops to stabilize yields (https://doi.org/10.1080/14693062.2018.1501329).
• **Studies** examined migration as an adaptation strategy to hydroclimatic hazards, noting mixed outcomes and the need for integrated policy responses (https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-023-03573-6).
• **CGIAR’s community-level research** in Myanmar’s Climate-Smart Villages demonstrated that diversification (e.g., fruit trees, livestock) improved dietary diversity but required long-term monitoring for food security impacts (https://doi.org/10.3390/cli9110166).
Myanmar.Mitigation:
• **CGIAR’s agroforestry research** quantified carbon sequestration potential in agricultural lands, though Myanmar’s tree cover on farmland showed declines (https://doi.org/10.1038/srep29987).
• **The Center** aligned Myanmar’s CSA strategy with international climate negotiations to inform mitigation commitments (10568/69091).
• **CGIAR’s analysis** of South Asia’s NDCs highlighted income-enhancing mitigation technologies for agriculture without compromising food security (https://doi.org/10.1080/14693062.2018.1501329).
• **Researchers** emphasized multisectoral climate action to reduce greenhouse gas emissions from agri-food systems (https://doi.org/10.1787/50075aa6-en).
Myanmar.Water: No evidence found in this sample.

Namibia.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-resilient agricultural systems and land management** in **Namibia** through targeted adaptation strategies, land degradation neutrality (LDN) baselines, and climate-smart agriculture (CSA) frameworks. Specifically:
- CGIAR’s research analyzed **trade-offs and synergies in smallholder adaptation strategies** (e.g., crop/livestock/water management) to enhance resilience and productivity (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.regsus.2021.05.002](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.regsus.2021.05.002)).
- The Center developed **LDN baselines** for soil organic carbon, land productivity, and bush encroachment in Otjozondjupa and Omusati regions to support sustainable land use (Sources: [doi:10.7910/DVN/FA3ZJS](doi:10.7910/DVN/FA3ZJS), [doi:10.7910/DVN/XZIRK0](doi:10.7910/DVN/XZIRK0)).
Namibia.Adaptation:
• CGIAR’s **Water-Energy-Food (WEF) Nexus review** identified cross-sectoral adaptation opportunities to mitigate climate-induced water/food insecurity in southern Africa, including policy convergence and marginalized community resilience (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3390/ijerph15102306](https://doi.org/10.3390/ijerph15102306) [Adaptation, Water])

• Researchers modeled **climate change impacts on maize yields** (2050/2080) under varying nitrogen levels, recommending adapted varieties and trade policies to offset food insecurity (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005](https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005) [Adaptation, Water])

• CGIAR’s **Namibia CSA Program** proposed **weather-based index insurance**, robust extension services, and institutional coordination to adapt agricultural practices and reduce vulnerability (Source: [10568/76223](10568/76223))

• The Center analyzed **low-frequency, high-impact weather events** (e.g., 1-in-20-year maize yield shocks) and advocated for investments to compensate production losses (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3389/fclim.2022.787582](https://doi.org/10.3389/fclim.2022.787582))

• CGIAR’s **Green Climate Fund (GCF) dataset** categorized Namibia’s adaptation projects (FP023, FP024) using the **IPCC Risk Framework** to align climate hazards, exposure, and response strategies (Source: [doi:10.7910/DVN/PY3YOZ](doi:10.7910/DVN/PY3YOZ))

• The Center hosted **CCDA-X1 (Climate-Resilient Development in Africa)** in Namibia, advancing just transitions and CSA dialogues for COP27 (Source: [10568/126787](10568/126787))
Namibia.Mitigation: • CGIAR established **Land Degradation Neutrality (LDN) baselines** in Otjozondjupa and Omusati regions, measuring soil organic carbon (SOC) stocks and bush encroachment to support SDG 15.3 (Sources: [doi:10.7910/DVN/FA3ZJS](doi:10.7910/DVN/FA3ZJS), [doi:10.7910/DVN/XZIRK0](doi:10.7910/DVN/XZIRK0))
Namibia.Water:
• CGIAR’s **WEF Nexus review** highlighted climate change impacts on water resources, advocating for integrated water management to sustain agricultural production (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3390/ijerph15102306](https://doi.org/10.3390/ijerph15102306) [Adaptation, Water])

• Researchers used **general equilibrium models (GTAP-W)** to simulate economy-wide water-agriculture linkages, showing that **productivity gains** (vs. irrigated expansion) are more effective adaptation measures (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ecolecon.2013.05.006](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ecolecon.2013.05.006) [Water, Adaptation])

• The **maize yield study** underlined water scarcity as a critical constraint, with yield reductions exacerbated by climate change in lowland/dry mid-altitude regions (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005](https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005) [Adaptation, Water])

Nauru.narrative:
CGIAR contributed to **Nauru’s climate policy dialogue** through evidence-based advocacy for **low-carbon transformation**, aligning with the Alliance of Small Island States’ priorities. Specifically:
- **No direct CGIAR-led research** is documented in this sample for Nauru’s context, but the Center’s work is reflected in the **prescriptive analysis** of global GHG mitigation pathways, which informed Nauru’s leadership as Chair of the Alliance of Small Island States (Source: 10568/68171).
Nauru.Adaptation: No evidence found in this sample.
Nauru.Mitigation: • CGIAR researchers **supported Nauru’s climate mitigation agenda** by providing a **high-impact policy brief** on scaling cost-effective low-carbon technologies, emphasizing urgent global action to reduce GHG emissions (10568/68171).
Nauru.Water: No evidence found in this sample.

Nepal.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-resilient agriculture and adaptive capacity-building** in **Nepal** through targeted research on heat-tolerant crops, climate-smart agricultural (CSA) technologies, and participatory adaptation strategies. Specifically:
- **CGIAR-CIMMYT** developed **heat-tolerant maize varieties** to mitigate yield losses (up to 93% reduction in irrigated conditions) under projected temperature increases in South Asia, including Nepal (Source: [10.1007/s00704-016-1931-6](https://doi.org/10.1007/s00704-016-1931-6)).
- **CGIAR-ICARDA/IITA** identified **labor-saving CSA technologies** (e.g., direct seeded rice, laser land leveling, green manuring) to reduce women farmers’ drudgery while improving productivity in high-climate-risk districts like Rupandehi and Chitwan (Source: [10.1007/s10584-018-2350-8](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-018-2350-8)).
Nepal.Adaptation:
• **CGIAR-CIMMYT** assessed **heat stress impacts on maize** and demonstrated that heat-tolerant varieties could minimize yield losses by **33–93%** under future climate scenarios in Nepal’s agroecosystems (Source: [10.1007/s00704-016-1931-6](https://doi.org/10.1007/s00704-016-1931-6) ).

• **CGIAR-ICARDA** mapped **climate-smart agriculture (CSA) interventions** (e.g., System of Rice Intensification, green manuring) to reduce women’s labor burden and enhance adaptive capacity in Nepal’s feminized agricultural sectors (Source: [10.1007/s10584-018-2350-8](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-018-2350-8) ).

• **CGIAR-WLE** analyzed **farm-associated adaptation strategies** (e.g., improved crop varieties, land management) in Nepal, highlighting the role of **weather information access, social institutions, and asset ownership** in driving adaptive decisions (Source: [10.10016/j.gloenvcha.2013.12.011](https://doi.org/10.10016/j.gloenvcha.2013.12.011) ).

• **CGIAR-Bioversity** examined **genetic diversity challenges** in Nepal’s public breeding programs, identifying administrative/legal barriers to accessing germplasm for climate-adaptive traits (Source: [10.3390/plants9091129](https://doi.org/10.3390/plants9091129) ).

• **CGIAR-ICRAF** documented **local adaptation strategies** in the **Upper Koshi region**, including **water harvesting, altered cropping patterns, and ecosystem-based management** to cope with reduced precipitation and pest outbreaks (Source: [10.1080/21513732.2015.1027793](https://doi.org/10.1080/21513732.2015.1027793) ).

• **CGIAR-CCAFS** identified **household and community-level adaptation practices** in **Panchase Mountain Ecological Region**, such as **livelihood diversification, multipurpose plant species use, and collaborative forest/water management** (Source: [10.3390/environments5030042](https://doi.org/10.3390/environments5030042) ).
Nepal.Mitigation: No evidence found in this sample.
Nepal.Water:
• **CGIAR-IWMI** modeled **hydrological changes in the Chamelia Watershed (Mahakali Basin)**, projecting **8–15% increases in streamflow** by 2100 due to climate change, with implications for water infrastructure planning (Source: [10.1016/j.scitotenv.2018.09.053](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.scitotenv.2018.09.053) ).

• **CGIAR-IWMI** analyzed **seasonal flow variability in the Karnali River Basin**, forecasting **reduced winter/spring discharge (3–23%)** and **increased monsoon/post-monsoon flows (up to 51%)**, alongside rising irrigation demands for paddy and wheat (Source: [10.2166/nh.2025.193](https://doi.org/10.2166/nh.2025.193) ).

• **CGIAR-WLE** studied **local water harvesting and management adaptations** in the **Upper Koshi**, linking reduced precipitation to shifts in paddy cultivation and pest prevalence (Source: [10.1080/21513732.2015.1027793](https://doi.org/10.1080/21513732.2015.1027793) ).

Netherlands.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-smart agriculture, adaptation strategies, and mitigation research** in the **Netherlands** through policy engagement, modeling, and financing innovations. Specifically:
- CGIAR led **farm-household modeling workshops** to refine tools for climate adaptation, risk management, and mitigation (Source: [10568/24766](https://doi.org/10.1515/intag-2017-0043)).
- The Center developed **self-assessment tools for SMEs** to accelerate investment readiness in sustainable finance, funded by Dutch ministries (Source: [10568/114725](https://doi.org/10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en)).
- CGIAR collaborated with Dutch partners to **catalyze private-sector finance** for climate-resilient agribusiness in East Africa, leveraging Dutch government funding (Source: [10568/121858](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eja.2019.125974)).
Netherlands.Adaptation:
• CGIAR organized a **workshop in Amsterdam** to advance farm-household modeling for climate adaptation, risk management, and mitigation, emphasizing data repositories and tool accessibility (Source: [10568/24766](https://doi.org/10.1515/intag-2017-0043)).

• The Center designed a **SME self-assessment tool** to integrate impact and investment scans, fostering climate-smart agriculture adoption, with support from the Dutch Ministry of Nature, Agriculture, and Food Quality (Source: [10568/114725](https://doi.org/10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en)).

• CGIAR partnered with **SNV and the Dutch Government** to catalyze **€34 million in private-sector co-investment** for climate-resilient agribusiness, benefiting 237,000 farmers in East Africa (Source: [10568/121858](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eja.2019.125974)).

• Researchers modeled **adaptation strategies** (e.g., new crop varieties and irrigation tech) using the **IMPACT model**, quantifying their effects on yields, prices, and food security (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en](https://doi.org/10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en)).

• CGIAR facilitated **participatory climate services** (e.g., PICSA) in East Africa, supported by Dutch organizations like NWO-WOTRO, to upscale climate-smart agriculture (Source: [10568/92851](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eja.2019.125974)).

• The Center analyzed **protein-rich crop suitability** under future climates, identifying regional shifts (e.g., quinoa expansion in northern Europe) and recommending breeding strategies for abiotic stress tolerance (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eja.2019.125974](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eja.2019.125974)).
Netherlands.Mitigation:
• CGIAR standardized **eddy covariance data processing** for CO₂ and energy flux measurements in the **Integrated Carbon Observation System (ICOS)**, ensuring comparability across European stations (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1515/intag-2017-0043](https://doi.org/10.1515/intag-2017-0043)).

• The Center piloted **low-emission dairy mitigation options** in China, in collaboration with Wageningen University & Research, to evaluate feasibility and stakeholder engagement (Source: [10568/111194](https://doi.org/10.7910/DVN/VZP5CG)).

• CGIAR’s workshop in Amsterdam addressed **mitigation options** as part of farm-household modeling, highlighting the need for standardized tools and data repositories (Source: [10568/24766](https://doi.org/10.1515/intag-2017-0043)).
Netherlands.Water: No evidence found in this sample.

New Zealand.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-smart agriculture (CSA) and agricultural resilience** in **New Zealand** through policy analysis, GHG mitigation research, and adaptation strategies. Specifically:
- The Center analyzed **integrated national CSA policies** to address climate change, food insecurity, and sustainability (Source: **10568/41648**).
- Researchers explored **adaptation measures** like new crop varieties and irrigation technologies to enhance climate resilience (Source: **https://doi.org/10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en**).
- CGIAR contributed to **low-emission dairy farming** and GHG inventory practices, aligning with global mitigation efforts (Sources: **10568/80890**, **10568/105709**, **10568/110571**, **10568/121310**).
New Zealand.Adaptation:
• CGIAR examined **New Zealand’s national climate-smart agriculture (CSA) policies**, highlighting sustainability strategies, capacity building, and institutional innovation to link climate adaptation with food security (Source: **10568/41648**).
• The Center modeled **adaptation strategies**—including R&D for climate-resilient crop varieties and irrigation upgrades—to mitigate climate risks in agriculture (Source: **https://doi.org/10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en**).
• CGIAR hosted **Adaptation Futures 2025** in Christchurch, advancing **locally led, equitable adaptation** and climate-resilient food systems through partnerships with policymakers and communities (Source: **10568/177760**).
New Zealand.Mitigation:
• CGIAR analyzed **livestock GHG emissions** in New Zealand, emphasizing measurement, reporting, and verification (MRV) improvements to support Paris Agreement commitments (Source: **10568/80890**).
• Researchers presented **dairy sector GHG reduction strategies**, including low-emission development options, at global workshops (Sources: **10568/105709**, **10568/110571**).
• The Center contributed to **global agricultural climate action** by showcasing New Zealand’s GHG inventory practices and mitigation frameworks (Source: **10568/109424**).
• CGIAR supported **East African and New Zealand collaborations** on low-emission livestock policies, leveraging New Zealand’s expertise in GHG mitigation (Source: **10568/121310**).
New Zealand.Water: No evidence found in this sample.

Nicaragua.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-smart agricultural adaptation** in **Nicaragua** through participatory vulnerability assessments, crop-specific adaptation frameworks, and citizen science-driven variety management. Specifically:
- **CIAT** developed **climate-risk management interventions** (e.g., stress-adapted crop germplasm, conservation agriculture) to stabilize smallholder incomes and reduce poverty (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agsy.2018.01.019](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agsy.2018.01.019)).
- **CIP** and **CIAT** leveraged **citizen science trials** to generate scalable, climate-informed variety recommendations for beans and wheat, improving risk analysis and geographic extrapolation (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.1813720116](https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.1813720116)).
Nicaragua.Adaptation:
• **CIAT** assessed **climate suitability shifts for coffee** in Nicaragua, designing a **two-dimensional adaptation framework** (incremental vs. transformative) to guide long-term planning across altitudes (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-016-1788-9](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-016-1788-9) and [https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0088463](https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0088463) for Mesoamerica).

• **CGIAR’s Climate Risk Vulnerability Assessment** mapped **hotspots for rice/maize decline** in Nicaragua, combining exposure (flooding, drought) with adaptive capacity (infrastructure, education) to inform **National Adaptation Plans (NAPs)** (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0213641](https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0213641)).

• **CIAT** and **CIP** developed **participatory vulnerability indices** for coffee farmers, identifying **flexible adaptation strategies** (e.g., agroforestry transformations) to address **climatic suitability loss** and weak post-harvest infrastructure (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0088463](https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0088463) and [https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-019-45491-7](https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-019-45491-7)).

• **CGIAR** modeled **coupled impacts of climate change on coffee and pollinators**, revealing **73–88% loss in coffee-suitable areas** by 2050, and proposed **shade adjustment/crop rotation** as targeted management practices (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.1617940114](https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.1617940114)).

• **CIAT** ranked **municipal adaptive capacity** in Central America, highlighting disparities between urban (higher capacity) and drought-prone rural areas, to prioritize **innovation and action for adaptation** (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-016-1792-0](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-016-1792-0)).
Nicaragua.Mitigation: • **CGIAR** analyzed **forest landscape restoration projects** in Latin America (including Nicaragua’s drylands), quantifying their potential to **store additional aboveground biomass** as part of the **Bonn Challenge** (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3390/f10060510](https://doi.org/10.3390/f10060510)).
Nicaragua.Water: No evidence found in this sample.

Niger.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-smart agriculture (CSA) and adaptive resilience** in **Niger** through evidence-based interventions targeting smallholder farmers. Specifically:
- CGIAR researchers evaluated **climate-resilient crop varieties, agroforestry, and water conservation practices** as critical adaptation strategies for Niger’s rainfed agriculture (Sources: [1](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jclepro.2018.03.199), [2](https://doi.org/10.3390/su14052847)).
- The Center assessed **household-level adaptation strategies** (e.g., crop diversification, income diversification, and improved agronomic practices) and their impact on food security and income, demonstrating measurable benefits for rural households (Source: [2](https://doi.org/10.3390/su14052847)).
Niger.Adaptation:
• CGIAR’s **Climate-Smart Agriculture (CSA) research** identified **agroforestry (farmer-managed natural regenerations), soil/water conservation technologies (zai, half-moon, contour ridges), and climate information services** as high-potential adaptation strategies for Niger’s climate-vulnerable farming systems, despite challenges like limited policy support and financing (Source: [1](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jclepro.2018.03.199)).

• Researchers quantified **household adaptation strategies** (e.g., crop diversification, drought-resilient varieties, irrigation/water conservation) in Niger’s Sahelian regions, linking their adoption to **7–9% higher food security** and **FCFA 721,526 income gains** compared to non-adopters, while highlighting determinants like access to credit, extension services, and market integration (Source: [2](https://doi.org/10.3390/su14052847)).

• CGIAR evaluated **cultivar adaptation potential** for Niger’s key rainfed cereals (maize, millet, sorghum, wheat), finding that **better-adapted cultivars could offset 67% of projected yield losses in West Africa by 2050**, though **33% of production faces unaddressed adaptation gaps** under climate change (Source: [3](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eja.2024.127137)).

• The Center analyzed **agricultural diversification** as a resilience strategy, showing that households with **higher farming diversity** (e.g., food/livestock/cash crops) achieve greater food security, particularly in regions with **500–1000mm rainfall and 17–22% variability**—conditions critical for Niger’s agroecological zones (Source: [4](https://doi.org/10.1111/gcb.14158)).

• CGIAR’s **cost-benefit framework** prioritized **improved seeds, conservation agriculture, and good agricultural practices** as economically viable CSA options for Niger’s smallholders, with **net present values (NPV) up to US$28,044** in select value chains (Source: [5](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.heliyon.2022.e09228)).

• Researchers linked **agroforestry** to **planetary health benefits** in Niger, including altered microclimates and hydrology, while noting its potential to **reduce infectious disease risks** despite some trade-offs (Source: [6](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.oneear.2019.10.017)).

• CGIAR’s **multi-country analysis** (including Niger) revealed that **access to weather information, assets, and social institutions** drives farm-associated adaptations (e.g., improved crop varieties, land management), though effectiveness varies by context (Source: [7](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.gloenvcha.2013.12.011)).

• The Center demonstrated that **bundling CSA practices** (e.g., climate-smart groundnut varieties + intercropping + organic fertilizers) yields **greater food security and productivity gains** than single interventions in West Africa, including Niger (Source: [8](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.gloenvcha.2023.102697)).
Niger.Mitigation: • CGIAR’s research in West Africa (including Niger) framed **climate-smart agriculture (CSA) as a dual strategy** for **GHG mitigation and resilience**, emphasizing **climate-smart groundnut varieties and bundling practices** to reduce emissions while increasing yields and food security (Source: [8](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.gloenvcha.2023.102697)).
Niger.Water: • CGIAR developed the **TAMSAT daily rainfall dataset** (v2.0/v3.0), providing **near-real-time, high-resolution rainfall monitoring** for Niger and Africa, critical for water resource management and climate-informed agriculture (Source: [9](https://doi.org/10.1038/sdata.2017.63)).

Nigeria.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-resilient agricultural systems** in **Nigeria** through evidence-based adaptation strategies and risk management interventions. Specifically:
- CGIAR assessed the potential of **cultivar adaptation** to offset climate change impacts on key rainfed cereals (maize, millet, sorghum, wheat) in Nigeria, demonstrating that better-adapted cultivars could stabilize yields under future climate scenarios (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eja.2024.127137](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eja.2024.127137)).
- The Center analyzed **farmers’ perceptions of climate change** in southwestern Nigeria, validating their observations of delayed rainfall onset and reduced growing-season precipitation with meteorological data, while identifying adaptation strategies like conservation agriculture and diversified production systems (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.wace.2016.12.001](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.wace.2016.12.001)).
Nigeria.Adaptation:
• CGIAR evaluated **climate-risk management interventions** (e.g., stress-adapted crop germplasm, conservation agriculture, diversified systems) to stabilize production and incomes for smallholder farmers in risk-prone agricultural systems, though evidence of poverty reduction remains limited (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agsy.2018.01.019](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agsy.2018.01.019)).

• Researchers at CGIAR demonstrated that **agricultural diversification** (e.g., integrating food crops, cash crops, livestock) enhances household food security in Nigeria, particularly in regions with moderate rainfall variability (500–1000mm/year), while highlighting land and market constraints (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1111/gcb.14158](https://doi.org/10.1111/gcb.14158)).

• CGIAR’s **genomic-assisted breeding** programs were highlighted as critical for developing climate-resilient crop varieties, with a focus on yield stability and environmental resilience to climate variability (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1111/pbi.12467](https://doi.org/10.1111/pbi.12467)).

• The Center conducted a **cost-benefit analysis** of climate-smart agricultural practices (e.g., improved seeds, conservation agriculture) in Nigeria, identifying viable options like improved seed adoption in soybean and peanut value chains (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.heliyon.2022.e09228](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.heliyon.2022.e09228)).

• CGIAR reviewed **climate-smart agriculture policies** in West Africa, including Nigeria, to improve resilience in crop, livestock, and fishery sectors through tailored adaptation strategies and institutional mechanisms (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1186/s40066-016-0075-3](https://doi.org/10.1186/s40066-016-0075-3)).

• Researchers assessed **drought characteristics and rainfall variability** in Nigeria’s agro-climatic zones, confirming farmers’ perceptions of increased dry spells (3–10 days) and recommending subsidized adaptation equipment (e.g., crop hybrids) to enhance coping mechanisms (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.scitotenv.2018.02.196](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.scitotenv.2018.02.196)).

• CGIAR explored **agroforestry’s role** in altering microclimates and hydrology to improve resilience, though uptake barriers (e.g., social/environmental drivers) were identified as limiting future adoption in Nigeria (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.oneear.2019.10.017](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.oneear.2019.10.017)).
Nigeria.Mitigation: No evidence found in this sample.
Nigeria.Water: • CGIAR developed the **TAMSAT daily satellite-based rainfall dataset** (v2.0/v3.0) to provide operational, high-resolution rainfall monitoring for Nigeria, enabling improved climate-informed decision-making (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1038/sdata.2017.63](https://doi.org/10.1038/sdata.2017.63)).

Northern Mariana Islands.narrative: CGIAR **did not conduct climate-related research** in the Northern Mariana Islands based on the provided sample. The sole available record pertains to **economic data collection** rather than climate science or agricultural research.
Northern Mariana Islands.Adaptation: No evidence found in this sample.
Northern Mariana Islands.Mitigation: No evidence found in this sample.
Northern Mariana Islands.Water: No evidence found in this sample.

Norway.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-smart food systems transformation** in **Norway** through strategic partnerships and research-driven adaptation and mitigation frameworks. Specifically:
- CGIAR hosted the **Alliance of Champions for Food Systems Transformation (ACF)**, funded by Norway, to accelerate climate mitigation, adaptation, and resilience in global food systems (10568/170000).
Norway.Adaptation:
• CGIAR researchers surveyed **European forest owners and managers** to assess perceptions of **forest genetic resources (FGR) management** as a climate adaptation strategy, highlighting the need for evidence-based guidance to counteract drought and pest threats (https://doi.org/10.1007/s10342-020-01311-6).
• CGIAR contributed to **gender-integrated monitoring frameworks** for climate-smart agriculture (CSA) in **MAP-Norway**, proposing indicators to measure women’s empowerment, equity in decision-making, and food security outcomes (https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-018-2231-1).
• CGIAR modeled **adaptation strategies in agriculture** (e.g., new crop varieties and irrigation technologies) using the **IMPACT model**, quantifying investment needs for resilience under climate change (https://doi.org/10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en).
Norway.Mitigation:
• CGIAR standardized **eddy covariance data processing** for CO₂ and energy flux measurements in **ICOS ecosystem stations**, ensuring comparability and reliability of greenhouse gas monitoring across Europe (https://doi.org/10.1515/intag-2017-0043).
• CGIAR analyzed **Norway’s bilateral REDD+ agreements** (with Tanzania, Brazil, Guyana, and Indonesia), identifying challenges in **results-based aid (RBA) design** for deforestation mitigation, including performance criteria and funding credibility (https://doi.org/10.1111/rode.12271).
Norway.Water: No evidence found in this sample.

Oman.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **agricultural R&D infrastructure and forage innovation** in **Oman** through data-driven policy support and farmer training. Specifically:
- **IFPRI** compiled national-level agricultural R&D metrics (financial, human, institutional) to inform investment strategies (doi:10.7910/DVN/IR12XU).
- **CGIAR researchers** introduced **Farmers Field Schools (FFS)** to demonstrate **Buffel grass and spineless cactus cultivation** as drought-resilient forage alternatives (10568/36076).
Oman.Adaptation: • **CGIAR researchers** established **Farmers Field Schools (FFS)** in Oman to promote **drought-tolerant forage crops** (Buffel grass, spineless cactus) as adaptive livestock feed solutions for marginal lands (10568/36076).
Oman.Mitigation: No evidence found in this sample.
Oman.Water: No evidence found in this sample.

Pakistan.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-resilient agriculture and water security** in **Pakistan** through targeted research on heat-tolerant crops, hydrological modeling, and gender-inclusive adaptation strategies. Specifically:
- **CIMMYT** developed heat-tolerant maize varieties to mitigate yield losses under projected temperature increases (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1007/s00704-016-1931-6](https://doi.org/10.1007/s00704-016-1931-6)).
- **ICARDA** and **CIAT** assessed climate-agriculture-gender inequality hotspots to address structural barriers limiting women’s adaptive capacities (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3389/fsufs.2023.1197809](https://doi.org/10.3389/fsufs.2023.1197809)).
Pakistan.Adaptation:
• **CIMMYT** developed heat-tolerant maize varieties to reduce yield losses (3.3–12.2% in rainfed/irrigated systems) under future climate scenarios, with potential yield gains of up to **93%** in irrigated conditions (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1007/s00704-016-1931-6](https://doi.org/10.1007/s00704-016-1931-6) ).

• **CGIAR’s Climate Change, Agriculture, and Food Security (CCAFS)** analyzed climate-resilient agricultural value chains in South Asia, emphasizing landscape-level adaptation and ecosystem-based strategies to protect food security (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1080/14693062.2018.1501329](https://doi.org/10.1080/14693062.2018.1501329) ).

• **World Agroforestry Centre (ICRAF)** and **CIAT** highlighted gendered climate risks in agri-food systems, mapping hotspots where women face disproportionate vulnerability due to droughts, floods, and shortened growing seasons (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3389/fsufs.2023.1197809](https://doi.org/10.3389/fsufs.2023.1197809) ).

• **CIMMYT** and **IRRI** promoted climate-smart agriculture (e.g., Direct Seeded Rice Consortium) to enhance resilience to extreme weather in rice-based systems (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3390/cli8020035](https://doi.org/10.3390/cli8020035) ).

• **ICARDA** assessed climate change impacts on wheat in South Asia, demonstrating that **timely sowing and input efficiency** could offset yield reductions (6–25%) by mid-century (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3354/cr01212](https://doi.org/10.3354/cr01212) ).
Pakistan.Mitigation:
• **CGIAR’s CCAFS** evaluated Nationally Determined Contributions (NDCs) in South Asia, identifying income-enhancing mitigation technologies (e.g., agroforestry) that reduce greenhouse gas emissions without compromising food security (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1126/science.1217941](https://doi.org/10.1126/science.1217941) ).

• **CGIAR** contributed to global mitigation efforts by demonstrating that **agroforestry** (e.g., in Niger) sequesters carbon while improving yields, with potential for replication in Pakistan’s agroecological zones (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1126/science.1217941](https://doi.org/10.1126/science.1217941) ).
Pakistan.Water:
• **ICARDA** and **IWMI** used the **SWAT hydrological model** to project **7–11% increases in consumptive water use** in Pakistan’s Lower Chenab Canal (Indus Basin) by 2020, requiring adaptive water allocation plans (Source: [https://doi.org/10.2166/nh.2016.102](https://doi.org/10.2166/nh.2016.102) ).

• **IWMI** analyzed climate projections (CMIP6) for the **Astore River Basin**, revealing **reduced snowmelt/groundwater contributions** and **increased glacier melt/rainfall runoff**, with streamflow declines of **1–11%** by 2070 (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1007/s11629-022-7872-x](https://doi.org/10.1007/s11629-022-7872-x) ).

Palestine.narrative:
CGIAR’s International Water Management Institute (IWMI) prioritized **climate and water resilience** in **Palestine refugee camps and host communities** through **integrated risk mapping and participatory adaptation frameworks**. Specifically:
- IWMI developed **watershed-scale climate risk hotspot mapping** to identify flash flood, drought, and infrastructure vulnerabilities in refugee camps, enabling anticipatory action and adaptation planning (Source: **10568/181646**).
Palestine.Adaptation: • CGIAR’s IWMI led **integrated watershed and climate risk hotspot mapping** in Palestine refugee camps (Jordan), combining modeling, humanitarian data, and participatory inputs to strengthen disaster risk reduction and adaptation planning (Source: **10568/181646**).
Palestine.Mitigation: No evidence found in this sample.
Palestine.Water: • CGIAR contributed **multi-year Feed the Future (FTF) water-related indicators** for Palestine (2012–2017), tracking water productivity and resource management metrics across the region (Sources: **hdl:20.500.11766.1/FK2/IJPUJG**, **hdl:20.500.11766.1/FK2/XINDTC**, **hdl:20.500.11766.1/FK2/CJTCIY**, **hdl:20.500.11766.1/FK2/HKYRDC**, **hdl:20.500.11766.1/FK2/GEPLSF**, **hdl:20.500.11766.1/FK2/FOHYFF**).

Panama.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-resilient agriculture and forest systems** in **Mesoamerica (including Panama)** through **species suitability assessments, agroforestry transformation, and policy frameworks**. Specifically:
- **CIAT** evaluated climate suitability for cocoa and agroforestry trees to inform land-use planning under climate change (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-019-45491-7](https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-019-45491-7)).
- **Bioversity/CIAT** modeled pollinator-crop coupling to guide shade management and forest conservation strategies (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.1617940114](https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.1617940114)).
Panama.Adaptation:
• **CIAT** assessed climate suitability for cocoa and agroforestry trees, revealing high vulnerability of current tree species and recommending species composition shifts to adapt coffee/cocoa systems (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-019-45491-7](https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-019-45491-7)).

• **CIAT** developed a **Species Distribution Modeling (SDM) framework** to evaluate climate suitability for coffee, cocoa, and agroforestry trees across Mesoamerica (including Panama), enabling data-driven adaptation planning (Source: [doi:10.7910/DVN/0O1GW1](doi:10.7910/DVN/0O1GW1)).

• **CIAT** analyzed **pollinator-crop interactions** under climate change, identifying regions where coffee suitability declines while bee richness increases, and proposing **shade adjustment, crop rotation, and forest conservation** as targeted adaptation measures (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.1617940114](https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.1617940114)).

• **CGIAR** led the **formulation of the *Estrategia Agricultura Sostenible Adaptada al Clima (EASAC)*** for Central America (SICA), a participatory policy framework to integrate climate-adapted agriculture and risk management (Source: [10568/89328](10568/89328)).

• **CIAT** evaluated **biological corridors (BCs)** as adaptation tools for plant dispersal in fragmented Mesoamerican landscapes, identifying high-altitude corridors as critical for species migration under climate change (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1002/ece3.672](https://doi.org/10.1002/ece3.672)).

• **CGIAR** designed **Local Technical Agroclimatic Committees (MTAs)** as participatory platforms to generate climate-risk recommendations for farmers, fostering dialogue among scientists, policymakers, and producers (Source: [10568/114943](10568/114943), [10568/108780](10568/108780)).

• **CIAT/CCAFS** developed a **Climate-Smart Food Systems (CSFS) framework** to scale resilient agroforestry (coffee/cocoa) and silvipastoral systems, addressing productivity and climate risks (Source: [10568/116921](10568/116921)).
Panama.Mitigation:
• **CGIAR** researched **greenhouse gas (GHG) mitigation in forests**, highlighting gaps in policy integration for rural development and natural resource management in Latin America (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3390/su9101831](https://doi.org/10.3390/su9101831)).

• **CIAT/CCAFS** integrated **mitigation considerations** into the **MTA manual**, including **emissions measurement, carbon markets, and MRV (Measurement, Reporting, Verification)** for climate-smart agriculture (Source: [10568/114943](10568/114943)).

• **CGIAR** analyzed **silvopastoral systems (SPS)** as mitigation opportunities, advocating for their inclusion in national GHG inventories to meet **Paris Agreement commitments**, with case studies from **Panama, Colombia, and Peru** (Source: [10568/111256](10568/111256)).

• **CIAT/CCAFS** identified **high-impact business models** for Climate-Smart Food Systems (CSFS), emphasizing **carbon sequestration, emissions reductions, and access to carbon markets** for agroforestry and regenerative livestock (Source: [10568/116921](10568/116921)).
Panama.Water: No evidence found in this sample.

Papua New Guinea.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-smart adaptation and socio-economic resilience** in **Papua New Guinea (PNG)** through trans-disciplinary research, gender-responsive climate services, and climate-resilient agricultural systems. Specifically:
- **Bioversity International** developed climate-adaptive crop varieties (taro and sweet potato) using genebank resources and GIS-based climate projections to enhance drought resistance and farmer adaptability (10568/49620).
- **CGIAR researchers** assessed gender disparities in access to climate services, recommending ICTs and women’s group inclusion to address socio-cultural barriers (https://doi.org/10.1080/17565529.2019.1613216).
Papua New Guinea.Adaptation:
• **CGIAR’s climate resilience research** analyzed PNG’s vulnerability to extreme weather (droughts, cyclones) and projected **1.6–6.4% taro yield reductions** under climate models, recommending drought- and flood-tolerant crop varieties (10568/173654).

• **Bioversity International** partnered with NARI to **characterize and distribute climate-resilient taro/sweet potato varieties**, integrating GIS-based climate scenarios to guide farmer adaptation (10568/49620).

• **CGIAR’s Pacific food systems research** highlighted risks to fisheries (coral bleaching) and agriculture (flooding/landslides), emphasizing policy tools for resilience among fishers and farmers (https://doi.org/10.2499/p15738coll2.136871).

• **CGIAR-led surveys** (2018, 2023) mapped rural household food security, dietary diversity, and economic shocks in PNG, identifying pathways to **resilient local food systems** (doi:10.7910/DVN/ZXRD6N, doi:10.7910/DVN/BYZMZ6).

• **CGIAR’s gender-responsive adaptation work** revealed disparities in women’s access to climate information (ICTs, group processes) and proposed **cross-sectoral collaboration** to meet their needs (https://doi.org/10.1080/17565529.2019.1613216).

• **CGIAR’s Pacific Islands study** framed PNG’s limited adaptive capacity (69% agriculture-dependent) as a barrier, urging **socioeconomic diversification** to reduce climate vulnerability (10568/145066).

• **CGIAR’s ODA analysis** noted PNG’s **high vulnerability (EAAPC: 0.57)** but **low adaptation-focused aid**, advocating for targeted climate finance (https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pclm.0000382).
Papua New Guinea.Mitigation: No evidence found in this sample.
Papua New Guinea.Water: • **CGIAR’s climate impact research** projected **increasing rainfall (vs. moderate temperature rise)** in PNG, warning of **extreme droughts/floods** and recommending **crop diversification** to navigate precipitation variability (10568/173654).

Paraguay.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-smart agriculture, silvopastoral systems, and climate risk management** in **Paraguay** through **scaling Local Technical Agroclimatic Committees (MTAs), climate services, and policy-informed mitigation strategies**. Specifically:
- **CIAT** developed **climate-smart food systems (CSFS) business models** (e.g., agroforestry, silvipastoral systems) to enhance resilience and productivity (Source: [10568/116921](https://doi.org/10.3390/su9101831)).
- **CCAFS** implemented **Climate Risk Management (CRM) strategies** to strengthen community-level resilience and decision-making (Source: [10568/121217](https://doi.org/10.3390/su9101831)).
Paraguay.Adaptation:
• **CGIAR-CCAFS** facilitated **Local Technical Agroclimatic Committees (MTAs)** in Paraguay to foster dialogue among farmers, scientists, and institutions, generating **climate-risk reduction recommendations** tailored to local needs (Source: [10568/108780](https://doi.org/10.3390/su9101831)).

• **CGIAR-CCAFS** supported **disaster risk management** in Paraguay by aligning the **Regional Strategy for Disaster Risk Management** with context-specific MTA implementation, alongside FAO (Source: [10568/121200](https://doi.org/10.3390/su9101831)).

• **CGIAR-CIAT** scaled **climate-smart agriculture (CSA) practices** (e.g., silvipastoral systems, agroforestry) to improve **adaptation and productivity**, while addressing challenges like **access to capital and technical assistance** (Source: [10568/116921](https://doi.org/10.3390/su9101831)).

• **CGIAR-CCAFS** strengthened **climate resilience** through **capacity-building workshops** for MTAs, enabling Paraguay to adopt **climate-smart decision-making frameworks** (Source: [10568/174584](https://doi.org/10.3390/su9101831)).
Paraguay.Mitigation:
• **CGIAR researchers** quantified **proximate drivers of deforestation** (e.g., pasture expansion) in **Western Paraguay**, linking land-use change to **carbon losses** and informing **REDD+ interventions** (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1088/1748-9326/10/12/124004](https://doi.org/10.1088/1748-9326/10/12/124004)).

• **CGIAR-CIAT** highlighted **silvopastoral systems (SPS)** as a **mitigation tool** in Paraguay, demonstrating their potential to **reduce GHG emissions (CH₄, N₂O)** and **increase carbon stocks** while supporting **Nationally Determined Contributions (NDCs)** (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-031-43063-3_2](https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-031-43063-3_2)).

• **CGIAR-CCAFS** integrated **mitigation considerations** into **Local Technical Agroclimatic Committees (MTAs)**, emphasizing **emissions measurement, carbon markets, and MRV (Measurement, Reporting, Verification)** for climate-smart practices (Source: [10568/114943](https://doi.org/10.3390/su9101831)).
Paraguay.Water: No evidence found in this sample.

Peru.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-resilient agriculture and forest-based mitigation** in **Peru** through targeted research on genetic adaptation, crop diversification, and land restoration. Specifically:
- **CGIAR’s Bioversity and CIP** enhanced plant breeders’ capacity to access and utilize genetic diversity for climate-adaptive traits, addressing administrative and technical barriers to germplasm access (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3390/plants9091129](https://doi.org/10.3390/plants9091129)).
- **CIAT and CIP** mapped climate-tolerant cacao genotypes and modeled future suitability shifts to guide conservation and breeding strategies (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1111/ddi.13294](https://doi.org/10.1111/ddi.13294)).
Peru.Adaptation:
• **CGIAR’s Bioversity and CIP** analyzed global survey data to identify challenges in accessing genetic resources for climate adaptation, highlighting administrative/legal and technical barriers faced by public breeders in Peru and other developing countries (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3390/plants9091129](https://doi.org/10.3390/plants9091129) ).

• **CGIAR’s CIAT and partners** studied farmers’ perceptions of crop diversity on the Bolivian Altiplano (adjacent to Peru), revealing maladaptation risks from introduced commercial crops and the resilience of traditional varieties like bitter potatoes and wild quinoa relatives (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1007/s10668-016-9906-4](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10668-016-9906-4) ).

• **CGIAR’s CIP** modeled future climate impacts on cacao cultivation in Peru, identifying regions where tolerant genotypes may persist and guiding targeted collection missions (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1111/ddi.13294](https://doi.org/10.1111/ddi.13294) ).

• **CGIAR’s CIAT** coupled coffee suitability and pollinator distribution models in Latin America, demonstrating that 34–51% of future coffee-suitable areas in Peru may face **negative coupling** (declining bee richness), necessitating shade adjustment and forest conservation (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.1617940114](https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.1617940114) ).

• **CGIAR’s researchers** analyzed Peruvian farmers’ responses to climate shocks (drought/abnormal rainfall), finding that water conservation measures increase after drought but decline with high precipitation, while pesticide use spikes post-drought (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1111/ajae.12177](https://doi.org/10.1111/ajae.12177) ).

• **CGIAR’s World Agroforestry Centre (ICRAF)** linked rural livelihood diversification in Andean-Amazon foothills (Peru/Colombia) to reduced climate vulnerability, though most households lack agroecological practices (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pclm.0000051](https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pclm.0000051) ).
Peru.Mitigation:
• **CGIAR’s ICRAF** overviewed 154 land restoration projects in Latin America (including Peru), emphasizing natural/assisted regeneration over plantations to maximize aboveground biomass for GHG mitigation under the **Bonn Challenge** (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3390/f10060510](https://doi.org/10.3390/f10060510) ).

• **CGIAR’s researchers** assessed **REDD+ programs in Peru**, revealing misalignment between interventions and local deforestation drivers (e.g., agricultural expansion, mining), with remote sensing and household surveys identifying critical gaps (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.envsci.2020.08.002](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.envsci.2020.08.002) ).

• **CGIAR’s ICRAF** synthesized deforestation dynamics in Peru, linking land-use change to **agricultural expansion, mining, and infrastructure**, and advocating for integrated governance to mitigate emissions (Source: [https://doi.org/10.4160/cip.2023.12.007](https://doi.org/10.4160/cip.2023.12.007) ).
Peru.Water: No evidence found in this sample.

Philippines.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-resilient agriculture and mitigation strategies** in the **Philippines** through **crop adaptation, water-efficient technologies, and greenhouse gas reduction**. Specifically:
- **CGIAR’s Climate Change, Agriculture and Food Security (CCAFS) and International Rice Research Institute (IRRI)** developed **climate-adapted rice varieties** and **risk-management interventions** to stabilize yields and farmer incomes (Sources: [Adaptation: 10.1016/j.agsy.2018.01.019](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agsy.2018.01.019), [Adaptation: 10.1371/journal.pone.0066218](https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0066218)).
- **IRRI and CGIAR’s Genetic Resources Platform** advanced **plant breeding for climate-resilient traits** and assessed barriers to accessing germplasm for adaptation (Sources: [Adaptation: 10.3390/plants9091129](https://doi.org/10.3390/plants9091129)).
- **CGIAR’s Water Management Research** scaled **Alternate Wetting and Drying (AWD) technology** to reduce water use by **30%** while cutting methane emissions from rice paddies (Sources: [Water/Mitigation: 10.1371/journal.pone.0145268](https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0145268), [Mitigation: 10.1029/2022jg006848](https://doi.org/10.1029/2022jg006848)).
- **CGIAR’s Regional Climate Resilience Programs** mapped **climate risks and adaptation options** for Luzon, Visayas, and Mindanao, identifying **on-farm and off-farm resilience strategies** (Sources: [Adaptation: 10568/113513](https://doi.org/10.1088/1748-9326/8/3/035009), [Adaptation: 10568/113512](https://doi.org/10.1088/1748-9326/8/3/035009), [Adaptation: 10568/113568](https://doi.org/10.1088/1748-9326/8/3/035009)).
Philippines.Adaptation:
• **CGIAR’s CCAFS and IRRI** demonstrated that **stress-adapted crop germplasm, conservation agriculture, and diversified production systems** stabilize yields and reduce climate-related risks for smallholder farmers in dryland regions (Source: [10.1016/j.agsy.2018.01.019](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agsy.2018.01.019)).

• **IRRI researchers** identified **transpirational cooling as a key factor** in rice quality, developing breeding strategies to improve **chalkiness resistance and head rice yield** under varying humidity and temperature conditions (Source: [10.1371/journal.pone.0066218](https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0066218)).

• **CGIAR’s Genetic Resources Platform** analyzed challenges in accessing **climate-adaptive germplasm**, highlighting administrative and technical barriers faced by public breeders in integrating genetic diversity for resilience (Source: [10.3390/plants9091129](https://doi.org/10.3390/plants9091129)).

• **CGIAR’s Regional Programs** conducted **climate risk profiling** for Luzon, Visayas, and Mindanao, mapping **crop vulnerabilities to typhoons and drought** and proposing **on-farm adaptation options** (Sources: [10568/113513](https://doi.org/10.1088/1748-9326/8/3/035009), [10568/113512](https://doi.org/10.1088/1748-9326/8/3/035009), [10568/113568](https://doi.org/10.1088/1748-9326/8/3/035009)).

• **CGIAR promoted trans-disciplinary networks** (e.g., **Direct Seeded Rice Consortium**) to integrate **climate-smart agriculture** and enhance resilience to extreme weather events (Source: [10.3390/cli8020035](https://doi.org/10.3390/cli8020035)).
Philippines.Mitigation:
• **CGIAR’s Water Management Research** developed **Alternate Wetting and Drying (AWD)** as a **dual-purpose technology**, reducing **water use by 30%** while cutting **methane (CH₄) emissions by 23%** in Philippine rice systems (Sources: [10.1371/journal.pone.0145268](https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0145268), [10.1029/2022jg006848](https://doi.org/10.1029/2022jg006848)).

• **CGIAR’s modeling studies** quantified AWD’s **national-scale mitigation potential**, showing **seasonal variability** (higher reductions in dry seasons) and emphasizing **CH₄ dominance (>95%)** in rice-related radiative forcing (Source: [10.1029/2022jg006848](https://doi.org/10.1029/2022jg006848)).

• **CGIAR’s policy analyses** evaluated **forest carbon sequestration** as a **low-cost mitigation option**, though findings warned of **trade-offs with agricultural output and food prices** for poor households (Source: [10.1088/1748-9326/8/3/035009](https://doi.org/10.1088/1748-9326/8/3/035009)).
Philippines.Water: • **CGIAR’s Water Solutions Initiative** designed a **spatial-temporal suitability assessment method** for **Alternate Wetting and Drying (AWD)**, identifying **60% of wet-season rice areas in Cagayan Province** as climatically suitable for adoption (Source: [10.1371/journal.pone.0145268](https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0145268)).

Poland.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-resilient agriculture and hydrogeological assessments** in **Poland** through targeted research on crop adaptation and shallow aquifer systems. Specifically:
- **CGIAR’s Climate Change, Agriculture, and Food Security (CCAFS) program** analyzed climate-driven shifts in protein-rich crop suitability to inform breeding and policy strategies (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eja.2019.125974](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eja.2019.125974)).
- **CGIAR’s Water, Land, and Ecosystems (WLE) program** investigated climate-induced subsidence and groundwater decline in the Gniezno Lakeland, integrating InSAR and hydrogeological data to assess aquifer resilience (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.rse.2025.114682](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.rse.2025.114682)).
Poland.Adaptation:
• **CGIAR’s CCAFS program** modeled heterogeneous impacts of climate change on protein-rich crop suitability (e.g., quinoa expansion in northern Europe, chickpea/lentil decline in southern regions) and recommended breeding programs to enhance abiotic stress tolerance in faba beans, lentils, and chickpeas (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eja.2019.125974](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eja.2019.125974)).
• **CGIAR’s IMPACT modeling team** proposed adaptation strategies—including R&D for climate-resilient crop varieties and improved irrigation technologies—to mitigate yield risks in OECD regions, with estimates for public/private investment needs (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en](https://doi.org/10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en)).
Poland.Mitigation: No evidence found in this sample.
Poland.Water: • **CGIAR’s WLE program** analyzed climate-driven groundwater decline in Poland’s Gniezno Lakeland, linking subsidence (−0.9 mm/year), hydraulic head fluctuations (8–50 cm), and vertical water exchange to drying trends (temperature +0.5°C/year, evaporation +3.8 mm/year), while highlighting aquifer resilience compared to surface water (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.rse.2025.114682](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.rse.2025.114682)).

Portugal.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-smart agricultural and forestry adaptation** in **Portugal** through evidence-based strategies for crop resilience and forest genetic resource (FGR) management. Specifically:
- **CGIAR’s Climate Change, Agriculture and Food Security (CCAFS) program** modeled shifts in protein-rich crop suitability under future climates, identifying regional adaptation needs for chickpea, lentil, and quinoa production (Source A).
- **CGIAR’s Forestry Research Center (CIFOR) and Bioversity International** surveyed European forest managers to assess perceptions of FGR-based adaptation, highlighting demand for non-local genotypes to match projected climate conditions (Source B).
- **CGIAR’s International Food Policy Research Institute (IFPRI)** analyzed adaptation strategies (e.g., new crop varieties and irrigation tech) to quantify investment needs for climate-resilient agriculture (Source C).
Portugal.Adaptation:
• **CGIAR’s CCAFS** analyzed heterogeneous impacts of climate change on protein-rich crop suitability in Europe, recommending breeding programs to improve faba bean, lentil, and chickpea tolerance to abiotic stresses and regional production shifts (e.g., quinoa expansion in northern Europe) (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eja.2019.125974).
• **CGIAR’s CIFOR and Bioversity International** surveyed 200+ European forest owners/managers to evaluate FGR-based adaptation strategies, finding support for non-local genotypes to enhance forest resilience to drought/windstorms, though local sources remained preferred (https://doi.org/10.1007/s10342-020-01311-6).
• **CGIAR’s IFPRI** modeled adaptation measures (new crop varieties + irrigation tech) using IMPACT, estimating public/private R&D investment needs to offset climate-induced yield risks (https://doi.org/10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en).
