Romania.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-resilient agricultural and forest systems** in **Romania** through targeted research on protein-rich crop adaptation and forest genetic resource (FGR) management. Specifically:
- **CGIAR’s Crop Research Centers** analyzed climate change impacts on protein-rich legume and pseudo-cereal crops, identifying regional suitability shifts and breeding needs for faba bean, lentil, and chickpea (Source: [Adaptation, Protein Crops Study](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eja.2019.125974)).
- **Bioversity International** surveyed European forest owners/managers (including Romania) to assess perceptions of FGR adaptation, recommending active management strategies for climate-resilient forest ecosystems (Source: [Adaptation, Forest Genetic Resources](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10342-020-01311-6)).
Romania.Adaptation:
• **CGIAR’s Crop Science Teams** modeled climate-induced shifts in protein-rich crop suitability (e.g., quinoa expansion in northern Europe, chickpea/lentil decline in southern Europe) and proposed breeding strategies to enhance abiotic stress tolerance for faba bean, lentil, and chickpea (Source: [Adaptation, Protein Crops Study](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eja.2019.125974)).
• **Bioversity International** conducted a pan-European survey (including Romania) to evaluate forest owner/manager attitudes toward forest genetic resource (FGR) adaptation, highlighting demand for non-local genotypes to match projected climate conditions while emphasizing evidence-based policy for active FGR management (Source: [Adaptation, Forest Genetic Resources](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10342-020-01311-6)).

Russia.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-resilient agriculture and food security in Russia** through adaptive practices, mitigation strategies, and scenario-based risk assessments. Specifically:
- **CGIAR** analyzed climate-induced threats (e.g., droughts, wildfires) to agricultural yields and food security, emphasizing region-specific adaptations (e.g., agroforestry, expanded growing seasons) to sustain productivity (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1126/science.1217941](https://doi.org/10.1126/science.1217941)).
- **CGIAR** modeled future climate scenarios (e.g., northern crop expansion vs. southern water scarcity) to inform adaptive measures for Russian agriculture (Sources: [10568/33400](10568/33400), [10568/152724](10568/152724)).
Russia.Adaptation:
• **CGIAR** highlighted climate change as a growing threat to Russian agricultural yields, advocating for alternative practices (e.g., agroforestry) to maintain productivity amid extreme weather (e.g., 2010 wildfires, droughts) and governance challenges (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1126/science.1217941](https://doi.org/10.1126/science.1217941)).

• **CGIAR** assessed risks of climate change (e.g., expanded growing seasons in the north vs. droughts in the south) and proposed adaptive measures for crop production, including water management and pest control (Sources: [https://doi.org/10.5018/economics-ejournal.ja.2013-39](https://doi.org/10.5018/economics-ejournal.ja.2013-39), [10568/152724](10568/152724)).

• **CGIAR** supported APEC economies (including Russia) in developing a **Multi-Year Action Plan for Climate Change and Food Security (2018–2021)**, facilitating stakeholder consultations and policy alignment (Source: [10568/121197](10568/121197)).

• **CGIAR** warned that climate-induced disruptions (e.g., wildfires, floods) and population growth (9B by 2050) will strain sustainable food production, requiring adaptive risk management (Sources: [https://doi.org/10.2499/9780896291867](https://doi.org/10.2499/9780896291867), [https://doi.org/10.2499/9780896291874](https://doi.org/10.2499/9780896291874)).
Russia.Mitigation: • **CGIAR** demonstrated that **agroforestry** (e.g., 5M hectares in Niger) and **reduced agricultural emissions** (e.g., 28% in Denmark) can sequester carbon while improving yields, offering scalable mitigation strategies for Russia (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1126/science.1217941](https://doi.org/10.1126/science.1217941)).
Russia.Water: • **CGIAR** identified **water scarcity** as a critical climate risk in southern Russia, linking droughts to reduced crop yields and livestock productivity, and recommended adaptive water management (Sources: [https://doi.org/10.5018/economics-ejournal.ja.2013-39](https://doi.org/10.5018/economics-ejournal.ja.2013-39), [10568/152724](10568/152724)).

Rwanda.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-smart adaptation** in **Rwanda** through transformative agricultural and food systems interventions. Specifically:
- **CGIAR’s CCAFS program** demonstrated climate-smart agriculture (CSA) as a transformative approach to enhance resilience in Rwanda, integrating lessons from regional projects (e.g., East Africa) to inform food-system transformation frameworks (reroute, de-risk, reduce, realign) (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3390/su13084305](https://doi.org/10.3390/su13084305)).
Rwanda.Adaptation:
• **CGIAR’s CCAFS program** analyzed climate-smart agriculture (CSA) as a tool to rapidly transform food systems under climate pressure, with case studies from Rwanda highlighting actionable pathways for resilience (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3390/su13084305](https://doi.org/10.3390/su13084305) and [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.oneear.2019.10.017](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.oneear.2019.10.017) for agroforestry’s role in microclimate adaptation).

• **CGIAR researchers** assessed climate-risk management interventions (e.g., stress-adapted crop germplasm, conservation agriculture, diversified systems) to stabilize production and incomes for smallholder farmers in risk-prone regions, including Rwanda’s agricultural systems (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agsy.2018.01.019](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agsy.2018.01.019)).

• **CGIAR’s Climate Services team** developed participatory communication processes to bridge gaps between climate information and farmer decision-making, ensuring locally relevant climate data for adaptation (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3389/fsufs.2019.00021](https://doi.org/10.3389/fsufs.2019.00021)).

• **CGIAR’s CIP/IITA** identified climate-proof root, tuber, and banana varieties (e.g., drought-tolerant, heat-tolerant) for the Great Lakes Region, including Rwanda, to improve crop suitability under future climates (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agsy.2021.103246](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agsy.2021.103246)).

• **CGIAR’s gender-inclusive research** highlighted disparities in women’s access to climate services and ICTs, proposing inclusive strategies (e.g., women’s groups, tailored ICTs) to enhance adaptation equity (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1080/17565529.2019.1613216](https://doi.org/10.1080/17565529.2019.1613216)).

• **CGIAR’s Bioversity/CGIAR Genebank** explored plant breeding innovations for climate adaptation, addressing challenges in accessing genetic diversity for drought/heat tolerance in Rwanda’s crops (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3390/plants9091129](https://doi.org/10.3390/plants9091129)).

• **CGIAR’s scenario-guided adaptation team** developed multi-actor frameworks for strategic planning in East Africa, including Rwanda, to enable resilience through collaborative scenario-based action (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.gloenvcha.2014.03.001](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.gloenvcha.2014.03.001)).
Rwanda.Mitigation: • **CGIAR’s CCAFS program** linked agroforestry to biogeochemical and hydrological benefits, positioning it as a mitigation strategy for Sub-Saharan Africa, including Rwanda (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.oneear.2019.10.017](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.oneear.2019.10.017)).

Sao Tome And Principe.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-smart agricultural adaptation and biodiversity conservation** in **Sao Tome and Príncipe** (and broader Central Africa) through **bioeconomic modeling, vulnerability mapping, and climate-resilient crop strategies**. Specifically:
- **CGIAR’s maize research (CIMMYT/CIP)** modeled climate change impacts on maize yields under varying nitrogen inputs and adaptation scenarios, identifying region-specific vulnerabilities (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005](https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005)).
- **CGIAR’s climate adaptation team** systematically reviewed trade-offs/synergies in smallholder adaptation strategies (e.g., water/soil/livestock management) to enhance resilience (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.regsus.2021.05.002](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.regsus.2021.05.002)).
- **Bioversity International (CGIAR)** mapped vulnerability of 100 priority tree species to climate/anthropogenic threats, guiding restoration priorities (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.biocon.2022.109554](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.biocon.2022.109554)).
- **CGIAR’s agricultural economics team** evaluated economy-wide adaptation scenarios (e.g., yield increases vs. irrigation expansion) using water-agriculture models (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ecolecon.2013.05.006](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ecolecon.2013.05.006)).
Sao Tome And Principe.Adaptation:
• **CGIAR’s maize research (CIMMYT/CIP)** assessed biophysical and socioeconomic impacts of climate change on maize production in 2050/2080, identifying yield reductions under medium/high-input intensification and region-specific mega-environment vulnerabilities, while proposing bioeconomic models to evaluate adaptation measures (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005](https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005)).

• **CGIAR’s climate adaptation team** conducted a systematic review of smallholder adaptation strategies (crop/soil/water/livestock management), highlighting trade-offs (e.g., labor costs, resource competition) and synergies (e.g., yield stability, sustainability) to inform low-cost, complementary interventions (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.regsus.2021.05.002](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.regsus.2021.05.002)).

• **Bioversity International (CGIAR)** developed vulnerability maps for 100 priority tree species in Central Africa, integrating climate and anthropogenic threats (fire, habitat conversion) to prioritize **in-situ/ex-situ conservation and restoration actions**, with an online decision-support platform (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.biocon.2022.109554](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.biocon.2022.109554)).

• **CGIAR’s agricultural economics team** analyzed two adaptation scenarios (doubling irrigated area vs. 25% yield increases) using **IMPACT and GTAP-W models**, concluding that **productivity gains** (not irrigation expansion) offer better outcomes for climate adaptation in Sub-Saharan Africa (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ecolecon.2013.05.006](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ecolecon.2013.05.006)).
Sao Tome And Principe.Mitigation: No evidence found in this sample.
Sao Tome And Principe.Water:
• **CGIAR’s agricultural economics team** integrated a **water simulation module** into partial/general equilibrium models (IMPACT/GTAP-W) to evaluate climate adaptation scenarios, including the efficacy of irrigated area expansion vs. yield improvements (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ecolecon.2013.05.006](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ecolecon.2013.05.006)).

• **CGIAR’s maize research** implicitly addressed **water-nitrogen interactions** by modeling yield impacts under three nitrogen levels in climate change scenarios, though no standalone water-focused interventions were identified (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005](https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005)).

Saudi Arabia.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-resilient food systems and adaptive agricultural practices** in **Saudi Arabia** through policy-informed research and climate-vulnerable crop analysis. Specifically:
- CGIAR researchers evaluated **healthy and sustainable food system policies** in G20 nations, including Saudi Arabia’s low red meat consumption (≤10 kg/person/year) and its alignment with climate-resilient dietary guidelines (Source: [https://doi.org/10.4060/cc2805en](https://doi.org/10.4060/cc2805en)).
- The Center documented **rice production under extreme arid conditions** (e.g., Al Hasa Oasis, <100 mm rainfall/year) as a case study for food security and climate vulnerability (Source: [10568/33961](10568/33961)).
Saudi Arabia.Adaptation:
• CGIAR researchers analyzed **Saudi Arabia’s food-based dietary guidelines (FBDGs)** within the G20, highlighting its low red meat availability (<10 kg/person/year) as a potential adaptation strategy for climate-resilient nutrition while reducing health risks (e.g., cancer/heart disease) (Source: [https://doi.org/10.4060/cc2805en](https://doi.org/10.4060/cc2805en)).
• The Center studied **rice cultivation in hyper-arid climates** (e.g., Al Hasa Oasis, <100 mm rainfall) to assess **food security vulnerabilities** under climate change, emphasizing adaptive practices for water-scarce regions (Source: [10568/33961](10568/33961)).
Saudi Arabia.Mitigation: No evidence found in this sample.
Saudi Arabia.Water: • CGIAR documented **rice production in extreme arid conditions** (e.g., Saudi Arabia’s Al Hasa Oasis, <100 mm rainfall/year) as a case study for water-efficient crop systems, though no direct mitigation interventions were identified (Source: [10568/33961](10568/33961)).

Senegal.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-smart agriculture (CSA) and adaptive resilience** in **Senegal** through **soil-water conservation, climate information services (CIS), and gender-inclusive risk management**. Specifically:
- **CGIAR’s Climate Change, Agriculture, and Food Security (CCAFS) Program** demonstrated **transformative CSA practices** (e.g., agroforestry, farmer-managed natural regenerations, and conservation agriculture) to enhance resilience under climate variability (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3390/su13084305](https://doi.org/10.3390/su13084305)).
- **CGIAR researchers** assessed **gender-responsive climate services** to improve women’s access to climate information via ICTs and participatory networks (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1080/17565529.2019.1613216](https://doi.org/10.1080/17565529.2019.1613216)).
Senegal.Adaptation:
• **CGIAR’s CCAFS Program** implemented **climate-smart agriculture (CSA) frameworks** in Senegal, integrating **agroforestry, soil-water conservation (e.g., zai, half-moon techniques), and climate information services (CIS)** to mitigate climate risks for smallholders (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jclepro.2018.03.199](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jclepro.2018.03.199)).

• **CGIAR researchers** evaluated **diversified production systems** (e.g., crop-livestock integration) as adaptive strategies to enhance food security under rainfall variability, identifying optimal diversification zones (500–1000mm annual rainfall) for Senegal’s agricultural systems (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1111/gcb.14158](https://doi.org/10.1111/gcb.14158)).

• **CGIAR’s CCAFS** analyzed **climate-resilient strategies** for livestock, fishery, and crop sectors, reviewing policy mechanisms to scale CSA adoption in West Africa, including Senegal (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1186/s40066-016-0075-3](https://doi.org/10.1186/s40066-016-0075-3)).

• **CGIAR assessed climate information service (CIS) uptake** in Senegal, documenting **8,500+ users** receiving SMS/voice-based forecasts and recommending private-sector engagement to sustain CIS governance (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3390/cli6010013](https://doi.org/10.3390/cli6010013)).

• **CGIAR’s International Maize and Wheat Improvement Center (CIMMYT)** modeled **climate impacts on maize yields** in Senegal’s **dry mid-altitude mega-environments**, highlighting yield reductions under medium/high-input systems and proposing adapted varieties (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005](https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005)).

• **CGIAR reviewed climate-risk management interventions** (e.g., stress-adapted germplasm, conservation agriculture) to stabilize incomes and assets for Senegalese smallholders, though poverty reduction impacts remain limited (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agsy.2018.01.019](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agsy.2018.01.019)).

• **CGIAR examined gender disparities** in climate service access, advocating for **women’s inclusion in CIS networks** and ICT-based solutions to address socio-cultural barriers (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1080/17565529.2019.1613216](https://doi.org/10.1080/17565529.2019.1613216)).

• **CGIAR analyzed farm-associated adaptations** (e.g., improved crop varieties, land management) driven by **weather information access, social institutions, and asset ownership**, with findings applicable to Senegal’s smallholder contexts (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.gloenvcha.2013.12.011](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.gloenvcha.2013.12.011)).
Senegal.Mitigation: No evidence found in this sample.
Senegal.Water:
• **CGIAR’s CCAFS Program** promoted **soil and water conservation techniques** (e.g., zai pits, contour ridges) and **drought-tolerant crops** as adaptive tools to combat rainfall variability in Senegal (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jclepro.2018.03.199](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jclepro.2018.03.199)).

• **CGIAR assessed climate information services (CIS) for water management**, documenting farmer adoption of **SMS/voice-based forecasts** to inform irrigation and drought coping strategies in Senegal (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3390/cli6010013](https://doi.org/10.3390/cli6010013)).

Seychelles.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-smart agriculture (CSA) and integrated water management** in **Seychelles** through targeted resilience-building and institutional capacity strengthening. Specifically:
- **CGIAR’s Climate-Smart Agriculture (CSA) team** advanced climate-responsive agricultural practices to enhance productivity, resilience, and GHG reduction in Seychelles (10568/106073).
- **The Center’s Urban Water Management initiative** supported integrated urban water governance in Seychelles to improve water security and sustainability (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.aqpro.2016.06.002).
Seychelles.Adaptation: • **CGIAR’s Climate-Smart Agriculture (CSA) program** designed climate-responsive agricultural systems in Seychelles to sustainably increase productivity, enhance resilience, and reduce GHG emissions (10568/106073).
Seychelles.Mitigation: • **CGIAR’s CSA research** demonstrated mitigation strategies in Seychelles by integrating practices that reduce/remove greenhouse gases while improving food security (10568/106073).
Seychelles.Water: • **CGIAR’s Urban Water Management initiative** implemented integrated urban water management (IUWM) in Seychelles to address water supply, sanitation, and governance challenges, fostering sustainability in fast-growing urban contexts (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.aqpro.2016.06.002).

Sierra Leone.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate resilience and agricultural adaptation** in **Sierra Leone** through evidence-based strategies and regional evaluations. Specifically:
- **CIAT/IWMI** evaluated **land and water management practices** to enhance farmer resilience to climate variability (doi:10.7910/DVN/2PZOPV).
- **Bioversity/CGIAR** assessed **shade tree adaptation strategies** for cocoa systems under rising temperatures (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.scitotenv.2016.03.024).
Sierra Leone.Adaptation:
• **CIAT, IWMI, and IFPRI** conducted a **household survey** in Sierra Leone (among six other West African countries) to assess **integrated land and water management** for climate adaptation, identifying scalable practices to improve smallholder resilience (doi:10.7910/DVN/2PZOPV).

• **Bioversity International (CGIAR)** analyzed **cocoa vulnerability to climate change** in West Africa (including Sierra Leone), recommending **shade tree adoption** and **agronomic adjustments** to mitigate heat stress and drought risks (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.scitotenv.2016.03.024).

• **CGIAR researchers** reviewed **climate-smart agriculture (CSA) strategies** for West Africa, including **crop diversification, soil/land management, and water management** to enhance smallholder productivity and resilience (https://doi.org/10.1186/s40066-016-0075-3).

• **CGIAR** examined **regional adaptation zones** for cocoa, proposing **intensification/diversification strategies** tailored to climate projections for the 2030s–2050s (https://doi.org/10.1007/s11027-016-9707-y).

• **CGIAR** studied **trade-offs and synergies** in smallholder climate adaptation, highlighting **low-cost strategies** (e.g., minimum tillage) and **complementary water/soil management** to balance productivity and sustainability (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.regsus.2021.05.002).

• **CGIAR** assessed **dairy cattle heat stress** in West Africa, warning of **reduced milk production** due to increased heat events and advocating for **adaptive livestock systems** (https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-020-02733-2).

• **CGIAR** evaluated **COVID-19 lockdown impacts** on food security, emphasizing **policy reforms** to sustain agricultural planting seasons in Sub-Saharan Africa (including Sierra Leone) (https://doi.org/10.1038/s41538-020-00073-0).
Sierra Leone.Mitigation: • **CGIAR researchers** quantified **biomass carbon stocks** on agricultural land globally, noting **Sierra Leone’s decline in tree cover** (2000–2010) and its implications for national carbon budgets (https://doi.org/10.1038/srep29987).
Sierra Leone.Water: No evidence found in this sample.

Singapore.narrative:
CGIAR’s research in **Singapore** (via regional Southeast Asia programs) advanced **climate-smart agriculture** and **food security** through policy support and resilience frameworks, while quantifying **peatland fire emissions** as a critical mitigation focus. Specifically:
- **CCAFS** aligned regional R4D thrusts to integrate climate resilience into food systems (Source 1).
- **CCAFS (CIAT, FP2)** co-designed a multi-year climate-food security plan for APEC economies (Source 3).
- **CIFOR** mapped and quantified GHG emissions from localized peatland fires in Indonesia, impacting transboundary haze in Singapore (Source 4).
Singapore.Adaptation:
• **CCAFS Southeast Asia** designed a **climate-smart agriculture (CSA) framework** (2019–2021) to enhance food security and regional resilience across Southeast Asia, including Singapore’s policy alignment (10568/99018).
• **ASEAN-CRN (supported by CGIAR partners)** developed **regional CSA guidelines** to standardize climate-resilient agricultural practices, adopted by member states (10568/92487).
• **CCAFS (CIAT, FP2, RPL SEA)** facilitated **APEC’s Multi-Year Action Plan (2018–2021)** on climate change and food security, including stakeholder consultations and policy endorsement (10568/121197).
Singapore.Mitigation: • **CIFOR** quantified **172 ± 59 Tg CO₂-eq emissions** from a 2013 peatland fire event in Indonesia, demonstrating localized but high-impact GHG sources linked to transboundary haze affecting Singapore (doi:10.17528/CIFOR/DATA.00082).
Singapore.Water: No evidence found in this sample.

Slovakia.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-resilient protein-rich crop systems and adaptive agricultural strategies** in **Slovakia** through **climate-smart research and policy-relevant modeling**. Specifically:
- **CGIAR’s Climate Change and Food Security Program** analyzed climate suitability shifts for protein-rich crops (e.g., faba bean, lentil, chickpea) under future scenarios, identifying regional vulnerabilities and opportunities for northern European expansion (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eja.2019.125974](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eja.2019.125974)).
- **CGIAR’s Agricultural Systems Modeling Team** quantified adaptation strategies (e.g., R&D for climate-resilient crop varieties and irrigation technology upgrades) to enhance yield stability and food security in OECD-aligned regions (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en](https://doi.org/10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en)).
Slovakia.Adaptation:
• **CGIAR’s Climate Change and Food Security Program** mapped heterogeneous climate impacts on protein-rich crop suitability (e.g., reduced suitability for chickpea/lentil in southern Europe, expanded quinoa potential), recommending **breeding programs to improve abiotic stress tolerance** and **geographic production shifts** to align with climate projections (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eja.2019.125974](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eja.2019.125974)).

• **CGIAR’s Agricultural Systems Modeling Team** modeled **two adaptation pathways**—**R&D for climate-resilient crop varieties** and **irrigation technology upgrades**—to mitigate yield risks and enhance resilience, with cost estimates for OECD-aligned investments (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en](https://doi.org/10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en)).
Slovakia.Mitigation: No evidence found in this sample.
Slovakia.Water: No evidence found in this sample.

Slovenia.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-smart agricultural adaptation and forest resilience** in **Slovenia and Europe** through targeted research on crop suitability, forest genetic resources (FGR), and adaptive policy frameworks. Specifically:
- **CGIAR’s Climate Change, Agriculture and Food Security (CCAFS) program** modeled shifts in protein-rich crop suitability under future climates, identifying regional adaptation needs (e.g., northern Europe’s potential for quinoa, southern Europe’s risks to chickpea/lentil production) and recommending breeding strategies to enhance abiotic stress tolerance (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eja.2019.125974](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eja.2019.125974)).
- **Bioversity International** surveyed European forest owners/managers to assess perceptions of FGR management, advocating for active adaptation (e.g., non-local genotypes) to counteract drought/windstorm threats while acknowledging local material preferences (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1007/s10342-020-01311-6](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10342-020-01311-6)).
- **The Center’s modeling team** (via IMPACT) quantified adaptation strategies—such as R&D for climate-resilient crop varieties and irrigation technology upgrades—to mitigate yield risks and food security vulnerabilities in OECD regions (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en](https://doi.org/10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en)).
Slovenia.Adaptation:
• **CGIAR-CCAFS** analyzed climate projections to demonstrate heterogeneous impacts on protein-rich crop suitability across Europe, recommending **geographic shifts in production** (e.g., expanding quinoa in northern Europe) and **breeding programs** to improve faba bean, lentil, and chickpea tolerance to abiotic stresses (e.g., drought, heat) (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eja.2019.125974](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eja.2019.125974)).

• **Bioversity International** conducted a pan-European survey (including Slovenia) to evaluate forest owners’/managers’ attitudes toward **forest genetic resource (FGR) adaptation**, highlighting support for non-local genotypes to match future climates while noting resistance to fully adopting foreign material; findings underscore the need for **evidence-based guidance** to scale active FGR management (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1007/s10342-020-01311-6](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10342-020-01311-6)).

• **CGIAR’s modeling division** used the **IMPACT framework** to simulate adaptation strategies—**new crop varieties** and **irrigation technology upgrades**—to offset climate-induced yield declines, estimating public/private investment needs for OECD regions (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en](https://doi.org/10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en)).
Slovenia.Mitigation: No evidence found in this sample.
Slovenia.Water: No evidence found in this sample.

Solomon Islands.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate resilience and adaptation in Solomon Islands** through participatory research, economic modeling, and community-based resource management. Specifically:
- **CGIAR’s fisheries and food systems research** evaluated climate adaptation strategies (e.g., FADs, aquaculture, NRM) to mitigate per capita fish consumption decline by 2050 (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.marpol.2016.01.004](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.marpol.2016.01.004)).
- **Bioversity International and CGIAR’s Indigenous Knowledge programs** documented Melanesian territorial management techniques to preserve biodiversity and food security under climate stress (Source: [https://doi.org/10.4060/cb5131en](https://doi.org/10.4060/cb5131en)).
Solomon Islands.Adaptation:
• **CGIAR’s fisheries economists** modeled economic impacts of climate change on Solomon Islands’ fisheries, demonstrating that **fish aggregating devices (FADs), aquaculture, and natural resource management (NRM)** could augment domestic fish supplies and prevent per capita consumption decline by 2050 (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.marpol.2016.01.004](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.marpol.2016.01.004)).

• **CGIAR’s Pacific food systems research** analyzed baseline scenarios (without adaptation) and projected Solomon Islands would become a **net fish importer** due to climate-induced production stagnation, emphasizing urgent need for adaptation (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.marpol.2015.12.011](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.marpol.2015.12.011)).

• **CGIAR’s climate vulnerability assessments** highlighted **coastal biodiversity loss, tropical cyclones, and droughts** as threats to Solomon Islands’ food security, advocating for **participatory action research** to build resilient fishing/farming communities (Source: [10568/69495](10568/69495)).

• **WorldFish (CGIAR Center)** demonstrated that **nearshore FADs increased fish supply by 45% in rural Solomon Islands communities**, improving nutrition and income but also revealing trade-offs like intra-household conflict (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0115386](https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0115386)).

• **CGIAR’s socioeconomics team** found **limited adaptive capacity** in Solomon Islands due to high reliance on agriculture/fisheries (68% employment, 39% GDP), recommending integrated climate-resilient livelihood strategies (Source: [10568/145066](10568/145066)).

• **CGIAR’s community-based fisheries management** used **catch trophic signatures** in Malaita Province to tailor **localized, gear-specific management solutions**, linking fishing patterns to environmental features (Source: [doi:10.7910/DVN/SFZNWM](doi:10.7910/DVN/SFZNWM)).

• **CGIAR’s policy research** examined national environmental conservation policies in Solomon Islands, framing adaptation as essential for **livelihood protection** amid climate-induced coastal/marine biodiversity decline (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.marpol.2015.12.022](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.marpol.2015.12.022)).
Solomon Islands.Mitigation: No evidence found in this sample.
Solomon Islands.Water: No evidence found in this sample.

Somalia.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate resilience and agricultural adaptation** in **Somalia** through **climate-smart interventions, agro-climatic risk management, and livelihood-focused research**. Specifically:
- **CGIAR’s Climate Change, Agriculture and Food Security (CCAFS) Program** developed **climate information products** for risk management, including agro-climatic variables like rainfall onset/cessation dates, to support the **Horn of Africa’s agriculture and food security sectors** (10568/92093).
- **CGIAR researchers** assessed **trade-offs and synergies in climate adaptation strategies** for smallholder farmers, emphasizing **low-cost, complementary measures** to enhance productivity and resilience (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.regsus.2021.05.002).
Somalia.Adaptation:
• **CGIAR’s CCAFS Program** analyzed **climate adaptation strategies** for smallholder farmers in sub-Saharan Africa, identifying **trade-offs (e.g., labor costs, resource competition)** and **synergies (e.g., yield stability, sustainability)** across **crop, water, soil, and livestock management** (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.regsus.2021.05.002).

• **CGIAR researchers** evaluated **climate extremes (droughts, floods, heat stress)** in East Africa, projecting **heterogeneous agro-climatic shifts** (e.g., northern Somalia shifting to wetter zones) and **critical implications for livestock and agriculture**, while advocating for **context-specific resilience interventions** (https://doi.org/10.1007/s00704-025-05405-2).

• **CGIAR’s CCAFS** explored the **nexus between climate change, mobility, and conflict in Somalia**, linking **climate shocks (erratic rainfall, droughts)** to **displacement, poverty, and violence**, and recommending **sustainable agricultural practices and community-based conflict resolution** for pastoralists (10568/173200).

• **CGIAR assessed long-term climate impacts on livelihoods**, finding that **increased drought frequency in East Africa correlates with higher youth emigration**, and highlighted the need for **climate-informed migration policies** (10568/110278).

• **CGIAR modeled future climate benefits for livestock smallholders in Somalia**, projecting **increased rainfall and temperatures could boost pasture production and profitability** under **agile farm management**, with **SSP585 scenarios yielding higher profits** (https://doi.org/10.1088/2976-601x/ad6fa0).

• **CGIAR’s CCAFS** advanced **gridded climate information for risk management** in the Horn of Africa, supporting **NMHSs and agricultural stakeholders** with **seasonal forecasts and agro-climatic variables** (10568/92093).

• **CGIAR reviewed climate-smart agriculture (CSA) in East Africa**, synthesizing **livestock production systems, GHG emissions, and food security linkages** to inform **climate-resilient agricultural practices** (10568/106995).
Somalia.Mitigation: • **CGIAR’s CCAFS Program** reviewed **greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions from livestock systems** in eastern Africa, including Somalia, and highlighted **climate-smart agriculture (CSA) as a mitigation strategy** to align livestock production with **low-emission, climate-resilient practices** (10568/106995).
Somalia.Water:
• **CGIAR researchers** modeled **economy-wide impacts of climate change on agriculture**, comparing **expansion of irrigated areas vs. yield improvements** and finding that **productivity gains (e.g., 25% yield increases) outperform irrigation expansion** for adaptation in sub-Saharan Africa (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ecolecon.2013.05.006).

• **CGIAR assessed water stress and extreme precipitation events** in East Africa, projecting **increased heavy rainfall and declining drought stress** in some regions (e.g., northern Somalia), with **critical implications for water management in agriculture and livestock** (https://doi.org/10.1007/s00704-025-05405-2).

• **CGIAR’s CCAFS** integrated **water simulation modules** into agricultural models (e.g., IMPACT, GTAP-W) to evaluate **climate adaptation scenarios**, emphasizing **water-agriculture linkages** and **global food market impacts** (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ecolecon.2013.05.006).

South Africa.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-resilient agriculture and water-energy-food nexus solutions** in **South Africa** through **remote sensing, adaptive crop management, and gender-inclusive climate services**. Specifically:
- **CGIAR’s Sen2-Agri system** (developed in collaboration with partners) automated near-real-time agricultural monitoring at parcel resolution in South Africa, generating high-accuracy **cropland masks, crop type maps, and vegetation status indicators** via Sentinel-2/Landsat data (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.rse.2018.11.007](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.rse.2018.11.007)).
South Africa.Adaptation:
• **CGIAR’s International Maize and Wheat Improvement Center (CIMMYT)** modeled climate change impacts on maize yields in South Africa, identifying **region-specific yield reductions under medium/high-input intensification** and recommending **adapted maize varieties** to mitigate food insecurity by 2050/2080 (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005](https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005) and [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agee.2023.108511](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agee.2023.108511) for ecological intensification via push-pull technology).

• **CGIAR’s Gender and Climate Change team** analyzed gender disparities in access to **rural climate services**, advocating for **ICT-based solutions and women’s group inclusion** to address climate vulnerability in South Africa’s smallholder communities (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1080/17565529.2019.1613216](https://doi.org/10.1080/17565529.2019.1613216)).

• **CGIAR’s Resilience team** documented **smallholder adaptation strategies** in South Africa’s Free State Province, highlighting **traditional practices (e.g., crop diversification, fodder trees)** and **scientific interventions (e.g., soil conservation)**, with adoption driven by **awareness and financial access** (Sources: [https://doi.org/10.3390/agriculture10090410](https://doi.org/10.3390/agriculture10090410) and [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cosust.2013.11.008](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cosust.2013.11.008)).

• **CGIAR’s Climate Change and Food Security team** synthesized **trade-offs/synergies in adaptation strategies** (e.g., minimum tillage vs. water management), emphasizing **low-cost, complementary approaches** to enhance resilience for smallholder farmers (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.regsus.2021.05.002](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.regsus.2021.05.002)).
South Africa.Mitigation: No evidence found in this sample.
South Africa.Water:
• **CGIAR’s Water Security team** contributed to **national-scale water resource management** by validating **Sentinel-2-based cropland masks** (accuracy >90%) to support **water productivity monitoring** in South Africa’s agricultural systems (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.rse.2018.11.007](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.rse.2018.11.007)).

• **CGIAR’s WEF Nexus team** analyzed **climate change impacts on hydropower potential** in South Africa, projecting **declining gross hydropower potential** due to rainfall trends and advocating for **integrated water-energy-food policy coordination** (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.renene.2017.10.030](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.renene.2017.10.030)).

• **CGIAR’s Climate Resilience team** assessed **rainfall variability (1983–2020)** in South Africa’s west coast river basin, identifying **statistically insignificant declines** (-0.360 mm/year) to inform **drought/flood risk mitigation** and **crop planning** (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3390/rs14010096](https://doi.org/10.3390/rs14010096)).

South Sudan.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate resilience and agricultural adaptation** in **South Sudan and East Africa** through **climate-informed risk management, agro-climatic modeling, and policy coherence frameworks**. Specifically:
- **CGIAR’s Climate Change, Agriculture and Food Security (CCAFS) Program** developed **climate information products** (e.g., gridded rainfall onset/cessation data) to support **risk management in the Horn of Africa**, enabling tailored agro-climatic decision-making for food security (10568/92093).
- **CGIAR researchers** assessed **climate extremes (droughts, floods, heat stress)** and their **heterogeneous impacts on agro-climatic zones**, projecting shifts toward wetter conditions in **northern Somalia and South Sudan** while warning of critical implications for livestock and agriculture (10.1007/s00704-025-05405-2).
South Sudan.Adaptation:
• **CGIAR’s CCAFS Program** analyzed **trade-offs and synergies in climate adaptation strategies** for smallholder farmers, identifying **low-cost, complementary measures** (e.g., minimum tillage) to balance productivity, resilience, and resource competition (10.1016/j.regsus.2021.05.002).

• **CGIAR researchers** evaluated **two adaptation scenarios for Sub-Saharan Africa**: (1) doubling irrigated area by 2050, and (2) increasing rainfed/irrigated yields by 25%, concluding that **productivity gains outperform irrigation expansion** for climate resilience (10.1016/j.ecolecon.2013.05.006).

• **CGIAR’s International Livestock Research Institute (ILRI)** examined **climate impacts on tick-borne pathogens** (e.g., *Rhipicephalus appendiculatus* expansion into South Sudan via **anthropogenic cattle movement**), linking vector dynamics to **livestock health adaptation needs** (10.1079/9781789249637.0070).

• **CGIAR’s Policy Coherence Framework** assessed **climate-security risks in East Africa**, revealing **gaps in policy integration** between climate and peace strategies while highlighting **youth migration as a climate vulnerability** (10.3828/idpr.2023.9, 10568/110278, 10568/106796).

• **CGIAR’s maize research (CIMMYT/CIP)** modeled **biophysical and socioeconomic impacts of climate change on maize yields** in South Sudan’s **maize mega-environments (MMEs)**, identifying **regional yield reductions under medium/high-input systems** and trade-offs for food security (10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005).

• **CGIAR’s situational analysis** mapped **cross-border climate adaptation investments** in the **Karamoja-Turkana/Eastern Equatoria cluster**, informing a **Green Climate Fund proposal** for scaled-up mitigation and adaptation (10568/128661).
South Sudan.Mitigation: • **CGIAR’s policy research** linked **Nationally Determined Contributions (NDCs)** to **climate mitigation priorities** in East Africa, aligning adaptation strategies with **Green Climate Fund proposals** for regional collaboration (10568/128661).
South Sudan.Water:
• **CGIAR’s IMPACT and GTAP-W models** simulated **water-agriculture linkages** under climate change, showing that **expanded irrigation alone is less effective than yield improvements** for Sub-Saharan Africa’s adaptation (10.1016/j.ecolecon.2013.05.006).

• **CGIAR’s CCAFS Program** developed **climate information products** (e.g., drought/flood indices) to support **water risk management** in the Horn of Africa, though **no direct South Sudan-specific water interventions** were identified in this sample (10568/92093, 10.1007/s00704-025-05405-2).

Spain.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-smart agriculture, forest resilience, and GHG monitoring** in **Spain** through **genomic adaptation, traditional agroecological knowledge, and standardized flux measurement**. Specifically:
- **Bioversity/CGIAR** integrated **forest genomics** to enhance drought-resistant tree varieties (Source: [doi:10.21223/7QY6KU](https://doi.org/10.21223/7QY6KU)).
- **ICRAF/CGIAR** surveyed European forest managers to assess **active adaptation strategies** for climate-resilient planting material (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1007/s10342-020-01311-6](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10342-020-01311-6)).
- **ICOS/CGIAR** standardized **eddy covariance methods** for CO₂ flux monitoring in Mediterranean ecosystems (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1515/intag-2017-0043](https://doi.org/10.1515/intag-2017-0043)).
Spain.Adaptation:
• **CGIAR/ILRI** documented **pastoralist-led rangeland restoration** in Spain as a climate-resilient livelihood strategy (Source: [10568/128162](10568/128162)).
• **CGIAR/IFPRI** modeled **adaptation via new crop varieties and irrigation tech** to offset climate-induced yield risks (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en](https://doi.org/10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en)).
• **CGIAR/Bioversity** explored **traditional agroecological knowledge (TAeK)** to transform Spanish agri-food systems through **biocultural diversity** and **value-chain innovation** (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3390/su11051222](https://doi.org/10.3390/su11051222)).
• **CGIAR/EVOLTREE** advanced **genomic adaptation** for drought-resistant trees, identifying candidate genes in Mediterranean species (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1007/s11295-011-0378-2](https://doi.org/10.1007/s11295-011-0378-2)).
• **CGIAR/ICARDA** assessed **protein-rich crop suitability** under future climates, recommending shifts in Spanish agricultural policy (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eja.2019.125974](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eja.2019.125974)).
Spain.Mitigation: • **CGIAR/ICOS** developed **standardized eddy covariance protocols** to improve comparability of CO₂ and energy flux measurements across European ecosystems (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1515/intag-2017-0043](https://doi.org/10.1515/intag-2017-0043)).
Spain.Water: • **CGIAR/remote sensing researchers** conducted **water stress experiments** in Mediterranean fescue fields, using **fluorescence-based detection** to assess drought impacts (Source: [doi:10.21223/7QY6KU](doi:10.21223/7QY6KU)).

Sri Lanka.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-resilient agriculture and water security** in Sri Lanka through **adaptation planning, crop resilience, and climate-smart agricultural bundles**. Specifically:
- **CGIAR’s Climate Change, Agriculture and Food Security (CCAFS)** developed a **framework for prioritizing adaptation approaches** to address uncertainty in agricultural planning, including stakeholder scenario exercises and robust adaptation options for extreme events (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.1219441110](https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.1219441110)).
- **Bioversity International** assessed **heat-tolerant maize varieties** to mitigate yield losses due to climate change, demonstrating up to **93% yield protection** under future heat stress scenarios (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1007/s00704-016-1931-6](https://doi.org/10.1007/s00704-016-1931-6)).
Sri Lanka.Adaptation:
• **CGIAR-CCAFS** designed a **framework for incremental vs. transformative adaptation pathways**, emphasizing stakeholder-led scenario exercises to enhance predictability in agricultural planning amid uncertainty (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.1219441110](https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.1219441110) ).

• **ICRISAT** and **CIAT** analyzed **spatial rainfall trends (1989–2019)** to inform **drought and flood management**, identifying increased flood risks in southern/western provinces and drought risks in eastern districts (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3390/ijgi10020084](https://doi.org/10.3390/ijgi10020084) ).

• **World Agroforestry Centre (ICRAF)** and **CIMMYT** promoted **heat-tolerant maize varieties**, reducing projected yield losses by **33–93%** under rainfed/irrigated conditions by 2050 (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1007/s00704-016-1931-6](https://doi.org/10.1007/s00704-016-1931-6) ).

• **CGIAR’s Systemwide Program on Water, Land and Ecosystems (WLE)** evaluated **Village Tank Cascade Systems (VTCS)**, demonstrating how **continuous water supply** could improve resilience but warning of **increased vulnerability** due to shifting rainfall patterns (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3390/su15129271](https://doi.org/10.3390/su15129271) ).

• **Bioversity International** mapped **climate vulnerability of crop wild relatives (CWR)**, identifying **22–37% habitat loss by 2050** and prioritizing conservation zones for evidence-based management (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.crsust.2021.100092](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.crsust.2021.100092) ).

• **CGIAR’s Climate-Smart Agriculture (CSA) program** bundled **weather index insurance, climate-resilient seeds, and SMS advisories** to protect smallholders from drought, enhancing financial resilience (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3389/fsufs.2023.1145147](https://doi.org/10.3389/fsufs.2023.1145147) ).

• **CGIAR’s CSA initiative** proposed **trans-disciplinary networks** (e.g., Direct Seeded Rice Consortium) to integrate research, policy, and practice for climate-resilient rice systems (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3390/cli8020035](https://doi.org/10.3390/cli8020035) ).

• **CGIAR’s CSA program** developed a **country profile** to mainstream climate-smart practices, addressing trade-offs between productivity, adaptation, and mitigation in Sri Lankan agro-systems (Source: [10568/69548](10568/69548) ).
Sri Lanka.Mitigation:
• **CGIAR-CCAFS** analyzed **South Asia’s Nationally Determined Contributions (NDCs)**, highlighting gaps in agricultural emission reductions while proposing **climate-resilient value chains** to balance food security and mitigation (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1080/14693062.2018.1501329](https://doi.org/10.1080/14693062.2018.1501329) ).

• **CGIAR’s CSA program** framed mitigation as a **core pillar of climate-smart agriculture**, emphasizing GHG reduction alongside resilience and productivity (Source: [10568/69548](10568/69548) ).
Sri Lanka.Water:
• **ICRISAT** and **WLE** assessed **spatial rainfall trends (1989–2019)**, revealing **increased annual rainfall** (highest in wet zones) and **regional drought/flood risks**, recommending **targeted water management** (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3390/ijgi10020084](https://doi.org/10.3390/ijgi10020084) ).

• **CGIAR-WLE** studied **VTCS water productivity**, linking **tank storage systems** to resilience but warning of **climate-induced vulnerability** in paddy farming (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3390/su15129271](https://doi.org/10.3390/su15129271) ).

• **CGIAR’s CSA program** bundled **satellite-based weather index insurance** to mitigate **financial risks from water-related disasters** (e.g., drought) for smallholders (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3389/fsufs.2023.1145147](https://doi.org/10.3389/fsufs.2023.1145147) ).

Sudan.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-resilient agriculture and food security** in **Sudan and sub-Saharan Africa** through **bioeconomic modeling, adaptation strategies, and agroclimatic monitoring**. Specifically:
- **CGIAR’s maize and sorghum research centers** developed climate-adaptive crop varieties and bioeconomic models to assess yield impacts under future climate scenarios (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005](https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005)).
- **CGIAR’s climate resilience programs** analyzed trade-offs and synergies in smallholder adaptation strategies, emphasizing low-cost, multi-objective interventions to enhance productivity and sustainability (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.regsus.2021.05.002](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.regsus.2021.05.002)).
Sudan.Adaptation:
• **CGIAR’s maize research** evaluated biophysical and socioeconomic impacts of climate change on maize production, identifying region-specific yield reductions and adaptation measures for lowland/dry mid-altitude mega-environments (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005](https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005)).

• **CGIAR’s systematic reviews** synthesized climate adaptation strategies (crop/soil/water/livestock management) for smallholder farmers, highlighting trade-offs (e.g., labor costs) and synergies (e.g., yield stability) to inform policy (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.regsus.2021.05.002](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.regsus.2021.05.002)).

• **CGIAR’s agroclimatic research** identified **hybrid rainy seasons** (false starts, erratic rainfall) in the Sahel, threatening rain-fed farming systems, and linked these patterns to atmospheric circulation shifts (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1088/1748-9326/11/10/104008](https://doi.org/10.1088/1748-9326/11/10/104008)).

• **CGIAR’s modeling (APSIM)** assessed sorghum productivity under climate change, testing management practices (e.g., radiation use efficiency) to enhance adaptive capacity in Sudanian West Africa (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.fcr.2019.107685](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.fcr.2019.107685)).

• **CGIAR’s climate security analysis** linked climate variability to conflict drivers in Sudan, including **reduced agricultural production, food insecurity, and pastoralist displacement** due to land/water/pasture pressure (Source: [10568/128061](10568/128061)).

• **CGIAR’s weed science** identified *Rhamphicarpa fistulosa* as a **60% yield-loss threat** to rice in Sudan, advocating integrated management to prevent spread (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1111/wre.12129](https://doi.org/10.1111/wre.12129)).

• **CGIAR’s tick-borne disease research** examined climate impacts on vector survival (e.g., *Rhipicephalus* ticks) and anthropogenic cattle movement expanding tick ranges into South Sudan (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1079/9781789249637.0070](https://doi.org/10.1079/9781789249637.0070)).

• **CGIAR’s economic modeling** compared adaptation scenarios (e.g., doubling irrigated area vs. yield increases), concluding productivity gains outperform irrigation expansion in Sub-Saharan Africa (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ecolecon.2013.05.006](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ecolecon.2013.05.006)).
Sudan.Mitigation: No evidence found in this sample.
Sudan.Water:
• **CGIAR’s water-agriculture research** integrated water simulation modules into economic models to evaluate irrigation expansion as an adaptation strategy, though productivity gains proved more effective (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ecolecon.2013.05.006](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ecolecon.2013.05.006)).

• **CGIAR’s agroclimatic studies** analyzed **drought/flood stress** and heavy precipitation trends in East Africa, projecting heterogeneous shifts in agro-climatic zones (e.g., wetter conditions in South Sudan) with implications for livestock (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1007/s00704-025-05405-2](https://doi.org/10.1007/s00704-025-05405-2)).

• **CGIAR’s remote sensing** quantified fertilizer response variability using **VHR NDVI** in rain-fed Mali, revealing crop-dependent NDVI sensitivity and within-field fertility gradients (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3390/rs8060531](https://doi.org/10.3390/rs8060531)).

• **CGIAR’s climate security analysis** highlighted **land/water/pasture pressure** as a conflict driver in Sudan, exacerbated by climate variability (Source: [10568/128061](10568/128061)).

Suriname.narrative:
CGIAR’s research in **Suriname** (as part of the broader Amazon region) focused on **climate-smart agricultural systems, forest conservation, and rural livelihood resilience**. Specifically:
- **CIAT** modeled **climate-coupled impacts on coffee suitability and pollinator availability** to guide adaptive management (e.g., shade adjustment, crop rotation) in shifting climates (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.1617940114](https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.1617940114)).
- The **Amazon Livelihoods and Environment Network (RAVA)**—led by **ICRAF**—integrated **agroforestry and smallholder agriculture** to optimize household income, nutrition, and natural resource management in deforested landscapes (Source: [10568/56615](10568/56615)).
- **CIAT** advanced **REDD+ and sustainable agrosilvopastoral systems** to mitigate deforestation-driven emissions while recuperating degraded lands (Source: [10568/56615](10568/56615)).
Suriname.Adaptation:
• **CIAT** modeled **geographic shifts in coffee suitability and pollinator richness** under climate change, identifying regions where **shade adjustment, crop rotation, or forest conservation** could enhance resilience (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.1617940114](https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.1617940114)).

• **ICRAF (via RAVA)** developed **integrated agroforestry systems** to improve smallholder resource management, linking farm-level practices to landscape-scale livelihood security (Source: [doi:10.34725/DVN/QT5CJF](doi:10.34725/DVN/QT5CJF)).

• **CIAT** analyzed **vulnerability to climate change in deforested Amazonian areas**, proposing **recovery strategies** (e.g., sustainable agrosilvopastoral systems) to adapt degraded lands (Source: [10568/56615](10568/56615)).
Suriname.Mitigation:
• **CIAT** emphasized **REDD+ mechanisms and carbon storage** as critical mitigation strategies in the Amazon, while piloting **intensive agrosilvopastoral systems** to reduce emissions from extensive livestock and agriculture (Source: [10568/56615](10568/56615)).

• **CIAT** contributed to **forest-based mitigation policies**, highlighting the role of **sustainable land-use systems** in offsetting greenhouse gas emissions from deforestation (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3390/su9101831](https://doi.org/10.3390/su9101831)).
Suriname.Water: No evidence found in this sample.

Sweden.narrative:
CGIAR’s research in **Sweden** focused on **climate-smart agriculture, forest resilience, and greenhouse gas monitoring**, with a particular emphasis on **adaptation strategies** and **mitigation frameworks** for European ecosystems. Specifically:
- **Adaptation**: CGIAR led scenario-based sustainability transitions (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1007/s11625-019-00714-8](https://doi.org/10.1007/s11625-019-00714-8)).
- **Mitigation**: The Center standardized **CO₂ flux monitoring** via the **Integrated Carbon Observation System (ICOS)** to ensure comparability across European ecosystems (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1515/intag-2017-0043](https://doi.org/10.1515/intag-2017-0043)).
Sweden.Adaptation:
• **CGIAR’s Climate Change, Agriculture and Food Security (CCAFS)** developed **sustainability scenarios** for Northern Europe, identifying **gender, governance, and behavioral change** as critical drivers for transitions toward positive futures (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1007/s11625-019-00714-8](https://doi.org/10.1007/s11625-019-00714-8) and [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eja.2019.125974](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eja.2019.125974) for crop suitability modeling).

• **CGIAR’s Forestry Research** assessed **forest genetic resources (FGR) management** as a climate adaptation strategy, surveying European forest owners to evaluate perceptions of **non-local planting material** for resilience (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1007/s10342-020-01311-6](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10342-020-01311-6)).

• **CGIAR’s Modeling Team** used the **IMPACT model** to evaluate **adaptation measures** (e.g., **new crop varieties** and **irrigation technology**) for climate-resilient agriculture in OECD regions (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en](https://doi.org/10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en)).

• **CGIAR’s Crop Science Division** analyzed **protein-rich crop suitability** under future climates, identifying **quinoa expansion in Northern Europe** and **stress-tolerant breeding needs** for faba bean, lentil, and chickpea (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eja.2019.125974](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eja.2019.125974)).
Sweden.Mitigation: • **CGIAR’s ICOS Collaboration** standardized **eddy covariance data processing** for **CO₂ and energy flux measurements**, ensuring methodological consistency across European ecosystems to improve **GHG monitoring** (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1515/intag-2017-0043](https://doi.org/10.1515/intag-2017-0043)).
Sweden.Water: No evidence found in this sample.

Switzerland.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate resilience and mitigation strategies** in **Switzerland** through policy-focused research and cross-sectoral collaborations. Specifically:
- **CGIAR’s Climate Resilience and Livestock & Climate Initiatives** launched the **Climate Security Observatory** in Geneva to integrate climate risks with security frameworks (Source: [10568/138454](https://doi.org/10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en)).
- **CGIAR researchers** modeled **adaptation measures** (e.g., new crop varieties and irrigation tech) to enhance agricultural resilience using the **IMPACT model** (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en](https://doi.org/10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en)).
Switzerland.Adaptation:
• **CGIAR’s Climate Resilience and Livestock & Climate Research Initiatives** launched the **Climate Security Observatory** in Geneva, Switzerland, to analyze climate-livestock-security linkages and inform policy (Source: [10568/138454](https://doi.org/10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en)).
• **CGIAR researchers** quantified adaptation impacts (e.g., **new crop varieties** and **irrigation technology upgrades**) on yields, prices, and food security using the **IMPACT model**, estimating public/private R&D investment needs (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en](https://doi.org/10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en)).
Switzerland.Mitigation: • **CGIAR’s CCAFS (Climate Change, Agriculture and Food Security)** presented **ecological farming as a mitigation co-benefit** at the **Biovision-CCAFS workshop in Zurich**, highlighting synergies between sustainable agriculture and greenhouse gas reduction (Source: [10568/100230](https://doi.org/10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en)).
Switzerland.Water: No evidence found in this sample.

Syria.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate resilience and agricultural adaptation** in **Syria** through participatory breeding, vulnerability assessments, and water-efficient strategies. Specifically:
- **ICARDA** developed **drought-tolerant wheat varieties** and decentralized participatory plant breeding to align crops with farmers’ needs in marginal environments (10568/36078, 10568/36077).
- **CGIAR researchers** assessed **climate-induced economic vulnerabilities**, including declining yields and welfare losses across rural/urban households, while proposing **supplemental irrigation** as an adaptation strategy (10568/42086, 10568/154452).
- **CGIAR’s Water Solutions team** mapped **agricultural water productivity** in irrigated and rainfed areas of the **Euphrates-Tigris Basin**, identifying inefficiencies for targeted improvements (hdl:20.500.11766.1/CUZ56W, hdl:20.500.11766.1/D1D3GI).
Syria.Adaptation:
• **ICARDA** led **participatory plant breeding programs** in Syria to develop drought-tolerant wheat varieties and decentralized selection methods tailored to farmers’ needs and marginal climates (10568/36078, 10568/36077).
• **CGIAR’s Climate Change team** modeled **economic impacts of climate change** in Syria, highlighting **declining crop yields** and **welfare losses** across households, while proposing **supplemental irrigation** to reduce yield variability (10568/42086, 10568/154452).
• **CGIAR researchers** evaluated **species vulnerability to climate change** in Syrian rangelands, identifying **grazing pressure management** as a critical adaptation strategy for high-risk plant species (https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-013-0701-z).
• **CGIAR’s Economics of Climate Change program** framed **climate-resilient agriculture** as a recovery engine for Syria’s post-conflict stabilization, emphasizing **climate-sensitive investments** in agricultural systems (10568/173986).
Syria.Mitigation: No evidence found in this sample.
Syria.Water:
• **CGIAR’s Water Solutions team** assessed **agricultural water productivity (WPM)** in **irrigated areas** (2008, a drought year) and **rainfed areas** (2002, a surplus year) across the **Euphrates-Tigris Basin**, mapping inefficiencies for improved water management (hdl:20.500.11766.1/CUZ56W, hdl:20.500.11766.1/D1D3GI).
• **CGIAR’s Climate Impact Modeling** proposed **supplemental irrigation (SI)** as a strategy to mitigate **inter-annual yield variability** in Syrian wheat production under climate change (10568/42086).

Taiwan.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate adaptation in Taiwan** through **policy support and crop breeding innovations**. Specifically:
- **CIAT** facilitated stakeholder consultations and developed a **Multi-Year Action Plan for Climate Change and Food Security (2018–2021)** for APEC economies, including Taiwan (Source: **10568/121197**).
- **CGIAR researchers** introgressed **drought tolerance QTLs (qDTYs)** into the Taiwanese japonica rice variety *Tainan 11*, identifying high-yielding drought-tolerant lines for deployment (Source: **doi:10.7910/DVN/JQDVVN**).
Taiwan.Adaptation:
• **CIAT** provided strategic support to APEC economies—including Taiwan—by **co-designing and endorsing a Multi-Year Action Plan (2018–2021) for climate change and food security**, including stakeholder consultations and policy alignment (Source: **10568/121197**).
• **CGIAR researchers** developed **drought-tolerant japonica rice varieties** by introgressing seven major-effect qDTYs into *Tainan 11*, yielding early-flowering, high-yield lines under drought conditions for potential deployment in Taiwan (Source: **doi:10.7910/DVN/JQDVVN**).
Taiwan.Mitigation: No evidence found in this sample.
Taiwan.Water: No evidence found in this sample.

Tajikistan.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-smart agriculture, water security, and adaptive resilience** in **Tajikistan** through **policy integration, smallholder adaptation strategies, and sectoral vulnerability assessments**. Specifically:
- **CGIAR’s Climate Change, Agriculture, and Food Security (CCAFS) program** aligned climate action with national agricultural and poverty-reduction strategies to reduce greenhouse gas emissions and enhance food systems resilience (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1787/50075aa6-en](https://doi.org/10.1787/50075aa6-en)).
Tajikistan.Adaptation:
• **CGIAR’s International Food Policy Research Institute (IFPRI)** modeled climate change impacts on Tajikistan’s agriculture, projecting **declining crop yields and food security risks**, and proposed **adaptation strategies** to mitigate vulnerabilities (Source: [https://doi.org/10.16997/srjed.33](https://doi.org/10.16997/srjed.33) and [10568/138814](10568/138814) [truncated])
• **CGIAR researchers** mapped **stakeholder roles and institutional gaps** in Tajikistan’s Nationally Determined Contributions (NDCs) to strengthen **vertical/horizontal coordination** for climate action (Source: [10568/138814](10568/138814)).
• **CGIAR’s Water, Land, and Ecosystems (WLE) program** analyzed **smallholder adaptation strategies** to water scarcity and extreme events, emphasizing **interdisciplinary research** to address rural mobility and climate-vulnerability nexuses (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1080/17565529.2024.2436090](https://doi.org/10.1080/17565529.2024.2436090)).
• **CGIAR’s Agri-Food Systems Division** proposed a **conceptual framework** to integrate climate policies with agri-food systems, identifying **synergies and gaps** in Tajikistan’s national commitments (Source: [10568/138815](10568/138815)).
• **CGIAR’s Genetic Resources Program (Bioversity)** conducted **genetic studies on *Juglans regia*** to assess biodiversity resilience in wild and cultivated populations, supporting **climate-adaptive tree conservation** (Source: [doi:10.7910/DVN/VUP7OH](doi:10.7910/DVN/VUP7OH)).
Tajikistan.Mitigation:
• **CGIAR’s CCAFS program** explored **climate-smart agriculture** as a mitigation strategy to reduce greenhouse gas emissions from food systems while aligning with **Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs)** in Tajikistan (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1787/50075aa6-en](https://doi.org/10.1787/50075aa6-en)).
• **CGIAR’s Nexus Solutions program** assessed **carbon neutrality pathways** for Central Asia’s water-agriculture-energy sectors, highlighting **GHG mitigation** as critical for low-income provinces (Source: [10568/128220](10568/128220)).
Tajikistan.Water:
• **CGIAR’s WLE program** evaluated **water scarcity risks** in Tajikistan’s irrigated agriculture, linking **climate change to reduced river discharge** and **upstream water demand conflicts** (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agee.2014.02.033](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agee.2014.02.033) and [10568/128220](10568/128220)).
• **CGIAR’s TAWA (Tajikistan Agriculture and Water Activity)** conducted **household surveys** to assess **nutrition-sensitive value chains** and water-use efficiency in USAID’s Zone of Influence (Source: [doi:10.7910/DVN/BMKEMG](doi:10.7910/DVN/BMKEMG)).
• **CGIAR’s IFPRI** collected **population-based water-activity data** to monitor USAID’s interventions in Khatlon Province, including **irrigation practices and migration patterns** (Source: [doi:10.7910/DVN/DHRV46](doi:10.7910/DVN/DHRV46)).

Thailand.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-smart agriculture, water-efficient rice production, and transdisciplinary adaptation strategies** in **Thailand** through **resilience-building networks, methane mitigation technologies, and policy-informed climate adaptation**. Specifically:
- CGIAR facilitated **transdisciplinary networks** like the **Direct Seeded Rice Consortium (DSRC)** to enhance climate resilience in rice-based systems (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3390/cli8020035](https://doi.org/10.3390/cli8020035)).
- The Center developed **Climate-Smart Villages (CSV) frameworks** to integrate food security and adaptive capacity in rural communities (Source: [10568/107725](10568/107725)).
Thailand.Adaptation:
• CGIAR led **transdisciplinary climate-smart agriculture networks**, including the **Direct Seeded Rice Consortium (DSRC)**, to improve resilience to extreme weather events and reduce yield losses in Thailand’s rice systems (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3390/cli8020035](https://doi.org/10.3390/cli8020035) ).

• The Center analyzed **adaptation options for rainfed and irrigated rice** (e.g., adjusted planting dates, supplementary irrigation) to offset climate-induced yield declines in the **Mekong Basin**, including Thailand, ensuring food security by 2050 (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1007/s12571-011-0154-z](https://doi.org/10.1007/s12571-011-0154-z) ).

• CGIAR assessed **mobility as an adaptation strategy** to freshwater and hydroclimatic hazards, highlighting its role in risk reduction amid droughts, floods, and extreme heat in inland regions (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-023-03573-6](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-023-03573-6) ).

• Researchers evaluated **long-term rainfall trends in Northeast Thailand**, demonstrating high adaptive capacity in rural livelihoods despite climate variability, with potential for future resilience through mobility and cropping system adjustments (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1051/cagri/2017006](https://doi.org/10.1051/cagri/2017006) ).

• CGIAR provided **guide steps for establishing Climate-Smart Villages (CSV)**, offering a structured approach to integrate climate adaptation, food security, and community-led resilience (Source: [10568/107725](10568/107725) ).

• The Center synthesized **mitigation and adaptation options** for smallholder farmers in Southeast Asia, including Thailand, through symposia and policy recommendations (Source: [10568/107003](10568/107003) ).
Thailand.Mitigation:
• CGIAR investigated **forest carbon sequestration policies** in developing countries, including Thailand, revealing trade-offs between poverty impacts and mitigation benefits (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1088/1748-9326/8/3/035009](https://doi.org/10.1088/1748-9326/8/3/035009) ).

• Researchers assessed the **methane (CH₄) mitigation potential of alternate wetting and drying (AWD) irrigation** in Thailand’s paddy fields, estimating **57,600 t CH₄/year reductions** (1.61 Mt CO₂-eq/year) and mapping climatic suitability for AWD adoption (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3389/fsufs.2020.575823](https://doi.org/10.3389/fsufs.2020.575823) ).

• CGIAR explored **low-emission rice production practices** and circular economy policies in Southeast Asia, emphasizing methane-reducing techniques and rice straw valorization to align climate goals with food security (Source: [10568/163419](10568/163419) ).
Thailand.Water:
• CGIAR analyzed **climate-induced variability in water cycles** across the **Mekong Basin**, including Thailand, and evaluated adaptation strategies like **supplementary irrigation and adjusted planting dates** to safeguard rice yields (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1007/s12571-011-0154-z](https://doi.org/10.1007/s12571-011-0154-z) ).

• The Center reviewed **water resource management challenges** in the **Greater Mekong Subregion**, identifying priority actions to enhance resilience and food production amid climate and socio-economic pressures (Source: [https://doi.org/10.5337/2010.212](https://doi.org/10.5337/2010.212) ).

• Researchers examined **alternate wetting and drying (AWD) irrigation** as a **water-saving technique** in Thailand’s paddy fields, linking it to **methane mitigation** while assessing climatic and soil constraints (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3389/fsufs.2020.575823](https://doi.org/10.3389/fsufs.2020.575823) ).

• CGIAR studied **rainfall distribution trends in Northeast Thailand**, highlighting the need for adaptive water management to support cropping systems amid shifting seasonal patterns (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1051/cagri/2017006](https://doi.org/10.1051/cagri/2017006) ).

Timor-Leste.narrative: CGIAR prioritized **climate change adaptation in Timor-Leste** through participatory, community-driven strategies and economic modeling to enhance resilience in fisheries and agriculture. Specifically:
Timor-Leste.Adaptation:
• **WorldFish** (in collaboration with the National Directorate of Fisheries and Aquaculture) led participatory community adaptation planning for coastal fishing/agricultural communities, mainstreaming gender and assessing climate impacts on livelihoods (10568/52197).

• **CGIAR researchers** developed a **four-step participatory action research framework** to evaluate climate adaptation options for fishing and farming communities, including decision-tree analyses, impact workshops, and ecosystem services mapping (10568/69495, 10568/69491, 10568/69488, 10568/69490, 10568/69494).

• **Economic modeling** demonstrated that **aquaculture development, marine protected areas, and low-cost fish aggregating devices** could significantly improve coastal and freshwater fish production in Timor-Leste by 2035 and 2050 (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.marpol.2015.12.010).

• **Supply-demand modeling** highlighted the risks of declining domestic fish production under climate change without adaptation, projecting Timor-Leste’s potential shift to net fish imports (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.marpol.2015.12.011).

• **Policy-focused research** emphasized the vulnerability of coastal and marine biodiversity and livelihoods, advocating for national policies on environmental conservation and climate-resilient fisheries management (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.marpol.2015.12.022).
Timor-Leste.Mitigation: No evidence found in this sample.
Timor-Leste.Water: No evidence found in this sample.

Togo.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-smart agriculture, water resilience, and gender-inclusive adaptation** in **Togo and West Africa** through **multi-sectoral research, hydrological modeling, and policy-relevant interventions**. Specifically:
- **CGIAR’s Climate Change, Agriculture, and Food Security (CCAFS) program** synthesized **climate-smart agriculture (CSA) strategies** for crop, livestock, and fishery sectors, tailoring adaptation policies to West Africa’s subregional needs (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1186/s40066-016-0075-3](https://doi.org/10.1186/s40066-016-0075-3)).
- **The Center for Development Research (ZEF) and CGIAR-affiliated researchers** modeled **hydrological shifts in the Volta Basin**, linking climate projections to water-energy-food security risks for rainfed agriculture (Source: [https://doi.org/10.5194/hess-26-1481-2022](https://doi.org/10.5194/hess-26-1481-2022)).
Togo.Adaptation:
• **CGIAR’s CCAFS and International Maize and Wheat Improvement Center (CIMMYT)** analyzed **biophysical and socioeconomic impacts of climate change on maize production**, identifying region-specific yield reductions and adaptation measures for lowland/dry mid-altitude mega-environments in West Africa (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005](https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005)).

• **CGIAR’s International Institute of Tropical Agriculture (IITA) and partners** conducted a **systematic review of trade-offs/synergies in smallholder climate adaptation strategies**, categorizing crop/soil/water/livestock management tactics and recommending low-cost, complementary interventions for Togo’s farming systems (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.regsus.2021.05.002](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.regsus.2021.05.002)).

• **CGIAR’s International Livestock Research Institute (ILRI)** assessed **heat stress risks for dairy cattle in West Africa**, projecting increased severe heat events (5–22% by 2071–2100) and advocating for **adaptive dairy production systems** to mitigate milk yield losses (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-020-02733-2](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-020-02733-2)).

• **CGIAR’s World Agroforestry Centre (ICRAF) and partners** modeled **future biome shifts and tree cover change in West Africa**, evaluating uncertainties in climate projections to inform land-use adaptation (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1365-2699.2011.02560.x](https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1365-2699.2011.02560.x)).

• **CGIAR’s International Food Policy Research Institute (IFPRI)** quantified **gender-differentiated adaptation impacts on soybean farmers in Togo**, finding women gain **higher revenue from adaptation** but face **0.268% revenue loss if non-adaptive**, and recommended **FBO access, credit, and extension services** to bridge gaps (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1080/23311932.2020.1743625](https://doi.org/10.1080/23311932.2020.1743625)).
Togo.Mitigation: No evidence found in this sample.
Togo.Water:
• **CGIAR’s Water, Land, and Ecosystems (WLE) program** integrated **hydrological modeling (mHM) and Water Accounting Plus (WA+)** to project **water flows/consumption in the Volta Basin**, revealing **8% runoff availability** (419 km³/year) and **34% agricultural water use**, while highlighting future variability risks (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jhydrol.2023.130092](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jhydrol.2023.130092)).

• **CGIAR’s International Water Management Institute (IWMI)** evaluated **economy-wide adaptation scenarios for Sub-Saharan Africa**, comparing **irrigated area expansion vs. yield increases**, and concluded **productivity gains** (25% yield boost) outperform irrigation scaling due to limited baseline water resources (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ecolecon.2013.05.006](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ecolecon.2013.05.006)).

• **CGIAR researchers** analyzed **trends in evapotranspiration (ETo) and aridity in Togo (1961–2011)**, detecting **significant ETo increases at Tabligbo/Sokode** and **declining precipitation/ETo ratios**, threatening **rainfed agriculture** (practiced by 90% of farmers) (Source: [https://doi.org/10.2166/wcc.2015.111](https://doi.org/10.2166/wcc.2015.111)).

Tokelau.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate resilience and adaptation in Pacific Island food systems, including Tokelau**, through scenario-based policy insights. Specifically:
- The Center developed **four alternative future scenarios for Pacific food systems**—focusing on fisheries, forests, trade, affordability, and public health—to inform climate adaptation strategies for fishers and farmers (Source: **10568/75610**).
Tokelau.Adaptation: • CGIAR developed **four alternative scenarios for Pacific food systems** to enhance resilience against climate change impacts (e.g., tropical cyclones, droughts) among fishers and farmers, providing policy-makers with actionable insights for adaptation (10568/75610).
Tokelau.Mitigation: No evidence found in this sample.
Tokelau.Water: No evidence found in this sample.

Tonga.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate resilience and adaptation in Pacific Island food systems, including Tonga**, through scenario-based policy insights. Specifically:
- The Center developed **four alternative future scenarios for Pacific food systems**—focusing on fisheries, forests, trade, affordability, and public health—to inform climate adaptation strategies for fishers and farmers (Source: **10568/75610**).
Tonga.Adaptation: • CGIAR developed **four alternative scenarios for Pacific food systems** to enhance resilience and adaptation among fishers and farmers, addressing climate vulnerability to droughts and tropical cyclones (10568/75610).
Tonga.Mitigation: No evidence found in this sample.
Tonga.Water: No evidence found in this sample.

Trinidad And Tobago.narrative:
