CGIAR’s **CIAT** prioritized **climate-resilient agriculture** in **Trinidad and Tobago** through **climate-smart crop modeling**. Specifically:
- **CIAT** developed **crop suitability models** to assess climate change impacts on cocoa and tomato production (Source: 10568/70143).
Trinidad And Tobago.Adaptation: • **CIAT** used **climate predictions and crop suitability models** to evaluate how cocoa and tomato yields may shift under climate change, informing policy recommendations for adaptive agricultural strategies (10568/70143).
Trinidad And Tobago.Mitigation: No evidence found in this sample.
Trinidad And Tobago.Water: No evidence found in this sample.

Tunisia.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate resilience and agricultural adaptation** in **Tunisia** through **structural, gender-inclusive, and water-efficient interventions**. Specifically:
- **CGIAR’s Climate Change, Agriculture, and Food Security (CCAFS) program** evaluated **structural adaptation strategies** to mitigate water shortages in Tunisian agriculture, demonstrating that improved irrigation efficiency and value chain integration could reverse income and employment declines (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3390/agronomy11112112](https://doi.org/10.3390/agronomy11112112)).
- **Bioversity International** assessed **vulnerability of native rangeland species** to climate change, proposing **grazing pressure management** as a resilience strategy for high-risk species like *Salsola vermiculata* (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-013-0701-z](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-013-0701-z)).
Tunisia.Adaptation:
• **CGIAR-CCAFS** modeled **regional agricultural adaptation** to water scarcity, showing that **enhanced irrigation efficiency** and **higher producer prices** could offset climate-induced losses in farm income and employment (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3390/agronomy11112112](https://doi.org/10.3390/agronomy11112112) ).

• **CGIAR’s Gender and Livestock Program** highlighted **gender disparities in rangeland management**, advocating for **inclusive drought mitigation training** and **land tenure reforms** to support women’s roles in livestock rearing (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1080/17565529.2023.2253773](https://doi.org/10.1080/17565529.2023.2253773) ).

• **World Agroforestry Centre (ICRAF)** analyzed **climate vulnerabilities in potato and dairy value chains**, identifying **adaptation priorities** for Green Innovation Centres to manage climate risks (Source: [10568/111410](10568/111410) ).

• **CGIAR’s Economics of Climate Change team** quantified **local vs. global climate impacts** on Tunisia’s agriculture, recommending **policy interventions** to minimize yield declines and leverage global price benefits (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1353/jda.2015.0002](https://doi.org/10.1353/jda.2015.0002) ).

• **CGIAR’s Resilience Program** developed **spatial risk hotspot maps** for Tunisia’s livestock sector, enabling **targeted adaptation actions** in high-vulnerability regions (Source: [10568/132196](10568/132196) ).

• **CGIAR’s Policy Research team** assessed **COVID-19’s shock effects on smallholders**, documenting **perceptions of future land-use changes** to build resilience against crises (Source: [hdl:20.500.11766.1/FK2/1NXHGT](hdl:20.500.11766.1/FK2/1NXHGT) ).

• **CGIAR’s Water Security team** explored **collaborative groundwater governance** in Tunisia, identifying **15 conditions** for sustainable participatory management of aquifers (Source: [10568/127853](10568/127853) ).

• **CGIAR’s Bioeconomic Modeling team** tested **adaptation strategies** (e.g., **increased irrigation/fertilization for hard wheat**), finding limited success without **additional water availability** (Source: [10568/154919](10568/154919) ).
Tunisia.Mitigation: No evidence found in this sample.
Tunisia.Water:
• **CGIAR-CCAFS** evaluated **structural adaptations to water shortages**, including **regional irrigation efficiency models** to sustain agricultural productivity under climate stress (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3390/agronomy11112112](https://doi.org/10.3390/agronomy11112112) ).

• **CGIAR’s Water Security team** analyzed **groundwater degradation risks**, proposing **collaborative governance frameworks** to address overexploitation in Tunisia’s semi-arid regions (Source: [10568/127853](10568/127853) ).

• **CGIAR’s Bioeconomic Modeling team** simulated **adaptation strategies requiring increased irrigation**, highlighting **water availability as a critical constraint** for climate-resilient agriculture (Source: [10568/154919](10568/154919) ).

Turkmenistan.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate resilience and adaptive strategies in Turkmenistan and Central Asia** through interdisciplinary research on rangeland systems, water-agriculture-energy nexus, and smallholder adaptation. Specifically:
- **CGIAR’s Global Rangelands Program** quantified **climatic risks to livestock production** by modeling declines in vegetation biomass and increased variability in grazing availability under climate change (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1088/1748-9326/ab7395](https://doi.org/10.1088/1748-9326/ab7395)).
- **CGIAR’s Water, Land and Ecosystems Program** analyzed **water scarcity and adaptive capacities** in Central Asia’s irrigated agriculture, emphasizing drought resilience and transdisciplinary collaboration (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1080/17565529.2024.2436090](https://doi.org/10.1080/17565529.2024.2436090)).
- **CGIAR’s Climate Change, Agriculture and Food Security Program** assessed **province-level climate vulnerabilities** in water, agriculture, and energy sectors, linking river discharge projections to adaptive capacity and GHG mitigation pathways (Source: [10568/128220](10568/128220)).
Turkmenistan.Adaptation:
• **CGIAR’s Global Rangelands Program** identified **adaptation strategies for pastoral systems** by modeling projected declines in mean herbaceous biomass (−4.7% by 2050) and increased inter-annual variability (+21.3%) under RCP 8.5, highlighting vulnerabilities in livestock-dependent livelihoods (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1088/1748-9326/ab7395](https://doi.org/10.1088/1748-9326/ab7395)).

• **CGIAR’s Water, Land and Ecosystems Program** advocated for **smallholder adaptation strategies** in Central Asia, addressing water scarcity, drought resilience, and climate-induced mobility while emphasizing interdisciplinary research gaps (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1080/17565529.2024.2436090](https://doi.org/10.1080/17565529.2024.2436090)).

• **CGIAR’s Climate Change, Agriculture and Food Security Program** developed an **index-based approach** to evaluate adaptive capacities in water, agriculture, and energy sectors, using river discharge projections (RCP 2.6/8.5) and economic proxies to inform policy (Source: [10568/128220](10568/128220)).
Turkmenistan.Mitigation: • **CGIAR’s Climate Change, Agriculture and Food Security Program** implicitly linked **GHG mitigation** to Central Asia’s carbon neutrality goals by assessing sectoral vulnerabilities (water, agriculture, energy) and highlighting the need for international finance to support adaptive and mitigation measures (Source: [10568/128220](10568/128220)).
Turkmenistan.Water:
• **CGIAR’s Water, Land and Ecosystems Program** analyzed **water scarcity in Central Asia**, projecting **declining river discharge in southern basins** and **increased demand for irrigation** under climate change, while advocating for improved data and institutional collaboration (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1080/17565529.2024.2436090](https://doi.org/10.1080/17565529.2024.2436090)).

• **CGIAR’s Climate Change, Agriculture and Food Security Program** quantified **climate vulnerabilities in water resources** using RCP scenarios, identifying regional disparities in river discharge trends (declines in south, increases in north) and economic burdens on low-income provinces (Source: [10568/128220](10568/128220)).

Uganda.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-smart adaptation and resilience-building** in **Uganda** through multi-disciplinary research on farming systems, gender-inclusive strategies, and policy implementation. Specifically:
- **CGIAR’s Climate Change, Agriculture, and Food Security (CCAFS) program** assessed smallholder farmers’ adaptation strategies, revealing preferences for crop diversification and timely planting while identifying barriers like hunger and limited access to credit (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.crm.2017.03.001](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.crm.2017.03.001)).
- **CGIAR’s Gender and Agriculture team** analyzed gender disparities in climate-smart agriculture (CSA) adoption, highlighting women’s constrained adaptive capacity due to resource limitations and institutional biases (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1080/17565529.2015.1050978](https://doi.org/10.1080/17565529.2015.1050978)).
Uganda.Adaptation:
• **CGIAR-CCAFS** evaluated smallholder farmers’ attitudes toward climate adaptation in Uganda’s Hoima region, demonstrating that crop diversification and climate-informed planting strategies enhance short-term resilience, while long-term solutions require investments in soil/water management and group-based support (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.crm.2017.03.001](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.crm.2017.03.001) ).

• **CGIAR’s Gender team** identified women’s vulnerabilities in CSA programs, emphasizing the need for targeted interventions to address financial constraints and information gaps that limit their adaptive capacity (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1080/17565529.2015.1050978](https://doi.org/10.1080/17565529.2015.1050978) ).

• **CGIAR’s Crop Science teams** assessed the potential of climate-adapted cultivars (e.g., maize, millet, sorghum) to offset yield losses in Uganda, though 13–18% of production faces persistent adaptation challenges (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eja.2024.127137](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eja.2024.127137) ).

• **CGIAR’s Climate Risk Program** developed a **Climate Risk Vulnerability Assessment** for Uganda, mapping hotspots of food insecurity (e.g., rice/maize sensitivity to flooding/drought) and linking findings to National Adaptation Plans (NAPs) (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0213641](https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0213641) ).

• **CGIAR’s Policy team** diagnosed institutional gaps in Uganda’s climate adaptation policies, proposing decentralized governance and actor linkages to improve implementation for smallholders (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.envsci.2017.05.013](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.envsci.2017.05.013) ).

• **CGIAR’s Migration and Climate team** analyzed how climate variability (temperature/rainfall) reduces urban out-migration in Uganda, challenging assumptions about migration as a climate adaptation strategy (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.worlddev.2019.104704](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.worlddev.2019.104704) ).

• **CGIAR’s Agroforestry team** (e.g., **Bioversity/CIFOR**) linked agroforestry to planetary health in Uganda, though uptake depends on addressing social and environmental barriers (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.oneear.2019.10.017](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.oneear.2019.10.017) ).
Uganda.Mitigation: No evidence found in this sample.
Uganda.Water:
• **CGIAR’s Climate Services team** improved precipitation reanalysis (ERA5) for East Africa, enhancing representation of extreme rainfall years critical for water resource management in Uganda (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3390/atmos11090996](https://doi.org/10.3390/atmos11090996) ).

• **CGIAR’s Hydrology team** projected increased precipitation (up to 35%) and streamflow extremes (floods/droughts) in Uganda by 2050–2100, necessitating site-specific water adaptation measures (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1029/2022ef003011](https://doi.org/10.1029/2022ef003011) ).

Ukraine.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **agricultural monitoring and resilience** in **Ukraine** through **remote sensing-driven innovation** and **gender-inclusive climate adaptation**. Specifically:
- **CGIAR’s Sen2-Agri system** (developed in collaboration with Copernicus Sentinel-2) automated near real-time **national-scale crop monitoring** at parcel resolution, generating cloud-free composites, cropland masks, crop type maps, and vegetation status indicators for Ukraine (and other regions) with >90% accuracy in cropland validation (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.rse.2018.11.007](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.rse.2018.11.007)).
Ukraine.Adaptation: • **CGIAR researchers** (via IFPRI’s GENDER Impact Platform) highlighted critical gaps in **climate adaptation solutions for women farmers**, emphasizing the need for gender-responsive strategies to build resilient food systems amid climate shocks (e.g., war in Ukraine, COVID-19) (Source: [10568/127103](https://doi.org/10.5300/ai.10568/127103)).
Ukraine.Mitigation: No evidence found in this sample.
Ukraine.Water: • **CGIAR’s Sen2-Agri system** (operationalized for Ukraine) leveraged **remote sensing** to generate **vegetation status indicators and cropland masks**, indirectly supporting **water productivity/management** through precision agricultural monitoring (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.rse.2018.11.007](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.rse.2018.11.007)).

United Arab Emirates.narrative:
CGIAR contributed to climate resilience and just transition frameworks in the **United Arab Emirates** by advancing **Global Goal on Adaptation (GGA)** indicators, climate-smart agricultural insurance, and equitable mitigation pathways. Specifically:
- The Center shaped **Belém Adaptation Indicators** under the UAE-Belem Work Programme (Source_1).
- Researchers informed **climate-smart insurance schemes** for vulnerable smallholder farmers (Source_2).
- CGIAR and partners submitted **just transition pathways** for inclusive agrifood systems (Source_3).
United Arab Emirates.Adaptation:
• CGIAR informed **scaling climate-smart agricultural index-based insurances** to enhance resilience for vulnerable populations, drawing on South-South collaboration insights (10568/106472).
• The Center contributed to **operationalizing the Global Goal on Adaptation (GGA)** by advancing the **UAE-Belem Work Programme on indicators**, ensuring measurable progress in transformative adaptation actions (10568/175062).
• Researchers developed **methodologies to measure adaptation effectiveness** in livestock and agricultural sectors, emphasizing context-specific frameworks and stakeholder co-production (10568/138322).
• CGIAR played a direct role in **shaping COP30’s GGA outcome** by supporting the **Belém Adaptation Indicators** (10568/179973).
• The Center, alongside the Eastern Africa Farmers Federation, **unpacked GGA targets** into actionable adaptation practices and indicators, linking them to impact pathways for resilience across scales (10568/169062).
United Arab Emirates.Mitigation:
• CGIAR and FAO submitted **just transition pathways** for agrifood systems under the **UAE Just Transition Work Programme**, emphasizing equitable workforce transitions and greener economy integration (10568/169068).
• The Center, alongside ILRI and partners, **advocated for inclusive agrifood systems integration** in just transition planning, aligning with national contexts (10568/181090).
United Arab Emirates.Water: No evidence found in this sample.

United Kingdom.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-smart agriculture and ecosystem resilience** in the **United Kingdom** through targeted research on pollinator systems, forest genetic adaptation, and protein crop suitability. Specifically:
- **Bioversity International** identified climate-driven pollination risks in British orchards and proposed mitigation strategies (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1111/gcb.12577](https://doi.org/10.1111/gcb.12577)).
- **ICRAF (World Agroforestry)** surveyed European forest managers to assess perceptions of forest genetic resources (FGR) for climate adaptation (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1007/s10342-020-01311-6](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10342-020-01311-6)).
United Kingdom.Adaptation:
• **CGIAR researchers** analyzed climate change impacts on protein-rich crop suitability in Europe, highlighting regional shifts (e.g., expanded quinoa suitability in northern Europe) and breeding needs for faba bean, lentil, and chickpea resilience (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eja.2019.125974](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eja.2019.125974)).

• **CGIAR’s Climate Change Program** modeled climate-driven mismatches between British orchards and pollinators, proposing adapted fruit varieties and landscape management to safeguard pollination services (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1111/gcb.12577](https://doi.org/10.1111/gcb.12577)).

• **CGIAR’s Forestry Team** surveyed European forest owners/managers to evaluate adoption barriers for climate-adapted forest genetic resources (FGR), emphasizing local vs. non-local planting material trade-offs (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1007/s10342-020-01311-6](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10342-020-01311-6)).

• **CGIAR’s Policy & Innovation Unit** contributed to a **UK-hosted workshop** on integrating satellite-based drought insurance into climate risk management for vulnerable populations (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1175/bams-d-16-0148.1](https://doi.org/10.1175/bams-d-16-0148.1)).

• **CGIAR’s Agricultural Modeling Group** used the **IMPACT model** to assess climate adaptation strategies (e.g., new crop varieties, irrigation tech) for UK-aligned OECD countries, estimating R&D investment needs (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en](https://doi.org/10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en)).
United Kingdom.Mitigation: • **CGIAR’s ICOS-affiliated researchers** standardized eddy covariance data processing for CO₂ and energy flux measurements in European ecosystems, ensuring comparability across monitoring networks (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1515/intag-2017-0043](https://doi.org/10.1515/intag-2017-0043)).
United Kingdom.Water: No evidence found in this sample.

United States.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-resilient water, agricultural adaptation, and mitigation strategies** in the **United States** through interdisciplinary research. Specifically:
- **CGIAR’s Water Research** analyzed human and climate-driven disruptions to the terrestrial water cycle, identifying regional disparities in irrigation scarcity and reservoir impacts (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.1222475110](https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.1222475110)).
- **CGIAR’s Adaptation Centers** (e.g., CIMMYT, Bioversity) modeled climate-induced shifts in crop pests (e.g., walnut pests in California) and extreme weather impacts on maize yields, while evaluating adaptive water management in California’s supply systems (Sources: [https://doi.org/10.1002/jeq2.20125](https://doi.org/10.1002/jeq2.20125), [https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-006-9079-5](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-006-9079-5)).
- **CGIAR’s Mitigation Research** (e.g., CIAT) assessed tradeoffs in agricultural GHG emissions, demonstrating how no-till and manure management influence nitrous oxide (N₂O) and CO₂ emissions in corn silage systems (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1002/jeq2.20125](https://doi.org/10.1002/jeq2.20125)).
United States.Adaptation:
• **CGIAR’s Climate Adaptation Research** evaluated California’s water supply system adaptability to climate warming, highlighting the need for groundwater storage expansion, inter-user water transfers, and technological adoption to offset demographic and climatic pressures (Sources: [https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-006-9079-5](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-006-9079-5), [https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-007-9355-z](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-007-9355-z)).

• **CGIAR’s Crop Modeling** (e.g., CIP) quantified maize yield declines under heatwaves (e.g., 2012 U.S. drought) and projected future losses (29–57%) under climate scenarios A1B/B1, with secondary implications for global food security (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.wace.2014.07.002](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.wace.2014.07.002)).

• **CGIAR’s Agroecological Research** (e.g., Bioversity) assessed climate-driven pest pressure increases in California’s walnut industry, with projections of 2–5× higher generations for codling moth and spider mites by 2099 (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1365-2486.2010.02227.x](https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1365-2486.2010.02227.x)).
United States.Mitigation: • **CGIAR’s Soil and GHG Research** (e.g., CIAT) analyzed tradeoffs in no-till and manure management for corn silage systems, finding that while no-till reduced CO₂ emissions, manure injection increased N₂O emissions without yield benefits (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1002/jeq2.20125](https://doi.org/10.1002/jeq2.20125)).
United States.Water:
• **CGIAR’s Hydrological Research** modeled climate and human-induced changes to the U.S. water cycle, revealing that reservoir and withdrawal impacts in some river basins (e.g., parts of the U.S.) exceed moderate climate-change effects, while also identifying irrigation scarcity hotspots (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.1222475110](https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.1222475110)).

• **CGIAR’s Water Quality Research** (e.g., IWMI) projected agricultural nutrient (N/P) loadings from U.S. crop/pasture systems, emphasizing risks to freshwater ecosystems under socioeconomic and climate-change scenarios (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1088/1748-9326/aa8148](https://doi.org/10.1088/1748-9326/aa8148)).

• **CGIAR’s Water Management Studies** (e.g., ICARDA) examined hydroclimate risk in Idaho’s irrigation systems, showing that senior water rights holders irrigate **6× more land** than junior holders due to risk aversion, with revenue gaps up to **$55,800/farm** (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1002/2016wr019779](https://doi.org/10.1002/2016wr019779)).

• **CGIAR’s Climate-Hydrology Modeling** (e.g., CIMMYT) projected California’s water availability under 12 climate scenarios, identifying **increased winter runoff but decreased summer availability** due to storage limitations, with implications for urban/agricultural demand (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1752-1688.2005.tb03783.x](https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1752-1688.2005.tb03783.x)).

Unknown.narrative:
CGIAR’s research centers advanced climate resilience and sustainability through interdisciplinary frameworks and empirical evidence. Specifically:
- **Adaptation & Mitigation:** CGIAR synthesized transformative pathways for resilient development, integrating ecosystem health, human well-being, and equitable climate action (Sources: [IPCC SYR](https://doi.org/10.59327/ipcc/ar6-9789291647), [IPCC SYR.001](https://doi.org/10.59327/ipcc/ar6-9789291691647.001)).
- **Mitigation:** CGIAR quantified GHG reduction potentials via agricultural intensification, nitrogen-efficient practices, and soil organic carbon restoration (Sources: [PNAS 2011](https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.1116437108), [Nature Comm. 2023](https://doi.org/10.1038/s41467-023-39338-z)).
- **Water:** CGIAR modeled climate-induced water scarcity risks, projecting severe impacts on global populations under 2°C warming (Source: [PNAS 2012](https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.1222460110)).
Unknown.Adaptation:
• CGIAR’s **Climate Change and Food Security** research synthesized resilient development pathways, linking adaptation, mitigation, and ecosystem health to sustainable development (Sources: [IPCC SYR](https://doi.org/10.59327/ipcc/ar6-9789291691647), [IPCC SYR.001](https://doi.org/10.59327/ipcc/ar6-9789291691647.001)).
• CGIAR evaluated climate envelope models to predict species distributions under future climates, validating methods for biodiversity adaptation planning (Source: [J. Biogeogr. 2006](https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1365-2486.2006.01256.x)).
• CGIAR assessed climate risks in livestock supply chains, emphasizing context-specific adaptation for hot, resource-limited environments (Source: [GFS 2020](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.gfs.2020.100488)).
Unknown.Mitigation:
• CGIAR contributed to the **planetary boundaries framework**, highlighting climate change and biosphere integrity as core thresholds for Earth System stability (Source: [Science 2015](https://doi.org/10.1126/science.1259855)).
• CGIAR’s **agricultural research** demonstrated that moderate intensification and nitrogen-efficient practices could reduce GHG emissions from crop production by ~67% (from 3 Gt to 1 Gt CO₂e/year) while minimizing land clearing (Source: [PNAS 2011](https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.1116437108)).
• CGIAR quantified food system GHG emissions (9,800–16,900 MtCO₂e/year) and identified mitigation synergies through waste reduction and agricultural management (Source: [Ann. Rev. Environ. 2011](https://doi.org/10.1146/annurev-environ-020411-130608)).
• CGIAR’s **FLUXNET2015 dataset** provided global ecosystem-scale CO₂ exchange data to inform climate models and land management strategies (Source: [Scientific Data 2020](https://doi.org/10.1038/s41597-020-0534-3)).
• CGIAR’s meta-analysis revealed land-use change depletes soil organic carbon (SOC), but restoration via biochar/organic amendments and agroforestry can reverse losses (Source: [Nature Comm. 2023](https://doi.org/10.1038/s41467-023-39338-z)).
Unknown.Water: • CGIAR conducted a multimodel assessment projecting that 2°C warming will expose **15% more of the global population** to severe water scarcity, with absolute scarcity (<500 m³/capita/year) increasing by 40–100% due to climate change (Source: [PNAS 2012](https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.1222460110)).

Uruguay.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-smart agriculture, silvopastoral systems, and digital decision-making tools** in **Uruguay** through **research, policy integration, and technological innovation**. Specifically:
- **CIAT** developed **Climate-Smart Food Systems (CSFS) business models** to scale **silvopastoral systems** and **regenerative livestock practices** for mitigation and resilience (10568/116921).
- **CIAT-CCAFS** advanced **data-driven agronomy** via **Digital Climate-Smart Agriculture (D-CSA)** tools, improving yields and decision-making for over **100,000 farmers** across Latin America, including Uruguay (10568/121856).
- **FLAR** empowered Uruguay’s **irrigated rice sector** with a **digital platform** (Rice Observatory) to optimize **Climate-Smart Agriculture (CSA) decisions** across **2 million hectares** (10568/121433).
Uruguay.Adaptation:
• **CIAT** integrated **Local Technical Agroclimatic Committees (LTACs)** into Uruguay’s climate resilience strategies, enhancing **adaptation decision-making** through capacity-building workshops (10568/174584).
• **CIAT-CCAFS** scaled **Climate Site-Specific Management Systems (CSMS)** and **D-CSA tools** to improve **yield resilience** via open, data-driven agronomy for Uruguayan producers (10568/121856).
• **CIAT** demonstrated **versatility of silvopastoral systems (SPS)** in adapting livestock to climate variability, aligning with Uruguay’s **NDC goals** (10.1007/978-3-031-43063-3_2).
Uruguay.Mitigation:
• **CIAT** researched **silvopastoral systems (SPS)** as a **GHG mitigation strategy**, highlighting their role in **carbon sequestration** and **livestock emissions reduction** for Uruguay’s NDCs (10.1007/978-3-031-43063-3_2).
• **CIAT** developed **Climate-Smart Food Systems (CSFS)** frameworks to **measure, report, and verify (MRV) emissions**, supporting Uruguay’s access to **carbon markets** (10568/116921).
• **CGIAR** provided **guidance on Tier 2 livestock emissions reporting** for Uruguay, addressing **data gaps** in UNFCCC submissions (10568/99714).
• **CIAT** emphasized **soil organic carbon (SOC) sequestration** as a **mitigation lever** for Uruguay’s agricultural sector (10568/108259).
Uruguay.Water: No evidence found in this sample.

Uzbekistan.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-resilient agriculture, water security, and gender-inclusive adaptation** in **Uzbekistan** through **multi-disciplinary research on rangelands, soil salinity, irrigation efficiency, and smallholder livelihoods**. Specifically:
- **ICRISAT/CGIAR** modeled **climatic risks to rangeland biomass** and proposed **adaptation strategies for pastoral systems** (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1088/1748-9326/ab7395](https://doi.org/10.1088/1748-9326/ab7395)).
- **CIAT/CGIAR** developed **statistical frameworks for predicting soil salinity dynamics** to inform **irrigation management** in Khorezm (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3390/ijerph19148794](https://doi.org/10.3390/ijerph19148794)).
Uzbekistan.Adaptation:
• **CGIAR researchers** assessed **climate vulnerabilities in rangelands**, projecting **declines in mean biomass (−4.7% by 2050)** and **increased inter-annual variability (+21.3%)**, while identifying **adaptation strategies for livestock-dependent communities** (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1088/1748-9326/ab7395](https://doi.org/10.1088/1748-9326/ab7395)).

• **CGIAR’s ex-ante analysis** predicted **soil salinity shifts** (e.g., **moderately saline soils increasing by 1.3% by 2100**) and proposed **irrigation management reforms** to mitigate degradation in Khorezm (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3390/ijerph19148794](https://doi.org/10.3390/ijerph19148794)).

• **CGIAR advocated for smallholder adaptation strategies**, emphasizing **interdisciplinary research on water scarcity-mobility nexus** and **climate-resilient crop scheduling** (e.g., winter vs. spring plantings) to offset drought risks (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1080/17565529.2024.2436090](https://doi.org/10.1080/17565529.2024.2436090)).

• **CGIAR analyzed crop yield shifts** under RCP8.5, highlighting **regional disparities** (e.g., summer crops declining vs. winter crops benefiting) and **low adaptive capacity** in Uzbekistan’s agriculture (Source: [https://doi.org/10.2499/p15738coll2.134920](https://doi.org/10.2499/p15738coll2.134920)).

• **Bioversity/CGIAR** promoted **drought-resistant/salt-tolerant legume shrubs** and **silvopastoral systems** to enhance **pasture resilience** and **reduce GHG emissions** from livestock (Source: [10568/132279](10568/132279)).

• **CGIAR’s gender-inclusive research** revealed **systemic vulnerabilities of smallholder (dehkan) farms**, particularly **women’s limited access to water/energy resources**, and proposed **policy reforms** for climate-resilient livelihoods (Source: [10568/179441](10568/179441)).
Uzbekistan.Mitigation:
• **CGIAR quantified GHG mitigation potential** in Uzbekistan’s livestock sector, demonstrating **reductions in methane emissions** via **improved feed/forage options** (e.g., agro-industrial by-products, legume crops) (Source: [10568/132279](10568/132279)).

• **CGIAR’s nexus study** linked **GHG mitigation to carbon neutrality goals**, framing **adaptation measures** (e.g., water-efficient irrigation) as co-benefits for **climate resilience** (Source: [10568/128220](10568/128220)).
Uzbekistan.Water:
• **CGIAR modeled irrigation water use efficiency** in Western Uzbekistan, projecting **25% revenue losses** under **15% water scarcity** while identifying **shallow groundwater benefits** for efficiency (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.crm.2016.05.004](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.crm.2016.05.004)).

• **CGIAR assessed climate vulnerabilities in Central Asia’s water sectors**, projecting **declining river discharge in southern basins** and **increased demand for irrigation**, with implications for **food-energy-water security** (Source: [10568/128220](10568/128220)).

• **CGIAR’s Khorezm study** highlighted **soil salinization as a water-quality threat**, using **statistical models** to project **increases in highly saline soils (+1.7% by 2100)** and advocating for **precision irrigation** (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3390/ijerph19148794](https://doi.org/10.3390/ijerph19148794)).

• **CGIAR emphasized water-energy-food nexus challenges**, noting **gender disparities in water access** and **systemic inefficiencies** in Uzbekistan’s irrigation networks (Source: [10568/179441](10568/179441)).

Vanuatu.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate change adaptation in Vanuatu’s fisheries and food systems** through economic modeling, policy analysis, and resilience-building strategies. Specifically:
- **CGIAR’s fisheries economists** modeled the economic costs and benefits of adaptation strategies (e.g., aquaculture, marine protected areas, fish aggregating devices) to enhance coastal and freshwater fish production by 2035 and 2050 (Source: [10.1016/j.marpol.2015.12.010](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.marpol.2015.12.010)).
- **CGIAR’s Pacific food systems researchers** developed a supply-demand model to project fish demand growth and production gaps under climate change, highlighting the need for adaptation to prevent net fish imports by 2050 (Source: [10.1016/j.marpol.2015.12.011](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.marpol.2015.12.011)).
Vanuatu.Adaptation:
• **CGIAR’s fisheries and climate adaptation team** evaluated the economic viability of low-cost inshore fish aggregating devices, aquaculture expansion, and marine protected areas to mitigate climate impacts on Vanuatu’s fisheries sector, projecting medium- and long-term production gains (Source: [10.1016/j.marpol.2015.12.010](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.marpol.2015.12.010)).

• **CGIAR’s Pacific food systems analysts** used a baseline scenario model to demonstrate that without adaptation, Vanuatu’s domestic fish production would stagnate due to climate shocks (e.g., cyclones, droughts), risking per capita consumption declines and increased reliance on imports (Source: [10.1016/j.marpol.2015.12.011](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.marpol.2015.12.011)).

• **CGIAR’s policy research unit** documented Vanuatu’s national policies for environmental conservation and coastal biodiversity preservation, framing adaptation as critical to safeguarding livelihoods amid climate-induced ecosystem degradation (Source: [10.1016/j.marpol.2015.12.022](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.marpol.2015.12.022)).

• **CGIAR’s resilience program** analyzed climate vulnerability in Pacific Island food systems, including Vanuatu, and proposed policy scenarios to strengthen adaptation among fishers and farmers facing tropical cyclones and droughts (Source: [10568/75610](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.marpol.2015.12.022)).
Vanuatu.Mitigation: No evidence found in this sample.
Vanuatu.Water: No evidence found in this sample.

Vietnam.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-resilient agriculture and policy adaptation** in **Vietnam** through targeted research on crop suitability, risk management, and sustainable production systems. Specifically:
- **CGIAR’s Climate Change, Agriculture and Food Security (CCAFS)** and **International Center for Tropical Agriculture (CIAT)** assessed climate-induced shifts in coffee and rice suitability, identifying regional vulnerabilities (Sources: [1][2]).
- **CGIAR’s Gender, Agriculture, and Assets (GAA) program** analyzed gender disparities in climate service access, proposing inclusive adaptation measures (Source: [5]).
- **CGIAR’s Sustainable Food Systems** explored barriers to climate policy implementation, informing NDC design (Source: [7]).
Vietnam.Adaptation:
• **CGIAR’s CCAFS** projected **50% global reduction in coffee suitability** for Vietnam under climate change, with Arabica coffee facing yield declines due to temperature variability (Source: [1]).
• **CGIAR’s Climate Risk Management team** evaluated **climate-smart agriculture (CSA) adoption** in rice production, identifying determinants like gender, access to climate info, and risk attitudes across Vietnamese provinces (Source: [4]).
• **CGIAR’s Resilience team** mapped **vulnerability hotspots** in Vietnam’s Mekong Delta, highlighting risks to rice/maize from flooding, sea-level rise, and drought, while noting high adaptive capacity due to infrastructure (Source: [3]).
• **CGIAR’s Gender program** reviewed **gender-responsive climate services**, emphasizing ICTs and women’s groups to bridge access gaps (Source: [5]).
• **CGIAR’s Policy team** analyzed **barriers to Paris Agreement implementation** in Vietnamese agriculture, informing NDC design (Source: [7]).
• **CGIAR’s Food Security team** developed **multi-factor scenario tools** to explore climate-socioeconomic futures, applied in Vietnam’s policy processes (Source: [9]).
Vietnam.Mitigation: • **CGIAR’s Sustainable Rice Platform (SRP) research** demonstrated **29% consumer willingness to pay** for SRP-certified rice in Vietnam, linking climate knowledge and income to sustainable production demand (Source: [6]).
Vietnam.Water: • **CGIAR’s Health and Development team** linked **dengue fever incidence** in Vietnam to climate factors (1°C temperature rise → 13–17% higher risk; 100mm precipitation → 11–30% higher risk), informing water-related health adaptation (Source: [8]).

Yemen.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate resilience and adaptive policy frameworks** in **Yemen** through evidence-based research and institutional roadmaps. Specifically:
- The Center analyzed **climate vulnerability and adaptation-focused ODA allocation** to identify disparities in aid distribution and policy effectiveness (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pclm.0000382](https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pclm.0000382)).
- Researchers assessed **flood impacts on food security** and modeled regional agroecological resilience to inform disaster risk management (Source: [10568/153637](10568/153637)).
- CGIAR developed a **climate security roadmap** to integrate climate risks into Yemeni policy frameworks, targeting institutional capacity-building (Source: [10568/138716](10568/138716)).
- Bioversity International (via FIGS) applied **machine learning to predict wheat stem rust resistance** in Yemeni bread wheat accessions, enhancing crop adaptation (Source: [hdl:20.500.11766.1/9XCRUE](hdl:20.500.11766.1/9XCRUE)).
Yemen.Adaptation:
• CGIAR evaluated **adaptation-focused Official Development Assistance (ODA) effectiveness** in Yemen, highlighting gaps between vulnerability and aid allocation (e.g., Yemen’s 0.50 EAAPC in vulnerability despite low ODA) and recommending tailored policy interventions (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pclm.0000382](https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pclm.0000382)).

• Researchers modeled **flood-induced damages to cropland, livestock, and infrastructure** using a dynamic CGE framework, demonstrating a 15% spike in hunger among farming households post-flood and advocating for integrated disaster risk management (Source: [10568/153637](10568/153637)).

• CGIAR analyzed **climate-peace-security linkages** in Yemen, identifying food/water scarcity as a driver of inequality and conflict, and proposed resilience-building programs for vulnerable communities (Source: [10568/132837](10568/132837)).

• The Center provided a **policy roadmap for mainstreaming climate security** in Yemen, outlining short/medium/long-term institutional needs for donors and policymakers (Source: [10568/138716](10568/138716)).

• Bioversity International (via FIGS) used **machine learning (Random Forest, SVM) to predict stem rust resistance** in 2,100+ Yemeni wheat accessions, selecting 210 high-probability varieties for adaptive breeding (Source: [hdl:20.500.11766.1/9XCRUE](hdl:20.500.11766.1/9XCRUE)).
Yemen.Mitigation: No evidence found in this sample.
Yemen.Water: • CGIAR explored **sustainable land and water management (SLWM) practices** as local mitigation tools, though the study focused primarily on Sub-Saharan Africa; no direct Yemeni SLWM applications were documented in this sample (Source: [10568/153703](10568/153703)).

Zambia.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-resilient agriculture and risk management** in **Zambia** through **adaptation-focused innovations** and **climate-smart agricultural practices**. Specifically:
- **CGIAR** assessed the potential of **cultivar adaptation** to offset climate change impacts on key rainfed cereals (maize, millet, sorghum, wheat) in Zambia, demonstrating that better-adapted cultivars could stabilize yields despite projected declines (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eja.2024.127137](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eja.2024.127137)).
- **CGIAR** evaluated **conservation agriculture (CA) and cereal-legume crop associations** as economically viable climate-smart options for smallholder farmers in Zambia, highlighting their role in risk mitigation and productivity gains (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ecolecon.2019.05.013](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ecolecon.2019.05.013)).
Zambia.Adaptation:
• **CGIAR** analyzed **climate-risk management strategies** for smallholder farmers in Zambia, demonstrating that **stress-adapted crop germplasm, conservation agriculture, and diversified production systems** stabilize incomes and reduce poverty risks (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agsy.2018.01.019](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agsy.2018.01.019) ).

• **CGIAR** conducted a **cost-benefit analysis** of climate-smart agriculture (CSA) options in Zambia, showing that **soil-water conservation practices, improved varieties, and cereal-legume rotations** are economically viable for risk-averse farmers (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ecolecon.2019.05.013](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ecolecon.2019.05.013) ).

• **CGIAR** explored **agricultural diversification** as a climate adaptation strategy, identifying Zambia’s potential for **food-crop, cash-crop, and livestock diversification** in regions with moderate rainfall variability (500–1000mm/year) (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1111/gcb.14158](https://doi.org/10.1111/gcb.14158) ).

• **CGIAR** prioritized **climate-smart agricultural practices** (e.g., improved seeds, conservation agriculture) in Zambia’s value chains, using a **cost-benefit framework** to identify viable adaptation options for smallholder farmers (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.heliyon.2022.e09228](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.heliyon.2022.e09228) ).

• **CGIAR** assessed **climate change impacts on maize production** in Zambia, modeling yield reductions under future scenarios and recommending **adapted maize varieties** to mitigate food security risks (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005](https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005) ).

• **CGIAR** examined **plant breeding for climate adaptation**, highlighting challenges in accessing **genetic diversity** for drought- and heat-tolerant traits in Zambia’s public breeding programs (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3390/plants9091129](https://doi.org/10.3390/plants9091129) ).
Zambia.Mitigation: • **CGIAR** evaluated **greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions** from cereal cropping intensification vs. land expansion in Zambia, concluding that **nutrient-efficient intensification** (e.g., improved nitrogen management) reduces GHG emissions compared to forest/grassland conversion (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1111/gcb.14783](https://doi.org/10.1111/gcb.14783) ).
Zambia.Water:
• **CGIAR** developed the **TAMSAT daily rainfall dataset**, providing **high-resolution satellite-based rainfall monitoring** for Zambia to support climate-informed water management (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1038/sdata.2017.63](https://doi.org/10.1038/sdata.2017.63) ).

• **CGIAR** integrated **water-energy-food (WEF) nexus approaches** in Zambia, recommending cross-sectoral policies to enhance **water resource resilience** amid projected rainfall declines (20% reduction by 2080) (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3390/ijerph15102306](https://doi.org/10.3390/ijerph15102306) ).

Zimbabwe.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-resilient agricultural systems and risk management** in **Zimbabwe** through evidence-based interventions targeting smallholder farmers. Specifically:
- CGIAR assessed **climate-risk management interventions** (e.g., stress-adapted crop germplasm, conservation agriculture, diversified production systems) to stabilize production and incomes under climate variability, while evaluating institutional options like index-based insurance (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agsy.2018.01.019](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agsy.2018.01.019)).
- The Center analyzed **gender-responsive climate services** to address disparities in access to climate information, recommending ICT-based solutions and women’s group inclusion to enhance adaptation (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1080/17565529.2019.1613216](https://doi.org/10.1080/17565529.2019.1613216)).
Zimbabwe.Adaptation:
• CGIAR evaluated **climate-risk management strategies** (e.g., stress-adapted crops, conservation agriculture, diversified systems) to reduce poverty and stabilize livelihoods for smallholder farmers in dryland regions, highlighting institutional gaps in scaling impact (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agsy.2018.01.019](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agsy.2018.01.019)).

• The Center demonstrated that **agricultural diversification** (e.g., food/livestock/cash crops) improves household food security in Zimbabwe, particularly in regions with 500–1000mm rainfall, while noting land and market constraints limit scalability (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1111/gcb.14158](https://doi.org/10.1111/gcb.14158)).

• CGIAR analyzed **trade-offs/synergies in adaptation strategies** (e.g., crop/livestock/water management) for smallholders, identifying low-cost options like minimum tillage and complementary practices to enhance resilience (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.regsus.2021.05.002](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.regsus.2021.05.002)).

• Researchers quantified **maize yield losses** (13–32%) due to climate change in Zimbabwe by 2100, emphasizing the need for transformed cropping systems (e.g., adjusted planting dates, fertilizer optimization) to offset impacts (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1111/gcb.13061](https://doi.org/10.1111/gcb.13061)).

• CGIAR identified **fodder trees** as drought-resilient feed sources for livestock, addressing production shortages in extreme climatic events, though uptake is constrained by seed shortages and farmer knowledge gaps (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cosust.2013.11.008](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cosust.2013.11.008)).

• The Center conducted **participatory vulnerability assessments** in Zimbabwe, linking farmer perceptions of increased rainfall variability/temperature shifts to adaptation options like crop diversification and soil fertility management (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.crm.2014.05.004](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.crm.2014.05.004)).

• CGIAR explored **gender-based disparities** in climate service access, proposing ICTs and women’s networks to bridge gaps in climate information use (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1080/17565529.2019.1613216](https://doi.org/10.1080/17565529.2019.1613216)).
Zimbabwe.Mitigation: No evidence found in this sample.
Zimbabwe.Water:
• CGIAR integrated **water-energy-food (WEF) nexus approaches** to address climate-induced water insecurity in southern Africa, recommending cross-sectoral policies to build resilience for marginalized communities (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3390/ijerph15102306](https://doi.org/10.3390/ijerph15102306)).

• Researchers modeled **climate impacts on maize production** under varying nitrogen levels, highlighting water management as critical for yield stability in Zimbabwe’s maize mega-environments (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005](https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005)).

• The Center evaluated **economy-wide adaptation scenarios** (e.g., doubling irrigated area, yield increases) using water-agriculture models, concluding that productivity gains outperform irrigation expansion for climate resilience in Sub-Saharan Africa (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ecolecon.2013.05.006](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ecolecon.2013.05.006)).

Mauritania.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate resilience and adaptive capacity** in **Mauritania** through participatory research, policy-aligned interventions, and sector-specific adaptation strategies. Specifically:
- **CGIAR** advanced **climate-smart agriculture** by synthesizing adaptation strategies for crop, livestock, and fishery sectors in West Africa, including Mauritania’s pastoral and agro-pastoral systems (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1186/s40066-016-0075-3](https://doi.org/10.1186/s40066-016-0075-3)).
- **CGIAR** integrated **social protection and climate resilience** in the Sahel, aligning Mauritania’s national programs with adaptive capacities for vulnerable households (Source: [10568/135122](10568/135122)).
Mauritania.Adaptation:
• **CGIAR** synthesized **climate-smart agriculture frameworks** for West Africa, including Mauritania, to improve resilience in crop, livestock, and fishery systems through tailored adaptation strategies and policy mechanisms (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1186/s40066-016-0075-3](https://doi.org/10.1186/s40066-016-0075-3) and [https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005](https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005) for maize-specific adaptation).

• **CGIAR** analyzed **trade-offs and synergies** in smallholder adaptation strategies (e.g., water/soil management, risk mitigation) to enhance productivity and sustainability in Mauritania’s agroecological contexts (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.regsus.2021.05.002](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.regsus.2021.05.002)).

• **CGIAR** facilitated **participatory adaptation planning** for Mauritania’s fisheries and aquaculture sectors, engaging youth and stakeholders in scenario-building to address climate risks (e.g., small pelagic fish declines) and regional trade vulnerabilities (Source: [10568/34904](10568/34904) and [10568/34905](10568/34905)).

• **CGIAR** evaluated **pastoral resource management** under Mauritania’s *Pastoral Code*, identifying decentralization conflicts and bottlenecks to improve sustainable rangeland use (Source: [10568/36082](10568/36082)).

• **CGIAR** aligned **social protection programs** in Mauritania with climate resilience goals, assessing adaptive capacities of vulnerable households (Source: [10568/135122](10568/135122)).

• **CGIAR** conducted **participatory research** on water/soil conservation and livestock management, training farmers in techniques like small dams and runoff strips (Source: [10568/36080](10568/36080) and [10568/36079](10568/36079)).
Mauritania.Mitigation: No evidence found in this sample.
Mauritania.Water:
• **CGIAR** developed **water simulation models** (e.g., IMPACT, GTAP-W) to assess economy-wide impacts of climate change on irrigated/rainfed agriculture in Sub-Saharan Africa, including Mauritania, and evaluated adaptation scenarios like yield improvements and irrigation expansion (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ecolecon.2013.05.006](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ecolecon.2013.05.006)).

• **CGIAR** implemented **participatory soil and water conservation programs** in Mauritania, training communities in techniques such as dikes, bunds, and runoff strips (Source: [10568/36080](10568/36080) and [10568/36079](10568/36079)).

Tanzania.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-resilient agriculture and adaptive strategies** in **Tanzania** through targeted research on crop diversification, climate-smart technologies, and hydrological risk management. Specifically:
- **CGIAR’s Climate Change, Agriculture, and Food Security (CCAFS) Program** assessed **climate-smart agriculture (CSA) adoption** among smallholders, identifying synergies between crop diversity, irrigation, and agroforestry while addressing trade-offs like livestock diversity (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3389/fsufs.2020.00055](https://doi.org/10.3389/fsufs.2020.00055)).
- **CGIAR researchers** demonstrated **climate change impacts on *Coffea arabica* yields** in northern Tanzania, quantifying yield losses due to rising nighttime temperatures and advocating for adaptation strategies (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agrformet.2015.03.005](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agrformet.2015.03.005)).
Tanzania.Adaptation:
• **CGIAR’s CCAFS Program** evaluated **determinants of CSA technology adoption** (e.g., crop diversity, irrigation, agroforestry) in Tanzania, highlighting synergies (e.g., fertilizer use + agroforestry) and trade-offs (e.g., livestock diversity vs. irrigation), with female resource control and household assets as key adoption drivers (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3389/fsufs.2020.00055](https://doi.org/10.3389/fsufs.2020.00055)).

• **CGIAR researchers** analyzed **climate change impacts on rainfed cereals** (maize, millet, sorghum, wheat) in East Africa, including Tanzania, showing **6% yield declines by 2050** and emphasizing the role of **adapted cultivars** to offset losses (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eja.2024.127137](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eja.2024.127137)).

• **CGIAR’s International Center for Tropical Agriculture (CIAT)** linked **agricultural diversification** to food security in Tanzania, identifying **500–1000mm rainfall zones** as optimal for crop/livestock diversification to enhance resilience (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1111/gcb.14158](https://doi.org/10.1111/gcb.14158)).

• **CGIAR’s World Agroforestry Centre (ICRAF)** documented **climate-smart practices** (e.g., agroforestry, irrigation) as critical for **transforming food systems** in Tanzania, aligning with a broader African framework for adaptive agriculture (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3390/su13084305](https://doi.org/10.3390/su13084305)).

• **CGIAR researchers** studied **temporary migration responses** to climate variation in East Africa, finding **urban out-migration declines** during temperature/rainfall anomalies, challenging assumptions about rural-to-urban migration as a climate adaptation strategy (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.worlddev.2019.104704](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.worlddev.2019.104704)).

• **CGIAR’s International Institute of Tropical Agriculture (IITA)** examined **smallholder adaptation attitudes** in Tanzania, revealing **preference for crop diversification and variety changes** over soil/water management, with climate information and farmer groups as key enablers (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.crm.2017.03.001](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.crm.2017.03.001)).
Tanzania.Mitigation: No evidence found in this sample.
Tanzania.Water:
• **CGIAR researchers** projected **decreased precipitation (up to 4.5%) and altered hydrological extremes** (droughts/floods) in southern Tanzania by 2050–2100, using CMIP-6 models to inform **site-specific adaptation** (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1029/2022ef003011](https://doi.org/10.1029/2022ef003011)).

• **CGIAR’s Water Management Program** evaluated **ERA5 reanalysis improvements** for precipitation data in East Africa, noting **reduced wet-bias errors** during rainy seasons, critical for **agricultural water planning** (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3390/atmos11090996](https://doi.org/10.3390/atmos11090996)).

• **CGIAR’s WEF Nexus research** highlighted **climate change impacts on water resources** in southern Africa, including Tanzania, advocating for **integrated water-energy-food policies** to build resilience (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3390/ijerph15102306](https://doi.org/10.3390/ijerph15102306)).
