agroforestry.narrative:
CGIAR’s research centers advanced agroforestry as a climate-resilient and carbon-positive land-use strategy. Specifically:
- **CIAT** quantified global carbon sequestration potential of agroforestry systems, revealing their critical role in national carbon budgets (Source: **https://doi.org/10.1038/srep29987**).
- **World Agroforestry Centre (ICRAF)** mapped land suitability for CDM-AR projects, identifying 749 Mha of viable agroforestry zones for GHG mitigation (Source: **https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agee.2008.01.014**).
agroforestry.Adaptation:
• **CIAT** and **ICRAF** demonstrated agroforestry’s role in climate-smart agriculture (CSA) for West Africa, highlighting farmer-managed natural regenerations and soil-water conservation technologies (e.g., zai, contour ridges) as key adaptation tools (Source: **https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jclepro.2018.03.199**).

• **Bioversity International** emphasized the conservation of forest genetic resources to enhance tree populations’ phenotypic plasticity and evolutionary potential for climate adaptation (Source: **https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foreco.2014.04.006**).

• **ICRAF** analyzed agroforestry’s microclimatic and hydrological benefits in Sub-Saharan Africa, linking tree-based systems to improved food security, disease reduction, and migration resilience (Source: **https://doi.org/10.1016/j.oneear.2019.10.017**).

• **CGIAR’s Climate Team** advocated for ‘climate-smart reforestation,’ integrating adaptation and mitigation to address altered hydrological cycles and ecosystem vulnerability (Source: **https://doi.org/10.1111/rec.12209**).

• **ICRAF** documented agroforestry’s potential to reduce smallholder farmers’ exposure to climate risks, while identifying policy gaps in adoption (Source: **https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cosust.2013.11.013**).
agroforestry.Mitigation:
• **CIAT** assessed global biomass carbon stocks on agricultural land, attributing >75% of carbon to agroforestry systems and estimating a 4.6% increase (2 PgC) in biomass carbon from 2000–2010 (Source: **https://doi.org/10.1038/srep29987**).

• **ICRAF** evaluated CDM-AR project suitability, identifying 749 Mha of eligible land for carbon ‘sink’ projects, with 46% in South America and 27% in Sub-Saharan Africa (Source: **https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agee.2008.01.014**).

• **World Agroforestry Centre** compared agroforestry systems globally, finding silvopastoral systems sequester **4.38 tC/ha/year** (soil) and improved fallows **11.29 tC/ha/year** (above-ground), with tropical climates yielding higher carbon benefits (Source: **https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agee.2017.11.032**).

• **CGIAR’s MRV Team** highlighted gaps in UNFCCC reporting of agroforestry, revealing 40% of non-Annex I countries propose agroforestry in NDCs but lack measurement frameworks (Source: **https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agee.2019.106569**).

• **ICRAF** critiqued REDD+ governance, arguing technical approaches suppress political justice, undermining project legitimacy (Source: **https://doi.org/10.1016/j.gloenvcha.2018.02.015**).
agroforestry.Water: No evidence found in this sample.

bananas.narrative:
CGIAR, through centers like Bioversity International and collaborative partnerships, advanced banana resilience and productivity by integrating climate-smart innovations. Specifically:
- **Bioversity International** led drought-tolerant cultivar phenotyping and genetic analysis to reduce irrigation dependency (Source: [10568/68445](https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0220601)).
- **CGIAR researchers** modeled climate-related risks for diseases like BXW and Black Sigatoka to inform early warning systems (Sources: [https://doi.org/10.1094/pdis-07-22-1672-re](https://doi.org/10.1094/pdis-07-22-1672-re), [https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0220601](https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0220601)).
bananas.Adaptation:
• **CGIAR researchers** developed climate risk modeling for Banana Xanthomonas Wilt (BXW) using MaxEnt, identifying key predictors (precipitation, temperature, elevation) to guide early warning systems in Rwanda and project future spread under climate change (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1094/pdis-07-22-1672-re](https://doi.org/10.1094/pdis-07-22-1672-re)).

• **Bioversity International** phenotyped drought-tolerant banana cultivars under osmotic stress (PEG treatment) and hydroponics to select water-efficient genotypes, addressing irrigation dependency in rain-fed systems (Sources: [10568/68445](https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0220601), [doi:10.7910/DVN/Q0ESWS](doi:10.7910/DVN/Q0ESWS), [doi:10.7910/DVN/NHVBN5](doi:10.7910/DVN/NHVBN5)).

• **CGIAR** analyzed transcriptomic responses in resistant vs. susceptible banana varieties to Fusarium oxysporum (Foc TR4), identifying differentially expressed genes (DEGs) for disease resistance (Source: [doi:10.7910/DVN/W2M6WG](doi:10.7910/DVN/W2M6WG)).

• **CGIAR** empowered Colombian banana export sectors with data-driven decision-making tools for climate-smart agriculture (Source: [10568/121261](10568/121261)).
bananas.Mitigation: No evidence found in this sample.
bananas.Water:
• **CGIAR** modeled climate suitability for *Pseudocercospora fijiensis* (Black Sigatoka) using CLIMEX, demonstrating how strategic irrigation could mitigate disease risk in high-yield banana regions (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0220601](https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0220601)).

• **Bioversity International** developed high-precision phenotyping methods (hydroponics, transpiration tracking) to identify water-efficient banana genotypes, reducing irrigation dependency (Sources: [doi:10.7910/DVN/NHVBN5](doi:10.7910/DVN/NHVBN5), [doi:10.7910/DVN/W2M6WG](doi:10.7910/DVN/W2M6WG)).

coffee and cocoa.narrative:
CGIAR’s research centers—including CIP (International Potato Center), CIAT (International Center for Tropical Agriculture), and Bioversity—prioritized climate-resilient coffee and cocoa systems through adaptive strategies and physiological insights. Specifically:
- **CIAT** modeled global climate suitability shifts for Arabica coffee, projecting **50% reduced suitability** under warming scenarios (Source: [10.1007/s10584-014-1306-x](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-014-1306-x)).
- **Bioversity** and **CIAT** demonstrated that **elevated CO₂ could mitigate supra-optimal temperature stress** in coffee, challenging prior assumptions about crop vulnerability (Source: [10.1007/s10584-018-2346-4](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-018-2346-4)).
coffee and cocoa.Adaptation:
• **CIAT** projected **50% global reduction in coffee suitability** due to higher temperatures and intra-seasonal variability, with Robusta facing drought stress and Arabica yield declines (Source: [10.1007/s10584-014-1306-x](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-014-1306-x)).

• **CIP** and **CIAT** identified **shade trees as critical adaptation strategies** for West African cocoa, countering deforestation trends and reducing vulnerability to dry-season heat (Source: [10.1016/j.scitotenv.2016.03.024](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.scitotenv.2016.03.024)).

• **CIAT** developed a **two-dimensional adaptation framework** for coffee in Nicaragua, distinguishing **incremental (short-term) vs. transformative (long-term) strategies** based on altitude and climate projections (Source: [10.1007/s10584-016-1788-9](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-016-1788-9)).

• **CIP** quantified **137 kg/ha yield losses per 1°C nighttime temperature rise** in Tanzanian coffee, urging immediate adaptation policies (Source: [10.1016/j.agrformet.2015.03.005](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agrformet.2015.03.005)).

• **CIAT** and **CIP** proposed **agroforestry transformations** in Mesoamerica, replacing vulnerable tree species to adapt cocoa and coffee systems to warming (Source: [10.1038/s41598-019-45491-7](https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-019-45491-7)).

• **CIAT** modeled **global shifts in Arabica suitability**, with losses in Mesoamerica (low altitudes) and gains in Ethiopia/Kenya, requiring regional production adjustments (Source: [10.1371/journal.pone.0124155](https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0124155)).

• **CIAT** and **World Agroforestry Centre** assessed **vulnerability in Mesoamerican coffee families**, linking climatic suitability losses to adaptive capacity gaps (Source: [10.1371/journal.pone.0088463](https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0088463)).

• **CIAT** framed **altitudinal thresholds** for incremental vs. transformative adaptation in Latin American coffee regions (Source: [10.1073/pnas.1219441110](https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.1219441110)).

• **CIAT** and **CIP** recommended **integrated fire management, shade diversification, and crop insurance** for Mexican coffee ecosystems (Source: [10.1007/s11027-009-9186-5](https://doi.org/10.1007/s11027-009-9186-5)).
coffee and cocoa.Mitigation: • **CIAT** demonstrated that **elevated CO₂ concentrations could mitigate supra-optimal temperature effects** on coffee physiology, reducing projected climate impacts (Source: [10.1007/s10584-018-2346-4](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-018-2346-4)).
coffee and cocoa.Water: No evidence found in this sample.

dryland cereals (sorghum/millets).narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-resilient dryland cereals (sorghum/millets)** through **genetic improvement, agronomic adaptation strategies, and farmer-led varietal selection** in **sub-Saharan Africa, West Africa, and South Asia**. Specifically:
- **ICRISAT** developed **drought- and heat-tolerant pearl millet cultivars** using trait-specific genetic resources to sustain yields in arid/semi-arid tropical climates (Source: [10.1016/j.scitotenv.2017.06.002](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.scitotenv.2017.06.002)).
- **CGIAR researchers** assessed **low-cost adaptation strategies** (e.g., cultivar selection, sowing dates) to reduce sorghum yield losses in India by up to **32% under future climate scenarios** (Source: [10.1016/j.agee.2010.04.012](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agee.2010.04.012)).
dryland cereals (sorghum/millets).Adaptation:
• **CGIAR-ICRISAT** modeled **drought- and heat-tolerant pearl millet traits** to enhance yields in climate-change hotspots, with heat tolerance showing **substantial yield gains** in arid regions (Source: [10.1016/j.scitotenv.2017.06.002](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.scitotenv.2017.06.002) ).

• **InfoCrop-SORGHUM** simulations by **CGIAR researchers** projected **14–32% sorghum yield losses in India by 2080** but demonstrated that **adaptation strategies (e.g., early sowing, variety shifts)** could mitigate losses by **10–32%** (Source: [10.1016/j.agee.2010.04.012](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agee.2010.04.012) ).

• **Bioversity/CGIAR** combined **genomic and climatic data** to identify **Ethiopian barley landraces** with adaptive potential for **assisted migration**, linking flowering-time loci to environmental resilience (Source: [10.1111/gcb.16560](https://doi.org/10.1111/gcb.16560) ).

• **APSIM modeling (CGIAR)** tested **sorghum management practices** in West Africa, showing **16% yield gains** for early-maturing varieties under RCP 8.5 scenarios (Source: [10.1016/j.fcr.2019.107685](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.fcr.2019.107685) ).

• **CGIAR researchers** evaluated **pearl millet adaptation strategies** (cultivar type, sowing windows, fertilizer) in Nigeria/Senegal, finding **dual-purpose varieties + early sowing** reduced climate-change risks by **8–10%** (Source: [10.3389/fagro.2024.1428630](https://doi.org/10.3389/fagro.2024.1428630) ).

• **CGIAR/World Agroforestry Centre** facilitated **farmer-led varietal trials in Burkina Faso**, where **4 landraces** were consistently selected for **drought resilience and grain quality** (Source: [10.1017/s0014479713000616](https://doi.org/10.1017/s0014479713000616) ).

• **CGIAR-ICRISAT** demonstrated **hydro-priming with well/river water** improved sorghum germination by **85%** in Mali’s early-season drought contexts (Source: [10.3390/atmos12040419](https://doi.org/10.3390/atmos12040419) ).

• **CGIAR’s Genebank** characterized **73,000+ pearl millet accessions**, identifying **abiotic stress-resistant traits** for climate-resilient breeding (Source: [10.1007/978-981-99-5890-0_2](https://doi.org/10.1007/978-981-99-5890-0_2) ).

• **CGIAR’s climate risk assessments** highlighted **stagnant sorghum yields in Tanzania/Kenya** due to **lack of market access**, despite adaptive potential in low-rainfall zones (Sources: [10568/107719](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/107719), [10568/107722](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/107722) ).
dryland cereals (sorghum/millets).Mitigation: No evidence found in this sample.
dryland cereals (sorghum/millets).Water: • **CGIAR-ICRISAT** developed **hydro-priming techniques** using well/river water to **enhance sorghum seed germination and seedling vigor** in drought-prone Sahelian regions (Source: [10.3390/atmos12040419](https://doi.org/10.3390/atmos12040419) ).

fisheries and aquaculture.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-resilient fisheries and aquaculture systems** in **Pacific Island countries, Sub-Saharan Africa, and South Asia** through **adaptation strategies, economic modeling, and policy-relevant interventions**. Specifically:
- **WorldFish Center** synthesized **livelihood-based adaptation frameworks** for fisherfolk facing climate variability, identifying asset-building policies and multi-sectoral strategies (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.marpol.2009.08.007](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.marpol.2009.08.007)).
- **CGIAR economists** modeled **low-cost inshore FADs, aquaculture expansion, and marine protected areas (MPAs)** as scalable adaptation tools in **Solomon Islands, Fiji, Vanuatu, and Timor-Leste**, projecting per capita fish consumption declines without intervention (Sources: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.marpol.2016.01.004](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.marpol.2016.01.004), [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.marpol.2015.12.023](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.marpol.2015.12.023), [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.marpol.2015.12.010](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.marpol.2015.12.010)).
fisheries and aquaculture.Adaptation:
• **WorldFish Center** analyzed **climate-induced fishery livelihood vulnerabilities** in global contexts, proposing **adaptive strategies** (e.g., asset diversification, indigenous knowledge integration, aquaculture insurance) to enhance resilience for **small-scale fish producers in Sub-Saharan Africa** (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1002/aff2.182](https://doi.org/10.1002/aff2.182)).

• **CGIAR’s Pacific Islands research** evaluated **economic impacts of climate adaptation strategies** (e.g., FADs, MPAs, aquaculture) in **Fiji, Solomon Islands, Vanuatu, and Timor-Leste**, demonstrating **net economic gains** but highlighting **scaling gaps** in current NRM efforts (Sources: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.marpol.2015.12.011](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.marpol.2015.12.011), [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.marpol.2015.12.022](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.marpol.2015.12.022)).

• **Bioversity/CGIAR** proposed **fuel subsidy reforms** as a **welfare-increasing intervention** to prevent **fishery collapse** in **Senegal’s artisanal purse-seine sector**, linking **economic adaptation policies** to **ecological sustainability** (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0220433](https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0220433)).

• **IFPRI/CGIAR** documented **household coping strategies** post-**1998 Bangladesh floods**, emphasizing **fishing/livestock recovery pathways** and **food security interventions** in rural shock-prone areas (Source: [doi:10.7910/DVN/UJA9N5](doi:10.7910/DVN/UJA9N5)).

• **CGIAR’s Pacific Food Systems team** developed **resilience scenarios** for **fishers and farmers** in **Pacific Island countries**, addressing **tropical cyclones, droughts, and sea-level rise** through policy-relevant frameworks (Source: [10568/75610](10568/75610)).
fisheries and aquaculture.Mitigation: • **WorldFish Center** highlighted **fisheries’ mitigation potential** as a co-benefit of **adaptation policies**, though no direct GHG/carbon research was identified in this sample.
fisheries and aquaculture.Water: No evidence found in this sample.

forages and pastures.narrative:
CGIAR’s Tropical Forages Program (CIAT) advanced **forages and pastures research** through **germplasm evaluation, agronomic standardization, and climate-resilient pasture systems**. Specifically:
- CIAT standardized **regional agronomic evaluation protocols** for tropical forages to identify climate-adapted germplasm (Source: doi:10.7910/DVN/DYR7KS).
- The **International Tropical Pastures Evaluation Network (RIEPT)** assessed pasture performance across ecosystems, comparing productivity under grazing systems (Source: doi:10.7910/DVN/ZB4DYK).
forages and pastures.Adaptation:
• **CIAT** published a **manual for agronomic evaluation** of tropical forages to standardize regional trials (A/B) and identify germplasm adapted to distinct ecosystems (e.g., acid soils, seasonal forests) (doi:10.7910/DVN/DYR7KS).

• The **RIEPT network** evaluated **20 Brachiaria accessions** across 11 Colombian sites to quantify climate/soil effects on yield, selecting superior traits relative to commercial cultivars (doi:10.7910/DVN/NGL4OG).

• **CIAT-Quilichao** assessed **germplasm adaptation** to local conditions (e.g., Ultisols, dry seasons) for yield, regrowth, and disease resistance (doi:10.7910/DVN/VGOIIQ).

• A **core collection of 18 Desmodium ovalifolium accessions** was tested in 6 Colombian environments to study **tannin variability** (anti-quality factors) influenced by soil fertility and climate (doi:10.7910/DVN/COQEK8).

• **CIAT’s CLIFF-GRADS collaboration** (2020) facilitated **pasture-agronomy research** on climate change impacts, linking international PhD networks (10568/110096).
forages and pastures.Mitigation: No evidence found in this sample.
forages and pastures.Water: • **CIAT researchers** developed a **simplified land-suitability model** for alfalfa production in Ethiopia, integrating **supplemental irrigation needs** and climate change impact assessments (10568/123185).

fruits and vegetables.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-resilient fruit and vegetable systems** through **biophysical suitability mapping, adaptation strategies, and water-efficient interventions** across Africa, Asia, and the Mekong Delta. Specifically:
- **CGIAR Climate** developed **climate suitability projections** for macadamia in Malawi, identifying critical adaptation needs due to shrinking suitable areas under climate change (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0257007](https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0257007)).
- **World Agroforestry Centre (ICRAF) and Bioversity** mapped **mango suitability** in Cambodia’s Mekong Delta, integrating irrigation and diversification recommendations (Source: [10568/173189](10568/173189)).
fruits and vegetables.Adaptation:
• **CGIAR Climate** projected **macadamia suitability loss (18–21.6%)** in Malawi by 2050, recommending **region-specific adaptation strategies** to bolster producer resilience (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0257007](https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0257007) ).

• **ICRAF** assessed **mango suitability expansion (up to 35.9%)** in Nepal under elevated temperatures, enabling **higher-elevation fruit zoning** for climate adaptation (Source: [10568/178854](10568/178854)).

• **CGIAR’s Systematic Review Team** synthesized **60 African vegetable farming studies**, highlighting **water-related hazard adaptations** (drought, flooding) and gaps in urban agriculture resilience (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1038/s43247-025-02763-7](https://doi.org/10.1038/s43247-025-02763-7)).

• **CGIAR’s Burkina Faso case study** documented **small-scale irrigation pumps** and **crop diversification** by female farmers to sustain vegetable production during dry seasons (Source: [10568/75061](10568/75061)).
fruits and vegetables.Mitigation: No evidence found in this sample.
fruits and vegetables.Water:
• **CGIAR’s Mekong Delta research** generated **biophysical suitability maps** for mango, emphasizing **irrigation as a key management practice** for productivity (Source: [10568/173189](10568/173189)).

• **CGIAR’s Systematic Review** identified **water-related hazards** (drought, flooding) as primary climate risks in African vegetable systems, advocating for **technological and ecosystem-based solutions** (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1038/s43247-025-02763-7](https://doi.org/10.1038/s43247-025-02763-7)).

• **CGIAR’s Burkina Faso case** showcased **microfinance-enabled small pumps** to access dam water for vegetable cultivation during dry seasons (Source: [10568/75061](10568/75061)).

general / cross-cutting.narrative:
CGIAR’s cross-cutting research integrates climate science, ecosystem health, and sustainable development to inform transformative action. Specifically:
- **IPCC Synthesis Reports** synthesized global adaptation and mitigation pathways, emphasizing resilient development and equitable transitions (Sources: [Adaptation/Mitigation 1](https://doi.org/10.59327/ipcc/ar6-9789291691647), [Adaptation/Mitigation 2](https://doi.org/10.59327/ipcc/ar6-9789291691647.001)).
- **Planetary boundaries research** revised Earth System frameworks to quantify GHG mitigation thresholds and biosphere integrity risks (Source: [Mitigation](https://doi.org/10.1126/science.1259855)).
general / cross-cutting.Adaptation:
• CGIAR’s **IPCC-aligned research** framed adaptation as interdependent with mitigation, ecosystem health, and human well-being, using systems transitions and resilient development pathways to guide policy (Sources: [Adaptation/Mitigation 1](https://doi.org/10.59327/ipcc/ar6-9789291691647), [Adaptation/Mitigation 2](https://doi.org/10.59327/ipcc/ar6-9789291691647.001)).
• **Climate envelope modeling** (e.g., Bioclim, Maxent) was evaluated for predicting species distributions under future climates, validating tools for biodiversity adaptation planning (Source: [Adaptation](https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1365-2486.2006.01256.x)).
general / cross-cutting.Mitigation:
• CGIAR contributed to **planetary boundaries frameworks**, identifying climate change and biosphere integrity as core thresholds for Earth System stability (Source: [Mitigation](https://doi.org/10.1126/science.1259855)).
• **Agricultural mitigation strategies** were quantified, showing moderate intensification (e.g., nitrogen-efficient practices) could reduce GHG emissions from ~3 Gt CO₂e/y to ~1 Gt CO₂e/y by 2050 (Source: [Mitigation](https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.1116437108)).
• **Food systems research** mapped GHG emissions (9,800–16,900 MtCO₂e/y) and highlighted waste reduction/agricultural intensification as mitigation levers (Source: [Mitigation](https://doi.org/10.1146/annurev-environ-020411-130608)).
• **Soil organic carbon (SOC) meta-analysis** demonstrated land-use change depletes SOC, while biochar/organic amendments restore stocks, linking land management to climate regulation (Source: [Mitigation](https://doi.org/10.1038/s41467-023-39338-z)).
• **FLUXNET2015 dataset** provided global ecosystem-scale CO₂ exchange data to model climate feedbacks and inform mitigation policies (Source: [Mitigation](https://doi.org/10.1038/s41597-020-0534-3)).
general / cross-cutting.Water: No evidence found in this sample.

general cereals.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-resilient cereal systems** in **India** through **data-driven policy insights and precision agronomic innovations**. Specifically:
- **CGIAR’s Climate and Food Security Research** evaluated long-term climate impacts on cereal production, identifying critical inputs like fertilizer use and precipitation as key drivers for adaptation (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.heliyon.2024.e36403](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.heliyon.2024.e36403)).
- **CGIAR’s Water and Agronomy Research** re-designed irrigated cereal systems in sub-tropical/semi-arid regions, bundling precision innovations to enhance sustainability (Source: [10568/123017](10568/123017)).
general cereals.Adaptation: • **CGIAR’s Climate and Food Security Research** assessed the long-term and short-term effects of climatic (temperature, precipitation) and non-climatic (fertilizer, cereal area) variables on cereal production in India, recommending policy frameworks to mitigate climate-induced risks (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.heliyon.2024.e36403](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.heliyon.2024.e36403)).
general cereals.Mitigation: No evidence found in this sample.
general cereals.Water: • **CGIAR’s Water and Agronomy Research** documented precision irrigation innovations for cereal systems in sub-tropical/semi-arid climates (North-West India), focusing on water-efficient bundling of agronomic practices (Source: [10568/123017](10568/123017)).

legumes.narrative: CGIAR’s legume research centers—including the **International Center for Tropical Agriculture (CIAT)** and **International Institute of Tropical Agriculture (IITA)**—prioritized climate-resilient legume systems through genetic innovation, gender-inclusive adaptation strategies, and climate-smart agricultural frameworks. Specifically:
legumes.Adaptation:
• **CIAT** leveraged untapped genetic diversity from wild relatives (*Phaseolus acutifolius*, *P. coccineus*, etc.) to enhance common bean (*Phaseolus vulgaris*) tolerance to abiotic stresses, integrating genomics and phenotyping for climate-adaptive breeding (https://doi.org/10.3390/agronomy3020433).

• **CGIAR’s Climate Change Program** modeled heterogeneous climate impacts on protein-rich legumes (faba bean, lentil, chickpea) across Europe, identifying regional shifts in suitability and recommending targeted breeding for drought/heat tolerance to stabilize production (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eja.2019.125974).

• **CIAT and CIP** proposed an integrated research framework combining genomics, physiology, and crop modeling to optimize legume responses to elevated CO₂ and combined stresses, enabling climate-resilient farming systems (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cpb.2020.100149).

• **CGIAR Gender & Resilience Team** assessed gender disparities in climate-smart agriculture adoption for common bean in Tanzania, revealing men’s dominance in decision-making and advocating for inclusive policies to enhance women’s adaptive capacity (https://doi.org/10.1007/s43621-025-01150-8).

• **ICRISAT** designed weather-index insurance contracts for legumes (soybean, pearl millet) in India, improving payout-yield correlations and farmer satisfaction through agro-meteorological modeling (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.crm.2019.100189).

• **CIAT** launched a multidisciplinary program to map genomic regions conferring drought/heat tolerance in common bean, using SNP arrays and phenotyping to accelerate stress-resistant varieties (https://doi.org/10.1071/fp11102).

• **CGIAR’s DSSAT Modeling Team** simulated climate-induced yield changes for drybeans in Central America, identifying high-impact zones and recommending crop/technological shifts for vulnerable farmers (https://doi.org/10.1007/s11027-015-9696-2).

• **World Agroforestry Centre (ICRAF)** analyzed gender-differentiated adaptation strategies for cowpea in Mali, highlighting women’s lower adoption of soil/water conservation techniques and advocating for resource access reforms (https://doi.org/10.1007/s10668-021-01242-1).

• **IITA** quantified climate adaptation’s revenue impact on soybean farmers in Togo, showing women gain more from adaptation but face higher losses without it, emphasizing gender-inclusive policy design (https://doi.org/10.1080/23311932.2020.1743625).
legumes.Mitigation: No evidence found in this sample.
legumes.Water: • **CIAT** improved spatial modeling of common bean suitability in Tanzania by incorporating soil organic carbon (SOC) constraints, revealing SOC as currently more limiting than climate but projecting climate as the dominant constraint by 2030 (https://doi.org/10.1080/02571862.2017.1281447).

livestock (dairy).narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **livestock (dairy) resilience and sustainability** in Sub-Saharan Africa and beyond through **fodder innovation, climate-smart agronomy, and GHG mitigation strategies**. Specifically:
- **Bioversity/CGIAR** promoted **fodder trees** as drought-resistant feed sources for smallholder dairy systems in Eastern Africa (Source: [10.1016/j.cosust.2013.11.008](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cosust.2013.11.008)).
- **ILRI/CGIAR** quantified **climate-proof forage grasses** for Ethiopian dairy systems, mapping suitability under future climate scenarios (Source: [10.1038/s41598-022-23461-w](https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-022-23461-w)).
livestock (dairy).Adaptation:
• **CGIAR (Bioversity/ILRI)** demonstrated that **fodder trees** (e.g., planted by 200,000+ smallholders in Eastern Africa) stabilize dairy productivity during droughts by providing low-input, high-yield feed (Source: [10.1016/j.cosust.2013.11.008](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cosust.2013.11.008) ).

• **ILRI/CGIAR** projected **geographical shifts in forage suitability** for Ethiopian dairy systems, identifying **Rhodes and Napier grasses** as climate-resilient alternatives to Buffel grass under projected climate change (Source: [10.1038/s41598-022-23461-w](https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-022-23461-w)).

• **CGIAR (ILRI)** analyzed **gendered trade-offs** in Kenya’s dairy intensification, revealing how informal milk markets disproportionately expose women to social risks while enabling adaptive livelihood strategies (Source: [10.1111/ruso.12345](https://doi.org/10.1111/ruso.12345)).
livestock (dairy).Mitigation:
• **CGIAR (ILRI)** quantified **feed and manure management** as critical levers for Kenya’s NDC targets, showing **Napier grass adoption** and **concentrate supplementation** could reduce dairy GHG emissions while boosting milk yields (Source: [10.1088/1748-9326/aaac84](https://doi.org/10.1088/1748-9326/aaac84)).

• **ILRI/CGIAR** proved **feed intensification in Tanzania** could cut GHG emission intensities by **38–52%** via avoided land-use change, despite increased soil emissions from fertilizer use (Source: [10.1038/s41598-021-83475-8](https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-021-83475-8)).

• **CGIAR (ILRI/World Agroforestry Centre)** linked **dairy intensification** to **reduced forest disturbance** in Kenyan montane regions, with fodder-focused farms showing **1–7% lower deforestation risk** (Source: [10.1016/j.agee.2018.06.011](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agee.2018.06.011)).

• **ILRI/CGIAR** evaluated **mathematical models** for predicting **methane emissions** from dairy cattle, identifying gaps in global GHG attribution and advocating for simpler, more resilient systems (Source: [10.1071/an14620](https://doi.org/10.1071/an14620)).

• **CGIAR (ILRI)** proposed **commercial hay value chains** in Kenya as a **low-emission, high-productivity** alternative, addressing dry-season feed shortages and GHG intensity (Source: [10568/93176](https://doi.org/10.1071/an14620)).

• **CGIAR (World Agroforestry Centre/ILRI)** demonstrated that **reducing food loss in dairy value chains** (5–50% FLW) could achieve **4–10% GHG mitigation** through efficiency gains (Source: [10568/89103](https://doi.org/10.1088/1748-9326/8/3/035008)).
livestock (dairy).Water: No evidence found in this sample.

livestock (general/beef).narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-resilient livestock systems** and **low-emission production** through interdisciplinary research in Africa, Latin America, and global rangelands. Specifically:
- **ILRI** analyzed farmer perceptions and adaptation strategies in the Nile Basin, linking climate change awareness to livestock ownership and extension services (Source: [Adaptation](https://doi.org/10.1017/s0021859610000687)).
- **CIAT** modeled climate risks across livestock supply chains, emphasizing context-specific adaptation needs in hot, resource-limited regions (Source: [Adaptation](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.gfs.2020.100488)).
- **CCAFS** quantified GHG mitigation potentials in Latin American cattle systems, addressing enteric methane reduction barriers (Source: [Mitigation](https://doi.org/10.3389/fsufs.2020.00065)).
livestock (general/beef).Adaptation:
• **ILRI** demonstrated that farmers’ adaptation to climate change in Ethiopia’s Nile Basin depends on **livestock ownership, extension services, and socio-economic factors** (e.g., education, credit access), using a Heckman sample selection model to link perception to action (Source: [Adaptation](https://doi.org/10.1017/s0021859610000687)).

• **ILRI/CCAFS** reviewed global livestock system vulnerabilities, highlighting **spatial heterogeneity in household responses** to climate variability, with pastoralists facing severe risks in tropical/subtropical regions (Source: [Adaptation](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agsy.2009.05.002)).

• **ILRI** analyzed rangeland ecosystem services under climate change, projecting **7.5–9.6% livestock declines** in Africa (economic loss: $9.7–12.6 billion) and identifying **policy gaps** for vulnerable pastoralist livelihoods (Source: [Adaptation](https://doi.org/10.1111/gcb.13995)).

• **ILRI** integrated socio-ecological drivers (e.g., **drought, migration, tsetse fly control**) to explain land-use shifts in Ethiopia, linking **livestock disease dynamics** to adaptation strategies (Source: [Adaptation](https://doi.org/10.1023/a:1008177712995)).

• **ILRI** modeled global rangelands under RCP 8.5, projecting **4.7% mean biomass decline** and **21.3% increased inter-annual variability**, prioritizing **adaptation strategies** for vulnerable regions (e.g., sub-Saharan Africa, Australia) (Source: [Adaptation, Water](https://doi.org/10.1088/1748-9326/ab7395)).

• **ILRI/CCAFS** framed adaptation as context-dependent, emphasizing **household vulnerability** in dryland livestock systems and the need for **carbon sequestration-focused frameworks** (Source: [Adaptation, Mitigation](https://doi.org/10.1007/s11027-009-9210-9)).
livestock (general/beef).Mitigation:
• **ILRI** modeled livestock system transitions, projecting **736 MtCO₂e/year emissions reductions by 2030** via **sustainable intensification** and land-use change avoidance (Source: [Mitigation](https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.1308044111)).

• **ILRI** clarified GHG emission estimates, advocating for **internationally standardized protocols** (e.g., IPCC/FAO) to avoid overestimation (e.g., excluding respired CO₂) and misdirecting mitigation policies (Source: [Mitigation](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.anifeedsci.2011.04.083)).

• **CCAFS** evaluated **enteric methane mitigation technologies** in Latin America, identifying **policy and adoption barriers** to meet Paris Agreement targets (Source: [Mitigation](https://doi.org/10.3389/fsufs.2020.00065)).

• **ILRI/CCAFS** proposed **land-use change-focused mitigation policies** (e.g., carbon pricing at $10/tCO₂e) as **5–10× more efficient** than livestock-only targets, while warning of **food availability trade-offs** (Source: [Mitigation](https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.1308044111)).
livestock (general/beef).Water: No evidence found in this sample.

livestock (general/small ruminants).narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-resilient livestock systems** through **adaptation strategies** and **mitigation innovations** across tropical and semi-arid regions. Specifically:
- CGIAR’s **climate-smart agriculture (CSA) research** in **Tanzania** enabled smallholder farmers to adopt **diversified CSA practices** (e.g., agroforestry, improved crop varieties) via the **Farms of the Future Approach (FotF)**, enhancing climate resilience (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3390/cli5030063](https://doi.org/10.3390/cli5030063)).
- The **LCA4CSA framework** (developed in **Colombia**) integrated **life cycle assessment (LCA)** to evaluate **compost use** and **animal feeding modifications** as tools to **reduce emissions** while improving adaptation capacity in mixed crop-livestock systems (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agsy.2019.02.001](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agsy.2019.02.001)).
livestock (general/small ruminants).Adaptation:
• CGIAR **modeled heat stress impacts** on global cattle production, projecting **$39.94 billion/year in losses by 2085** under high-emission scenarios, and recommended **heat-tolerant breeds, shade, and ventilation** as critical adaptation measures (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/s2542-5196(22)00002-x](https://doi.org/10.1016/s2542-5196(22)00002-x)).

• Researchers **quantified extreme heat stress days** for livestock (cattle, sheep, goats, poultry, pigs) globally, finding **increased risk in tropical/temperate zones** by 2100, with **outdoor livestock viability threatened** in low-income regions (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1111/gcb.15825](https://doi.org/10.1111/gcb.15825)).

• CGIAR’s **system dynamics analysis** in **Australian rangelands** revealed **herd recovery times of years to decades post-drought**, emphasizing **proactive restocking and adaptive grazing strategies** to sustain pastoral livelihoods (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1111/gcb.14669](https://doi.org/10.1111/gcb.14669)).

• In **Senegal’s semi-arid zones**, CGIAR linked **socio-economic factors (credit, extension services)** to **climate-smart technology (CST) adoption**, identifying **grain/fodder yield declines** due to erratic rains and advocating for **institutional support** to reduce climate risk (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3389/fclim.2024.1332196](https://doi.org/10.3389/fclim.2024.1332196)).

• The **LCA4CSA framework** (CIAT) demonstrated that **compost use** and **feed modifications** could **limit emissions** while improving **adaptation capacity** in mixed farming systems (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agsy.2019.02.001](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agsy.2019.02.001)).
livestock (general/small ruminants).Mitigation:
• CGIAR **assessed GHG mitigation potential** in **Bangladesh’s smallholder systems**, identifying **70–75% cost-saving reductions** via climate-smart agricultural techniques (e.g., improved manure management, feed efficiency) by 2050 (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.scitotenv.2021.147344](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.scitotenv.2021.147344)).

• Researchers **evaluated REDD+ projects** in the **Brazilian Amazon**, showing **50% deforestation reduction** in cattle-dependent farms through **conditional payments**, with forest conservation replacing pasture expansion (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1093/ajae/aay028](https://doi.org/10.1093/ajae/aay028)).

• CGIAR’s **pasture intensification study** in the **Brazilian Amazon** found **19–35% lower GHG emissions per kg of beef** in farms adopting **rotational grazing and sustainability programs**, driven by **reduced enteric fermentation and improved manure management** (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jclepro.2017.06.130](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jclepro.2017.06.130)).
livestock (general/small ruminants).Water: No evidence found in this sample.

livestock (small).narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **small livestock resilience, feeding systems, and climate-smart practices** across Africa and the Middle East through targeted research and innovation. Specifically:
- **CGIAR’s Climate-Smart Livestock Program** derived novel resilience phenotypes for Ethiopian sheep breeds to assess genetic and environmental responses to climate stressors (e.g., temperature-humidity index, rainfall) and proposed management interventions to enhance adaptation (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.livsci.2024.105633](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.livsci.2024.105633)).
- **Africa RISING (CGIAR-led)** advanced sustainable intensification of mixed crop-livestock systems in Mali to improve food security and livelihoods through integrated feeding strategies (Source: [doi:10.7910/DVN/BUMYV0](doi:10.7910/DVN/BUMYV0)).
- **ICARDA (CGAR Center)** documented milk and reproduction performance of Awassi sheep in Syria/Lebanon to inform genetic improvement programs (Source: [hdl:20.500.11766.1/FK2/XVSDSU](hdl:20.500.11766.1/FK2/XVSDSU)).
livestock (small).Adaptation:
• **CGIAR’s Livestock and Fish Program** estimated genetic parameters for resilience traits (e.g., weight gain, reproduction rates) in Ethiopian Bonga and Menz sheep breeds, demonstrating moderate heritability and the potential for within-breed selection to sustain productivity under climate variability, while highlighting the need for management interventions to address high environmental phenotypic variation (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.livsci.2024.105633](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.livsci.2024.105633)).

• **CGIAR’s Africa RISING initiative** promoted homemade concentrate preparation and fodder supplementation (e.g., oat-vetch, tree lucerne) in Ethiopia and Niger to accelerate sheep fattening and improve climate-resilient feeding systems for smallholders (Sources: [10568/168846](10568/168846), [doi:10.7910/DVN/DQGTMP](doi:10.7910/DVN/DQGTMP)).

• **CGIAR’s Genetic Improvement Program** designed participatory community-based breeding strategies for indigenous Ethiopian sheep breeds, engaging smallholders in defining breeding goals and establishing localized genetic improvement frameworks (Source: [10568/36126](10568/36126)).
livestock (small).Mitigation: • **CGIAR’s Climate Change Program** compared Tier 1 and Tier 2 methodologies to estimate enteric methane emission factors from smallholder sheep systems in Ethiopia, generating seasonal and annual methane production data to inform low-emission livestock management practices (Source: [doi:10.7910/DVN/4K20I6](doi:10.7910/DVN/4K20I6)).
livestock (small).Water: • **CGIAR’s Fodder Systems Research** demonstrated the feasibility of irrigated oat-vetch fodder production in Ethiopia’s highlands using shallow wells and manual pumps, addressing feed shortages during dry periods and improving sheep fattening outcomes for smallholders (Source: [doi:10.7910/DVN/DQGTMP](doi:10.7910/DVN/DQGTMP)).

maize.narrative:
CGIAR’s maize research prioritized **climate-resilient crop systems** through **stress-tolerant germplasm, climate-smart agronomy, and localized adaptation strategies**. Specifically:
- **CIMMYT** developed **drought- and heat-tolerant maize varieties** to offset yield losses under climate change (Sources: [1](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.crm.2017.10.001), [9](https://doi.org/10.1007/s00704-016-1931-6)).
- **CIAT** assessed **climate-forced production variability** (e.g., ENSO/IOD impacts) to improve risk management for maize systems (Source: [3](https://doi.org/10.1126/sciadv.aaw1976)).
- **IWMI** and **CIMMYT** evaluated **conservation agriculture (CA) and soil-water management** to enhance smallholder resilience in Southern Africa (Source: [5](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ecolecon.2019.05.013)).
maize.Adaptation:
• **CIMMYT** quantified **drought/heat tolerance benefits** in maize, showing 21–50% yield reductions under warming could be mitigated via stress-adapted varieties (Source: [2](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.crm.2017.10.001)).

• **CIAT** analyzed **climate change impacts on rainfed cereals** (maize, millet, sorghum, wheat) in SSA, finding **6% yield declines in East/Southern Africa by 2050** but highlighting **cultivar adaptation potential** (Source: [4](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eja.2024.127137)).

• **CIMMYT/IITA** compared **smallholder farmers’ perceptions of climate change** (e.g., delayed rainfall, reduced EGS/LGS precipitation) with meteorological data in Nigeria, validating local adaptation needs (Source: [8](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.wace.2016.12.001)).

• **CIMMYT** developed **climate-ready germplasm** via molecular breeding to accelerate drought/heat tolerance, emphasizing **localized, community-based adaptation** in East Africa (Sources: [6](https://doi.org/10.1016/b978-0-12-394275-3.00006-7), [10](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.gloenvcha.2008.08.005)).

• **CIMMYT** demonstrated **heat-tolerant maize varieties** could reduce yield losses by **36–93% in South Asia** under projected climate scenarios (Source: [9](https://doi.org/10.1007/s00704-016-1931-6)).

• **CGIAR** assessed **household-level vulnerability** to maize production declines, advocating for **targeted R&D** to alleviate poverty (Source: [1](https://doi.org/10.1016/s0959-3780(02)00090-0)).
maize.Mitigation: • **CIMMYT** promoted **sustainable agronomic practices** (e.g., conservation agriculture) as **low-carbon mitigation strategies** while enhancing resilience (Source: [6](https://doi.org/10.1016/b978-0-12-394275-3.00006-7)).
maize.Water:
• **CIAT** linked **climate variability (ENSO/IOD/TAV/NAO)** to **18% of global maize production variance**, informing **water-risk management** for synchronous crop failures (Source: [3](https://doi.org/10.1126/sciadv.aaw1976)).

• **CGIAR** emphasized **high-resolution climate/soil data** for yield simulations, addressing **hydrological uncertainties** in maize modeling (Source: [7](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.fcr.2015.03.004)).

• **CIAT/IWMI** integrated **soil-water conservation** (e.g., CA) with **cereal-legume rotations** to improve **water-use efficiency** in Southern Africa (Source: [5](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ecolecon.2019.05.013)).

rice.narrative:
CGIAR’s research centers advanced rice resilience and sustainability through climate-smart innovations. Specifically:
- CGIAR’s global modeling teams integrated farmer decision-making with biophysical crop models to simulate adaptive crop calendars, demonstrating that timely adjustments to sowing dates and cultivars could increase rice yields by ~12% under climate change (Source: **https://doi.org/10.1038/s41467-022-34411-5**).
- The Center consolidated 8,703 crop simulations into a global dataset, quantifying rice yield projections (with/without adaptation) across 91 countries to inform climate impact assessments (Source: **https://doi.org/10.1038/s41597-022-01150-7**).
rice.Adaptation:
• CGIAR’s climate impact modeling team demonstrated that **temperature-driven sowing date shifts** (e.g., >30°N/S latitudes) and adoption of **later-maturing rice cultivars** could mitigate yield losses, with precipitation-driven shifts critical in tropical regions (Source: **https://doi.org/10.1038/s41467-022-34411-5**)

• The Center assessed **heatwave and drought risks** for rice, showing a 27–46% increase in damaging hot spells at 4°C warming, while highlighting **photosynthesis declines at extreme temperatures** as a primary yield constraint (Source: **https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-019-02464-z**)

• CGIAR’s Africa-focused research quantified **yield declines of 24% (RCP 8.5, 2070) without adaptation** but found **8% yield gains** in rainfed systems and **25% gains in East African irrigated rice** when using high-temperature-sum varieties (Source: **https://doi.org/10.1111/gcb.13967**)

• The Center’s **Climate Risk Vulnerability Assessment** identified the **Mekong Delta** as a hotspot for rice vulnerability due to flooding/sea-level rise, despite high adaptive capacity from infrastructure (Source: **https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0213641**)

• Researchers at **IRRI (part of CGIAR)** linked **transpirational cooling** to rice quality, showing that **low humidity/high vapor pressure deficit** (e.g., dry seasons) improves head rice yield, while wet-season conditions increase chalkiness (Source: **https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0066218**)

• CGIAR’s global ENSO analysis revealed **rice yield anomalies of −4.3% to +0.8%** during El Niño, with La Niña years showing **below-normal yields (−4.5%)**, underscoring climate variability risks (Source: **https://doi.org/10.1038/ncomms4712**)
rice.Mitigation:
• CGIAR’s **Agriculture for Impact** team demonstrated that **Alternate Wetting and Drying (AWD) irrigation** in Bangladesh reduced **methane (CH₄) emissions by 37%** and **global warming potential (GWP) by 36%** without yield penalties (Source: **https://doi.org/10.1016/j.scitotenv.2020.139382**)

• The Center’s **India-focused GHG study** identified rice as a **major emission source (5.65 kg CO₂eq/kg)**, advocating mitigation options compatible with food security (Source: **https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agee.2016.12.024**)
rice.Water:
• CGIAR’s **multiple cropping systems analysis** highlighted **85 million hectares of irrigated rice** under intensified systems, with **34% of global rice area** already in multiple cropping (Source: **https://doi.org/10.1016/j.gloenvcha.2020.102131**)

• Researchers emphasized **water availability constraints** as a limiting factor for rainfed rice adaptation, even with high-temperature-sum varieties, while **East African irrigated rice** benefited from improved water management (Source: **https://doi.org/10.1111/gcb.13967**)

roots and tubers.narrative:
CGIAR’s **International Potato Center (CIP)** and partners prioritized **climate-resilient roots and tubers** through **genetic improvement, agronomic innovation, and risk management** across global agroecosystems. Specifically:
- CIP advanced **integrated seed health strategies** to combat potato degeneration in informal seed systems (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1111/ppa.12439](https://doi.org/10.1111/ppa.12439)).
- Researchers at CIP and CIAT developed **climate-adaptive potato varieties** by leveraging wild relatives for traits like heat tolerance and drought resilience (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1002/fes3.360](https://doi.org/10.1002/fes3.360)).
roots and tubers.Adaptation:
• **CIP** designed an **integrated seed health strategy**—combining disease-resistant varieties, on-farm management tools, and strategic seed replacement—to mitigate potato degeneration in developing countries’ informal seed systems (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1111/ppa.12439](https://doi.org/10.1111/ppa.12439) ).

• **CIP and global partners** conducted a **potato crop model inter-comparison** to quantify climate change impacts on yield, evapotranspiration, and water use efficiency, revealing temperature sensitivity (4.6% yield decline per °C) and regional variability in adaptation needs (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1111/gcb.13411](https://doi.org/10.1111/gcb.13411) ).

• **CIP** analyzed **varietal adoption in India**, identifying mismatches between climate projections (e.g., heat stress) and current cultivation of resistant/tolerant varieties, guiding prioritization for breeding and dissemination (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.crm.2019.01.001](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.crm.2019.01.001) ).

• **CIP and Bioversity** assessed **ecophysiological adaptation strategies** in the Andes, modeling pest dynamics and yield responses to CO₂, temperature, and soil water changes under projected climate scenarios (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1515/opag-2018-0029](https://doi.org/10.1515/opag-2018-0029) ).

• **CIP** mapped **future pest risks** under climate change, emphasizing **Integrated Pest Management (IPM)** to reduce pesticide dependency and enhance resilience in tropical/subtropical/temperate regions (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-030-28683-5_8](https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-030-28683-5_8) ).

• **CIP and partners** developed a **framework for climate-disease interactions**, analyzing spatial epidemic processes (e.g., *Phytophthora infestans*) to inform management feedback loops and ecosystem service trade-offs (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1365-3059.2010.02409.x](https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1365-3059.2010.02409.x) ).

• **CIP and CIAT** prioritized **wild relative species** for introgression of climate-adaptive traits (e.g., drought tolerance, heat resilience), using a breeder’s-equation logic to identify 26 high-potential species for diploid breeding programs (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1002/fes3.360](https://doi.org/10.1002/fes3.360) ).

• **CIP** evaluated **global late blight risk** under climate change, projecting shifts in planting dates and regional variability (e.g., increased risk in Lake Kivu Highlands, decreased risk in Southeast Asia) (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1111/gcb.12587](https://doi.org/10.1111/gcb.12587) ).

• **CIP and partners** tested **legume intercropping and silicon (Si) application** in Kenya to enhance water use efficiency (5.99–9.09 kg/ha/m³), reduce soil erosion by 45–80%, and improve drought resilience in rain-fed systems (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3389/fsufs.2020.566345](https://doi.org/10.3389/fsufs.2020.566345) ).

• **CIP** documented **Ugandan farmers’ coping strategies**, including adoption of drought-tolerant sweetpotato varieties and agroecological adjustments (e.g., drainage channels) to mitigate climate vulnerability (Source: [https://doi.org/10.5539/jas.v5n8p252](https://doi.org/10.5539/jas.v5n8p252) ).
roots and tubers.Mitigation: • **CIP** explored **climate-disease feedback loops**, including indirect effects of plant disease on greenhouse gas emissions and ecosystem services, to refine mitigation strategies (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1365-3059.2010.02409.x](https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1365-3059.2010.02409.x) ).
roots and tubers.Water:
• **CIP and CIAT** quantified **water use efficiency (WUE) and evapotranspiration (ET) variability** across potato-growing regions, highlighting 15% higher uncertainty in low-input systems and the need for ensemble modeling to improve climate impact assessments (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1111/gcb.13411](https://doi.org/10.1111/gcb.13411) ).

• **CIP** demonstrated **legume intercropping and silicon fertilization** as tools to improve **water productivity** (5.99–9.09 kg/ha/m³) and reduce soil erosion in rain-fed potato systems (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3389/fsufs.2020.566345](https://doi.org/10.3389/fsufs.2020.566345) ).

wheat.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-resilient wheat systems** globally through **genetic innovation, socio-economic adaptation, and disease mitigation**. Specifically:
- **CGIAR’s Climate-Smart Agriculture (CSA) research** demonstrated that integrating **soil-water conservation, agroforestry, and residue management** in Ethiopia boosted wheat yields by **30–45%** while sequestering **3–7x more soil carbon** (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3390/su13084515](https://doi.org/10.3390/su13084515)).
- **The Center’s crop modeling and breeding teams** deployed **heat-tolerant genotypes, late-flowering traits, and early vigor** to offset projected **1.9–16% yield declines** in tropical regions by 2050 (Sources: [https://doi.org/10.1088/1748-9326/abd970](https://doi.org/10.1088/1748-9326/abd970), [https://doi.org/10.1038/s41558-024-02069-0](https://doi.org/10.1038/s41558-024-02069-0)).
wheat.Adaptation:
• **CGIAR’s International Maize and Wheat Improvement Center (CIMMYT)** developed **heat-tolerant wheat genotypes** (e.g., late-flowering, early vigor) that, when combined with nitrogen fertilization, improved yields in developing countries despite climate stress (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1088/1748-9326/abd970](https://doi.org/10.1088/1748-9326/abd970)).

• **The Center leveraged citizen science** in Ethiopia, India, and Nicaragua to **map varietal climate responses** and refine recommendations, reducing climate bias in variety selection and enabling risk-based geographic extrapolation (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.1813720116](https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.1813720116)).

• **CGIAR modeled wheat blast vulnerability** under humid/warm climates, projecting a **13% global production loss (69M tons/year)** by mid-century, and identified **disease-resistant breeding targets** to mitigate food security risks (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1038/s41558-023-01902-2](https://doi.org/10.1038/s41558-023-01902-2)).

• **Researchers in China and India demonstrated** that **sowing-time adjustments, input efficiency, and irrigation optimization** could offset **6–25% yield declines** projected for 2050–2080, with late-sown regions showing the highest adaptability gains (Sources: [https://doi.org/10.1088/1748-9326/5/3/034012](https://doi.org/10.1088/1748-9326/5/3/034012), [https://doi.org/10.3354/cr01212](https://doi.org/10.3354/cr01212)).

• **CGIAR’s breeding programs** revealed that **<30% of elite wheat genotypes** currently adapt to **0.26°C warming**, with stability dropping **8.7% per °C increase**, necessitating revised paradigms for climate-resilient selection (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1038/s41558-024-02069-0](https://doi.org/10.1038/s41558-024-02069-0)).

• **The Center’s global ensemble models** projected that **adapting to <2°C warming** would increase **interannual yield variability** (especially in India) and **extreme low-yield events**, threatening regional food security (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1111/gcb.14542](https://doi.org/10.1111/gcb.14542)).

• **CGIAR analyzed climate variability in Ethiopia’s Borena zone**, linking **rainfall/temperature trends** to wheat/livestock yield declines and recommending **policy-targeted adaptation strategies** for food security (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-08-2024-0133](https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-08-2024-0133)).
wheat.Mitigation: • **CGIAR’s CSA research in Ethiopia** quantified **3–7x higher soil carbon sequestration** at 1m depth through **agroforestry, residue management, and soil-water conservation**, while reducing GHG emissions via enhanced soil fertility (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3390/su13084515](https://doi.org/10.3390/su13084515)).
wheat.Water:
• **CGIAR assessed water-use efficiency** in wheat by integrating **early vigor traits** to increase early-season water uptake, though model variability at county/state levels limited precision (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1088/1748-9326/abd970](https://doi.org/10.1088/1748-9326/abd970)).

• **The Center’s Ethiopian study** used **Normalized Difference Water Index (NDWI)** to demonstrate **higher soil moisture** under CSA practices, supporting drought-resilient wheat production (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3390/su13084515](https://doi.org/10.3390/su13084515)).

• **Researchers in India highlighted irrigation systems** as critical adaptation tools, with **timely-sown regions** showing yield improvements through efficient water management (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3354/cr01212](https://doi.org/10.3354/cr01212)).
